        // Special case for negative hash (TreeBin, etc)
        else if (eh < 0)
            return (p = e.find(h, key)) != null ? p.val : null;

        // Search rest of chain
        while ((e = e.next) != null) {
            if (e.hash == h &&
                ((ek = e.key) == key || (ek != null && key.equals(ek))))
                return e.val;
        }
    }
    return null;
}

  2. Size Calculation:

    • Uses striped counters (CounterCell array) to reduce contention
    • Size approximation during concurrent updates
    • Exact counts require traversal in some cases

    • Unique Features:

    • Does not allow null keys or values

    • Provides atomic compound operations (putIfAbsent, replace, etc.)
    • Supports concurrent retrieval and high expected concurrency for updates

    • Performance Considerations:

    • Higher memory overhead than HashMap

    • Slightly slower than HashMap for single-threaded use
    • Vastly outperforms synchronized HashMap for concurrent access
    • Read operations don't block other operations

docs.title: CopyOnWriteArrayList
docs.text:
public class CopyOnWriteArrayList<E> implements List<E>, RandomAccess, Cloneable, Serializable {
    final transient Object lock = new Object();
    private transient volatile Object[] array;

    public CopyOnWriteArrayList() {
        setArray(new Object[0]);
    }

    final void setArray(Object[] a) {
        array = a;
    }

    // ...
}

The internal structure of CopyOnWriteArrayList:

  1. Copy-on-Write Strategy:

    • Maintains an immutable array internally
    • Every modification creates a new array copy
    • Reads never block and see a consistent snapshot
    • Write operations are synchronized via an internal lock

    • Key Operations Internal Implementation:

    • add(E e): Creates new array with additional element

    public boolean add(E e) {
    synchronized (lock) {
        Object[] elements = getArray();
        int len = elements.length;
        Object[] newElements = Arrays.copyOf(elements, len + 1);
        newElements[len] = e;
        setArray(newElements);
        return true;
    }
}
    • remove(int index): Creates new array without element
    public E remove(int index) {
    synchronized (lock) {
        Object[] elements = getArray();
        int len = elements.length;
        E oldValue = get(elements, index);
        int numMoved = len - index - 1;
        if (numMoved == 0)
            setArray(Arrays.copyOf(elements, len - 1));
        else {
            Object[] newElements = new Object[len - 1];
            System.arraycopy(elements, 0, newElements, 0, index);
            System.arraycopy(elements, index + 1, newElements, index, numMoved);
            setArray(newElements);
        }
        return oldValue;
    }
}
    • get(int index): Direct array access, no locking
    public E get(int index) {
    return get(getArray(), index);
}

private E get(Object[] a, int index) {
    return (E) a[index];
}

  2. Iterator Behavior:

    • Iterators work on a snapshot of the array at creation time
    • Never throw ConcurrentModificationException
    • Do not reflect modifications made after iterator creation
    public Iterator<E> iterator() {
    return new COWIterator<E>(getArray(), 0);
}

static final class COWIterator<E> implements ListIterator<E> {
    private final Object[] snapshot;
    private int cursor;

    COWIterator(Object[] elements, int initialCursor) {
        cursor = initialCursor;
        snapshot = elements;
    }
    // ...
}

  3. Performance Characteristics:

    • Read operations: O(1) for random access, very fast
    • Write operations: O(n) due to array copying, very expensive
    • Memory usage: Can be high during modifications
    • Exceptional for read-heavy, write-seldom scenarios

    • Important Notes:

    • Thread-safe without locking for reads

    • Very expensive for frequent modifications
    • Iterators reflect a moment-in-time snapshot
    • No support for structural modifications through iterators

docs.title: LinkedHashMap
docs.text:
public class LinkedHashMap<K,V> extends HashMap<K,V>
        implements Map<K,V> {

    // Doubly-linked list of entries
    transient LinkedHashMap.Entry<K,V> head;
    transient LinkedHashMap.Entry<K,V> tail;

    // True for access-order, false for insertion-order
    final boolean accessOrder;

    static class Entry<K,V> extends HashMap.Node<K,V> {
        Entry<K,V> before, after;
        Entry(int hash, K key, V value, Node<K,V> next) {
            super(hash, key, value, next);
        }
    }

    // ...
}

The internal structure of LinkedHashMap:

  1. Hybrid Structure:

    • Extends HashMap for basic hash table functionality
    • Adds doubly-linked list to maintain iteration order
    • Each entry is both in hash table and linked list

    • Entry Linking:

    • Custom Entry class extends HashMap.Node

    • Adds before and after pointers for linked list
    • Head and tail references track list ends
    • New entries added to end of list (tail)

    • Ordering Modes:

    • Insertion-order (default): Entries remain in order they were added

    • Access-order: Entries reordered when accessed (get/put)
    • Access-order mode enables LRU (Least Recently Used) cache behavior

    • Key Operations Internal Implementation:

    • Inherits put and get from HashMap, but overrides:

    • afterNodeInsertion: Called after node insertion to maintain order

    void afterNodeInsertion(boolean evict) {
    LinkedHashMap.Entry<K,V> first;
    // removeEldestEntry only matters in access-order mode
    if (evict && (first = head) != null && removeEldestEntry(first)) {
        K key = first.key;
        removeNode(hash(key), key, null, false, true);
    }
}
    • afterNodeAccess: Moves recently accessed node to end (for access-order)
    void afterNodeAccess(Node<K,V> e) {
    LinkedHashMap.Entry<K,V> last;
    if (accessOrder && (last = tail) != e) {
        LinkedHashMap.Entry<K,V> p =
            (LinkedHashMap.Entry<K,V>)e, b = p.before, a = p.after;
        p.after = null;
        if (b == null)
            head = a;
        else
            b.after = a;
        if (a != null)
            a.before = b;
        else
            last = b;
        if (last == null)
            head = p;
        else {
            p.before = last;
            last.after = p;
        }
        tail = p;
        ++modCount;
    }
}
    • removeEldestEntry: Hook for implementing LRU caches
    protected boolean removeEldestEntry(Map.Entry<K,V> eldest) {
    return false;  // Default implementation
}

  2. LRU Cache Implementation:

    • Create subclass that overrides removeEldestEntry
    class LRUCache<K,V> extends LinkedHashMap<K,V> {
    private final int capacity;

    public LRUCache(int capacity) {
        super(capacity, 0.75f, true);  // true for access-order
        this.capacity = capacity;
    }

    @Override
    protected boolean removeEldestEntry(Map.Entry<K,V> eldest) {
        //If the current number of entries exceeds the cache's capacity, return true to remove the eldest entry.
        return size() > capacity;
    }
}

  3. Performance Characteristics:

    • Get/put: O(1) average case, like HashMap
    • Iteration: O(n) but more efficient than HashMap
    • Slightly more overhead than HashMap for entry maintenance
    • Predictable iteration order unlike HashMap

docs.title: EnumMap
docs.text:
public class EnumMap<K extends Enum<K>, V> extends AbstractMap<K,V>
        implements Serializable, Cloneable {

    private final Class<K> keyType;
    private transient Object[] vals;
    private transient int size = 0;
    private transient int totalSize;

    // ...
}

The internal structure of EnumMap:

  1. Array-Based Implementation:

    • Uses a simple array indexed by enum ordinal values
    • No hash calculation required
    • Very compact and efficient representation
    • Size of array exactly matches number of possible enum values

    • Key Type Enforcement:

    • Requires keys to be of a specific enum type

    • Stores the enum class for type checking and reflection

    • Key Operations Internal Implementation:

    • put(K key, V value): Direct array access by ordinal

    public V put(K key, V value) {
    typeCheck(key);
    int index = key.ordinal();
    Object oldValue = vals[index];
    vals[index] = maskNull(value);
    if (oldValue == null)
        size++;
    return unmaskNull(oldValue);
}
    • get(Object key): Direct array access if key is valid enum
    public V get(Object key) {
    return (isValidKey(key)) ? unmaskNull(vals[((Enum<?>)key).ordinal()]) : null;
}
    • containsKey(Object key): Check array slot at ordinal
    public boolean containsKey(Object key) {
    return (isValidKey(key) && vals[((Enum<?>)key).ordinal()] != null);
}

  2. Null Handling:

    • Uses a special marker object for null values
    • maskNull() and unmaskNull() methods handle conversion

    • Performance Characteristics:

    • All basic operations: O(1) with minimal overhead

    • Extremely memory efficient
    • Faster than HashMap for enum keys
    • Iteration in natural enum order

    • Important Notes:

    • Type-safe by requiring specific enum type

    • Not thread-safe
    • Iterator reflects natural enum constant order

docs.title: EnumSet
docs.text:
public abstract class EnumSet<E extends Enum<E>> extends AbstractSet<E>
        implements Cloneable, Serializable {

    final Class<E> elementType;
    final Enum<?>[] universe;

    // Factory methods create appropriate implementation
    public static <E extends Enum<E>> EnumSet<E> noneOf(Class<E> elementType) {
        Enum<?>[] universe = getUniverse(elementType);
        if (universe.length <= 64)
            return new RegularEnumSet<>(elementType, universe);
        else
            return new JumboEnumSet<>(elementType, universe);
    }

    // ...
}

// Regular implementation for <= 64 elements
class RegularEnumSet<E extends Enum<E>> extends EnumSet<E> {
    private long elements = 0L;
    // ...
}

// Jumbo implementation for > 64 elements
class JumboEnumSet<E extends Enum<E>> extends EnumSet<E> {
    private long[] elements;
    // ...
}

The internal structure of EnumSet:

  1. Bit Vector Implementation:

    • Uses bits to represent presence/absence of enum values
    • Two concrete implementations:
      • RegularEnumSet: Uses a single long (for <= 64 elements)
      • JumboEnumSet: Uses long[] array (for > 64 elements)
    • Factory methods choose appropriate implementation

    • Bit Operations:

    • Set bit: elements |= (1L << ordinal)

    • Clear bit: elements &= ~(1L << ordinal)
    • Test bit: (elements & (1L << ordinal)) != 0

    • Key Operations Internal Implementation (RegularEnumSet):

    • add(E e): Sets the bit for element's ordinal

    public boolean add(E e) {
    typeCheck(e);
    long oldElements = elements;
    elements |= (1L << e.ordinal());
    return elements != oldElements;
}
    • remove(Object o): Clears the bit if valid element
    public boolean remove(Object o) {
    if (o == null || o.getClass() != elementType)
        return false;
    Enum<?> e = (Enum<?>) o;
    long oldElements = elements;
    elements &= ~(1L << e.ordinal());
    return elements != oldElements;
}
    • contains(Object o): Tests the bit if valid element
    public boolean contains(Object o) {
    if (o == null || o.getClass() != elementType)
        return false;
    Enum<?> e = (Enum<?>) o;
    return (elements & (1L << e.ordinal())) != 0;
}

  2. Performance Characteristics:

    • All basic operations: O(1) with minimal overhead
    • Extremely memory efficient (only a few longs even for large enum sets)
    • Iteration still O(n) where n is number of enum values
    • Set operations (union, intersection) use efficient bitwise operations

    • Important Notes:

    • Type-safe by requiring specific enum type

    • Not thread-safe
    • Iterator reflects natural enum constant order
    • Most efficient set implementation in Java

docs.title: CopyOnWriteArraySet
docs.text:
public class CopyOnWriteArraySet<E> extends AbstractSet<E>
        implements Serializable {

    private final CopyOnWriteArrayList<E> al;

    public CopyOnWriteArraySet() {
        al = new CopyOnWriteArrayList<E>();
    }

    // ...
}

CopyOnWriteArraySet wraps a CopyOnWriteArrayList to provide Set semantics:

  1. Internal Implementation:

    • Uses a CopyOnWriteArrayList for storage
    • Adds uniqueness check before adding elements
    • Inherits thread-safety from CopyOnWriteArrayList

    • Key Operations:

    • add(E e): Checks uniqueness, then adds to list

    public boolean add(E e) {
    return al.addIfAbsent(e);
}
    • contains(Object o): Delegates to list
    public boolean contains(Object o) {
    return al.contains(o);
}

  2. Performance Characteristics:

    • Inherits COW behavior (expensive writes, fast reads)
    • O(n) for add operations (must check for duplicates)
    • Thread-safe without locking for reads

docs.title: Comparator:
docs.text:
If you wanna sort other than default sorting order then use comparator**

here we provide list to sort as first argument and how to sort with custom comparator in second argument

Collections.sort(people, new PersonComparator());

Java Comparator Interface: A Deep Dive

The Comparator interface is a fundamental part of Java's Collections framework, residing in the java.util package. It's a functional interface that defines a strategy for comparing two objects of the same type.

It is often used when you need to sort objects in a ==non-natural order or when the objects do not have a natural ordering.==

This interface is crucial for sorting collections of objects or for maintaining sorted data structures like TreeSet or TreeMap.

docs.title: Example:
docs.text:
import java.util.*;

// Define the Person class
class Person {
    String name;
    int age;

    // Constructor
    Person(String name, int age) {
        this.name = name;
        this.age = age;
    }

    // Getter methods for name and age
    public String getName() { return name; }
    public int getAge() { return age; }

    @Override
    public String toString() {
        return name + " (" + age + ")";
    }
}

// Comparator to sort by age and then by name
class PersonComparator implements Comparator<Person> {
    @Override
    public int compare(Person p1, Person p2) {
        // Compare by age first
        int ageComparison = Integer.compare(p1.getAge(), p2.getAge());
        if (ageComparison != 0) {
            return ageComparison;
        }
        // If ages are equal, compare by name
        return p1.getName().compareTo(p2.getName());
    }
}

public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Create a list of Person objects
        List<Person> people = Arrays.asList(
            new Person("John", 25),
            new Person("Alice", 30),
            new Person("Bob", 25),
            new Person("Diana", 30)
        );

        // Sort using the custom comparator
        Collections.sort(people, new PersonComparator());

        // Print sorted list
        for (Person person : people) {
            System.out.println(person);
        }
    }
}

docs.title: Key Points:
docs.text:
1. Functional Interface: Comparator is annotated with @FunctionalInterface, meaning it has exactly one abstract method: compare(T o1, T o2).

  2. Type Parameter: It uses a type parameter <T>, allowing it to compare objects of any type.

  3. Method Signature:

    int compare(T o1, T o2)

  4. Return Value:

    • ==Negative integer if o1 < o2==
    • ==Zero if o1 == o2==
    • ==Positive integer if o1 > o2==
    • Consistency with equals(): For optimal behavior, the comparator should be consistent with equals(). If compare(x, y) == 0, then x.equals(y) should return true.

  5. Null Handling: The compare method should handle null values gracefully if they're expected. Java 8+ provides utility methods like Comparator.nullsFirst() and Comparator.nullsLast() to assist with this.

  6. Reversing Order: You can reverse the order of comparison using Collections.reverseOrder() or the reversed() method introduced in Java 8.

  7. Chaining Comparators: Java 8 introduced the thenComparing() method, allowing you to chain multiple comparators for multi-field sorting. Comparator<T> thenComparing(Comparator<? super T> other) Combines this comparator with another to create a composite comparator that first uses this comparator, then the other comparator if the first is equal.

  8. Static Factory Methods: Java 8 added several static methods to create comparators, like Comparator.comparing(), Comparator.comparingInt(), etc.

  9. Natural Ordering: When used with TreeSet or TreeMap, if no comparator is provided, the natural ordering of elements is used (which requires elements to implement Comparable).

  10. Thread Safety: Comparators are typically stateless and therefore thread-safe. However, if you implement a stateful comparator, be aware of potential concurrency issues.

  11. Performance Considerations: The compare method may be called multiple times during a sort operation. Ensure it's efficient, especially for large datasets.

  12. Serialization: Comparator extends Serializable. If you implement a custom comparator, consider implementing Serializable as well.

  13. Lambda Expressions: With Java 8+, you can use lambda expressions to create comparators concisely: like here we have created a comparator on String class which we will use to sort the string based on their length

    Comparator<String> lengthComparator = (s1, s2) -> Integer.compare(s1.length(), s2.length());

  14. Method References: You can use method references for even more concise comparators:

    Comparator<String> naturalOrder = String::compareTo;

Comparison of Comparator and Comparable:

  • Comparator: External to the class; allows multiple ways to sort objects. Useful when you need different sorting strategies.
  • Comparable: Internal to the class; defines a natural ordering. Useful when a single default ordering is sufficient.

docs.title: 2. Comparable
docs.text: The Comparable interface is way to define the natural ordering of objects. It is part of the java.lang package and ==is used when you want to define a default ordering for objects of a class.==

docs.title: Key Points of the Comparable Interface
docs.text:
1. Purpose:

    • It allows a class to define its own natural ordering, which can be used by sorting methods that don’t take a Comparator.

    • Methods:

    • int compareTo(T o): This method compares the current object (this) with another object o of type T. It returns:

      • A negative integer if this is less than o.
      • Zero if this is equal to o.
      • A positive integer if this is greater than o.

    • Usage:

    • The compareTo method is often used when you want to sort objects naturally (e.g., by default in collections or arrays).

import java.util.*;

// Define the Person class with Comparable
class Person implements Comparable<Person> {
    String name;
    int age;

    // Constructor
    Person(String name, int age) {
        this.name = name;
        this.age = age;
    }

    // Getter methods for name and age
    public String getName() { return name; }
    public int getAge() { return age; }

    // Implement compareTo to compare by age, then by name
    @Override
    public int compareTo(Person other) {
        int ageComparison = Integer.compare(this.age, other.age);
        if (ageComparison != 0) {
            return ageComparison;
        }
        return this.name.compareTo(other.name);
    }

    @Override
    public String toString() {
        return name + " (" + age + ")";
    }
}

public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Create a list of Person objects
        List<Person> people = Arrays.asList(
            new Person("John", 25),
            new Person("Alice", 30),
            new Person("Bob", 25),
            new Person("Diana", 30)
        );

        // Sort using natural ordering defined by compareTo
        Collections.sort(people);

        // Print sorted list
        for (Person person : people) {
            System.out.println(person);
        }
    }
}

  • Person Class: Now implements Comparable<Person>. The compareTo method first compares by age. If ages are equal, it compares by name.
  • Main Class: Creates a list of Person objects and sorts them using the natural ordering defined by compareTo.

Comparison of Comparator and Comparable:

  • Comparator: External to the class; allows multiple ways to sort objects. Useful when you need different sorting strategies.
  • Comparable: Internal to the class; defines a natural ordering. Useful when a single default ordering is sufficient.

Use  Comparable when you wanna sort on default sorting order then you dont need to provide any custom comparator this will work Collections.sort(people); but if you wanna sort other than default sorting order then use comparator

here we provide list to sort as first argument and how to sort with custom comparator in second argument

Collections.sort(people, new PersonComparator());

docs.title: 3. String Immutability
docs.text:
Java's decision to make strings immutable brings significant advantages to the language:

  1. String Pool (Interning):

  2. Concept: The String Pool is a specialized area in the Java heap where unique string literals are stored. When you create a string using a literal (e.g., String s = "hello";), the JVM checks if an identical string already exists in the pool. If it does, the reference to the existing string is returned, saving memory.

  3. Immutability is Key: If strings were mutable, changing one string would inadvertently modify all references to that same string in the pool, leading to unexpected behavior and potential bugs.

Example:

Java

String s1 = "hello";
String s2 = "hello";

// s1 and s2 point to the same object in the string pool
System.out.println(s1 == s2); // true

Use code with caution.

  1. Security:

  2. Sensitive Data: Strings often represent sensitive information like passwords, file paths, network addresses, and database connection parameters. Immutability ensures that these values cannot be altered once set, reducing the risk of unauthorized modifications and potential security breaches.

  3. Multithreading:

  4. Thread Safety: In multithreaded environments, immutable objects are inherently thread-safe. Multiple threads can access and use the same string object without fear of data races or inconsistent state.

  5. Caching and Performance:

  6. Hash Codes: Strings are frequently used in hash-based data structures (e.g., HashMaps). Immutability guarantees that a string's hash code remains constant throughout its lifetime. This allows for efficient caching of hash codes, improving the performance of lookups and comparisons.

  7. Object Creation: Immutable strings can be shared freely without the need for defensive copies, leading to potential performance gains.

  8. Class Loading:

  9. Reliable Identification: Java's class loading mechanism relies on strings to uniquely identify classes. Immutability ensures that the names of classes remain unchanged, preventing errors and ensuring the correct class is loaded.

Example of Immutability

String message = "Hello";
message.concat(" World!"); 

System.out.println(message); // Still prints "Hello"

// To get the modified string, assign the result to a new variable:
String newMessage = message.concat(" World!");
System.out.println(newMessage); // Prints "Hello World!"

Trade-offs

While immutability offers numerous benefits, it's important to be aware of the following:

  • String Concatenation: Repeatedly concatenating strings can be inefficient due to the creation of temporary string objects. Use StringBuilder or StringBuffer for efficient string manipulation.

  • Flexibility: In scenarios where you need to modify string content frequently, consider using mutable string alternatives like StringBuilder.

docs.title: 4.1 Java Generics
docs.text:
A generic type is a class or interface that is parameterized over types. For example, a generic class can work with any type specified by the user, and that type can be enforced at compile time.

The syntax for a generic type is:

class ClassName<T> {
    // Class body
}

Where T is the type parameter, which can be any valid identifier.

Conventionally, single-letter names are used for type parameters, such as

T for Type,

E for Element,

K for Key,

V for Value, etc.

public class Box<T> { //  one or more type parameters
//  These type parameters are placeholders that are replaced with specific types when the class is instantiated.
    private T value;

    public T getValue() {
        return value;
    }

    public void setValue(T value) {
        this.value = value;
    }
}

public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Box<Integer> box = new Box<>();  // Box is now type-safe
        box.setValue(1);  // No issue, it's an Integer
        Integer i = box.getValue();  // No casting needed
        System.out.println(i);
    }
}

Here, Box<T> is a generic class.

The type parameter T will be replaced with a specific type when an object of Box is created.

Now, the Box class is type-safe, and we will not encounter the ClassCastException because the types are enforced at compile time.

So, In simpler terms, generics allow you to write code that can work with any object type while ensuring type safety at compile time.

Generics help us write more flexible and reusable code. For example, without generics, we would have to write multiple versions of the same class to handle different data types, leading to code duplication.

A generic class can have more than one type parameter.

class Pair<K, V> {
    private K key;
    private V value;

    public Pair(K key, V value) {
        this.key = key;
        this.value = value;
    }

    public K getKey() {
        return key;
    }

    public V getValue() {
        return value;
    }
}

 This class can be used to store pairs of related data (like key-value pairs).

public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Pair<String, Integer> pair = new Pair<>("Age", 30);
        System.out.println("Key: " + pair.getKey());   // Prints: Key: Age
        System.out.println("Value: " + pair.getValue()); // Prints: Value: 30
    }
}

 Here, the Pair<K, V> class has two type parameters K and V, and you can specify the types when you create an instance of Pair.

Type Parameter Naming Conventions

While you can name type parameters anything, the convention is to use single letters that describe the purpose of the type parameter:

  • T: Type
  • E: Element (used in collections)
  • K: Key (used in maps)
  • V: Value (used in maps)
  • N: Number

docs.title: Generic Interface
docs.text:
A generic interface in Java allows you to define an interface with type parameters. This means that the interface can work with any type specified at the time of implementation. Generic interfaces are commonly used when the type of the objects that the interface deals with is not known until runtime.

You declare a generic interface in the same way you would declare a generic class or method, using angle brackets <> to specify type parameters. Here's a basic example of a generic interface:

interface Container<T> {
    void add(T item);
    T get();
}

In this example, T is the generic type parameter for the Container interface. The add method accepts an argument of type T, and the get method returns a value of type T.

When you implement a generic interface, you need to specify the type for the generic parameter, or you can continue to make the implementation generic by using type parameters.

Implementing with a specific type

class StringContainer implements Container<String> {
    private String item;

    @Override
    public void add(String item) {
        this.item = item;
    }

    @Override
    public String get() {
        return item;
    }
}

In this example, StringContainer implements the Container interface with String as the specified type parameter.

Implementing a generic interface generically

class GenericContainer<T> implements Container<T> {
    private T item;

    @Override
    public void add(T item) {
        this.item = item;
    }

    @Override
    public T get() {
        return item;
    }
}

 In this case, the class GenericContainer remains generic and can work with any type, just like the Container interface.

Generic Interfaces with Multiple Type Parameters

A generic interface can have multiple type parameters. This is useful when you need to work with more than one type in the same interface.

interface Pair {
    K getKey();
    V getValue();
}

Here, the Pair interface defines two generic type parameters K and V. Implementing this interface will require specifying both types.

class KeyValuePair implements Pair {
    private K key;
    private V value;

    public KeyValuePair(K key, V value) {
        this.key = key;
        this.value = value;
    }

    @Override
    public K getKey() {
        return key;
    }

    @Override
    public V getValue() {
        return value;
    }
}

You can create an instance of KeyValuePair like this:

Pair pair = new KeyValuePair<>("Age", 30);
System.out.println(pair.getKey() + ": " + pair.getValue());

docs.title: Bounded Types
docs.text:
Bounded types in generics allow you to restrict the types that can be used as arguments for type parameters.

This is useful when you want to enforce that a generic type must be a subtype or supertype of a specific class or implement a particular interface.

docs.title: Upper Bounded Wildcards
docs.text:
An upper bound restricts the type to be a specific type or a subclass of that type using the extends keyword.

class ClassName<T extends SuperClass> {
    // Class body
}

 Here, T is restricted to be either SuperClass or any subclass of SuperClass.

class Box<T extends Number> {
    private T value;

    public void setValue(T value) {
        this.value = value;
    }

    public T getValue() {
        return value;
    }
}

public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Box<Integer> intBox = new Box<>();
        intBox.setValue(100);  // Only Numbers or its subclasses allowed
        System.out.println(intBox.getValue());
    }
}

In this example, the Box class can only accept Number and its subclasses (like Integer, Double, etc.).

docs.title: Lower Bounded Wildcards
docs.text:
A lower bound restricts the type to be a specific type or a supertype of that type using the super keyword.

void someMethod(List<? super Integer> list) {
    // Code
}

This method accepts a list of Integer or its supertype (like Number or Object).

public static void addNumbers(List<? super Integer> list) {
    list.add(10);
    list.add(20);
}

docs.title: Multiple Bounds
docs.text:
You can use multiple bounds to enforce that a type parameter must extend more than one class or interface.

class ClassName<T extends Class1 & Interface1 & Interface2> {
    // Class body
}


class BoundedType<T extends Number & Comparable<T>> {
    private T value;

    public BoundedType(T value) {
        this.value = value;
    }

    public boolean isGreaterThan(T other) {
        return value.compareTo(other) > 0;
    }
}

In this example, T is restricted to Number types that also implement the Comparable interface.

Memory updated

Let's dive into bounded types, generics in enums, and generic methods. Below are your notes, covering these advanced topics in generics:

docs.title: Bounded Types
docs.text: Bounded types in generics allow you to restrict the types that can be used as arguments for type parameters. This is useful when you want to enforce that a generic type must be a subtype or supertype of a specific class or implement a particular interface.

docs.title: Upper Bounded Wildcards
docs.text:
An upper bound restricts the type to be a specific type or a subclass of that type using the extends keyword.

Syntax:

class ClassName<T extends SuperClass> { // Class body}

Here, T is restricted to be either SuperClass or any subclass of SuperClass.

Example:

public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Box<Integer> intBox = new Box<>();
        intBox.setValue(100); // Only Numbers or its subclasses allowed System.out.println(intBox.getValue()); 
    }
}

In this example, the Box class can only accept Number and its subclasses (like Integer, Double, etc.).

docs.title: Lower Bounded Wildcards
docs.text:
A lower bound restricts the type to be a specific type or a supertype of that type using the super keyword.

Syntax:

java

Copy code

void someMethod(List<? super Integer> list) { // Code}

This method accepts a list of Integer or its supertype (like Number or Object).

Example:

java

Copy code

public static void addNumbers(List<? super Integer> list) { list.add(10); list.add(20);}

Here, the list can accept Integer and any of its supertypes, such as Number or Object.

docs.title: Multiple Bounds
docs.text:
You can use multiple bounds to enforce that a type parameter must extend more than one class or interface.

Syntax:

java

Copy code

class ClassName<T extends Class1 & Interface1 & Interface2> { // Class body}

Example:

java

Copy code

class BoundedType<T extends Number & Comparable<T>> { private T value; public BoundedType(T value) { this.value = value; } public boolean isGreaterThan(T other) { return value.compareTo(other) > 0; }}

In this example, T is restricted to Number types that also implement the Comparable interface.

docs.title: Generics in Methods
docs.text:
Generic methods allow you to define methods that can take parameters of generic types. The type parameters are declared before the return type of the method.

public <T> void someMethod(T param) {
    // Method body
}


public class Utility {
    public static <T> void printArray(T[] array) {
        for (T element : array) {
            System.out.println(element);
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Integer[] intArray = {1, 2, 3, 4};
        String[] strArray = {"a", "b", "c"};

        Utility.printArray(intArray);
        Utility.printArray(strArray);
    }
}

Here, printArray is a generic method that can accept arrays of any type.

docs.title: Bounded Type Parameters in Methods
docs.text:
Just like with classes, you can use bounded types in generic methods to restrict the types allowed.

public static <T extends Number> void processNumber(T num) {
    System.out.println(num.doubleValue());
}

Here, the method accepts only Number or its subclasses, ensuring type safety while processing the number.

docs.title: Generics in Enums
docs.text:
While enums in Java don’t support generics directly, you can use generics within methods defined in enums or as part of their constructors.

enum Operation {
    ADD, SUBTRACT;

    public <T extends Number> double operate(T a, T b) {
        switch (this) {
            case ADD:
                return a.doubleValue() + b.doubleValue();
            case SUBTRACT:
                return a.doubleValue() - b.doubleValue();
            default:
                throw new AssertionError("Unknown operation");
        }
    }
}

public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        double result = Operation.ADD.operate(10, 20);
        System.out.println("Result: " + result);
    }
}

In this example, the Operation enum defines a generic method operate that works with any type that extends Number.

docs.title: Wildcards in Generics
docs.text:
Wildcards are used in generics to represent an unknown type. The two main types of wildcards are:

  1. Unbounded Wildcards (?): Represents any type. used when we are only doing reading with the values and not writing Example:
public void printList(List<?> list) {
    for (Object elem : list) {
        System.out.println(elem);
    }
}

docs.title: Advantages of Bounded Types
docs.text:
• Type Safety: Bounds enforce restrictions, preventing the use of inappropriate types.
  • Reusability: You can write more flexible and reusable code with the correct use of bounds.
  • Code Clarity: Proper bounds make the purpose of a generic class or method clearer to the user.

docs.title: HashMap: Internal Architecture
docs.text: At its heart, a HashMap is a data structure that implements the Map interface, allowing us to store key-value pairs and retrieve values in constant time O(1) under ideal conditions. The magic behind this efficiency lies in the hashing mechanism.

docs.title: *Hashing:*
docs.text: Hashing is a process of converting an object into integer form by using the method hashCode()

docs.title: Core Components of a HashMap
docs.text:
1. Buckets (Array): The backbone of a HashMap is an array, often called the bucket array
  2. Hash Function: The mechanism that converts keys into array indices
  3. Entries: The key-value pairs stored within the structure
  4. Collision Resolution: Techniques to handle when different keys hash to the same index

HashMap contains an array of Node and Node can represent a class having the following objects :

  1. int hash
  2. K key
  3. V value
  4. Node next

docs.title: 1. Initialization
docs.text:
When we create a new HashMap, it initializes with:

// Creating a new HashMap with default parameters
HashMap<String, Integer> userScores = new HashMap<>();

// With custom initial capacity and load factor
HashMap<String, Integer> customMap = new HashMap<>(32, 0.8f);
  • A default capacity (typically 16 buckets)
  • A load factor (typically 0.75)
  • An empty array of buckets

docs.title: 2. Insertion Process (put operation)
docs.text:
When we add a key-value pair:

  1. The hash code of the key is calculated using the key's hashCode() method
  2. The hash code is further processed to improve distribution (typically using bitwise operations)
  3. The processed hash is used to determine the bucket index
  4. If the bucket is empty, the entry is placed there
  5. If the bucket already contains entries (collision), the new entry is:
    • Added to the linked list (pre-Java 8)
    • Added to the tree structure when the linked list grows beyond a threshold (Java 8+)

// Adding entries to our HashMap
userScores.put("Alice", 95);
userScores.put("Bob", 87);
userScores.put("Charlie", 92);

docs.title: 3. Retrieval Process (get operation)
docs.text:
To retrieve a value:

  1. The hashCode of the key is calculated
  2. the same hash processing which was used earlier is used to calculate the hashCode
  3. The bucket (array) is searched for an entry with matching key
  4. If found, the value is returned; otherwise, null is returned

docs.title: 6. HashSet Internal Working
docs.text: A HashSet in Java is built on top of a HashMap. This means that a HashSet uses a HashMap internally to store its elements. Here’s how it works:

docs.title: 1. HashSet is Backed by a HashMap
docs.text:
• When you create a HashSet, it internally creates a HashMap.

  • In this HashMap, the keys are the elements of the HashSet, and the values are dummy objects (usually a single Object called PRESENT).

  • This is why a HashSet ensures uniqueness: since HashMap keys are unique, the HashSet elements (which are the keys) are also unique

docs.title: 2. Internal Structure
docs.text:
Here’s what the internal structure of a HashSet looks like:

  • map: This is the HashMap that stores the elements of the HashSet.

  • PRESENT: A dummy object used as the value for all keys in the HashMap. Since we only care about the keys (the HashSet elements), the value is irrelevant.

docs.title: 3. How Elements Are Stored
docs.text: When you add an element to a HashSet, it is added as a key in the backing HashMap, with the value set to the dummy PRESENT object.

docs.title: Example: Adding an Element
docs.text:
<span style="color: #88c0d0;">HashSet</span><span style="color: #81a1c1;"><</span><span style="color: #88c0d0;">String</span><span style="color: #81a1c1;">></span> set <span style="color: #81a1c1;">=</span> <span style="color: #81a1c1;">new</span> <span style="color: #88c0d0;">HashSet</span><span style="color: #81a1c1;"><</span><span style="color: #81a1c1;">></span><span style="color: #81a1c1;">(</span><span style="color: #81a1c1;">)</span><span style="color: #81a1c1;">;</span> set<span style="color: #81a1c1;">.</span><span style="color: #88c0d0;">add</span><span style="color: #81a1c1;">(</span><span style="color: #a3be8c;">"Apple"</span><span style="color: #81a1c1;">)</span><span style="color: #81a1c1;">;</span>

Internally, this translates to:

map<span style="color: #81a1c1;">.</span><span style="color: #88c0d0;">put</span><span style="color: #81a1c1;">(</span><span style="color: #a3be8c;">"Apple"</span><span style="color: #81a1c1;">,</span> <span style="color: #81a1c1;">PRESENT</span><span style="color: #81a1c1;">)</span><span style="color: #81a1c1;">;</span>

  • The key is "Apple", and the value is PRESENT.

  • If you try to add "Apple" again, the HashMap will detect that the key already exists and won’t add it again.

docs.title: 4. How Uniqueness is Ensured
docs.text: The HashMap ensures uniqueness by using the hashCode() and equals() methods of the keys (which are the HashSet elements).

docs.title: Step-by-Step Process for Adding an Element:
docs.text:
1. Calculate HashCode:

    • The hashCode() method of the element is called to determine the bucket (location) in the HashMap.

    • Example: If "Apple".hashCode() returns 1234, the element will go into bucket 1234.

  2. Check for Duplicates:

    • If the bucket is empty, the element is added to the bucket.

    • If the bucket is not empty, the equals() method is used to compare the new element with all existing elements in the bucket.

      • If equals() returns true for any element, the new element is a duplicate and is not added.

      • If equals() returns false for all elements, the new element is added to the bucket.

docs.title: What Does final Mean?
docs.text:
The final keyword in Java has different meanings depending on the context:

  1. For Variables :

    • If a variable is declared final, its value cannot be changed after it is initialized.
    • For primitive types (e.g., int, double), this means the value itself cannot change.
    • For reference variables (e.g., objects), this means the reference to the object cannot change. However, the state of the object being referenced can still change.

    • For Methods :

    • A final method cannot be overridden by subclasses.

    • For Classes :

    • A final class cannot be subclassed.

docs.title: Applying final to Objects
docs.text: When you declare an object as final, you're saying that the reference to the object cannot change. However, the internal state of the object (its fields or properties) can still change unless explicitly made immutable.

docs.title: Example 1: Primitive Type
docs.text:
final int x = 10;
x = 20; // ERROR: Cannot assign a new value to a final variable

docs.title: Example 2: Reference Variable (Object)
docs.text:
final ReentrantLock lock = new ReentrantLock();
lock = new ReentrantLock(); // ERROR: Cannot reassign a final reference

Here, lock is a reference to a ReentrantLock object. You cannot make lock point to a different ReentrantLock object because it's final.

However, you can still modify the internal state of the object:

lock.lock();   // This modifies the internal state of the lock (acquires the lock)
lock.unlock(); // This modifies the internal state of the lock (releases the lock)

The final keyword does not prevent you from calling methods on the object or modifying its internal state. It only prevents you from reassigning the reference to a different object.

docs.title: When to Use final for Objects?
docs.text:
Here are some common scenarios where declaring an object as final is appropriate:

  1. Shared Resources in Multithreading :

    • If an object is shared between threads (e.g., locks, queues, or counters), declare it as final to ensure all threads use the same instance. 

      final BlockingQueue<Integer> queue = new LinkedBlockingQueue<>();

  2. Constants or Configuration Objects :

    • If an object represents a constant or configuration value that should not change during the program's execution, declare it as final. 

      final Map<String, String> config = Map.of(
    "host", "localhost",
    "port", "8080"
);

  3. Immutable Objects :

    • If you want to create an immutable object, declare its fields as final. This ensures that the object's state cannot change after construction.

docs.title: 8. Default methods in java 8
docs.text:
TL;DR :

  • Default methods provide concrete implementations in interfaces.
  • They enhance APIs without breaking existing code.
  • Conflict resolution is required when multiple interfaces define the same method.
  • Interfaces cannot maintain state; default methods are purely behavioral.
  • Use default methods judiciously to avoid bloating interfaces.

Purpose : To enhance interfaces with new methods without forcing implementing classes to provide implementations.

Default methods were introduced in Java 8 to address the need for evolving interfaces without breaking existing implementations.

This feature allows interfaces to include concrete method implementations, enabling backward compatibility.

docs.title: How Default Methods Work
docs.text:
1. Definition :

    • A default method is defined in an interface using the default keyword.
    • It provides a concrete implementation that can be inherited by implementing classes.

    • Purpose :

    • Enhance interfaces with new functionality without forcing all implementing classes to provide their own implementations.

    • Facilitate API evolution (e.g., adding new methods to widely used interfaces like Collection, Map, or Iterable).

    • Inheritance :

    • If a class implements an interface with a default method, it inherits the method unless explicitly overridden.

    • If multiple interfaces define conflicting default methods, the implementing class must resolve the conflict.

    • Conflict Resolution :

    • When a class implements multiple interfaces with the same default method signature, the class must override the method and explicitly call the desired implementation using InterfaceName.super.method().

    • Interaction with Abstract Classes :

    • Unlike abstract classes, interfaces cannot maintain state (no instance variables).

    • Default methods are purely behavioral enhancements and cannot store data.

  • The forEach method relies on the iterator() method, which is already implemented by List.
  • Implementing classes don’t need to redefine forEach unless they want custom behavior.

docs.title: 2. JIT compiler
docs.text:
The JIT compiler is a ==runtime optimization component== of the Java Virtual Machine (JVM). When you write and compile Java code, it first gets converted into bytecode. This bytecode is platform-independent and is executed by the JVM.

  1. Java Source Code: Written by the developer.
  2. Compilation: The Java compiler compiles the source code into bytecode, which is platform-independent.
  3. Interpretation (Initial Stage): The JVM’s interpreter reads the bytecode and executes it line by line.
    • At this stage, there’s no native code involved yet. The bytecode is interpreted directly by the JVM.
  4. Hotspot Detection: The JVM identifies hot methods (frequently called methods) during execution.
  5. JIT Compilation (Optimization Stage): The JIT compiler converts the bytecode of these hot methods into native machine code.
  6. Native Code Execution: Once compiled into native machine code, the CPU executes this code directly, skipping interpretation for the hot methods.

Interpreter:

  • The interpreter reads the bytecode and executes it line by line or instruction by instruction.
  • No native machine code is generated directly by the interpreter.
  • This method is slow because every time an instruction is encountered, it must be interpreted, which introduces overhead. The bytecode is not converted into machine code but executed by the interpreter itself.

JIT Compiler:

  • ==The JIT compiler steps in after the JVM detects that a method (or a piece of code) is being executed frequently. The JVM then marks this code as hot and sends it to the JIT compiler.==
  • The JIT compiler compiles the bytecode into native machine code (specific to the operating system and CPU).
  • Once a method is JIT-compiled, the native code is stored, and future calls to that method execute directly as native code, bypassing interpretation entirely.

docs.title: What Problem Does Generics Solve?
docs.text:
• Type Safety: Generics ensure that you can work with collections and other types without ClassCastException. It enforces type checks at compile time.
  • Code Reusability: Allows code to work with different types while avoiding code duplication.
  • Elimination of Casts: With generics, explicit casting is not required when retrieving elements from a collection.

docs.title: Generics Resolution at Compile Time (Type Erasure)
docs.text:
• Erasure: Generics are resolved at compile time by the Java compiler. After compilation, the generic types are erased, and the code runs as if it were written without generics.
  • Type Erasure Steps:
    1. Type Parameters are replaced by their bounds or Object if unbounded.
    2. Cast Insertions: The compiler inserts necessary type casts.
    3. Bridge Methods: The compiler creates bridge methods to preserve polymorphism during type erasure.
class Box<T> {
    private T value;
    public T getValue() { return value; }
}

//After type erasure:


class Box {
    private Object value;
    public Object getValue() { return value; }
}

docs.title: Generics Cheatsheet
docs.text: Concept Syntax How to Read Example Comments Generic Class class ClassName<T> {} "Class with type parameter T" class Box<T> {} Enables defining a class that can operate on objects of any type. Generic Method <T> void method(T param) "Method with type parameter T" <T> void print(T param) Allows methods to accept a parameter of any type. Bounded Type class ClassName<T extends SuperType> "T must extend SuperType" class Box<T extends Number> Restricts the type to subclasses or a specified type. Multiple Bounds class ClassName<T extends A & B> "T must extend A and implement B" class Manager<T extends Person & Comparable<T>> A type must extend a class and implement an interface. Wildcard (?) List<?> "List of unknown type" void printList(List<?> list) Allows passing any type. Generally used for reading. Upper Bound List<? extends T> "List of objects that extend T" List<? extends Number> list Restricts the unknown type to be a subclass of T. Lower Bound List<? super T> "List of objects that are supertype of T" List<? super Integer> list Allows Integer or its supertype, ensuring write-safety for generic collections. Generic Interface interface InterfaceName<T> "Interface with type parameter T" interface Container<T> Allows defining an interface with type parameters. Diamond Operator new ClassName<>() "Inferred generic type" List<String> list = new ArrayList<>(); The diamond operator infers the generic type from the context, reducing verbosity. Type Erasure class ClassName<T> -> class ClassName "Generics erased to raw type" class Box<T> becomes class Box after compile Generics only exist during compilation; no generic info at runtime. Wildcard Capture <?> -> Object "Unknown type captured as Object" void method(List<?> list) -> internally Object Type inference converts unknown types to Object within method body. Raw Type ClassName "Class with no generic parameters" List list = new ArrayList(); Raw types disable type safety, reintroducing risks of ClassCastException.

docs.title: Key Points to Remember
docs.text:
• Generics at Runtime: Generics are compile-time constructs, and the JVM doesn't know the actual generic types at runtime.
  • Type Bounds: T extends Class restricts to subclasses of Class. Use & for multiple bounds.
  • Wildcard Types: Use ? extends T for covariance (read-only collections) and ? super T for contravariance (write operations).
  • Type Safety with Wildcards: Upper-bounded wildcards (<? extends T>) can be used for safe reading, but for adding elements, lower-bounded (<? super T>) should be used.

docs.title: Wildcard Use Cases
docs.text:
• Reading data: Use <? extends T>
public void process(List<? extends Number> list) {
    // Can read as Number or its subclasses
}

  • Writing data: Use <? super T>

    public void insert(List<? super Integer> list) {
    list.add(10); // Can add Integer or its subclass
}
  • Generics and Reflection: Since generics are erased, you cannot get the actual generic type through reflection at runtime.
  • Overloading with Generics: You cannot overload methods that only differ by their generic type parameters, as they are erased during compilation.
  • Instanceof with Generics: You cannot use instanceof with parameterized types. For example, if (obj instanceof List<String>) is not valid; instead, use List<?>.

Can you use primitives with generics? (No, use wrapper types like Integer, Double, etc.)

docs.title: What is "Happens-Before"?
docs.text:
It’s Java’s way of saying: “Hey, if Action A happens before Action B, I guarantee B will see everything A did!”

Think of it as rules that force threads to “sync” their views of shared data.

It’s not about time (like "earlier" or "later"). It’s about visibility guarantees . If Action A happens-before Action B, then:

  1. Action A’s changes (e.g., writing to a variable) are guaranteed to be visible to Action B.
  2. The JVM ensures no sneaky optimizations or CPU tricks mess up the order.

The happens-before relationship is only relevant in a multithreaded environment. In a single-threaded program, everything happens in a predictable, sequential order, so there’s no need to worry about visibility or ordering issues.

docs.title: 1️⃣ The Problem Without Happens-Before
docs.text:
• Thread 1: Writes x = 5.

  • Thread 2: Reads x… but might see 0 (old value) because the change isn’t visible yet!

Java defines specific rules for when one action happens-before another. These are like the "laws of the land" for threads. Let’s go through them one by one with examples:

docs.title: Rule 1: Program Order Rule
docs.text:
• What it says : In a single thread, actions happen in the order they appear in the code.

    int x = 42; // Action 1
int y = x + 1; // Action 2
    • Action 1 (x = 42) happens-before Action 2 (y = x + 1).
    • So, y will always be 43, no surprises here.

docs.title: Rule 2: Monitor Lock Rule
docs.text:
• What it says : Unlocking a lock happens-before every subsequent locking of the same lock.
synchronized (lock) {
    x = 42; // Action 1
} // Unlock happens here
synchronized (lock) {
    System.out.println(x); // Action 2
}
  • Action 1 happens-before Action 2 because of the lock.
  • Thread B sees x = 42 because Thread A unlocked the lock before Thread B locked it.

docs.title: Rule 3: Volatile Variable Rule
docs.text:
• What it says : Writing to a volatile variable happens-before every subsequent read of the same variable.

    volatile boolean flag = false;
int data = 0;

// Thread A
data = 42; // Action 1
flag = true; // Action 2 (volatile write)

// Thread B
if (flag) { // Action 3 (volatile read)
    System.out.println(data); // Action 4
}

docs.title: Rule 4: Thread Start Rule
docs.text:
• What it says : Starting a thread happens-before any action in the started thread.
int x = 42;
Thread t = new Thread(() -> {
    System.out.println(x); // Action 2
});
t.start(); // Action 1

docs.title: Rule 5: Thread Join Rule
docs.text:
• What it says : All actions in a thread happen-before any other thread successfully joins it.
int x = 0;

Thread t = new Thread(() -> {
    x = 42; // Action 1
});
t.start();
t.join(); // Action 2
System.out.println(x); // Action 3

docs.title: Rule 6: Interrupt Rule
docs.text:
• What it says : Interrupting a thread happens-before the interrupted thread detects the interrupt.
Thread t = new Thread(() -> {
    try {
        Thread.sleep(1000);
    } catch (InterruptedException e) {
        System.out.println("Interrupted!"); // Action 2
    }
});
t.start();
t.interrupt(); // Action 1
  • Action 1 happens-before Action 2.
  • The interrupted thread sees the interrupt and reacts accordingly.

The happens-before relationship is a set of rules in Java that ensures one thread’s actions are visible to another thread in a predictable order. It prevents confusion caused by CPU optimizations and ensures thread safety. Key rules include program order, locks, volatile, thread start/join, and interrupts.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Happens-before = Java’s promise that changes are visible across threads.

  2. It’s enforced by specific actions like synchronized, volatile, Thread.start(), etc.

  3. If you ignore it, threads live in their own “realities” (chaos!).

docs.title: 5. What is java memory model
docs.text:
Imagine Java’s memory model as the rules of a library where threads (like people) are reading and writing books (data). Without rules, chaos would reign—people might grab the wrong books, see outdated versions, or even lose track of what’s happening.

The Java Memory Model is the rulebook that keeps everything organized and predictable in a multithreaded environment. Let’s break it down step by step.

docs.title: What is the Java Memory Model (JMM)?
docs.text:
The JMM is a set of rules that defines:

  1. How threads interact with memory.

  2. When changes made by one thread are visible to others.

  3. What order operations can occur in.

It’s like a traffic controller for threads, ensuring they don’t crash into each other or get lost in the chaos of shared memory.

docs.title: Why Do We Need the JMM?
docs.text:
Without the JMM, multithreaded programs would behave unpredictably because:

  1. CPU caches: Each thread might have its own copy of data, leading to stale reads.

  2. Compiler optimizations: The compiler or CPU might reorder instructions for performance, breaking assumptions about execution order.

  3. Hardware differences: Different CPUs might handle memory differently, making programs non-portable.

The JMM abstracts these complexities and provides a consistent, predictable behavior across all platforms.

docs.title: 1️⃣ Main Memory vs. Working Memory
docs.text:
• Main Memory: The “library” where all shared data is stored.

  • Working Memory: Each thread’s “notebook” where it keeps a local copy of data it’s working on.

Problem: If Thread 1 updates a variable in its working memory, Thread 2 might not see the change unless it’s written back to main memory.

Solution: The JMM defines rules (like volatile, synchronized) to ensure updates are visible to other threads.

how memory works in Java:

docs.title: 1. Main Memory vs. Thread Local Cache
docs.text:
• Main Memory : This is where all variables live. Think of it as the "central storage" for your program.
  • Thread Local Cache : Each thread has its own local cache (registers or CPU caches) where it stores copies of variables it uses frequently.

Threads can read from and write to their local cache instead of always going to main memory . This is great for performance but can cause problems if the caches aren’t synchronized properly.

int x = 0;

// Thread 1
new Thread(() -> {
    x = 5; // Write to x
}).start();

// Thread 2
new Thread(() -> {
    System.out.println(x); // Might print 0 or 5!
}).start();

Without visibility rules, Thread 2 might see the old value (x = 0).

docs.title: 2. Ordering
docs.text:
The JMM defines rules for instruction reordering. Compilers and CPUs might reorder instructions for performance, but this can break assumptions in multithreaded code.

int x = 0;
boolean flag = false;

// Thread 1
new Thread(() -> {
    x = 5;       // Write 1
    flag = true; // Write 2
}).start();

// Thread 2
new Thread(() -> {
    if (flag) {
        System.out.println(x); // Might print 0 or 5!
    }
}).start();

Without ordering rules, Thread 2 might see flag = true but still see x = 0.

if we use volatile , it will guarantee to write the volatile variable before it is read , so thread 2 will see x=5;

docs.title: Happens-Before Relationship
docs.text: This is the core rule of the JMM. It defines a guarantee that if one action happens-before another, the results of the first are visible to the second.

docs.title: 6. Implement you own equals() and hashCode()
🧠 Key Concept: hashCode() and equals() Have Separate Roles
docs.text:
Method Purpose hashCode() Determines which bucket to store or look up an object in a hash-based collection (like HashSet, HashMap) equals() Determines if two objects are logically equal(i.e., same content)

hashCode() is for location. equals() is for identity.

You need both to work together to ensure logical equality in hash-based collections.

Java does this:

  1. Step 1: Compute hashCode()

    • Both objects have same name → same hashCode() (assuming we override it).
    • So both go into the same bucket .

    • Step 2: Within the bucket, call equals()

    • Java checks if any object in the bucket is equal to the new one.

    • If equals() returns true, it treats it as a duplicate and does not add it.
    • If equals() returns false (or uses default implementation), it adds the new object.

docs.title: Why Override equals() and hashCode()?
docs.text:
By default:

  • equals() compares object references (like ==).
  • hashCode() returns an integer based on object memory address.

But if we need to compare two objects based on their contents e.g., two Person objects are equal if their names and ages match), we need to override both methods.

docs.title: Steps to implement:
docs.text:
1. Check if the passed object is the same instance (this == obj)
  2. Check if the object is of the correct type (instanceof)
  3. Cast it to your class
  4. Compare all relevant fields for equality

Implementation of hashCode()

notice this is Objects class not Object

docs.title: Q: What happens if I override only equals() and not hashCode()?
docs.text:
A: Two equal objects might have different hash codes → They will go into different buckets in a HashMap or HashSet. So even though they're equal, they won’t be treated as duplicates. 

Suppose I only override equals

  • Even though the two Person("Alice") objects are equal via equals(),
  • They use the default hashCode() , which is based on memory address. giving different hashcode based on memory address with default implementation
  • So their hash codes are different , and they go into different buckets in the HashSet.

docs.title: Q: What if I override only hashCode()?
docs.text:
A: Objects may end up in the same bucket but equals() will say they’re different → inefficient hashing and confusion.

We only override hashcode()

  • Both objects get the same hash code (name.hashCode()).
  • So they go into the same bucket .
  • But since you didn't override equals(), Java uses the default equals() (reference comparison).
  • It sees them as two different objects (even though they have the same data), so it adds both.

docs.title: Implementing Custom Iterators in Java
docs.text: I'll guide you through implementing custom iterators in Java with a simple, interview-friendly example that's easy to understand and remember.

docs.title: What is an Iterator?
docs.text: An iterator is a design pattern that provides a way to access elements of a collection sequentially without exposing the underlying representation. In Java, it's formalized through the Iterator interface in the java.util package.

docs.title: Why Use Custom Iterators?
docs.text:
1. To provide controlled access to collection elements
  2. To implement specialized traversal behaviors
  3. To hide the internal structure of your collection
  4. To support the enhanced for-loop (for-each) syntax

docs.title: Step-by-Step Implementation Guide
docs.text:
Let's create a simple BookShelf collection that stores books and allows iteration through them:

import java.util.Iterator;
import java.util.NoSuchElementException;

// 1. Create your collection class
public class BookShelf implements Iterable<Book> {
    private Book[] books;
    private int numberOfBooks;

    public BookShelf(int capacity) {
        books = new Book[capacity];
        numberOfBooks = 0;
    }

    public void addBook(Book book) {
        if (numberOfBooks < books.length) {
            books[numberOfBooks] = book;
            numberOfBooks++;
        }
    }

    // 2. Implement the Iterable interface
    @Override
    public Iterator<Book> iterator() {
        // 3. Return a new instance of your custom iterator
        return new BookShelfIterator();
    }

    // 4. Create your custom iterator as an inner class
    private class BookShelfIterator implements Iterator<Book> {
        private int currentIndex = 0;

        // 5. Implement hasNext() method
        @Override
        public boolean hasNext() {
            return currentIndex < numberOfBooks;
        }

        // 6. Implement next() method
        @Override
        public Book next() {
            if (!hasNext()) {
                throw new NoSuchElementException("No more books on the shelf");
            }
            return books[currentIndex++];
        }
    }
}

// Simple Book class
class Book {
    private String title;
    private String author;

    public Book(String title, String author) {
        this.title = title;
        this.author = author;
    }

    @Override
    public String toString() {
        return "Book{title='" + title + "', author='" + author + "'}";
    }
}

docs.title: Using Your Custom Iterator
docs.text:
public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Create a bookshelf with capacity 3
        BookShelf bookShelf = new BookShelf(3);

        // Add books to the shelf
        bookShelf.addBook(new Book("Clean Code", "Robert Martin"));
        bookShelf.addBook(new Book("Effective Java", "Joshua Bloch"));
        bookShelf.addBook(new Book("Design Patterns", "Gang of Four"));

        // Method 1: Using iterator explicitly
        System.out.println("Using explicit iterator:");
        Iterator<Book> iterator = bookShelf.iterator();
        while (iterator.hasNext()) {
            Book book = iterator.next();
            System.out.println(book);
        }

        // Method 2: Using enhanced for-loop (uses iterator behind the scenes)
        System.out.println("\nUsing enhanced for-loop:");
        for (Book book : bookShelf) {
            System.out.println(book);
        }
    }
}

docs.title: Key Components Explained
docs.text:
1. Iterable Interface: Your collection class must implement Iterable<T> to support iteration

    • This requires implementing the iterator() method that returns an Iterator<T>

    • Iterator Interface: Your custom iterator class must implement Iterator<T> with two methods:

    • boolean hasNext(): Returns true if there are more elements

    • T next(): Returns the next element and advances the cursor position

    • Inner vs. Standalone Class: The iterator is typically implemented as an inner class because:

    • It needs access to the collection's private fields

    • It's conceptually tied to the collection it iterates over

docs.title: Common Implementation Patterns
docs.text:
1. Inner Class Iterator (as shown above)

    • Simplest approach, with direct access to private fields
    • Anonymous Inner Class Iterator
    @Override
public Iterator<Book> iterator() {
    return new Iterator<Book>() {
        private int currentIndex = 0;

        @Override
        public boolean hasNext() {
            return currentIndex < numberOfBooks;
        }

        @Override
        public Book next() {
            if (!hasNext()) {
                throw new NoSuchElementException();
            }
            return books[currentIndex++];
        }
    };
}

  2. External Iterator Class (when iterator logic is complex)

    • Create a separate class that takes the collection in its constructor

docs.title: How Java Uses Iterators
docs.text:
1. Collections Framework: All Java collections implement Iterable<T>

    • ArrayList, LinkedList, HashSet, TreeSet, etc.
    • Enhanced For Loop: When you use a for-each loop:
    for (Book book : bookShelf) { ... }

    Java translates this to:

    for (Iterator<Book> it = bookShelf.iterator(); it.hasNext(); ) {
    Book book = it.next();
    ...
}

  2. Stream API: Modern Java provides streams that use iterators behind the scenes:

    bookShelf.stream().filter(book -> book.getAuthor().equals("Joshua Bloch")).forEach(System.out::println);

docs.title: Interview Tips
docs.text:
1. Remember the Key Methods:

    • iterator() in your collection class (implements Iterable)
    • hasNext() and next() in your iterator class (implements Iterator)

    • Know When to Use Custom Iterators:

    • When you need special traversal logic

    • When implementing your own collection types
    • When you want to provide multiple ways to iterate

    • Be Aware of Thread-Safety Issues:

    • Standard iterators are not thread-safe

    • They may throw ConcurrentModificationException if collection changes during iteration

    • Optional remove() Method:

    • Iterator also has a remove() method that removes the last element returned by next()

    • By default, it throws UnsupportedOperationException
    • You can override it if you want to support removal during iteration

docs.title: Complete Template for Custom Iterator
docs.text:
1. Make your collection implement Iterable<T>
  2. Implement the iterator() method to return a custom iterator
  3. Create an iterator class that implements Iterator<T>
  4. Implement hasNext() and next() methods
  5. Optionally implement remove() if needed

This example with a bookshelf is intuitive, easy to remember, and demonstrates all the key components of custom iterators in Java.

docs.title: not understood1. Static and dynamic binding
docs.text: Concept related to method invocation They determine when and how the method is call is resolved , either at compile time for runtime

docs.title: 1. Static Binding (Early Binding)
docs.text:
• Definition: In static binding, the method to be called is resolved at compile time, ==based on the reference type (i.e., the type of the object variable).==
  • Key Characteristics:
    • Occurs with static methods, private methods, and final methods.
    • These methods cannot be overridden, so the compiler knows exactly which method to call.
    • Faster since it happens during compilation, and the method call is hardcoded into the bytecode.

consider example we have two classes Animal and Dog which extends Animal Animal class has sound() as private so cannot be overridden

class Animal {
    private void sound() {
        System.out.println("Animal is making a sound");
    }

    static void staticMethod() {
        System.out.println("Static method in Animal");
    }
}


class Dog extends Animal {
    private void sound() {
        System.out.println("Dog is barking");
    }

    static void staticMethod() {
        System.out.println("Static method in Dog");
    }
}


public class StaticBindingExample {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Animal obj = new Dog();

        // This will invoke the method in Animal
        // because private methods are statically bound
        obj.sound();

        // This will call the static method of the reference type (Animal)
        obj.staticMethod();
    }
}

here we are putting reference of Dog object in Animal object but now when we call obj.sound() it will call sound() in Animal , because sound() is private  and statically bound means it will be resolved on class name , also in case of staticMethod() , since it is static , hence it is statically bound so method in Animal class will be called

docs.title: 2. Dynamic Binding (Late Binding)
docs.text:
• Definition: In dynamic binding, the method to be called is resolved at runtime, ==based on the actual object type, not the reference type.==
  • Key Characteristics:
    • Occurs with instance methods that can be overridden.
    • The JVM decides which method to invoke based on the object type during execution.
    • Allows polymorphism, where a subclass can provide its own method implementation.

Example of Dynamic Binding:

class Animal {
    public void sound() {
        System.out.println("Animal is making a sound");
    }
}

class Dog extends Animal {
    @Override
    public void sound() {
        System.out.println("Dog is barking");
    }
}

public class DynamicBindingExample {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Animal obj = new Dog();

        // Dynamic binding: Dog's sound() will be called at runtime
        obj.sound();
    }
}

 In this example, sound() is an instance method that is overridden in the Dog class. The actual object is of type Dog, so at runtime, the JVM calls the sound() method of the Dog class, not Animal.

docs.title: Key Differences:
docs.text: Aspect Static Binding Dynamic Binding When resolved At compile-time At runtime Method type Static, private, final methods Instance methods that can be overridden Reference vs. Object Depends on reference type Depends on object type Polymorphism Not applicable Supports polymorphism Speed Faster Slightly slower due to runtime decision

docs.title: Summary:
docs.text:
• Static Binding: Determined at compile-time and used for methods that the compiler can resolve statically (e.g., static, private, and final methods).
  • Dynamic Binding: Determined at runtime based on the object type (e.g., overridden instance methods), allowing the same method call to behave differently depending on the actual object's class.

docs.title: Sealed Classes
docs.text:
Sealed classes restrict which other classes or interfaces can extend or implement them. This ensures better control over the class hierarchy.

Real-world example: Suppose you are developing a payment system and want to ensure only specific payment methods are used. You can seal the PaymentMethod class to restrict its subclasses to CreditCard, PayPal, and BankTransfer.

public sealed class PaymentMethod permits CreditCard, PayPal, BankTransfer { }

public final class CreditCard extends PaymentMethod { }
public final class PayPal extends PaymentMethod { }
public final class BankTransfer extends PaymentMethod { }

docs.title: Pattern Matching for instanceof
docs.text:
Pattern matching for instanceof simplifies the common pattern of using instanceof checks followed by casting.

Real-world example: When processing different shapes in a drawing application, you can directly match and extract the specific type.

if (obj instanceof Circle circle) {
    double radius = circle.getRadius();
    // Process the circle
} else if (obj instanceof Rectangle rectangle) {
    double length = rectangle.getLength();
    double width = rectangle.getWidth();
    // Process the rectangle
}

docs.title: Records
docs.text:
Records provide a concise way to create classes that are primarily data carriers. They automatically generate boilerplate code like constructors, getters, equals(), hashCode(), and toString().

Real-world example: Use records to represent simple data structures like a point in a 2D space.

public record Point(int x, int y) { }

Point point = new Point(10, 20);
System.out.println(point.x()); // 10
System.out.println(point.y()); // 20

docs.title: Text Blocks
docs.text:
Text blocks simplify the inclusion of multi-line string literals in the code, making them more readable and easier to maintain.

Real-world example: When writing JSON or XML within Java code, text blocks make it more readable.

String json = """
{
  "name": "John",
  "age": 30,
  "city": "New York"
}
""";
System.out.println(json);

docs.title: Enhanced Switch Statements
docs.text:
Enhanced switch statements provide a more concise and flexible way to write switch expressions, supporting both statement and expression forms.

Real-world example: Use an enhanced switch to determine the type of a day in a calendar application.

String dayType = switch (day) {
    case MONDAY, FRIDAY -> "Workday";
    case SATURDAY, SUNDAY -> "Weekend";
    default -> "Unknown";
};

docs.title: Foreign Function & Memory API (Incubator)
docs.text:
This API provides mechanisms to interact with native code and memory, facilitating tasks that were typically handled by JNI.

Real-world example: Interfacing with a C library for high-performance computations directly from Java.

try (var scope = MemorySession.openConfined()) {
    var intArray = scope.allocateArray(ValueLayout.JAVA_INT, 1, 2, 3);
    // Pass intArray to a native function
}

docs.title: Vector API (Incubator)
docs.text:
The Vector API allows developers to write complex vector computations that can be optimized at runtime to use hardware acceleration.

Real-world example: Performing large-scale mathematical operations on arrays efficiently.

var a = IntVector.fromArray(IntVector.SPECIES_256, new int[] {1, 2, 3, 4}, 0);
var b = IntVector.fromArray(IntVector.SPECIES_256, new int[] {5, 6, 7, 8}, 0);
var c = a.add(b); // Vector addition
int[] result = new int[4];
c.intoArray(result, 0);
System.out.println(Arrays.toString(result)); // [6, 8, 10, 12]

This cheatsheet covers the essential new features introduced in Java 17, offering clear explanations and practical examples to help you understand and apply them effectively.

docs.title: 2. Java Records
docs.text:
Records are a special kind of class that act as transparent carriers for immutable data. T==hey are essentially== ==data-only classes that automatically handle boilerplate code like constructors, getters, equals(), hashCode(), and toString().==

Here's a basic record example compared to a traditional class:

docs.title: Equivalent Record
docs.text:
we pass field similar to parameters,  and inside the curly braces we can add other methods also

That's it! The record version gives us all the same functionality in one line.

docs.title: Key Features of Records
docs.text:
1. Implicitly final - Cannot be extended

  2. Implicitly immutable - All fields are final

  3. Auto-generated methods:

    • Constructor

    • Accessor methods (getters)

    • equals()

    • hashCode()

    • toString()

docs.title: When to Use Records
docs.text:
✅ Perfect for:

  • Data transfer objects (DTOs)

  • Value objects (like Money, DateRange, etc.)

  • Return types from methods

  • Compound map keys (since they implement proper equals/hashCode)

  • Temporary data containers

docs.title: When NOT to Use Records
docs.text:
❌ Avoid when:

  • You need mutable data

  • You need to extend another class (records are final)

  • You need to add significant behavior (methods that modify state)

  • You need to hide implementation details (records are transparent)

  • You need serialization with special handling

  • You need complex construction logic (though compact constructors help)

Records can Implement Interfaces:

    • Banker's Algorithm for Deadlock Avoidance

    • Problem: Implement the Banker's Algorithm to avoid deadlocks in a multithreaded environment.

    • Resource: GeeksforGeeks: Banker's Algorithm

    • Implement a Custom Reentrant Lock

    • Problem: Create your own version of a reentrant lock using low-level synchronization primitives.

    • Resource: GeeksforGeeks: Custom Lock Implementation

    • Readers-Writers Problem using ReadWriteLock

    • Problem: Solve the Readers-Writers problem using ReadWriteLock to optimize read and write access.
