    • Resource: GeeksforGeeks: Readers-Writers Problem

docs.title: Roadmap to Master Multithreading in Java
Phase 1: Core Concepts Refresher (Understand the Foundation)
docs.text:
1. What is Multithreading?
    • Why it matters: Performance, responsiveness, concurrency.
    • Key terms: Thread, Process, Concurrency vs. Parallelism.
  2. Thread Creation
    • Extending Thread class.
    • Implementing Runnable interface.
  3. Thread Lifecycle
    • States: New, Runnable, Running, Blocked, Terminated.
  4. Thread Synchronization
    • synchronized keyword, locks, race conditions.
  5. Thread Safety
    • Immutable objects, atomic operations, volatile.

docs.title: Phase 2: Intermediate Concepts (Master Control & Coordination)
docs.text:
1. Thread Communication
    • wait(), notify(), notifyAll().
  2. Executor Framework
    • ExecutorService, thread pools, Callable and Future.
  3. Common Concurrency Issues
    • Deadlock, Livelock, Starvation.
  4. Java Memory Model (JMM)
    • volatile, happens-before relationship.

docs.title: Phase 3: Advanced Topics (Interview-Ready Depth)
docs.text:
1. Concurrent Utilities
    • Lock, ReentrantLock, ReadWriteLock.
    • BlockingQueue, CountDownLatch, CyclicBarrier, Semaphore.
  2. Fork/Join Framework
    • Parallel processing with recursive tasks.
  3. ThreadLocal
    • Per-thread data isolation.

docs.title: Phase 4: Practice & Application
docs.text:
1. Coding Exercises
    • Producer-Consumer problem, Dining Philosophers, etc.
  2. Interview Prep
    • Common questions: “How do you prevent race conditions?” “Explain ExecutorService.”
  3. Spring Boot Multithreading
    • How Spring Boot leverages multithreading (e.g., @Async, task executors).

docs.title: Phase 1: Core Concepts Refresher (1 Week)
docs.text:
1. Thread Fundamentals
    • Thread vs Process: Memory models and isolation
    • Thread creation methods (Thread class vs Runnable interface)
    • Thread lifecycle and state transitions
  2. Thread Synchronization Basics
    • The synchronized keyword (methods vs blocks)
    • Object locks and monitor concept
    • Common pitfalls: Nested synchronization and lock ordering
  3. Thread Safety Mechanisms
    • Immutable objects design patterns
    • Atomic classes and their performance characteristics
    • Volatile keyword and the Java Memory Model

docs.title: Phase 2: Intermediate Concurrency (2 Weeks)
docs.text:
1. Advanced Thread Communication
    • wait/notify pattern with practical examples
    • Thread interruption handling
    • Thread local variables and their use cases
  2. Executor Framework Mastery
    • Thread pool types and when to use each
    • Callable, Future, and handling timeouts
    • CompletableFuture for async operations
  3. Concurrent Collections
    • ConcurrentHashMap internals and performance
    • Copy-on-write collections
    • Blocking queues and their applications

docs.title: Phase 3: Advanced Topics (1 Week)
docs.text:
1. Lock API
    • ReentrantLock vs synchronized
    • ReadWriteLock for read-heavy scenarios
    • StampedLock (Java 8+) and optimistic reading
  2. Synchronization Utilities
    • CountDownLatch for one-time barriers
    • CyclicBarrier for repeated synchronization points
    • Semaphore for controlling resource access
  3. Modern Java Concurrency
    • Virtual Threads (Project Loom)
    • Structured Concurrency
    • Flow API and Reactive Programming basics

docs.title: Phase 4: Spring Boot Integration (1 Week)
docs.text:
1. Async Processing in Spring
    • @Async annotation and exception handling
    • Configuring custom task executors
    • Scheduling tasks with @Scheduled
  2. Spring Boot Concurrency Patterns
    • Thread pool configuration in application.properties
    • Handling concurrent database operations
    • WebClient vs RestTemplate for concurrent API calls

docs.title: Phase 5: Interview Preparation (1 Week)
docs.text:
1. Classic Concurrency Problems
    • Producer-Consumer implementation
    • Readers-Writers problem
    • Dining Philosophers solution
  2. Interview Questions
    • Explaining deadlock, livelock, and starvation
    • Debugging thread issues (thread dumps analysis)
    • Performance optimization in concurrent applications
  3. Practice Problems (Most Asked in Interviews)
    • Implement a thread-safe rate limiter
    • Create a concurrent cache with expiration
    • Design a connection pool with fair distribution
    • Build a thread-safe singleton with lazy initialization
    • Implement a simple job scheduler with cancellation support

docs.title: 1. Multithreading
docs.text:
what is it ?

what is concurrency

docs.title: 2. Thread creation
docs.text:
You can create threads in Java by:

  • Extending Thread class: Override the run() method to define thread behavior.
  • Implementing Runnable interface: Preferred, as it allows your class to extend another, avoiding Java’s single inheritance limit.

Creating thread by extending to Thread class

class MyThread extends Thread {
    @Override
    public void run() {
        System.out.println("Thread running: " + Thread.currentThread().getName());
    }
}

 By Implementing Runnable and overriding run() method

class MyRunnable implements Runnable {
    @Override
    public void run() {
        System.out.println("Runnable running: " + Thread.currentThread().getName());
    }
}

so If we you see there is no actual implementation to handle thread creating in runnable class's run()

implementing run() means , we are simply providing logic for run() but no thread is created yet  To actually execute Runnable in the thread

we need to pass Runnable implementation for Thread constructor

MyRunnable myRunnable=new MyRunnable();  
Thread myThread =new Thread(myRunnable)
thread.start()

 now calling thread.start(), will initiate new thread, it will trigger native os kernel level thread creating via JVM the jvm schedules the new thread and calls run() internally;

by calls run() , I am referring to run() method in Thread class

Generally thread creation is preferred as it will let the class to extend to another class, unlike Thread, where multiple inheritance will be the case. Threads in java stops execution when their run() method execution is completed or any unhandled exception occurs or due to external interruption

The JVM handles resource cleanup.

docs.title: 3. Thread Lifecycle  States and Transitions
docs.text:
The thread lifecycle is managed by the JVM, with states defined in Thread.State. State is enum in Thread class defining the possible states of thread. Here’s a detailed breakdown:

  • New: Thread is created but start() not called.
  • Runnable: Thread is ready to run or currently executing, scheduled by the JVM.
  • Blocked: Thread waits for a monitor lock (e.g., synchronized block held by another thread).
  • Waiting: Thread waits indefinitely for another thread (e.g., wait() without timeout).
  • Timed Waiting: Thread waits with a timeout (e.g., sleep(1000) or wait(1000)).
  • Terminated: Thread has completed execution or been interrupted.

NEW --> RUNNABLE : start(): When you call start() on a new thread, it transitions to the RUNNABLE state.

RUNNABLE --> BLOCKED : wait for lock If a thread tries to enter a synchronized block or method but the lock is held by another thread, it enters the BLOCKED state.

BLOCKED --> RUNNABLE : lock acquired: Once the lock becomes available , the thread transitions back to the RUNNABLE state.

RUNNABLE --> WAITING : wait(): Calling wait() on an object within a synchronized block or method causes the thread to release the lock and enter the WAITING state.

WAITING --> RUNNABLE : notify(): Another thread calling notify() or notifyAll() on the same object will wake up a waiting thread, transitioning it back to the RUNNABLE state.

RUNNABLE --> TIMED_WAITING : sleep() or wait(long): Calling sleep(long) or wait(long) causes the thread to enter the TIMED_WAITING state for the specified duration.

TIMED_WAITING --> RUNNABLE : time expires or notify(): The thread transitions back to RUNNABLE when the time expires or when another thread calls notify() or notifyAll().

RUNNABLE --> TERMINATED : run() completes: When the run() method of a thread finishes execution, the thread transitions to the TERMINATED state.

WAITING --> TERMINATED : interrupted: If a thread in the WAITING state is interrupted (by calling interrupt()), it will throw an InterruptedException and may transition to TERMINATED.

TIMED_WAITING --> TERMINATED : interrupted: Same as above, but for threads in TIMED_WAITING.

BLOCKED --> TERMINATED : interrupted: A thread in the blocked state can also be interrupted.

package com.pratik.thejavajourney.concurrency.printInorder.thread_creation;

public class ThreadStates {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws InterruptedException {
        Thread thread= new Thread(
                ()->{
                    try {
                        Thread.sleep(500);
                    } catch (InterruptedException e) {
                        throw new RuntimeException(e);
                    }
                }
        );
        System.out.println("state after thread creation "+thread.getState());
        thread.start();
        System.out.println("state after  thread is started "+thread.getState());
        Thread.sleep(100);
        System.out.println("State while running: " + thread.getState());
        //join() waits for thread to die
        thread.join();
        System.out.println("State after completion "+thread.getState());
    }
}

output:

state after thread creation NEW
state after  thread is started RUNNABLE
State while running: TIMED_WAITING
State after completion TERMINATED

The RUNNABLE state includes both ready-to-run and running, managed internally by the JVM, not directly observable in code.

docs.title: 4. Thread Synchronization  Ensuring Thread Safety
docs.text:
Synchronization prevents race conditions when multiple threads access shared resources.

Use synchronized on methods or blocks to lock the object’s monitor:

  • Synchronized Method: Locks the entire method.
  • Synchronized Block: Locks a specific section, more granular.

package com.pratik.thejavajourney.concurrency.printInorder.thread_creation;

public class ThreadSaftey {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws InterruptedException {
        Counter counter=new Counter();
        Thread t1= new Thread(()->{
            for(int i=0;i<2500;i++){
                counter.increment();
            }
        });

        Thread t2= new Thread(()->{
            for(int i=0;i<2500;i++){
                counter.increment();
            }
        });

        t1.start();
        t2.start();
        t1.join();
        t2.join();
        System.out.println(counter.getCount());
    }
}

class Counter {
    private int count = 0;
    //if we didnt use synchronized final count may be less than 5000

    public synchronized void increment() {
        count++;
    }

    public int getCount() {
        return count;
    }
}

Without synchronized, race conditions could result in count being less than 2000 due to interleaved updates. This is critical in Spring Boot for shared service state.

docs.title: 5.1 Thread Safety   Immutable Objects
docs.text:
When multiple threads access and modify shared data simultaneously, unexpected behaviors can occur.

To ensure thread safety there are few mechanisms provided by java:

docs.title: Immutable Objects
docs.text: Immutable objects are perhaps the simplest way to achieve thread safety. Once created, these objects cannot be modified.

docs.title: Why Immutable Objects Are Thread-Safe
docs.text:
When an object can't change, there's no risk of one thread seeing it in an inconsistent state modified by another thread.

package com.pratik.thejavajourney.concurrency.printInorder.thread_creation;

public class ImmutableCartessianPoint {
    private final int x;
    private final int y;
    // silly mistake to avoid
    // if x and y is final they must have initial value
    // initializing private fields at time of declaration is one way
    // but not only one, we can initialize it via constructor also
    public ImmutableCartessianPoint(int x, int y) {
        this.x = x;
        this.y = y;
    }

    public int getX() {
        return x;
    }

    public int getY() {
        return y;
    }

    // no setters since fields are final and we dont want attempt to modify it


    public ImmutableCartessianPoint ImmutableCartessianPoint(int deltaX, int deltaY) {
        return new ImmutableCartessianPoint(x+deltaX,y+deltaY);
    }
}

docs.title: Key Characteristics to Create Immutable Objects
docs.text:
1. Make the class final to prevent inheritance
  2. Make all fields private and final
  3. Don't provide any methods that modify the object's state
  4. Ensure proper construction (all fields initialized in constructor)
  5. Return copies or new objects from methods that would otherwise modify state

docs.title: Atomic Operations
docs.text: Some operations may look like a single step but actually involve multiple steps at the CPU level, making them vulnerable to race conditions. Java provides atomic classes to handle these scenarios safely.

docs.title: Atomic Operations
docs.text: Some operations may look like a single step but actually involve multiple steps at the CPU level, making them vulnerable to race conditions. Java provides atomic classes to handle these scenarios safely.

docs.title: Example Problem: Increment Operation
docs.text:
// Not thread-safe
int counter = 0;
// Thread 1 and Thread 2 both execute this:
counter++; // This is actually: read value, add 1, write back

If two threads try to increment the counter simultaneously, one update might be lost.

Using AtomicInteger:

package com.pratik.thejavajourney.concurrency.printInorder.thread_creation;

import java.util.concurrent.atomic.AtomicInteger;

public class AtomicCounter {
    private AtomicInteger counter=new AtomicInteger(0);
    public void increment(){
        counter.incrementAndGet();
    }

    public int getCounter() {
        return counter.get();
    }


    public static void main(String[] args) throws InterruptedException {
        AtomicCounter counter=new AtomicCounter();
        Thread[] threads=new Thread[5];
        for (int i=0;i<5;i++){
            threads[i]=new Thread(()->{
                for (int j = 0; j < 1000; j++) {
                    counter.increment();
                }
            });
            threads[i].start();
        }
        for (Thread thread:threads){
            thread.join();
        }
        System.out.println("Actual count after simulatenous increment by 5 threads is "+counter.getCounter());

    }

}

output will be : Actual count after simulatenous increment by 5 threads is 5000

docs.title: Volatile Keyword
docs.text: The volatile keyword ==ensures that a variable's value is always read from and written to main memory, not from CPU caches.==

docs.title: Visibility vs. Atomicity
docs.text:
• Visibility: When one thread modifies a variable, other threads can see that change
  • Atomicity: An operation completes in a single, uninterruptible step

docs.title: When to Use Volatile
docs.text:
• For variables accessed by multiple threads
  • When you need visibility guarantees but don't need atomic compound operations
  • For flags that can terminate threads

The volatile keyword ensures visibility of variable changes across threads, preventing caching issues.

Means it

package com.pratik.thejavajourney.concurrency.printInorder.thread_creation;

public class VolatileUsecase {
    private volatile boolean running=true;
    public void stop(){
        running=false;
    }

    public void run() {
        while(running){
            System.out.println("Thread is still running..");
        }
        try {
            Thread.sleep(1000);
        } catch (InterruptedException e) {
            throw new RuntimeException(e);
        }
        System.out.println("thread stopped execution ");
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) throws InterruptedException {
        VolatileUsecase volatileUsecase=new VolatileUsecase();
        Thread t=new Thread(volatileUsecase::run);
        t.start();
        Thread.sleep(1000);
        volatileUsecase.stop();
        t.join();
    }

}

docs.title: What Volatile Doesn't Do
docs.text:
Volatile ensures visibility but doesn't make compound operations atomic. Consider:

volatile int counter = 0;
counter++; // Still not thread-safe because it's a read-modify-write operation

docs.title: Thread Safety Comparison
docs.text: Mechanism Use When Limitations Immutable Objects You need objects that never change after creation May create many objects if frequent updates needed Atomic Classes You need simple variables with atomic operations Limited to specific operations (get, set, increment, etc.) Volatile You need visibility guarantees between threads Doesn't provide atomicity for compound operations

docs.title: 1.1 Thread Communication  wait notify pattern
docs.text:
The wait and notify methods, part of the Object class in Java, are fundamental for inter-thread       communication.

They enable threads to wait for certain conditions to be met before proceeding,ensuring synchronization in scenarios like producer-consumer problems

Basic Mechanisms:

  • wait(): Causes the current thread to release the lock on the object and enter a waiting state until another thread calls notify() or notifyAll() on the same object.
  • notify(): Wakes up one thread waiting on the object’s monitor, chosen arbitrarily by the JVM.
  • notifyAll(): Wakes up all threads waiting on the object’s monitor, useful when multiple threads are waiting for different conditions.

docs.title: Producer-Consumer with Wait/Notify
docs.text:
Consider a buffer that can hold one item, with a producer adding items and a consumer taking them. Here’s the implementation:

  • The produce method checks if the buffer is not empty. If so, it calls wait(), releasing the lock and waiting until notified.
  • Once the buffer is empty, it adds the item, sets isEmpty to false, and calls notify() to wake a waiting consumer.
  • The consume method similarly waits if the buffer is empty, then consumes the item, sets isEmpty to true, and notifies the producer.
  • This cycle repeats, ensuring threads coordinate without overwriting or reading stale data.

class Buffer {
    private String item;
    private boolean isEmpty = true;

    public synchronized void produce(String item) {
        while (!isEmpty) {
            try {
                wait(); // Wait if buffer’s not empty
            } catch (InterruptedException e) {
                e.printStackTrace();
            }
        }
        this.item = item;
        isEmpty = false;
        notify(); // Notify consumer an item is available
    }

    public synchronized String consume() {
        while (isEmpty) {
            try {
                wait(); // Wait if buffer’s empty
            } catch (InterruptedException e) {
                e.printStackTrace();
            }
        }
        String item = this.item;
        isEmpty = true;
        notify(); // Notify producer buffer is empty
        return item;
    }
}

class Producer implements Runnable {
    private Buffer buffer;

    public Producer(Buffer buffer) {
        this.buffer = buffer;
    }

    @Override
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
            buffer.produce("Item " + i);
            System.out.println("Produced: Item " + i);
            try {
                Thread.sleep(1000);
            } catch (InterruptedException e) {
                e.printStackTrace();
            }
        }
    }
}

class Consumer implements Runnable {
    private Buffer buffer;

    public Consumer(Buffer buffer) {
        this.buffer = buffer;
    }

    @Override
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
            String item = buffer.consume();
            System.out.println("Consumed: " + item);
            try {
                Thread.sleep(1000);
            } catch (InterruptedException e) {
                e.printStackTrace();
            }
        }
    }
}

public class WaitNotifyExample {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Buffer buffer = new Buffer();
        Thread producerThread = new Thread(new Producer(buffer));
        Thread consumerThread = new Thread(new Consumer(buffer));
        producerThread.start();
        consumerThread.start();
    }
}

The choice of notify() vs. notifyAll() can impact performance. notify() wakes one thread, which is efficient if only one thread needs to proceed, but notifyAll() ensures all waiting threads are considered, useful in complex scenarios where multiple conditions exist.

docs.title: 1.2 Thread Interruption Handling
docs.text:
Thread interruption in Java is a cooperative mechanism for signaling a thread to stop its execution. It’s not a forceful termination but a request that the thread can choose to honor.

Basic Mechanisms:

  • interrupt(): Sets the interrupted status of the thread, which can be checked by the thread.
  • interrupted(): A static method that returns the interrupted status and clears it for the current thread.
  • isInterrupted(): Returns the interrupted status without clearing it, for any thread.

Usage Scenario: Commonly used to stop long-running tasks, like a thread searching through a large dataset, if the user cancels the operation. The evidence leans toward interruption for graceful shutdowns, especially in server applications.

docs.title: Interrupting a Task Thread
docs.text:
package com.pratik.thejavajourney.concurrency.printInorder.thread_creation;

import lombok.SneakyThrows;

class TaskThread extends Thread{
    @Override
    public void run(){
        // the task thread runs in loop , sleeping for 1 second and then running again
        //print working everytime
        while (true){
            // task thread checks if it has been asked to stopped, this will be asked via interrupt() method
            if(Thread.interrupted()){
                System.out.println("Interrupt, stopping.");
                break;
            }
            try {
            // if the thead is interrupted while sleeping , it catches InterruptedException
            //print message and breaks the loop
                Thread.sleep(1000);
                System.out.println("Woking...");
            } catch (InterruptedException e) {
                System.out.println("caught interruption , stopping");
                break;
            }

        }
    }
}

//The main thread waits 5 seconds, calls interrupt() to request the task thread stop,
// and waits for it to finish with join().
public class ThreadInterruption {
    @SneakyThrows
    public static void main(String[] args) {
    TaskThread thread=new TaskThread();
    thread.start();
    // main thread sleep for 5 second before singalling task thead to stop through interrupt
    Thread.sleep(5000);
    thread.interrupt();
    // waits for thead to finish its execution
    thread.join();
    }
}

o/p:

Woking...
Woking...
Woking...
Woking...
caught interruption , stopping

Process finished with exit code 0

Interruption is cooperative, meaning the thread must check its status or handle InterruptedException. This is different from forceful termination, making it safer but requiring careful implementation.

docs.title: 2.1  ThreadLocal
docs.text: ThreadLocal is like giving each thread its own private locker. Instead of sharing variables between threads (which can lead to concurrency issues), ThreadLocal lets each thread have its own isolated copy of a variable.

docs.title: Why Use ThreadLocal?
docs.text:
Imagine you're working on a multi-threaded application that processes web requests. Each request might need to track information like:

  • User ID of the current user
  • Transaction context
  • Session information

Without ThreadLocal, you'd have to pass this information as parameters through many method calls

With ThreadLocal, this data becomes instantly available anywhere in the thread's execution path.

docs.title: How ThreadLocal Works
docs.text:
Think of ThreadLocal as a specialized map where:

  • The key is the current thread itself (automatically managed)
  • The value is the thread-specific data you want to store
ThreadLocal<T> = { 
    Thread-1 → value1,
    Thread-2 → value2,
    Thread-3 → value3
}

Each thread can only see and modify its own value, and these values don't interfere with each other.

docs.title: Basic ThreadLocal API
docs.text:
The core methods you'll use with ThreadLocal are:

  • set(T value) - Stores a value for the current thread
  • T get() - Retrieves the value for the current thread
  • remove() - Removes the value for the current thread
  • withInitial(() -> defaultValue) - Creates a ThreadLocal with a default value supplier

docs.title: Memory Leaks with ThreadLocal
docs.text:
ThreadLocal can cause memory leaks if not used properly. The most common issue occurs when:

  1. You store ThreadLocal values
  2. The thread comes from a thread pool (so it doesn't die)
  3. You forget to call remove() when you're done
// Always do this in a finally block
try {
    userContext.set("user123");
    // do work
} finally {
    userContext.remove(); // Clean up!
}

docs.title: ThreadLocal in Spring Boot
docs.text:
Spring Boot uses ThreadLocal extensively.

For instance, SecurityContextHolder stores authentication information:

// How Spring Security makes authentication available throughout the app
Authentication auth = SecurityContextHolder.getContext().getAuthentication();

 Under the hood, it uses ThreadLocal to store this context.

docs.title: Comparison with Other Thread Safety Approaches
docs.text: Approach When to Use Pros Cons ThreadLocal When data belongs to a specific thread/request No synchronization needed, clean code Possible memory leaks, thread pool issues Synchronization When multiple threads need to coordinate access Simple to understand Performance cost, potential for deadlocks Immutability When data doesn't need to change Inherently thread-safe Can't modify data Atomic classes For simple shared counters/values Good performance Limited to specific operations

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• User Sessions: Each thread handles a user’s request, so store their session data here.
  • Database Connections: Give each thread its own connection, so no need for pooling or locks.
  • Loggers: Each thread can have its own logger, like logging with thread-specific info.
  • Transaction Context: For apps with transactions, each thread can have its own transaction data.

docs.title: Real-World Use Cases for ThreadLocal
docs.text:
1. User authentication context

    // Store current user after authentication
public static ThreadLocal<User> currentUser = new ThreadLocal<>();

// Later access it from anywhere
User user = currentUser.get();

  2. *Transaction management :* 

    // Store the current transaction
ThreadLocal<Transaction> currentTransaction = new ThreadLocal<>();

// Later in code
currentTransaction.get().commit();

  3. Request-scoped data in web applications Many web frameworks use ThreadLocal to store request-specific data.

docs.title: 2.2 ThreadLocal Example
docs.text:
This example simulates a web application that processes requests from different users simultaneously: 

package com.pratik.thejavajourney.concurrency.printInorder.thread_creation;

import java.util.concurrent.ExecutorService;
import java.util.concurrent.Executors;
import java.util.concurrent.TimeUnit;

public class ThreadLocalDemo {
    // This ThreadLocal holds the user ID for the current request/thread
    private static final ThreadLocal<String> userContext = new ThreadLocal<>();

    private static final ThreadLocal<String> threadName = ThreadLocal.withInitial(() -> Thread.currentThread()
                                                                                              .getName());

    // Simulates logging with user context
    public static void logActivity(String activity) {
        System.out.println("[User: " + userContext.get() +
                ", Thread: " + threadName.get() +
                "] " + activity);
    }


    public static void performBusinessLogic() {
        // Notice we don't pass the user ID, yet the method "knows" which user it's for
        // since the thread handling the request will retrive the userId from the context
        logActivity("performing business logic "+userContext.get());

    }

    public static void processRequest(String userId) {
        // setting userConext for this thread , means current thread
        try {
            userContext.set(userId);
            logActivity("Started processing request "+userContext.get());
            // Simulate some processing time

            Thread.sleep((int) (5 * 1000));


            performBusinessLogic();
            logActivity("Completed request "+userContext.get());
        } catch (InterruptedException e) {
            Thread.currentThread()
                  .interrupt();
            logActivity("Request interrupted");
        } finally {
            // IMPORTANT: Always clean up ThreadLocal values when done
            //otherwise it will lead to memory leak
            userContext.remove();
        }
    }


    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Simulate multiple concurrent requests using a thread pool
        ExecutorService executorService= Executors.newFixedThreadPool(3);
        //submit 5 request for different users
        for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
            final String userId= "user"+i;
            executorService.submit(()->processRequest(userId));

        }
        executorService.shutdown();
        try {
            executorService.awaitTermination(10, TimeUnit.SECONDS);
        } catch (InterruptedException e) {
            throw new RuntimeException(e);
        }

    }

}

o/p:

[User: user1, Thread: pool-1-thread-2] Started processing request user1
[User: user2, Thread: pool-1-thread-3] Started processing request user2
[User: user0, Thread: pool-1-thread-1] Started processing request user0
[User: user2, Thread: pool-1-thread-3] performing business logic user2
[User: user1, Thread: pool-1-thread-2] performing business logic user1
[User: user2, Thread: pool-1-thread-3] Completed request user2
[User: user1, Thread: pool-1-thread-2] Completed request user1
[User: user4, Thread: pool-1-thread-2] Started processing request user4
[User: user3, Thread: pool-1-thread-3] Started processing request user3
[User: user0, Thread: pool-1-thread-1] performing business logic user0
[User: user0, Thread: pool-1-thread-1] Completed request user0
[User: user4, Thread: pool-1-thread-2] performing business logic user4
[User: user4, Thread: pool-1-thread-2] Completed request user4
[User: user3, Thread: pool-1-thread-3] performing business logic user3
[User: user3, Thread: pool-1-thread-3] Completed request user3

 Notice how each thread maintains its own user context throughout the entire request processing, even though we're not passing it as a parameter!

docs.title: 3.1 Executor Framework   Thread Pools
docs.text:
The Executor Framework is like a manager for your threads, making it easier to run tasks without manually creating threads. Thread pools are pre-created groups of threads that handle tasks, saving time and resources.

Instead of spawning threads yourself and managing their lifecycle, you hand off tasks to an executor service that handles all the thread management for you.

We will use example of managing individual taxi  as manual thread creation and Executors as  running an entire taxi dispatch system.

docs.title: The Core Components of the Executor Framework
docs.text:
The Executor framework gives us several key interfaces and classes:

  1. Executor: The simplest interface with just one method: execute(Runnable)
  2. ExecutorService: Extends Executor with additional lifecycle methods
  3. ScheduledExecutorService: Adds scheduling capabilities
  4. Executors: Factory class to create different types of executor services

ExecuterService uses data structure like blocking queue to store set of tasks, which are executed

by threads

docs.title: Creating Executor Services: The Factory Approach
docs.text:
Let's look at how to create different types of executor services using the Executors factory class: following are examples of creating  

import java.util.concurrent.ExecutorService;
import java.util.concurrent.Executors;
import java.util.concurrent.ScheduledExecutorService;
import java.util.concurrent.TimeUnit;

public class ExecutorDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Fixed thread pool - like having a set number of taxis always available
        ExecutorService fixedPool = Executors.newFixedThreadPool(4);

        // Cached thread pool - creates new threads as needed, reuses idle ones
        // Like a taxi service that scales up during rush hour
        ExecutorService cachedPool = Executors.newCachedThreadPool();

        // Single thread executor - processes tasks sequentially
        // Like having just one taxi that takes passengers one after another
        ExecutorService singleThread = Executors.newSingleThreadExecutor();

        // Scheduled executor - for delayed or periodic tasks
        // Like scheduling airport pickups in advance
        ScheduledExecutorService scheduler = Executors.newScheduledThreadPool(2);

        // Work stealing pool (Java 8+) - designed for recursive tasks
        // Like having taxis that can help each other when one area gets busy
        ExecutorService workStealing = Executors.newWorkStealingPool();

        // Don't forget to shut them down when done!
        fixedPool.shutdown();
        cachedPool.shutdown();
        singleThread.shutdown();
        scheduler.shutdown();
        workStealing.shutdown();
    }
}

docs.title: Submitting Tasks to an Executor
docs.text: There are three main ways to give work to an executor:

docs.title: 1. execute() - Fire and Forget Tasks
docs.text:
Use this when you just need something done and don't care about the result:

ExecutorService executor = Executors.newFixedThreadPool(2);

// Just run this, I don't need any result back
executor.execute(() -> {
    System.out.println("Executing task on thread: " + Thread.currentThread().getName());
    // Do some work...
});

docs.title: 2. submit() - Tasks That Return Results (Future)
docs.text:
When you need to get a result back eventually:

// Submit returns a Future object that will eventually contain the result
Future<String> future = executor.submit(() -> {
    // Simulate work
    Thread.sleep(1000);
    return "Task completed by " + Thread.currentThread().getName();
});

// Do other work while the task is running...

// Get the result (this will block until the result is available)
try {
    String result = future.get();
    System.out.println("Got result: " + result);
} catch (Exception e) {
    e.printStackTrace();

docs.title: 3. invokeAll() and invokeAny() - Batch Task Submission
docs.text:
For when you have multiple tasks to run:

// Create a list of tasks
List<Callable<String>> tasks = Arrays.asList(
    () -> "Result from task 1",
    () -> "Result from task 2",
    () -> "Result from task 3"
);

// Run all tasks and get all results
List<Future<String>> results = executor.invokeAll(tasks);
for (Future<String> result : results) {
    System.out.println(result.get());
}

// OR - run all tasks and get just the first completed result
String firstResult = executor.invokeAny(tasks);
System.out.println("First completed: " + firstResult);

docs.title: Shutting Down an Executor Service
docs.text:
Always shut down your executors when you're done:

ExecutorService executor = Executors.newFixedThreadPool(4);

// Submit some tasks...

// Option 1: Graceful shutdown - wait for tasks to complete
executor.shutdown();
try {
    // Wait up to 5 seconds for tasks to complete
    if (!executor.awaitTermination(5, TimeUnit.SECONDS)) {
        // If we get here, tasks didn't finish in time
        System.out.println("Some tasks didn't finish in time");
    }
} catch (InterruptedException e) {
    Thread.currentThread().interrupt();
}

// Option 2: Immediate shutdown - cancel running tasks
List<Runnable> unfinishedTasks = executor.shutdownNow();
System.out.println("Cancelled " + unfinishedTasks.size() + " tasks");

docs.title: Choosing the Right Executor Service
docs.text:
Here's a simple guide for picking the right executor:

  1. Fixed Thread Pool: Use when you have a specific number of tasks that can run in parallel, like the number of CPU cores for CPU-bound tasks.

    // For CPU-bound tasks
ExecutorService executor = Executors.newFixedThreadPool(
    Runtime.getRuntime().availableProcessors()
);

  2. Cached Thread Pool: Good for many short-lived tasks, especially I/O-bound tasks that spend time waiting.

    // For many I/O-bound tasks (network calls, file operations)
ExecutorService executor = Executors.newCachedThreadPool();

  3. Single Thread Executor: When tasks must run sequentially, but in a background thread.

    // For tasks that must happen one after another
ExecutorService executor = Executors.newSingleThreadExecutor();

  4. Scheduled Thread Pool: For delayed or periodic tasks.

    // For recurring maintenance tasks, timers, etc.
ScheduledExecutorService executor = Executors.newScheduledThreadPool(2);

  5. Work Stealing Pool: For recursive or highly variable workloads.

    // For fork/join tasks or variable workloads
ExecutorService executor = Executors.newWorkStealingPool();

docs.title: 3.2 Executor Framework
docs.text:
import java.util.ArrayList;
import java.util.List;
import java.util.concurrent.*;
import java.util.concurrent.atomic.AtomicInteger;

public class ExecutorServiceDemo {

    // A counter to track completed tasks
    private static final AtomicInteger completedTasks = new AtomicInteger(0);

    // Simulates a task that takes some time and returns a result
    private static class ComputeTask implements Callable<Integer> {
        private final int taskId;
        private final int complexity;

        public ComputeTask(int taskId, int complexity) {
            this.taskId = taskId;
            this.complexity = complexity;
        }

        @Override
        public Integer call() throws Exception {
            System.out.println("Task " + taskId + " started on thread: " + 
                              Thread.currentThread().getName());

            // Simulate CPU-intensive work
            int result = 0;
            for (int i = 0; i < complexity * 10_000_000; i++) {
                result += (i % 10);
            }

            // Track completion
            completedTasks.incrementAndGet();

            System.out.println("Task " + taskId + " completed on thread: " + 
                              Thread.currentThread().getName());

            return taskId * 100 + (result % 100); // Return a value based on task ID
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int processors = Runtime.getRuntime().availableProcessors();
        System.out.println("Running on a machine with " + processors + " processors/cores");

        // Create a thread pool with the optimal size (typically number of cores)
        ExecutorService executor = Executors.newFixedThreadPool(processors);

        try {
            // Create a list of tasks with varying complexity
            List<Callable<Integer>> tasks = new ArrayList<>();
            for (int i = 1; i <= 10; i++) {
                tasks.add(new ComputeTask(i, i % 3 + 1)); // Complexity 1-3
            }

            System.out.println("Submitting 10 tasks to the executor...");

            // Submit all tasks and get Future objects
            long startTime = System.currentTimeMillis();
            List<Future<Integer>> futures = executor.invokeAll(tasks);

            // Process the results as they complete
            int sum = 0;
            for (int i = 0; i < futures.size(); i++) {
                // This will block until the specific task completes
                Integer result = futures.get(i).get();
                System.out.println("Result from task " + (i+1) + ": " + result);
                sum += result;
            }

            long endTime = System.currentTimeMillis();
            System.out.println("\nAll tasks completed!");
            System.out.println("Total execution time: " + (endTime - startTime) + "ms");
            System.out.println("Sum of all results: " + sum);

            // Demonstrate the ScheduledExecutorService
            demonstrateScheduling();

        } catch (InterruptedException | ExecutionException e) {
            System.err.println("Error executing tasks:");
            e.printStackTrace();
        } finally {
            // Always shut down the executor
            System.out.println("\nShutting down executor...");
            shutdownAndAwaitTermination(executor);
        }
    }

    private static void demonstrateScheduling() {
        System.out.println("\n--- Scheduling Demo ---");
        ScheduledExecutorService scheduler = Executors.newScheduledThreadPool(1);

        // Run a task after a delay
        System.out.println("Scheduling a task to run after 2 seconds...");
        ScheduledFuture<?> future = scheduler.schedule(
            () -> System.out.println("Delayed task executed at: " + System.currentTimeMillis()),
            2, 
            TimeUnit.SECONDS
        );

        try {
            // Wait for the task to complete
            future.get();

            // Schedule a periodic task
            System.out.println("Starting a task that runs every second...");
            scheduler.scheduleAtFixedRate(() -> {
                System.out.println("Periodic task executed at: " + System.currentTimeMillis());
            }, 0, 1, TimeUnit.SECONDS);

            // Let the periodic task run for a few seconds
            Thread.sleep(5000);

        } catch (InterruptedException | ExecutionException e) {
            e.printStackTrace();
        } finally {
            System.out.println("Shutting down scheduler...");
            shutdownAndAwaitTermination(scheduler);
        }
    }

    // Utility method to shut down an ExecutorService
    private static void shutdownAndAwaitTermination(ExecutorService executor) {
        // Disable new tasks from being submitted
        executor.shutdown();
        try {
            // Wait for existing tasks to terminate
            if (!executor.awaitTermination(10, TimeUnit.SECONDS)) {
                // Cancel currently executing tasks forcefully
                executor.shutdownNow();
                // Wait a while for tasks to respond to being cancelled
                if (!executor.awaitTermination(10, TimeUnit.SECONDS)) {
                    System.err.println("Executor did not terminate");
                }
            }
        } catch (InterruptedException e) {
            // (Re-)Cancel if current thread also interrupted
            executor.shutdownNow();
            // Preserve interrupt status
            Thread.currentThread().interrupt();
        }
    }
}

docs.title: 4.1 Callable and Future
docs.text:
Think of it this way: you're at a modern coffee shop. You want a fancy latte.

Callable and Future: Placing Your Order and Getting a Ticket

Think of Callable as your order to the barista. It's like saying, "Hey, make me a latte!" The key thing about a Callable is that it's a task that returns a value(latte)

And in this analogy just like ordering might go wrong , similarly Callable can throw exception.

import java.util.concurrent.*;

public class CallableFutureExample {

    public static void main(String[] args) throws InterruptedException, ExecutionException {
        ExecutorService executor = Executors.newSingleThreadExecutor();

        // Callable represents the order to the barista: "Make me a latte!"
        Callable<String> makeLatteTask = () -> {
            System.out.println("Barista starting to make latte in thread: " + Thread.currentThread().getName());
            Thread.sleep(2000); // Simulate barista taking 2 seconds to make latte
            System.out.println("Barista finished making latte in thread: " + Thread.currentThread().getName());
            return "Latte"; // The barista returns a Latte (String result)
        };

        System.out.println("Placing latte order..."); // Submitting task is like placing the order
        Future<String> latteFuture = executor.submit(makeLatteTask); // Submit the order and get the ticket (Future)

        System.out.println("Order placed, waiting for latte to be prepared (but can do other things)...");

        // ... imagine doing other things here while the latte is being made ...
        // For example, checking emails, reading a book, etc.

        System.out.println("Checking if latte is ready..."); // Checking if the order is done
        if (latteFuture.isDone()) {
            System.out.println("Latte is ready!"); // Order is ready, like order ticket being called
            String latte = latteFuture.get(); // Get the latte using the ticket (Future.get()) - might block!
            System.out.println("Latte received: " + latte); // You got your latte!
        } else {
            System.out.println("Latte is still brewing..."); // Latte is not ready yet
        }

        executor.shutdown(); // Coffee shop closing, no more orders for now
    }
}

observe

  • we create newSingleThreadExecutor using Executors Factory
  • then we created task called makeLatteTask of type Callable
    • just like Runnable has run() which returns nothing , 
    • Callable has call() which returns result , here expected result is of type string
  • then we submitted the Callable task to our executor we created eariler
    • which has return type of Future, which is kind of order ticket in our example, saying you will get your latte ,not now necessarily, go and do other task and check if result is ready 
  • then we check if result is ready by using isDone() on future object
    • if result is ready, we go and retrieve it using latteFuture.get()
  • Lastly we make sure to shutdown the executorService

docs.title: Handling Timeouts with Future: What if the Barista is Slow?
docs.text:
Now, what if the barista is taking forever? You don't want to stand there waiting indefinitely, right? You need a timeout. Future gives you that control.

public class FutureTimeoutExample {

    public static void main(String[] args) throws InterruptedException, ExecutionException {
        ExecutorService executor = Executors.newSingleThreadExecutor();

        Callable<Integer> slowTask = () -> {
            System.out.println("Starting slow task...");
            Thread.sleep(3000); // Simulate a very slow task
            return 100;
        };

        Future<Integer> future = executor.submit(slowTask);

        try {
            System.out.println("Waiting for result with a timeout...");
            Integer result = future.get(2, TimeUnit.SECONDS); // Wait at most 2 seconds
            System.out.println("Result: " + result);
        } catch (TimeoutException e) {
            System.out.println("Timeout! Task took too long.");
            // Maybe cancel the task if it's still running
            boolean cancelled = future.cancel(true); // Attempt to interrupt the task
            System.out.println("Task cancelled: " + cancelled);
        } finally {
            executor.shutdownNow(); // Best practice to shutdown the executor
        }
    }
}

Observe

  • we created Callable called slowTask

docs.title: 6. ForkJoinPool
docs.text:
The ForkJoinPool in Java is a special type of thread pool introduced in Java 7 as part of the java.util.concurrent package.

It's specifically designed to work efficiently with divide-and-conquer algorithms

docs.title: What is ForkJoinPool?
docs.text:
At its core, ForkJoinPool manages a set of worker threads that execute tasks , which can split themselves into smaller subtasks (fork) and then wait for the results of those subtasks (join) .

This model is ideal for recursive problem-solving where a large task can be divided into smaller chunks that can be processed independently.

docs.title: 1. Fork
docs.text:
• A task breaks itself into smaller subtasks.
  • Each subtask can also fork again recursively.

docs.title: 2. Join
docs.text: • After forking, a task may call join() on each subtask to wait for its result.

docs.title: 3. Work-Stealing Algorithm
docs.text:
• Worker threads in the ForkJoinPool use a work-stealing algorithm .
  • If one thread runs out of tasks, it "steals" work from another thread’s deque (double-ended queue) .

docs.title: 1. Thread Pool Management
docs.text: Both ForkJoinPool and ExecutorService are built to manage a pool of threads that execute tasks concurrently.

docs.title: 2. Asynchronous Task Execution
docs.text:
They both support asynchronous execution of tasks using methods like:

  • submit() – to submit a task for background execution.
  • execute() – to run a task without expecting a result.

docs.title: 1. Core Design Philosophy
docs.text:
• ExecutorService : Designed for general-purpose concurrency — handling independent tasks such as network calls, logging, background processing, etc.
  • ForkJoinPool : Built specifically for recursive divide-and-conquer algorithms — where one big task splits into smaller subtasks recursively (think sorting, searching, matrix multiplication).

docs.title: 2. Task Granularity and Structure
docs.text:
• ExecutorService handles relatively coarse-grained , standalone tasks — each task usually runs independently and doesn’t break into subtasks.
  • ForkJoinPool is optimized for fine-grained , recursive tasks — a single task forks itself into multiple subtasks, which may themselves fork again.

docs.title: 3. Work Distribution Strategy
docs.text:
• ExecutorService uses a shared work queue . All worker threads pull tasks from this central queue in FIFO (First In First Out) order. This can lead to contention and inefficiency when many small tasks are submitted.

  • ForkJoinPool uses a work-stealing algorithm . Each thread has its own deque (double-ended queue). When a thread finishes its own tasks, it "steals" from other threads’ deques — typically from the end (LIFO), reducing contention.

docs.title: 4. Task Types
docs.text:
• ExecutorService works well with:

    • Runnable – for tasks with no return value.
    • Callable<V> – for tasks that return a value.

  • ForkJoinPool is designed for:

    • RecursiveTask<V> – for tasks that return a result after forking and joining.
    • RecursiveAction – for void-returning tasks. Both extend ForkJoinTask<V>, which is a special kind of task supporting fork() and join() semantics.

docs.title: 5. Use Case Example
docs.text:
Let’s take the example of summing all elements in a very large array:

  • With ExecutorService , you'd likely manually split the array into chunks, submit each chunk as separate Callable<Integer> tasks, collect all futures, and sum the results — a lot of boilerplate.
  • With ForkJoinPool , you define a RecursiveTask<Integer>, and let it naturally split itself into halves until reaching a base case. Then, it combines results via join() — clean, recursive, and scalable.

If you’re working on something like a web server handling client requests, ExecutorService is perfect. But if you’re implementing a fast  merge sort, or any recursive computation — go for ForkJoinPool.

docs.title: 1 .ConcurrentHashMap
docs.text:
A standard HashMap is an array of buckets (often called Node<K,V>[ ] table).

  • Structure:

    • A HashMap is backed by an array of buckets (Node<K,V>[] table).

    • Each bucket holds a linked list (or tree if collisions exceed a threshold).

    • The hash function maps keys to bucket indices.

  • Collision Handling:

    • Linked List: If two keys hash to the same bucket, they are stored as a linked list.

      • Problem: In the worst case, search time is O(n).

        • Tree (Java 8+): If the linked list exceeds TREEIFY_THRESHOLD (default: 8), it converts to a balanced tree (TreeBin).

      • Benefit: Reduces search time to O(log n) in case of collisions.

Index 0: [Node1] → [Node2] → [Node3]  
Index 1: [Node4]  
...  
Index N: [TreeBin] (balanced tree of TreeNodes)

docs.title: Concurrency Challenges
docs.text:
• Problem with HashMap:

    • Concurrent put/get operations can lead to data corruption.

      • Example: Two threads inserting into the same bucket → lost updates or infinite loops.
        • Why: No synchronization → race conditions.

  • Problem with Global Locks (e.g., Hashtable or Collections.synchronizedMap):

    • A single lock is used for all operations (put, get, etc.).

    • Consequence: Threads block each other, even if they are working on different parts of the map.

    • Result: Poor scalability in high-concurrency scenarios.

docs.title: Core Mechanics
docs.text:
• Bucket-Level Locking:

    • Lock Granularity: Instead of locking the entire map or segments, lock individual buckets.

      • Each bucket is locked using synchronized on the first node of the bucket.

      • Threads accessing different buckets do not block each other.

  • Lock-Free Reads:

    • get() Operations: No locks are used.

      • Uses volatile reads to ensure memory visibility.
        • Why Safe?: Writes (under locks) and volatile ensure that changes are visible to readers.

  • Tree Bins for Collisions:

    • TreeBin: A balanced tree (Red-Black Tree) structure.

      • Used when a bucket exceeds TREEIFY_THRESHOLD (default: 8).

        • Locking: Uses read-write locks for concurrent access to tree nodes.

      • Multiple readers can access the tree simultaneously.

      • Writers acquire an exclusive lock.

  • Dynamic Resizing:

    • Trigger: When the map exceeds load factor * capacity.

    • Incremental Migration:

      • A new table is created (double the size).

      • Threads help migrate old → new buckets incrementally (no global lock).

    • ForwardingNode: A marker node indicating a bucket is being migrated.

      • Threads encountering a ForwardingNode help with migration.

docs.title: Java 8+ Workflow
docs.text:
• Thread 1: Locks Bucket 3 → adds a node → unlocks.

  • Thread 2: Reads Bucket 5 (no lock).

  • Thread 3: Finds ForwardingNode → helps migrate Bucket 7.

docs.title: Pre-Java 8 (Historical Context)
docs.text:
• Segment Locking:

    • The map was divided into 16 segments (default), each acting as a mini-HashMap.

    • Each segment had its own lock → 16 concurrent writers max.

  • Problems:

    • Fixed segments → limited concurrency.

    • Uneven load distribution → some segments could be overloaded.

  • Why Java 8 Changed:

    • Bucket-level locking → finer granularity.

    • Trees → resilience against collision attacks.

docs.title: Key Differences from HashMap
Performance Scenarios
docs.text:
• Best Case:

    • Threads spread across buckets → linear scalability.

    • Worst Case:

    • All threads hit the same bucket → synchronized-like performance.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
• Write-heavy concurrent scenarios (e.g., caches, counters).

  • Avoid:

    • Read-heavy (use CopyOnWriteArrayList).

    • Requires strict consistency (use Collections.synchronizedMap).

docs.title: 1. The Problem: Read-Heavy Workloads with Global Locks
docs.text:
Imagine you’re building a configuration manager for a server.

  • 100 threads read the configuration every second.

  • 1 thread updates the configuration once a day.

If you use a Collections.synchronizedList (global lock):

  • Every get() (read) must wait for the lock → 100 threads compete for the same lock.

  • Even though writes are rare, reads become slow due to unnecessary contention.

Question: How can we allow concurrent reads without locks while ensuring writes are thread-safe?

docs.title: 2. The Solution: Copy-On-Write (COW)
docs.text:
Core Idea:

  • Reads: No locks! Readers see an immutable snapshot of the data.

  • Writes: Create a new copy of the data, modify it, and replace the old snapshot.

Java provides CopyOnWriteArrayList and CopyOnWriteArraySet.

CopyOnWriteArrayList<String> list
            = new CopyOnWriteArrayList<>();

Internal Mechanics: 1. Initial State: Backed by a volatile Object[] array (immutable).

2. Read Operation (e.g., get(0)):

  • Directly access elements[0] → no locks.

3. Write Operation (e.g., add("D")):

  • Acquire a lock (to ensure only one writer at a time).
  • Copy the current(old) array:
  • Modify the copy meaning writing existing element modification or writing new value  newElements[3] \= "D";
  • Replace the old array with the new one (volatile write)

Key Insight:

  • Readers always see a consistent snapshot (the old or new array, never a half-modified state).

  • Writers pay the cost of copying, but this is acceptable if writes are rare.

docs.title: When to Use COW
docs.text:
• Read-heavy, write-rarely scenarios:

    • Configuration data.

    • Listeners/observers (e.g., GUI event listeners).

    • Caches that rarely change.

  • Avoid for:

    • Write-heavy workloads (e.g., real-time counters).

    • Large datasets (copying 1 MB array on every write is expensive).

  1. "What happens if a thread is iterating while another writes?"

    • Answer: The iterator uses the original snapshot → it doesn’t see the new changes. This avoids ConcurrentModificationException.

docs.title: 1. The Problem: Thread Coordination
docs.text:
Imagine a food delivery app:

  • Producers: Kitchen staff preparing orders.

  • Consumers: Delivery drivers picking up orders.

Challenges:

  1. If the order queue is empty, drivers wait.

  2. If the order queue is full, kitchen staff wait.

How do we coordinate threads so they wait efficiently instead of busy-wasting CPU cycles?

docs.title: 2. The Solution: BlockingQueue
docs.text:
Core Idea: A thread-safe queue with blocking operations:

  • Producers  are block if the queue is full.

  • Consumers are block if the queue is empty.

Java Interface: BlockingQueue<E> Key Implementations:

  • ArrayBlockingQueue: Fixed-size, array-backed.

  • LinkedBlockingQueue: Optionally bounded, linked-list nodes.

  • SynchronousQueue: Direct handoff (no storage).

docs.title: Key Methods:
docs.text:
• put(E e):

    • Inserts an element, blocks if the queue is full.

    • take():

    • Removes and returns an element, blocks if the queue is empty.

    • offer(E e, long timeout, TimeUnit unit):

    • Inserts an element, blocks for up to a timeout if full.

    • poll(long timeout, TimeUnit unit):

    • Removes an element, blocks for up to a timeout if empty.

Under the Hood:

  • Uses locks and condition variables (e.g., ReentrantLock and Condition).

  • Example for ArrayBlockingQueue:

docs.title: When to Use BlockingQueue
docs.text:
• Producer-Consumer Patterns:

    • Thread pools (e.g., ThreadPoolExecutor uses a BlockingQueue for tasks).

    • Data pipelines (e.g., processing logs from multiple sources).

  • Flow Control:

    • Prevent resource exhaustion (e.g., using a bounded queue to limit memory usage).

docs.title: 1. What Is Reentrancy?
docs.text:
Reentrancy means that a thread can acquire the same lock multiple times without deadlocking itself.

Each time the thread acquires the lock, an internal counter is incremented. The lock is only released when the counter reaches zero.

docs.title: 2. Why Is Reentrancy Needed?
docs.text:
Reentrancy is essential for avoiding deadlocks in scenarios where a thread needs to reacquire a lock it already holds. Without reentrancy, a thread would block itself, leading to a deadlock.

Meaning threadA goes and tries to reacquire lock it holds, but but it is already hold by same threadA itself and it wont be release unless its execution is complete which wont since it is needing lock again, this causes deadlock

docs.title: 1: Recursive Methods
docs.text:
Imagine a method that calls itself recursively and uses a lock to protect shared data:

  • Without reentrancy, the thread would block itself on the second lock.lock() call.

  • With reentrancy, the thread can proceed because it already holds the lock.

docs.title: 2: Callbacks or Delegation
docs.text:
A method might delegate work to another method that also requires the same lock:

  • Without reentrancy, the thread would block itself when calling validate().

  • With reentrancy, the thread can proceed because it already holds the lock.

docs.title: 4. How Reentrancy Works Internally
docs.text:
• Lock Counter: Each lock maintains a counter to track how many times it has been acquired by the same thread.

  • Owner Thread: The lock also tracks which thread currently holds it.

  • Unlocking: The lock is only released when the counter reaches zero.

docs.title: 5. Why Is Reentrancy Important?
docs.text:
1. Avoids Deadlocks: Prevents a thread from blocking itself.

  2. Simplifies Code: Allows methods to call other methods that require the same lock without worrying about deadlocks.

  3. Supports Recursion: Enables recursive algorithms to work seamlessly with locks.

docs.title: Reentrancy in Java Locks
docs.text:
• synchronized: Reentrant by default.

  • ReentrantLock: Explicitly reentrant.

  • StampedLock: Non-reentrant (a thread cannot reacquire the same lock).

docs.title: What Problem Does It Solve?
docs.text:
The synchronized keyword is simple but lacks flexibility. For example:

  • If a thread is waiting for a lock, it cannot be interrupted.

  • There’s no way to specify a timeout for acquiring a lock.

  • There’s no control over fairness (which thread gets the lock next).

ReentrantLock addresses these limitations by providing:

  • Reentrancy: A thread can acquire the same lock multiple times.

  • Fairness: Ensures the longest-waiting thread gets the lock first.

  • Interruptible locks: Threads can respond to interrupts while waiting.

  • Timeout support: Threads can attempt to acquire a lock for a limited time.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. Reentrancy:

    • A thread can acquire the same lock multiple times.

    • Each lock() call increments a counter; each unlock() decrements it.

    • The lock is released only when the counter reaches zero.

Fairness:

  • By default, ReentrantLock is unfair (no guarantee of order).

  • Set fairness to true to ensure the longest-waiting thread gets the lock.

Interruptible Locks:

  • Use lockInterruptibly() to allow a thread to respond to interrupts while waiting.

Timeout Support:

  • Use tryLock(timeout) to attempt acquiring the lock for a limited time.

docs.title: Comparison with synchronized
docs.text: Feature synchronized ReentrantLock Reentrancy Yes Yes Fairness No Configurable Timeout Support No tryLock(timeout) Interruptible No (blocks indefinitely) lockInterruptibly() Code Flexibility Limited (block-scoped) Explicit lock()/unlock()

docs.title: When to Use ReentrantLock
docs.text:
• Fine-grained control: When you need fairness, timeouts, or interruptible locks.

  • Complex locking logic: When synchronized blocks are not sufficient.

docs.title: How Condition Works with ReentrantLock
docs.text:
• A ReentrantLock can have multiple Condition objects.

  • Each Condition represents a separate wait set.

  • Threads can wait on a specific condition and be signaled independently.

Key Methods:

  • await(): Releases the lock and waits for a signal.

  • signal(): Wakes up one waiting thread.

  • signalAll(): Wakes up all waiting threads.

package com.pratik.thejavajourney.concurrency.printInorder;

import java.util.ArrayList;
import java.util.List;
import java.util.concurrent.locks.Condition;
import java.util.concurrent.locks.ReentrantLock;

class ProducerConsumer {
    private final ReentrantLock lock = new ReentrantLock();
    private final Condition full = lock.newCondition();
    private final Condition empty = lock.newCondition();
    private final List<Integer> buffer = new ArrayList<>();
    private final int BUFFER_INITIAL_CAPACITY = 5;

    public void produce(int item) throws InterruptedException {
        lock.lock();
        try {
            while (buffer.size() == BUFFER_INITIAL_CAPACITY) {
                System.out.println("buffer is full");
                full.await();
            }

            //if not full produce

            buffer.add(item);
            System.out.println("Produced " + item);
            empty.signal();
        } finally {
            lock.unlock();
        }

    }

    public void consume() throws InterruptedException {
        lock.lock();
        try {
            while (buffer.size() == 0) {
                System.out.println("buffer is empty nothing to consume");
                //wait on condition empty
                empty.await();
            }
            Integer item = buffer.remove(0);
            System.out.println("consumed item from buffer "+item);
            full.signal();
        } finally {
            lock.unlock();
        }

    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        ProducerConsumer pc = new ProducerConsumer();
        Thread producer = new Thread(() -> {
            try {
                for (int i = 1; i <= 10; i++) {
                    pc.produce(i);
                    Thread.sleep(500); // Simulate production time
                }
            } catch (InterruptedException e) {
                e.printStackTrace();
            }
        });

        // Consumer thread
        Thread consumer = new Thread(() -> {
            try {
                for (int i = 1; i <= 10; i++) {
                    pc.consume();
                    Thread.sleep(1000); // Simulate consumption time
                }
            } catch (InterruptedException e) {
                e.printStackTrace();
            }
        });

        // Start threads
        producer.start();
        consumer.start();
    }
}

docs.title: 4. How It Works
docs.text:
1. Producers:

    • If the buffer is full, they wait on full.

    • After adding an item, they signal empty to wake up consumers.

  2. Consumers:

    • If the buffer is empty, they wait on empty.

    • After removing an item, they signal full to wake up producers.

docs.title: What Problem Does It Solve?
docs.text:
In read-heavy scenarios, using a single lock (like synchronized or ReentrantLock) forces all threads to wait, even if they’re only reading. This is inefficient.

ReadWriteLock solves this by providing two locks read and write lock:

  • Allowing multiple threads to read concurrently.

  • Ensuring only one thread can write at a time.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. Two Locks in One:

      • Read Lock: Shared, non-exclusive. Multiple threads can hold it simultaneously.

      • Write Lock: Exclusive. Only one thread can hold it, and it blocks all reads/writes

  2. Reentrancy:

    • A thread can acquire the same read/write lock multiple times.

    • Lock Downgrading:

    • A thread holding a write lock can acquire a read lock (useful for atomic writes followed by reads)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Writer Starvation: If reads are constant, writers may wait indefinitely.

  • No Upgrade from Read to Write Lock: Attempting this causes deadlock.

docs.title: What Problem Does It Solve?
docs.text:
ReadWriteLock can still be slow under high contention. StampedLock introduces:

  • Optimistic reads: No locks for reads, with validation checks.

  • Better throughput: Combines features of ReentrantLock and ReadWriteLock.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. Lock Modes:

    • Write Lock: Exclusive (like ReentrantLock).

    • Read Lock: Shared (like ReadWriteLock).

    • Optimistic Read: No lock; checks if data changed after read.

  2. Optimistic Reads:

    • tryOptimisticRead() returns a stamp (version number).

    • After reading, validate using validate(stamp).

  3. We can upgrade optimistic read to write locks

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• No Reentrancy: Can deadlock if a thread reacquires the same lock.

  • Complexity: Manual validation required for optimistic reads.

docs.title: 5. CountDownLatch
docs.text: Analogy: Imagine a race organizer (the main thread) waiting to fire the starting gun. The gun can only be fired (await() finishes) after all the runners (worker threads) have signaled they are in position (countDown()). Once the gun fires (count reaches zero), the gate is open, and it stays open.

docs.title: 6. CyclicBarriers
docs.text:
Analogy: Imagine a group of friends hiking different paths to reach a viewpoint (the barrier). Each friend waits at the viewpoint (await()) until the entire group has arrived. Once the last friend arrives, they all admire the view together (barrier breaks, threads are released) and then decide to hike to the next viewpoint (the barrier resets and can be used again).

Think of it like a meeting point for a fixed number of threads. Threads arrive at the barrier (await()) and wait there until all threads in the group have arrived. Once the last thread arrives, the barrier breaks, and all waiting threads are released simultaneously. The barrier then resets itself for potential reuse.

docs.title: Understanding the Layers: OS Threads, Platform Threads, and Virtual Threads
OS Threads
docs.text:
• What They Are: Threads managed directly by the operating system (OS).

  • Characteristics:

    • Heavyweight (each thread consumes ~1 MB of memory).

    • Limited by OS constraints (e.g., max threads per process).

    • Expensive to create and context-switch.

docs.title: Platform Threads
docs.text:
• What They Are: Java’s representation of OS threads.

    • In Java, every java.lang.Thread is a platform thread by default.

    • Characteristics:

    • 1:1 mapping with OS threads.

    • Same limitations as OS threads (memory, scalability).

docs.title: Virtual Threads
docs.text:
• What They Are: Lightweight threads managed entirely by the JVM.

  • Characteristics:

    • No direct mapping to OS threads.

    • Thousands can fit in 1 MB of memory.

    • Cheap to create and context-switch.

docs.title: How Virtual Threads Work Under the Hood
Carrier Threads
docs.text:
• Virtual threads run on a small pool of platform threads (called carrier threads).

  • The JVM schedules virtual threads onto these carrier threads.

docs.title: Continuations
docs.text:
• When a virtual thread blocks (e.g., waiting for I/O), the JVM pauses it and switches to another virtual thread. meaning it mounts another thread

  • No OS involvement → no context-switching overhead.

docs.title: Scheduler
docs.text:
• JVM-managed, work-stealing FIFO scheduler.

  • Q: What are virtual threads?

    • A: Lightweight, JVM-managed threads for high-throughput concurrent tasks.

    • Q: How do they differ from platform threads?

    • A: Virtual threads are cheap (1KB vs 1MB), managed by JVM, and scale to millions.

    • Q: When not to use virtual threads?

    • A: CPU-bound tasks (no benefit). Use platform threads or ForkJoinPool.

    • Q: Can virtual threads replace reactive programming?

    • A: Yes for blocking I/O. No for non-blocking (e.g., CompletableFuture still has uses).

docs.title: What Is Blocking, and How Do Virtual Threads Handle It?
Blocking Operations
docs.text:
• Definition: When a thread waits for a resource (e.g., I/O, database, network).

  • Examples:

    • Reading from a file.

    • Waiting for a database query to complete.

    • Sleeping (Thread.sleep()).

docs.title: How Platform Threads Handle Blocking
docs.text:
• The thread is paused by the OS.

  • The CPU switches to another thread (expensive context switch).

  • Limited by the number of OS threads.

docs.title: How Virtual Threads Handle Blocking
docs.text:
• The JVM pauses the virtual thread (no OS involvement).

  • The CPU switches to another virtual thread (cheap context switch).

  • Millions of virtual threads can block without wasting resources.

docs.title: 1. High-Throughput Servers
docs.text:
• Example: A web server handling 100,000+ concurrent HTTP requests.

  • Why Virtual Threads?:

    • Each request can run in its own virtual thread.

    • Blocking I/O (e.g., database calls) doesn’t waste resources.

docs.title: 2. Blocking I/O Operations
docs.text:
• Example: Reading/writing files, making network requests.

  • Why Virtual Threads?:

    • Write synchronous code that scales like asynchronous code.

docs.title: 3. Legacy Code Modernization
docs.text:
• Example: Refactoring old code that uses thread pools.

  • Why Virtual Threads?:

    • Replace thread pools with virtual threads for instant scalability.

docs.title: When Not to Use Virtual Threads
CPU-Bound Tasks
docs.text:
• Example: Complex calculations, image processing.

  • Why Not?:

    • Virtual threads don’t improve performance for CPU-bound tasks.

    • Use platform threads or ForkJoinPool instead.

docs.title: Non-Blocking I/O
docs.text:
• Example: Reactive streams, CompletableFuture.

  • Why Not?:

    • Virtual threads are designed for blocking I/O.

    • Non-blocking I/O still has its place for advanced use cases.

docs.title: Starting a Virtual Thread
docs.text:
• Use Thread.startVirtualThread() to create and start a virtual thread.

  • Virtual threads are lightweight and cheap to create.

  • You can create millions of virtual threads without running out of memory.

docs.title: Differences from Platform Threads:
docs.text:
Platform Thread:

Platform threads map to OS threads (heavyweight).

Virtual Thread: Virtual threads are managed by the JVM (lightweight). here it is creating single virtual thread:

docs.title: Traditional Thread Pool
docs.text:
• Example using Executors.newFixedThreadPool():

  • Limited by the number of threads in the pool.

  • Blocking tasks can waste resources.

docs.title: Virtual Thread Per Task Executor
docs.text:
• Example using Executors.newVirtualThreadPerTaskExecutor():

  • Each task which we are submitting to the executor runs in its own virtual thread. since we are using newVirtualThreadPerTaskExecutor it will create new virtual thread per task

  • No need to worry about thread pool size or blocking tasks.

docs.title: Blocking Operations with Virtual Threads
docs.text:
• Virtual threads handle blocking operations efficiently.
  • The JVM pauses the virtual thread during blocking and switches to another task.
    • meaning JVM dismounts the virtual thread from carrier thread and mounts ready virtual threads

docs.title: Blocking I/O Example
docs.text: • Simulate a blocking operation (e.g., HTTP request):

docs.title: Comparison with Reactive Programming
docs.text:
• Reactive Code:
  • Virtual Thread Code:

Virtual threads provide normal stack traces, making debugging easier than reactive code.

docs.title: 1. Program to demonstrate DeadLock
docs.text:
Deadlock is a situation in concurrent programming where two or more threads are blocked forever, waiting for each other. The four necessary conditions for a deadlock to occur are: (all 4 are necessary) 

  • Mutual Exclusion: Resources involved are non-sharable; only one thread can use a resource at a time.
  • Hold and Wait: A thread holds at least one resource and is waiting for another resource held by another thread.
  • No Preemption: Resources cannot be forcibly taken from a thread; they must be released voluntarily.
  • Circular Wait: A set of threads are waiting for each other in a circular fashion (Thread A waits for a resource held by Thread B, Thread B waits for a resource held by Thread C, and Thread C waits for a resource held by Thread A).

The deadlock occurs if:

  1. thread1 acquires lock1.
  2. thread2 acquires lock2.
  3. Now, thread1 needs lock2 (held by thread2), and thread2 needs lock1 (held by thread1). Both threads are blocked indefinitely, waiting for the other to release the lock they need. The Thread.sleep(100) calls are added to increase the likelihood of this specific interleaving of operations, making the deadlock more probable.

docs.title: 2. Implement a simple Thread Pool using ExecutorSe
docs.text:
Implement a simple Thread Pool using ExecutorService. Explain the benefits of using a Thread Pool.

Creating and managing threads manually for every task can be resource-intensive and lead to performance issues due to the overhead of thread creation and destruction. A Thread Pool manages a set of worker threads that can execute multiple tasks.

Benefits of using a Thread Pool:

  • Reduced Overhead: Avoids the cost of creating and destroying threads for each task. Threads are reused.
  • Improved Performance: Tasks can be executed concurrently by available threads.
  • Resource Management: Limits the number of threads, preventing resource exhaustion.
  • Enhanced Responsiveness: Tasks can be submitted quickly without waiting for thread creation.
  • Task Management: Provides features like scheduling, task queues, and the ability to manage the lifecycle of submitted tasks.

We create a FixedThreadPool with 3 threads using Executors.newFixedThreadPool(3).

We then submit 10 WorkerTask instances (which implement Runnable) to the executor using executor.execute().

The thread pool's 3 threads will pick up and execute these tasks.

Once all tasks are submitted, executor.shutdown() is called to initiate a graceful shutdown. awaitTermination() is used to wait for the submitted tasks to complete within a given timeout. The output will show that tasks are executed by a limited number of threads from the pool.

docs.title: 3. Explain Callable and Future
docs.text:
Explain Callable and Future in Java concurrency. Provide a code example using ExecutorService to execute a Callable and retrieve its result.

  • Runnable vs. Callable:

    • Runnable is used for tasks that do not return a result and cannot throw checked exceptions Its run() method has a void return type.
    • Callable is used for tasks ==that return a result and can throw checked exceptions.== Its call() method returns a value of type V (generic).

    • Future:

    • Represents the result of an asynchronous computation.

    • Provides methods to check if the computation is complete, wait for its completion, and retrieve the result.
    • The get() method of Future blocks until the computation is complete and then returns the result. It can throw InterruptedException (if the waiting thread is interrupted) or ExecutionException (if the computation threw an exception).
    • The isDone() method checks if the task is complete without blocking.
    • The cancel() method attempts to cancel the execution of the task.

ExecutorService can execute both Runnable and Callable tasks. When submitting a Callable, it returns a Future object.

SumCalculator implements Callable<Integer>, meaning its call() method returns an Integer result.

We create an ExecutorService and submit instances of SumCalculator using executor.submit().

This returns Future<Integer> objects. We then call future.get() to retrieve the results.

The get() method blocks until the corresponding Callable task is completed.

The output shows the threads calculating the sums and the main thread retrieving and printing the results.

docs.title: 4. execute() and submit()
docs.text:
What is the difference between execute() and submit() methods of ExecutorService?

Both execute() and submit() are used to submit tasks to an ExecutorService, but they differ in their return type and the types of tasks they can accept.

execute(Runnable task):

  • Defined in the Executor interface (the superinterface of ExecutorService).
  • Takes a Runnable task as input.
  • Does not return any value.
  • Cannot return the result of the task or check its status (completion, cancellation).
