  • When the construction process is complex: If the process involves multiple steps or configurations, the Builder pattern provides a structured approach.
  • When an increase in the number of constructor parameters leads to a large list of constructors
  • When creating immutable objects, the Builder pattern allows for gradual construction of the object before it is finalized, ensuring that all required attributes are set.
  • Configuration Objects: In applications where configuration settings are needed, such as building a database connection or a web service client, the Builder pattern can provide a clear and flexible way to specify settings.

docs.title: Typical Steps/Pattern While Using Builder Design Pattern
docs.text:
1. Define the Product Class: Create a class that represents the complex object being built. This class should contain all the attributes that the object will have.
  2. Create a Builder Class: This class will contain methods to set the various attributes of the product. It can include both required and optional parameters.
  3. Implement a Build Method: The builder class should have a method (often named build()) that constructs and ==returns the final product object. This method typically calls the private constructor of the product class.==
  4. Use Method Chaining: Implement methods in the builder class that return this to allow for method chaining, making the code more readable.
  5. Client Code: In the client code, create an instance of the builder, set the desired parameters, and call the build() method to obtain the constructed object

We can either put the builder class inside product class for simplicity , there we can make the constructor of product class as private but if we needed to make the builder class separate , we need to make the constructor as package private (dont specify access modifier)

Example:

Computer Class: This is the product class that represents the complex object being built. It has required attributes (HDD and RAM) and optional attributes (graphics card and Bluetooth).

remember we made the constructor private to enforce use of builder(when putting the builder as inner class )

// Step 1: Create the Product class
// This class represents the complex object that will be built.
public class Computer {
    private final String HDD; // Required
    private final String RAM; // Required
    private final boolean isGraphicsCardEnabled; // Optional
    private final boolean isBluetoothEnabled; // Optional

    // Private constructor to enforce the use of the Builder
    private Computer(ComputerBuilder builder) {
        this.HDD = builder.HDD;
        this.RAM = builder.RAM;
        this.isGraphicsCardEnabled = builder.isGraphicsCardEnabled;
        this.isBluetoothEnabled = builder.isBluetoothEnabled;
    }

    // Getters for the fields
    public String getHDD() {
        return HDD;
    }

    public String getRAM() {
        return RAM;
    }

    public boolean isGraphicsCardEnabled() {
        return isGraphicsCardEnabled;
    }

    public boolean isBluetoothEnabled() {
        return isBluetoothEnabled;
    }

 here in the constructor of builder class we used required parameters while optional ones are set using setters.

 we may wanna keep the builder class inside the product class for simplicity while while creating object

so that it can be called like Computer.ComputerBuilder.

// Step 2: Create the Builder class
    // This inner static class provides the methods to build the Computer object.
    public static class ComputerBuilder {
        private final String HDD; // Required
        private final String RAM; // Required
        private boolean isGraphicsCardEnabled; // Optional
        private boolean isBluetoothEnabled; // Optional

        // Constructor for required parameters
        public ComputerBuilder(String hdd, String ram) {
            this.HDD = hdd;
            this.RAM = ram;
        }

        // Setter methods for optional parameters
        public ComputerBuilder setGraphicsCardEnabled(boolean isGraphicsCardEnabled) {
            this.isGraphicsCardEnabled = isGraphicsCardEnabled;
            return this;
        }

        public ComputerBuilder setBluetoothEnabled(boolean isBluetoothEnabled) {
            this.isBluetoothEnabled = isBluetoothEnabled;
            return this;
        }

        // Build method to create the Computer object
        public Computer build() {
            return new Computer(this);
        }
    }
}


// Step 3: Client code to demonstrate the Builder pattern
public class BuilderPatternDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Using the builder to create a Computer object
        Computer computer = new Computer.ComputerBuilder("500 GB", "2 GB")
                .setGraphicsCardEnabled(true)
                .setBluetoothEnabled(true)
                .build();

        // Displaying the computer configuration
        System.out.println("Computer Configuration:");
        System.out.println("HDD: " + computer.getHDD());
        System.out.println("RAM: " + computer.getRAM());
        System.out.println("Graphics Card Enabled: " + computer.isGraphicsCardEnabled());
        System.out.println("Bluetooth Enabled: " + computer.isBluetoothEnabled());
    }
}

docs.title: Why Adapter Pattern? (The Problem)
docs.text:
Imagine we have:

  • A client that expects data in a specific format (e.g., JSON).
  • A legacy class or third-party library that provides data in an incompatible format (e.g., XML).

Problem: The client can't directly use the legacy class because their interfaces don't match. Rewriting the legacy code may be risky or impossible.

Solution: We introduce an Adapter—a middleman that translates the incompatible interface into one the client understands.

docs.title: Essence of Adapter Pattern
docs.text:
The Adapter pattern:

  1. Bridges the gap between two incompatible interfaces.
  2. Wraps the adaptee (the incompatible class) and exposes the expected interface.
  3. Follows the "composition over inheritance" principle (usually).

docs.title: When to Use It?
docs.text:
1. Integrating legacy code with new systems.
  2. Using third-party libraries whose interfaces don't match your needs.
  3. Unifying multiple classes with similar functionality but different interfaces.

docs.title: Types of Adapters
docs.text:
1. Class Adapter (uses inheritance) – Extends the adaptee class.
  2. Object Adapter (uses composition) – Wraps an instance of the adaptee.

We'll focus on the Object Adapter (more flexible and preferred).

Example: Media Player

Let's model a media player that only plays MP3 files but needs to adapt to play MP4 and AVI files using an adapter.

docs.title: 1. Target Interface (What the Client Expects)
docs.text:
public interface MediaPlayer {
    void play(String audioType, String fileName);
}

docs.title: 2. Adaptee (Incompatible Class)
docs.text:
public class AdvancedMediaPlayer {
    public void playMp4(String fileName) {
        System.out.println("Playing MP4: " + fileName);
    }

    public void playAvi(String fileName) {
        System.out.println("Playing AVI: " + fileName);
    }
}

docs.title: 3. Adapter (Translator)
docs.text:
this adapter will have the adaptee i.e AdvancedMediaPlayer

and we will override original play method to make it compatible to play mp4 or avi

public class MediaAdapter implements MediaPlayer {
    private AdvancedMediaPlayer advancedPlayer;

    public MediaAdapter() {
        this.advancedPlayer = new AdvancedMediaPlayer();
    }

    @Override
    public void play(String audioType, String fileName) {
        if (audioType.equalsIgnoreCase("mp4")) {
            advancedPlayer.playMp4(fileName);
        } else if (audioType.equalsIgnoreCase("avi")) {
            advancedPlayer.playAvi(fileName);
        }
    }
}

docs.title: 4. Client Code
docs.text:
public class AudioPlayer implements MediaPlayer {
    private MediaAdapter mediaAdapter;

    @Override
    public void play(String audioType, String fileName) {
        if (audioType.equalsIgnoreCase("mp3")) {
            System.out.println("Playing MP3: " + fileName);
        } else if (audioType.equalsIgnoreCase("mp4") || audioType.equalsIgnoreCase("avi")) {
            mediaAdapter = new MediaAdapter();
            mediaAdapter.play(audioType, fileName);
        } else {
            System.out.println("Unsupported format: " + audioType);
        }
    }
}

docs.title: 5. Demo
docs.text:
public class AdapterDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        AudioPlayer player = new AudioPlayer();

        player.play("mp3", "song.mp3");
        player.play("mp4", "movie.mp4");
        player.play("avi", "video.avi");
        player.play("wav", "sound.wav"); // Unsupported
    }
}

Output:

Playing MP3: song.mp3
Playing MP4: movie.mp4
Playing AVI: video.avi
Unsupported format: wav

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Adapter acts as a bridge between incompatible interfaces.
  2. Object Adapter uses composition (wraps the adaptee).
  3. Client remains unchanged—it only sees the target interface.
  4. New adapters can be added without modifying existing code (Open/Closed Principle).

docs.title: Real-World Analogies
docs.text:
• Power Adapters: A travel adapter lets you plug a US device (client) into a European socket (adaptee).
  • Translators: A translator adapts a speaker's language (adaptee) to the listener's language (client).

docs.title: When NOT to Use It?
docs.text:
• If the interfaces are already compatible.
  • If you can refactor the adaptee directly (simpler solution).

docs.title: Essence of Template Method Pattern
docs.text:
1. What it does: Defines a ==skeleton algorithm in a base class, letting subclasses override specific steps without changing the structure.==
  2. Key Idea:
    • Reusable workflow with customizable parts.
    • "Don’t call us, we’ll call you" (Hollywood Principle).

docs.title: Simple Example: Pizza Maker
docs.text: Imagine a pizza-making process with fixed steps, but customizable toppings.

docs.title: 1. Abstract Template Class
docs.text:
public abstract class PizzaMaker {
    // Template method (FINAL to prevent overriding)
    public final void makePizza() {
        prepareDough();
        addSauce();
        addToppings();  // Subclasses implement this
        bake();
        serve();
    }

    // Fixed steps (common to all pizzas)
    private void prepareDough() {
        System.out.println("Rolling out dough...");
    }

    private void addSauce() {
        System.out.println("Adding tomato sauce...");
    }

    private void bake() {
        System.out.println("Baking at 200°C for 15 mins...");
    }

    private void serve() {
        System.out.println("Pizza ready! 🍕");
    }

    // Customizable step (to be implemented by subclasses)
    protected abstract void addToppings();
}

docs.title: 2. Concrete Implementations
docs.text:
public class MargheritaPizza extends PizzaMaker {
    @Override
    protected void addToppings() {
        System.out.println("Adding mozzarella and basil...");
    }
}

public class PepperoniPizza extends PizzaMaker {
    @Override
    protected void addToppings() {
        System.out.println("Adding pepperoni and cheese...");
    }
}

docs.title: 3. Client Code
docs.text:
public class PizzaShop {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        PizzaMaker margherita = new MargheritaPizza();
        margherita.makePizza();  // Uses the template

        PizzaMaker pepperoni = new PepperoniPizza();
        pepperoni.makePizza();
    }
}

Output:

Rolling out dough...
Adding tomato sauce...
Adding mozzarella and basil...  // Custom step
Baking at 200°C for 15 mins...
Pizza ready! 🍕

docs.title: Key Takeaways from the Example
docs.text:
1. Fixed Workflow: makePizza() enforces the order of steps (dough → sauce → toppings → bake).
  2. Customizable Parts: Subclasses only worry about addToppings().
  3. No Duplication: Common logic (baking, serving) stays in the base class.

docs.title: ==Template Pattern in Spring==
docs.text: Spring uses this pattern extensively to simplify repetitive tasks. Here’s how:

docs.title: 1. JdbcTemplate (Database Operations)
docs.text:
Problem: JDBC code has fixed steps (open connection, execute query, close resources), but the query logic varies.

Solution: JdbcTemplate handles the boilerplate, letting you focus on the SQL.

Example:

@Repository
public class UserDao {
    private final JdbcTemplate jdbcTemplate;

    public UserDao(JdbcTemplate jdbcTemplate) {
        this.jdbcTemplate = jdbcTemplate;
    }

    public List<User> getAllUsers() {
        // You only provide the SQL + row mapping logic
        return jdbcTemplate.query(
            "SELECT * FROM users",
            (rs, rowNum) -> new User(rs.getInt("id"), rs.getString("name"))
        );
    }
}

Template Steps in JdbcTemplate:

  1. Open connection.
  2. Execute query.
  3. Map results (custom logic via RowMapper).
  4. Handle exceptions.
  5. Close resources.

docs.title: 2. RestTemplate (HTTP Requests)
docs.text:
Problem: HTTP calls involve fixed steps (create request, send, parse response), but the URL and response type vary.

Solution: RestTemplate handles the boilerplate.

Example:

@Service
public class WeatherService {
    private final RestTemplate restTemplate;

    public WeatherService(RestTemplate restTemplate) {
        this.restTemplate = restTemplate;
    }

    public Weather fetchWeather(String city) {
        // You only provide the URL + response type
        return restTemplate.getForObject(
            "https://api.weather.com/v1/" + city,
            Weather.class
        );
    }
}

Template Steps in RestTemplate:

  1. Build HTTP request.
  2. Execute request.
  3. Parse response (custom logic via ResponseEntity).
  4. Handle errors.

Let’s explore RabbitMQTemplate and other commonly used templates in Spring, breaking down how they leverage the Template Method Pattern to simplify complex workflows. We’ll use practical examples and analogies to solidify understanding.

docs.title: What it Does:
docs.text: Simplifies sending/receiving messages via RabbitMQ by handling connection management, serialization, and error handling.

docs.title: Template Pattern in Action:
docs.text:
• Fixed Steps:
    1. Establish connection to RabbitMQ.
    2. Convert Java objects to messages (serialization).
    3. Publish to an exchange/queue.
    4. Handle acknowledgments/errors.
  • Customizable Parts:
    • Message content.
    • Routing keys.
    • Callbacks (e.g., confirmations).

docs.title: Example: Sending a Message
docs.text:
@Service
public class OrderService {
    private final RabbitTemplate rabbitTemplate;

    public OrderService(RabbitTemplate rabbitTemplate) {
        this.rabbitTemplate = rabbitTemplate;
    }

    public void placeOrder(Order order) {
        // You only define WHAT to send (order) and WHERE (routing key)
        rabbitTemplate.convertAndSend(
            "orders.exchange",  // Exchange
            "order.created",    // Routing key
            order               // Payload (auto-serialized to JSON)
        );
    }
}

docs.title: Example: Receiving a Message
docs.text:
@RabbitListener(queues = "orders.queue")
public void handleOrder(Order order) {
    System.out.println("Received order: " + order.getId());
}

Key Point: You focus on business logic (handleOrder), while RabbitTemplate manages connections, threading, and deserialization.

docs.title: What it Does:
docs.text: Simplifies producing/consuming messages with Apache Kafka.

docs.title: Template Pattern in Action:
docs.text:
• Fixed Steps:
    1. Create a producer/consumer.
    2. Serialize/deserialize messages.
    3. Handle batching/retries.
  • Customizable Parts:
    • Topic names.
    • Message payloads.
    • Listeners (for consumers).

docs.title: Example: Sending a Message
docs.text:
@Service
public class NotificationService {
    private final KafkaTemplate<String, String> kafkaTemplate;

    public NotificationService(KafkaTemplate<String, String> kafkaTemplate) {
        this.kafkaTemplate = kafkaTemplate;
    }

    public void sendAlert(String message) {
        // You define WHAT (message) and WHERE (topic)
        kafkaTemplate.send("alerts.topic", message);
    }
}

docs.title: Example: Receiving a Message
docs.text:
@KafkaListener(topics = "alerts.topic")
public void listen(String alert) {
    System.out.println("ALERT: " + alert);
}

docs.title: 2.1 Facade Design Patten
docs.text: The Facade Design Pattern is a structural design pattern that ==provides a simplified interface to a complex subsystem, making it easier for clients to interact with the system without needing to understand its complexities.== This pattern is particularly useful when working with a set of interfaces in a subsystem that may be difficult to use or understand.

docs.title: When It Can Be Used
docs.text:
• When a system is complex or difficult to understand: Facades can help hide the complexity of the underlying system, providing a simpler interface for clients.
  • When you want to decouple clients from the subsystem: This reduces the coupling between the client code and the subsystem, making it easier to change or refactor the subsystem without affecting clients.
  • When you need to provide a single entry point to a subsystem: Facades can serve as a single point of access for multiple classes in a subsystem.

docs.title: Example:
docs.text:
In a typical home theater setup, there are multiple components such as a DVD player, projector, sound system, and lighting controls. Each of these components has its own interface and requires specific commands to operate. The Facade pattern can simplify the interaction with these components by providing a unified interface.

// Step 1: Create the subsystem classes

// DVD Player class
public class DVDPlayer {
    public void on() {
        System.out.println("DVD Player is ON");
    }

    public void play(String movie) {
        System.out.println("Playing movie: " + movie);
    }

    public void stop() {
        System.out.println("Stopping the DVD Player");
    }

    public void off() {
        System.out.println("DVD Player is OFF");
    }
}


// Projector class
public class Projector {
    public void on() {
        System.out.println("Projector is ON");
    }

    public void setInput(String input) {
        System.out.println("Projector input set to: " + input);
    }

    public void off() {
        System.out.println("Projector is OFF");
    }
}


// Sound System class
public class SoundSystem {
    public void on() {
        System.out.println("Sound System is ON");
    }

    public void setVolume(int level) {
        System.out.println("Setting volume to: " + level);
    }

    public void off() {
        System.out.println("Sound System is OFF");
    }
}


// Lights class
public class Lights {
    public void dim(int level) {
        System.out.println("Dimming lights to: " + level + "%");
    }

    public void on() {
        System.out.println("Lights are ON");
    }

    public void off() {
        System.out.println("Lights are OFF");
    }
}

// Step 2: Create the Facade class

public class HomeTheaterFacade {
    private DVDPlayer dvdPlayer;
    private Projector projector;
    private SoundSystem soundSystem;
    private Lights lights;

    public HomeTheaterFacade(DVDPlayer dvdPlayer, Projector projector, SoundSystem soundSystem, Lights lights) {
        this.dvdPlayer = dvdPlayer;
        this.projector = projector;
        this.soundSystem = soundSystem;
        this.lights = lights;
    }

    public void watchMovie(String movie) {
        System.out.println("Get ready to watch a movie...");
        lights.dim(10); // Dim the lights
        projector.on(); // Turn on the projector
        projector.setInput("DVD"); // Set projector input to DVD
        soundSystem.on(); // Turn on the sound system
        soundSystem.setVolume(5); // Set volume
        dvdPlayer.on(); // Turn on the DVD player
        dvdPlayer.play(movie); // Play the movie
    }
     public void endMovie() {
        System.out.println("Shutting down the home theater...");
        dvdPlayer.stop(); // Stop the DVD player
        dvdPlayer.off(); // Turn off the DVD player
        soundSystem.off(); // Turn off the sound system
        projector.off(); // Turn off the projector
        lights.on(); // Turn on the lights
    }
}

// Step 3: Client

public class FacadePatternDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Create subsystem components
        DVDPlayer dvdPlayer = new DVDPlayer();
        Projector projector = new Projector();
        SoundSystem soundSystem = new SoundSystem();
        Lights lights = new Lights();

        // Create the facade
        HomeTheaterFacade homeTheater = new HomeTheaterFacade(dvdPlayer, projector, soundSystem, lights);

        // Use the facade to watch a movie
        homeTheater.watchMovie("Inception");

        // End the movie
        homeTheater.endMovie();
    }
}

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text:
• Subsystem Classes: The DVDPlayer, Projector, SoundSystem, and Lights classes represent the various components of the home theater system. Each class has methods to perform specific actions.
  • HomeTheaterFacade Class: This is the facade class that simplifies the interaction with the complex subsystem. It provides a high-level interface for watching and ending movies, coordinating the actions of the various components.
  • FacadePatternDemo Class: This is the client code that demonstrates how to use the facade. It creates instances of the subsystem components and the facade, then calls the watchMovie and endMovie methods.

docs.title: 2.2 Facade Design Patten in Spring Boot
docs.text: The Facade pattern is a structural design pattern that provides a simplified interface to a complex subsystem of classes, library, or framework. It defines a higher-level interface that makes the subsystem easier to use.

docs.title: When to Use It?
docs.text:
Use the Facade pattern when:

  1. You want to provide a simple interface to a complex subsystem.
  2. You need to layer your subsystems.
  3. You want to decouple your client implementation from any one subsystem.
  4. You need to integrate your application with a sophisticated library or API.

docs.title: 1. Service Layer as a Facade
docs.text:
In a typical Spring Boot application, the service layer often acts as a facade for underlying repositories and external services:

@Service
public class OrderFacade {
    private final OrderRepository orderRepository;
    private final PaymentService paymentService;
    private final InventoryService inventoryService;
    private final NotificationService notificationService;

    public OrderFacade(OrderRepository orderRepository,
                       PaymentService paymentService,
                       InventoryService inventoryService,
                       NotificationService notificationService) {
        this.orderRepository = orderRepository;
        this.paymentService = paymentService;
        this.inventoryService = inventoryService;
        this.notificationService = notificationService;
    }

    public OrderResult placeOrder(Order order) {
        // Check inventory
        if (!inventoryService.checkAvailability(order.getItems())) {
            return OrderResult.INSUFFICIENT_INVENTORY;
        }

        // Process payment
        PaymentResult paymentResult = paymentService.processPayment(order.getPaymentDetails());
        if (paymentResult != PaymentResult.SUCCESS) {
            return OrderResult.PAYMENT_FAILED;
        }

        // Save order
        Order savedOrder = orderRepository.save(order);

        // Update inventory
        inventoryService.updateInventory(order.getItems());

        // Send notification
        notificationService.sendOrderConfirmation(savedOrder);

        return OrderResult.SUCCESS;
    }
}

The placeOrder method in OrderFacade encapsulates the entire workflow of placing an order. It hides the details of interacting with multiple services (PaymentService, InventoryService, NotificationService) and the repository (OrderRepository).

The OrderFacade acts as a layer of abstraction between the client and the various services and repositories

docs.title: 2. Spring's JdbcTemplate
docs.text:
Spring's JdbcTemplate is a facade over JDBC, simplifying database operations:

@Repository
public class UserRepository {
    private final JdbcTemplate jdbcTemplate;

    public UserRepository(JdbcTemplate jdbcTemplate) {
        this.jdbcTemplate = jdbcTemplate;
    }

    public User findById(Long id) {
        return jdbcTemplate.queryForObject(
            "SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ?",
            new Object[]{id},
            (rs, rowNum) ->
                new User(
                    rs.getLong("id"),
                    rs.getString("username"),
                    rs.getString("email")
                )
        );
    }
}

JdbcTemplate simplifies JDBC interactions by encapsulating low-level details like:

  • Establishing connections to the database.
  • Creating and preparing statements.
  • Managing result sets.
  • Handling exceptions.
  • Closing resources (connections, statements, result sets) to avoid leaks.

docs.title: 3. Spring Boot Actuator
docs.text:
Spring Boot Actuator provides a facade for monitoring and managing your application:

@Configuration
public class ActuatorConfig {
    @Bean
    public HealthIndicator databaseHealthIndicator() {
        return new HealthIndicator() {
            @Override
            public Health health() {
                // Check database health
                if (isDatabaseHealthy()) {
                    return Health.up().withDetail("Database", "Operational").build();
                }
                return Health.down().withDetail("Database", "Not Available").build();
            }
        };
    }
}

The HealthIndicator interface provides a simple and consistent way to expose the health status of different components (database, disk space, external services, etc.).

databaseHealthIndicator encapsulates the complex logic for checking the database health (e.g., executing a simple query, checking connection status).

Actuator's health endpoint (/actuator/health) uses the HealthIndicator interface to aggregate the health status from all registered indicators, hiding the complexity of individual health checks.

The clients (e.g., monitoring tools, administrators) only need to interact with Actuator's endpoints. They don't need to know the specifics of how each health check is implemented.

docs.title: 4. Spring's RestTemplate
docs.text:
RestTemplate is a facade for making HTTP requests:

@Service
public class ExternalServiceClient {
    private final RestTemplate restTemplate;

    public ExternalServiceClient(RestTemplate restTemplate) {
        this.restTemplate = restTemplate;
    }

    public ExternalData fetchData(String id) {
        return restTemplate.getForObject("http://api.example.com/data/{id}", ExternalData.class, id);
    }
}

RestTemplate provides a higher-level, more intuitive API for making HTTP requests compared to working directly with lower-level libraries like HttpURLConnection or Apache HttpClient

Methods like getForObject, postForEntity, exchange, etc., abstract away the complexities of creating connections, setting headers, encoding parameters, and handling responses.

The internal workings of RestTemplate involve establishing connections, managing HTTP protocols, dealing with encoding and decoding, and handling potential errors.

docs.title: 5. Custom Facade for External Services
docs.text:
When integrating with complex external services, you might create a facade:

@Service
public class PaymentGatewayFacade {
    private final PaymentProcessor paymentProcessor;
    private final FraudDetector fraudDetector;
    private final Logger logger;

    public PaymentGatewayFacade(PaymentProcessor paymentProcessor,
                                FraudDetector fraudDetector,
                                Logger logger) {
        this.paymentProcessor = paymentProcessor;
        this.fraudDetector = fraudDetector;
        this.logger = logger;
    }

    public PaymentResult processPayment(PaymentDetails details) {
        try {
            if (fraudDetector.isPotentiallyFraudulent(details)) {
                logger.warn("Potential fraud detected for payment: " + details.getId());
                return PaymentResult.REJECTED;
            }

            PaymentResult result = paymentProcessor.process(details);
            logger.info("Payment processed: " + details.getId() + ", Result: " + result);
            return result;
        } catch (Exception e) {
            logger.error("Error processing payment: " + details.getId(), e);
            return PaymentResult.ERROR;
        }
    }
}

This facade simplifies payment processing by coordinating fraud detection, payment processing, and logging.

client only need to call processPayments()

docs.title: 3.1 Strategy Design Pattern
docs.text:
The Strategy Design Pattern: A Conceptual Overview

The Strategy Design Pattern ==lets you define a family of algorithms (strategies), encapsulate each one, and make them interchangeable.==

This allows the algorithm to vary independently from the client that uses it.

Strategy Design Pattern is a behavioral design pattern that allows the behavior of an object to be selected at runtime.

Components of the Strategy Design Pattern

Strategy Interface: The strategy interface defines a set of methods that encapsulate the different algorithms. All concrete strategy classes must implement this interface to provide their specific implementations.

The PaymentStrategy interface defines a contract for all payment strategies.

Concrete Strategies: These are the actual algorithm implementations. Each concrete strategy class implements the strategy interface, providing a specific behavior or algorithm.

CreditCardPaymentStrategy, PayPalPaymentStrategy, UPIpaymentStrategy implement the interface, providing specific payment logic.

Context: The context is the class that contains a reference to the strategy interface. It is the class that delegates the algorithm responsibilities to the strategy.

The ShoppingCart class holds a reference to a PaymentStrategy object. Its checkout method delegates payment processing to the chosen strategy.

// Strategy Interface
interface PaymentStrategy {
    void processPayment(double amount);
}

// Concrete Strategies
class CreditCardPaymentStrategy implements PaymentStrategy {
    @Override
    public void processPayment(double amount) {
        System.out.println("Processing credit card payment of $" + amount);
        // ... (Logic for credit card processing)
    }
}

class PayPalPaymentStrategy implements PaymentStrategy {
    @Override
    public void processPayment(double amount) {
        System.out.println("Processing PayPal payment of $" + amount);
        // ... (Logic for PayPal processing)
    }
}

class UPIpaymentStrategy implements PaymentStrategy {
    @Override
    public void processPayment(double amount) {
        System.out.println("Processing UPI payment of $" + amount);
        // ... (Logic for UPI processing)
    }
}

// Context Class
class ShoppingCart {
    private PaymentStrategy paymentStrategy;

    public void setPaymentStrategy(PaymentStrategy paymentStrategy) {
        this.paymentStrategy = paymentStrategy;
    }

    public void checkout(double amount) {
        paymentStrategy.processPayment(amount);
        // ... (Additional checkout logic)
    }
}

Now for usages  

public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
    ShoppingCart cart = new ShoppingCart();
    // The user selects credit card payment
    cart.setPaymentStrategy(new CreditCardPaymentStrategy());
    cart.checkout(100.0);

    // Later, the user switches to UPI payment
    cart.setPaymentStrategy(new UPIpaymentStrategy());
    cart.checkout(50.0);       

    }
}

docs.title: 4. Decorator design pattern
docs.text:
The Decorator pattern is a structural design pattern that ==allows behavior to be added to an individual object, either statically or dynamically, without affecting the behavior of other objects from the same class.== It's used to extend or alter the functionality of objects at runtime without altering their structure.

The Decorator pattern is like wrapping a gift. Imagine you have a simple gift (our basic object), and you want to make it fancier without changing the gift itself.

  1. Basic Concept:
    • You start with a basic object (like our basic pizza).
    • Instead of changing this object directly, you "wrap" it with another object that adds new features.
    • You can keep wrapping it with more objects to add more features.
  2. Real-world Analogy: Think of a basic pizza as a plain cheese pizza. Now, you want to add toppings:
    • Instead of changing the pizza itself, you're adding layers on top.
    • First, you might add pepperoni (one decorator).
    • Then, you might add extra cheese (another decorator).
    • Each addition doesn't change the original pizza; it just adds to it.
  3. Why Use It:
    • It's flexible: You can add or remove features easily.
    • It's clean: You don't have to change existing code to add new features.
    • It's modular: Each new feature (decorator) is separate and can be used independently.
  4. When to Use It:
    • When you want to add responsibilities to objects dynamically.
    • When you want to add features without affecting other objects of the same class.
    • When extending a class by subclassing is impractical or impossible.

So,, instead of creating a new subclass for every possible combination of features (like PepperoniCheesePizza, ExtraCheesePizza, etc.), you create "wrapper" classes that each add one feature. You can then combine these wrappers in any way you need.

// Component interface

interface Pizza {
    String getDescription();
    double getCost();
}

// Concrete component

class BasicPizza implements Pizza {
    @Override
    public String getDescription() {
        return "Basic pizza";
    }

    @Override
    public double getCost() {
        return 4.00;
    }
}

// Decorator abstract class

abstract class ToppingDecorator implements Pizza {
    protected Pizza pizza;

    public ToppingDecorator(Pizza pizza) {
        this.pizza = pizza;
    }

    @Override
    public String getDescription() {
        return pizza.getDescription();
    }

    @Override
    public double getCost() {
        return pizza.getCost();
    }
}

// Concrete decorators

class CheeseDecorator extends ToppingDecorator {
    public CheeseDecorator(Pizza pizza) {
        super(pizza);
    }

    @Override
    public String getDescription() {
        return pizza.getDescription() + ", extra cheese";
    }

    @Override
    public double getCost() {
        return pizza.getCost() + 1.50;
    }
}
//////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////

class PepperoniDecorator extends ToppingDecorator {
    public PepperoniDecorator(Pizza pizza) {
        super(pizza);
    }

    @Override
    public String getDescription() {
        return pizza.getDescription() + ", pepperoni";
    }

    @Override
    public double getCost() {
        return pizza.getCost() + 2.00;
    }
}

Usage:

public class PizzaShop {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Pizza myPizza = new BasicPizza();
        System.out.println(myPizza.getDescription() + " - $" + myPizza.getCost());

        myPizza = new CheeseDecorator(myPizza);
        System.out.println(myPizza.getDescription() + " - $" + myPizza.getCost());

        myPizza = new PepperoniDecorator(myPizza);
        System.out.println(myPizza.getDescription() + " - $" + myPizza.getCost());
    }
}

docs.title: 5. Observer Design Pattern
docs.text:
The Observer Design Pattern is a behavioral design pattern that ==allows objects to notify other objects about changes to their state.==

It defines a one-to-many dependency between objects so that when one object changes its state, all its dependents are notified and updated automatically.

docs.title: Observer Design Pattern: The Core Idea
docs.text:
Think of a magazine subscription. You (the observer) subscribe to a magazine (the subject). Whenever the magazine releases a new issue,it notifies all its subscribers. The Observer Pattern works on a similar principle in software.

It establishes a one-to-many dependency between objects. A subject maintains a list of its dependents, called observers, and notifies them automatically of any state changes.

docs.title: Key Characteristics of the Observer Design Pattern
docs.text:
• One-to-many dependency: The pattern establishes a one-to-many dependency between objects, where one object (the subject) maintains a list of its dependents (observers).
  • Notification: When the subject changes its state, it notifies all its observers, allowing them to react accordingly.
  • Decoupling: The pattern decouples the subject from its observers, allowing them to operate independently.

docs.title: ==Components of the Observer Design Pattern==
docs.text:
1. ==Subject: The subject is the object that maintains a list of observers and notifies them when its state changes.==
  2. ==Observer: The observer is the object that registers itself with the subject to receive notifications when the subject's state changes.==
  • Interfaces: Product defines methods for managing observers and notifying them. CustomerObserver defines the update method that observers must implement.
  • Concrete Subject: AmazonProduct keeps track of its stock status and a list of observers. When its setInStock method is called with true, it notifies all observers.
  • Concrete Observer: Customer stores an email address and implements the update method to send a notification.
// Subject Interface
interface Product {
    void registerObserver(CustomerObserver observer);
    void removeObserver(CustomerObserver observer);
    void notifyObservers();
}





// Observer Interface
interface CustomerObserver {
    void update(Product product);
}





// Concrete Subject (Product)
class AmazonProduct implements Product {
    private String name;
    private boolean inStock = false;
    private List<CustomerObserver> observers = new ArrayList<>();

    // ... (Constructor, getters/setters)

    public void setInStock(boolean inStock) {
        this.inStock = inStock;
        if (inStock) {
            notifyObservers();
        }
    }

    @Override
    public void registerObserver(CustomerObserver observer) {
        observers.add(observer);
    }

    @Override
    public void removeObserver(CustomerObserver observer) {
        observers.remove(observer);
    }

    @Override
    public void notifyObservers() {
        for (CustomerObserver observer : observers) {
            observer.update(this);
        }
    }
}







// Concrete Observer (Customer)
class Customer implements CustomerObserver {
    private String email;

    // ... (Constructor, getters/setters)

    @Override
    public void update(Product product) {
        System.out.println("Email to " + email + ": Product " + product.getName() + " is back in stock!");
        // ... (Logic to send an actual email)
    }
}

usage;

AmazonProduct iphone = new AmazonProduct("iPhone 15 Pro", false);

Customer customer1 = new Customer("customer1@example.com");
Customer customer2 = new Customer("customer2@example.com");

iphone.registerObserver(customer1);
iphone.registerObserver(customer2);

// iPhone is back in stock
iphone.setInStock(true); // Triggers notifications

o/p: Email to customer1@example.com: Product iPhone 15 Pro is back in stock!

Email to customer2@example.com: Product iPhone 15 Pro is back in stock!

docs.title: 6.1 Factory Design Pattern
docs.text:
The Factory design pattern is all about ==creating objects without exposing the exact creation logic to the client.==

It provides an interface for creating objects, but lets subclasses decide which class to instantiate.

Example :

// This interface defines the method that all notifications will implement.
public interface Notification {
    void send(String message);
}

// EmailNotification class that sends notifications via email.
public class EmailNotification implements Notification {
    @Override
    public void send(String message) {
        System.out.println("Email Notification: " + message);
    }
}

// SMSNotification class that sends notifications via SMS.
public class SMSNotification implements Notification {
    @Override
    public void send(String message) {
        System.out.println("SMS Notification: " + message);
    }
}

// PushNotification class that sends notifications via Push notifications.
public class PushNotification implements Notification {
    @Override
    public void send(String message) {
        System.out.println("Push Notification: " + message);
    }
}


// This factory class will decide which notification type to create based on input.
public class NotificationFactory {
    // Method to create notification objects based on the type provided.
    public Notification createNotification(String type) {
        if (type == null) {
            return null;
        }
        if (type.equalsIgnoreCase("EMAIL")) {
            return new EmailNotification();
        } else if (type.equalsIgnoreCase("SMS")) {
            return new SMSNotification();
        } else if (type.equalsIgnoreCase("PUSH")) {
            return new PushNotification();
        }
        return null;
    }
}

Here’s an updated, more practical list of Factory Pattern usages in Java/Spring, focusing on modern and commonly encountered scenarios:

docs.title: 1. Java Collections Framework
docs.text:
Factory Method: Collections.unmodifiableList(), List.of(), Set.of() Why? Immutable collections are created via factory methods.

List<String> immutableList = List.of("A", "B", "C");  // Factory method

docs.title: 2. Java Time API (java.time)
docs.text:
Factory Methods: LocalDate.now(), LocalDate.of() Why? Hide complex date initialization logic.

LocalDate date = LocalDate.of(2023, Month.JANUARY, 1);  // Factory method

docs.title: 3. Logging (SLF4J + Logback)
docs.text:
Factory Class: LoggerFactory (SLF4J) Why? Decouples logging implementation (Logback/Log4j2).

import org.slf4j.Logger;
import org.slf4j.LoggerFactory;

Logger logger = LoggerFactory.getLogger(MyClass.class);  // Factory method

docs.title: 4. Spring Dependency Injection
docs.text:
Factory Class: ApplicationContext Why? Creates and manages beans dynamically.

// Spring Boot's implicit factory
@Autowired
private MyService myService;  // Injected by Spring's bean factory

Explicit Factory Bean:

@Configuration
public class MyFactory {
    @Bean
    public MyService myService() {  // Factory method
        return new MyService();
    }
}

docs.title: Key Takeaways for Interviews
docs.text:
1. Factory Methods (e.g., List.of()) are simpler than full Factory Classes.
  2. Spring DI is the most powerful real-world factory example.
  3. SLF4J’s LoggerFactory is the gold standard for logging factories.

Pro Tip:

  • "Factories hide object creation complexity. Spring’s @Bean is a factory on steroids!"

docs.title: 6.2 Factory Design Pattern Spring boot
docs.text: The Factory pattern encapsulates the creation of objects. It provides a central place (the "factory") to manage how objects are instantiated, promoting loose coupling between the client code and the concrete classes being created.

docs.title: 1. Spring's BeanFactory
docs.text:
The core of Spring's IoC container is the BeanFactory interface, which is an advanced implementation of the factory pattern.

BeanFactory creates instances of beans when they are requested (either during application startup or on-demand). It manages their dependencies, wiring them together as needed.

The BeanFactory controls the scope of beans (e.g., singleton, prototype), determining how many instances are created and how they are shared.

@Configuration
public class AppConfig {

    @Bean
    public UserService userService() {
        return new UserServiceImpl();
    }

    @Bean
    public OrderService orderService() {
        return new OrderServiceImpl();
    }
}

  • @Configuration: Marks this class as a source of bean definitions.
  • @Bean: Indicates that the annotated method produces a bean that will be managed by the Spring container. The method name becomes the bean's ID, and the return value is the bean instance.

How It Works:

  • When your Spring application starts, it scans for classes annotated with @Configuration. It finds AppConfig and processes the userService() and orderService() methods.
  • Spring registers these methods as bean definitions in the BeanFactory. It knows to create instances of UserServiceImpl and OrderServiceImpl when needed.
  • If other components in your application depend on UserService or OrderService, Spring automatically injects the bean instances provided by the BeanFactory.

docs.title: 2. Abstract Factory with Spring Profiles
docs.text:
You can use Spring profiles to implement an Abstract Factory pattern:

public interface DataSource {
    void connect();
}

@Component
@Profile("dev")
public class DevDataSource implements DataSource {
    public void connect() {
        System.out.println("Connecting to Dev database");
    }
}

@Component
@Profile("prod")
public class ProdDataSource implements DataSource {
    public void connect() {
        System.out.println("Connecting to Prod database");
    }
}

@Service
public class DataManager {
    private final DataSource dataSource;

    @Autowired
    public DataManager(DataSource dataSource) {
        this.dataSource = dataSource;
    }

    public void initiateConnection() {
        dataSource.connect();
    }
}

Spring will inject the appropriate DataSource based on the active profile.

docs.title: 3. JPA EntityManagerFactory
docs.text:
In Spring Data JPA, The EntityManagerFactory serves as a factory for creating EntityManager instances. Just as a pizza factory produces pizzas, the EntityManagerFactory produces EntityManager objects.

The EntityManager is the central interface in JPA for interacting with the database. It provides methods for:

  • Persisting: Saving new entities to the database.
  • Retrieving: Fetching entities from the database.
  • Updating: Modifying existing entities.
  • Removing: Deleting entities.
  • Querying: Executing JPQL (Java Persistence Query Language) queries.

Why Use EntityManagerFactory?

  • Thread Safety: The EntityManagerFactory itself is thread-safe. It's designed to be shared across multiple threads in your application.
  • Resource Management: It handles the underlying resources (database connections, etc.) efficiently. You shouldn't create EntityManager instances directly, as they are not thread-safe.
  • Configuration: The EntityManagerFactory encapsulates the configuration of your JPA persistence unit (data source, entity classes, JPA provider, etc.).

@Configuration
@EnableJpaRepositories
public class JpaConfig {

    @Bean
    public LocalContainerEntityManagerFactoryBean entityManagerFactory() {
        LocalContainerEntityManagerFactoryBean em = new LocalContainerEntityManagerFactoryBean();
        em.setDataSource(dataSource());
        em.setPackagesToScan("com.example.domain");
        em.setJpaVendorAdapter(new HibernateJpaVendorAdapter());
        return em;
    }
}

This factory creates EntityManager instances, which are used to interact with the database.

How It Works:

  1. Spring creates the EntityManagerFactory bean based on your configuration.
  2. You inject the EntityManagerFactory into your repositories or other components that need to interact with the database.
  3. When you need to perform database operations:
    • Call EntityManagerFactory.createEntityManager() to get a new EntityManager instance.
    • Use the EntityManager to persist, retrieve, update, delete, or query entities.
    • Close the EntityManager when you're finished.

In the context of Spring Data JPA, you wouldn't typically call EntityManagerFactory.createEntityManager() directly to get an EntityManager

Inject the EntityManager:

  • If you're using Spring Data JPA repositories, the EntityManager is automatically injected into your repository classes. You can use it directly within repository methods:

  • If you need the EntityManager in a service or controller, you can inject it using the @PersistenceContext annotation:

    • @PersistenceContext private EntityManager entityManager;

docs.title: 4. RestTemplate Factory
docs.text:
You might create a factory for RestTemplate to ensure consistent configuration:

@Configuration
public class RestTemplateConfig {

    @Bean
    public RestTemplate restTemplate(RestTemplateBuilder builder) {
        return builder
            .setConnectTimeout(Duration.ofSeconds(5))
            .setReadTimeout(Duration.ofSeconds(5))
            .build();
    }
}

 This factory method creates a consistently configured RestTemplate bean.

docs.title: What is the Builder Pattern?
docs.text: The Builder pattern is a creational ==design pattern that lets you construct complex objects step by step==. It allows you to produce different types and representations of an object using the same construction code.

docs.title: When to Use It?
docs.text:
Use the Builder pattern when:

  1. You need to create an object with many possible configuration options.
  2. You want to create immutable objects.
  3. You need to construct objects that contain multiple parts.
  4. You want to separate the construction of a complex object from its representation.

docs.title: 1. Lombok @Builder
docs.text:
Lombok's @Builder annotation is a popular way to implement the Builder pattern in Spring Boot applications:

    import lombok.Builder;
import lombok.Data;

@Data
@Builder
public class User {
    private String username;
    private String email;
    private String firstName;
    private String lastName;
    private int age;
}

// Usage
User user = User.builder()
    .username("johndoe")
    .email("john@example.com")
    .firstName("John")
    .lastName("Doe")
    .age(30)
    .build();

@Builder: it automatically generates the code necessary to implement the Builder pattern for our class

  • Create a nested static class named UserBuilder within your User class.
  • Generate a builder() method in your User class that returns a new instance of UserBuilder.
  • Create setter-like methods (with the same names as your fields) in the UserBuilder class. These methods return the builder instance itself, enabling method chaining.
  • Generate a build() method in the UserBuilder class that constructs and returns a User object using the values set in the builder.

docs.title: 2. Spring's WebClient
docs.text:
Spring WebFlux's WebClient uses the Builder pattern for configuration:

WebClient webClient = WebClient.builder()
    .baseUrl("https://api.example.com")
    .defaultHeader(HttpHeaders.CONTENT_TYPE, MediaType.APPLICATION_JSON_VALUE)
    .defaultUriVariables(Collections.singletonMap("url", "https://api.example.com"))
    .build();

WebClient.Builder    is static nested inner class inside WebClient

  • Obtain Builder: WebClient.builder() gives you an instance of the WebClient.Builder class, which is the starting point for your configuration.

  • Chain Configuration: You chain method calls on the builder to set different properties. Each method (like baseUrl, defaultHeader, defaultUriVariables) returns the builder instance, allowing you to fluently add more configurations.

  • Build the Client: Finally, calling build() creates the fully configured WebClient instance that you can use to make HTTP requests.

docs.title: 3. TestRestTemplate Builder
docs.text:
In Spring Boot testing, TestRestTemplate.Builder is used to configure the template:

@Autowired
private TestRestTemplate.Builder builder;

@Test
public void testSomething() {
    TestRestTemplate template = builder
        .basicAuthentication("user", "password")
        .build();

    // Use the template for testing
}

we use TestRestTemplate   while doing intergration testing over restTemplate since it is designed to work seamlessly with the Spring Boot testing framework. It automatically configures itself to interact with your application's embedded server or a running instance if you've specified a port.

TestRestTemplate can be easily mocked or stubbed for unit tests that don't require a full integration test environment.

When using @SpringBootTest, the TestRestTemplate is auto-configured, and you can inject it directly into your test class.

Easier Authentication: Built-in support for basic auth and extensibility for other mechanisms.

code flow:

  • Builder Injection:

    • Spring Boot auto-configures a TestRestTemplate.Builder bean.
    • You inject this builder into your test class using @Autowired.

    • Configuration Chaining:

    • You call methods on the builder (e.g., .basicAuthentication()) to configure the TestRestTemplate.

    • Each configuration method returns the builder instance, allowing you to chain calls together.

    • Building the Template:

    • The .build() method creates a TestRestTemplate instance with the specified configuration. This instance is ready to make HTTP requests.

docs.title: 4. Custom Configuration Builder
docs.text:
You might create a builder for complex configuration objects:

@Configuration
public class DatabaseConfig {

    @Bean
    public DataSource dataSource() {
        return new DataSourceBuilder()
            .url("jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/mydb")
            .username("user")
            .password("password")
            .driverClassName("com.mysql.jdbc.Driver")
            .build();
    }
}

docs.title: 5. Building Complex Domain Objects
docs.text:
For complex domain objects, a custom builder can be very useful:

public class Order {
    private final String id;
    private final Customer customer;
    private final List<OrderItem> items;
    private final BigDecimal total;
    private final LocalDateTime orderDate;

    private Order(Builder builder) {
        this.id = builder.id;
        this.customer = builder.customer;
        this.items = builder.items;
        this.total = builder.total;
        this.orderDate = builder.orderDate;
    }

    public static class Builder {
        private String id;
        private Customer customer;
        private List<OrderItem> items = new ArrayList<>();
        private BigDecimal total = BigDecimal.ZERO;
        private LocalDateTime orderDate;

        public Builder id(String id) {
            this.id = id;
            return this;
        }

        public Builder customer(Customer customer) {
            this.customer = customer;
            return this;
        }

        public Builder addItem(OrderItem item) {
            this.items.add(item);
            this.total = this.total.add(item.getPrice());
            return this;
        }

        public Builder orderDate(LocalDateTime orderDate) {
            this.orderDate = orderDate;
            return this;
        }

        public Order build() {
            return new Order(this);
        }
    }
}

// Usage
Order order = new Order.Builder()
    .id("12345")
    .customer(customer)
    .addItem(new OrderItem("Item1", new BigDecimal("10.00")))
    .addItem(new OrderItem("Item2", new BigDecimal("15.00")))
    .orderDate(LocalDateTime.now())
    .build();

docs.title: 6. Spring Security
docs.text:
Spring Security uses the Builder pattern for configuring security rules:

@Configuration
@EnableWebSecurity
public class SecurityConfig extends WebSecurityConfigurerAdapter {

    @Override
    protected void configure(HttpSecurity http) throws Exception {
        http
            .authorizeRequests()
                .antMatchers("/", "/home").permitAll()
                .anyRequest().authenticated()
                .and()
            .formLogin()
                .loginPage("/login")
                .permitAll()
                .and()
            .logout()
                .permitAll();
    }
}

  • Builder Object:

    • The HttpSecurity object you receive in the configure method acts as the builder. It provides methods like authorizeRequests(), formLogin(), logout(), and more.

    • Chaining Methods:

    • Each method on the HttpSecurity builder returns the builder instance itself. This allows you to chain calls together in a fluent manner. For example: http.authorizeRequests()...and().formLogin()...and().logout()...

    • Build (Implicit):

    • In this specific case, there's no explicit build() method like in some other builder implementations. The Spring Security configuration is built implicitly when the configure() method completes.

Fluent Interface

A fluent interface is a design pattern where methods return the object they are called on (or a similar object) to allow for method chaining. This makes the code more concise and readable.

docs.title: 8.1 Proxy Design Pattern
docs.text:
The proxy design pattern is a structural design pattern, ==that provides a intermediary acting as interface to another resource or object.==

This pattern is particularly useful when you want to add an extra layer of control over access to an object, allowing for functionalities such as lazy loading, access control, logging, and more.

Common Usages in java:

  1. Access Control: Proxies can restrict access to certain functionalities based on user permissions. For instance, a proxy can allow only admin users to execute sensitive commands.
  2. Lazy Initialization: Proxies can delay the creation of expensive objects until they are needed, improving performance and resource management.
  3. Remote Method Invocation (RMI): Java's RMI framework uses proxies to facilitate communication between objects in different JVMs, allowing for seamless remote calls.
  4. Caching: Proxies can cache results of expensive operations, returning the cached result for subsequent requests to improve efficiency.
  5. Logging and Monitoring: Proxies can log requests and responses, providing insights into application behavior without modifying the original object. can be used for pre or post processing calls/requests

docs.title: 1. Basic Proxy Example (Virtual Proxy - Lazy Loading)
docs.text: Scenario: Load a heavy Database object only when needed.

docs.title: Step 1: Define the Subject Interface
docs.text:
public interface Database {
    void query(String sql);
}

docs.title: Step 2: Real Object (Expensive to Create)
docs.text:
public class RealDatabase implements Database {
    public RealDatabase() {
        System.out.println("Initializing heavy database connection...");
    }

    @Override
    public void query(String sql) {
        System.out.println("Executing query: " + sql);
    }
}

docs.title: Step 3: Proxy (Controls Access)
docs.text:
public class DatabaseProxy implements Database {
    private RealDatabase realDatabase;  // Lazy initialization

    @Override
    public void query(String sql) {
        if (realDatabase == null) {
            realDatabase = new RealDatabase();  // Create only on first use
        }
        realDatabase.query(sql);  // Delegate to real object
    }
}

docs.title: Step 4: Client Code
docs.text:
public class Client {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Database proxy = new DatabaseProxy();

        // Database not initialized yet
        proxy.query("SELECT * FROM users");  // Initializes here
        proxy.query("DROP TABLE users");
    }
}

Output:

Initializing heavy database connection...  
Executing query: SELECT * FROM users  
Executing query: DROP TABLE users

docs.title: 2. Dynamic Proxy (Java Built-in)
docs.text: Use Case: Log all method calls to a service.

docs.title: Step 1: Define Service Interface
docs.text:
public interface PaymentService {
    void pay(int amount);
}

docs.title: Step 2: Real Service
docs.text:
public class RealPaymentService implements PaymentService {
    @Override
    public void pay(int amount) {
        System.out.println("Processing payment: $" + amount);
    }
}

docs.title: Step 3: Invocation Handler (Proxy Logic)
docs.text:
import java.lang.reflect.*;

public class LoggingHandler implements InvocationHandler {
    private Object target;

    public LoggingHandler(Object target) {
        this.target = target;
    }

    @Override
    public Object invoke(Object proxy, Method method, Object[] args) throws Throwable {
        System.out.println("Log: Calling " + method.getName() + "()");
        return method.invoke(target, args);  // Delegate to real object
    }
}

docs.title: Step 4: Create Dynamic Proxy
docs.text:
public class Client {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        PaymentService realService = new RealPaymentService();

        PaymentService proxy = (PaymentService) Proxy.newProxyInstance(
            PaymentService.class.getClassLoader(),
            new Class[] { PaymentService.class },
            new LoggingHandler(realService)
        );

        proxy.pay(100);  // Logs + executes
    }
}

Output:

Log: Calling pay()  
Processing payment: $100

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
Type Use Case Implementation Static Proxy Lazy loading, access control Manual proxy class Dynamic Proxy Logging, AOP, caching InvocationHandler + Proxy.newProxyInstance()

Spring Uses Proxy For:

  • @Transactional (AOP proxies)
  • @Cacheable (Caching proxies)
  • Security (@PreAuthorize)

Interview Tip:

  • "Proxies add a layer of control without changing the original object’s code (Open/Closed Principle)."

Want a Spring-specific proxy example (e.g., @Transactional)? 😊

docs.title: 8.2. Proxy Design Pattern Spring boot usage
1. Spring AOP (Aspect-Oriented Programming)
docs.text:
Spring AOP heavily relies on the Proxy pattern to implement cross-cutting concerns.

Spring AOP is a framework that helps implement cross-cutting concerns (like logging, security, and performance monitoring) in a modular way.

It achieves this by creating proxies around your existing objects at runtime, allowing you to add behavior to them without directly modifying their code.

@Aspect
@Component
public class PerformanceAspect {

    @Around("execution(* com.example.service.*.*(..))")
    public Object measureExecutionTime(ProceedingJoinPoint joinPoint) throws Throwable {
        long start = System.currentTimeMillis();
        Object result = joinPoint.proceed();
        long executionTime = System.currentTimeMillis() - start;
        System.out.println(joinPoint.getSignature() + " executed in " + executionTime + "ms");
        return result;
    }
}

Spring AOP creates a proxy object at runtime for every bean that matches the pointcut. When you call a method on the original bean, Spring intercepts the call and invokes the proxy instead.

The proxy, in turn, executes the measureExecutionTime method (the advice) before and after the original method is called. This allows it to measure the execution time and log it, without any changes to the original service class code.

docs.title: 2. Lazy Loading in JPA
docs.text:
Spring Data JPA uses proxies for lazy loading of entity relationships.

@Entity
public class User {
    @Id
    private Long id;
    private String name;

    @OneToMany(fetch = FetchType.LAZY)
    private List<Order> orders;

    // getters and setters
}

Here, the orders collection is a proxy that loads the actual data only when accessed.

Lazy Loading in action in above code:

  1. Proxies: JPA utilizes proxies (dynamically generated subclasses) to implement lazy loading. In your example, the orders collection in the User entity is not an actual List when the User is initially loaded. Instead, it's a proxy object that stands in for the list.

  2. On-Demand Fetching: When you first access the orders collection (e.g., by calling user.getOrders()), the proxy object triggers a database query to fetch the actual orders for that specific user.

  3. Transparent Access: After the data is loaded, you can work with the orders collection like a regular List. Subsequent accesses to the collection don't require additional database queries, as the data is already cached in the proxy.

docs.title: 3. Caching
docs.text:
Spring's caching abstraction uses proxies to intercept method calls and manage cache operations.

@Service
public class UserService {

    @Cacheable("users")
    public User getUser(Long id) {
        // Expensive operation to fetch user
    }
}

Spring AOP  creates a proxy object around your annotated service bean (UserService in this case). This proxy intercepts method calls and adds caching behavior.

From the caller's perspective, they simply interact with the UserService as they normally would. The caching logic is completely transparent, handled behind the scenes by the proxy.

docs.title: 4. Transaction Management
docs.text:
Spring's declarative transaction management is implemented using proxies.

@Service
@Transactional
public class OrderService {

    public void placeOrder(Order order) {
        // Order processing logic
    }
}

The @Transactional annotation on the OrderService class (or specific methods) indicates that Spring should manage transactions for the methods within that scope.

Spring AOP (Aspect-Oriented Programming) dynamically creates a proxy object for the OrderService bean. This proxy intercepts calls to the annotated methods.

Transaction Handling: When a method like placeOrder is called:

  • Transaction Start: The proxy begins a new transaction (or participates in an existing one based on propagation settings).
  • Method Execution: The proxy invokes the actual placeOrder method on the original OrderService object.
  • Transaction Commit/Rollback: After the method completes, the proxy checks if the method executed successfully.
    • If successful: The proxy commits the transaction, making the changes permanent.
    • If an exception occurs: The proxy rolls back the transaction, reverting any changes made to the database.

The caller doesn't need to worry about the underlying transaction management details. They simply interact with the OrderService, and Spring's proxy handles the transactional aspect.

In above example , The proxy, created by Spring AOP, intercepts the placeOrder method call. It then:

  1. Starts a transaction.
  2. Calls the original placeOrder method in the OrderService.
  3. If the method completes successfully, it commits the transaction.
  4. If an exception is thrown, it rolls back the transaction.

The OrderService remains focused on the core business logic of placing orders, while the proxy handles the cross-cutting concern of transaction management. analogy: Think of the proxy like a middleman. You (the client) ask the middleman (the proxy) to place an order. The middleman takes care of all the transactional paperwork (starting the transaction, ensuring everything is in order, and finalizing the deal), while the actual order processing (the core business logic) happens elsewhere (in the OrderService).

docs.title: 5. Security
docs.text:
Spring Security provides a powerful framework for securing your application at various levels (web requests, authentication, etc.). Method-level security focuses on protecting individual methods within your service layer.

@Service
public class AdminService {

    @PreAuthorize("hasRole('ADMIN')")
    public void performAdminOperation() {
        // Admin operation
    }
}

The @PreAuthorize annotation is a Spring Security annotation, that you place on methods you want to secure. In the example, it specifies that only users with the "ADMIN" role can call the performAdminOperation method.

Spring AOP (Aspect-Oriented Programming) creates a proxy object for the AdminService bean. This proxy intercepts calls to the annotated methods.

When a secured method like performAdminOperation is called ,the proxy checks if the currently authenticated user (obtained from the Spring Security context) has the necessary authorization (in this case, the "ADMIN" role).

Again The caller doesn't need to explicitly write authorization logic. Spring Security's proxy handles the security checks transparently.

docs.title: 6. Remote Method Invocation
docs.text:
When working with remote services, Spring can use proxies to handle remote method invocation.

Feign is a declarative web service client that simplifies interaction with remote services. It allows you to define a client interface with annotated methods representing REST endpoints. Feign then generates a proxy implementation of this interface that handles the underlying HTTP communication details.

@FeignClient("user-service")
public interface UserClient {

    @GetMapping("/users/{id}")
    User getUser(@PathVariable("id") Long id);
}

we annotate class with @FeignClient and method with  @GetMapping to specify the details of the remote service and the HTTP method, path, and parameters for each endpoint.

Feign generates a proxy object at runtime that implements the UserClient interface. This proxy is responsible for:

  • Constructing the HTTP request based on the method call and annotations.
  • Sending the request to the remote service over the network.
  • Handling the response (deserialization, error handling).

Again From your application's perspective, you simply call methods on the UserClient interface as if it were a local object. Feign's proxy takes care of the remote invocation transparently.

docs.title: 7. Custom Proxy Implementation
docs.text:
public interface ExpensiveService {
    void performExpensiveOperation();
}

@Service
public class ExpensiveServiceImpl implements ExpensiveService {
    public void performExpensiveOperation() {
        // Expensive operation
    }
}

@Primary
@Service
public class ExpensiveServiceProxy implements ExpensiveService {

    private ExpensiveService expensiveService;

    public void performExpensiveOperation() {
        if (expensiveService == null) {
            expensiveService = new ExpensiveServiceImpl();
        }
        // Additional logic (e.g., logging, performance measurement)
        expensiveService.performExpensiveOperation();
    }
}

When a client component needs to use the ExpensiveService, they'll typically inject it through dependency injection (e.g., using @Autowired).

Because of the @Primary annotation, they'll receive the ExpensiveServiceProxy instance.

docs.title: 9.1 Chain of Responsibility Pattern
docs.text:
The Chain of Responsibility is a behavioral design pattern ==that lets you pass requests along a chain of handlers.==

Upon receiving a request, each handler decides either to process the request or to pass it to the next handler in the chain.

docs.title: When to Use It?
docs.text:
Use the Chain of Responsibility pattern when:

  1. When multiple handlers can process a request: This is useful in scenarios where the exact handler that will process the request is not known in advance.
  2. When you want to decouple the sender from the receiver: This allows for more flexible and maintainable code.
  3. When the order of processing is important: The chain can be configured to ensure that requests are processed in a specific sequence.
  4. The set of objects that can handle a request should be specified dynamically.(like in logging systems we specify if the message should be treated as error, debug or info).

docs.title: Common Real-Life Usages in Java
docs.text:
1. Event Handling in GUI Applications: In graphical user interfaces, events (like button clicks) are often propagated through a chain of components until one of them handles the event.

  2. Logging Frameworks: Different logging levels (info, debug, error) can be handled by different loggers in a chain, allowing for flexible logging configurations.

  3. Middleware in Web Applications: ==Middleware components can process HTTP requests in a chain, allowing for tasks like authentication, logging, and data validation to be handled sequentially.==

  4. Customer Support Systems: Different levels of support staff can handle customer inquiries based on their complexity, passing the request up the chain if they cannot resolve it.

docs.title: Key Components:
docs.text:
1. Handler: An interface defining a method for handling requests.
  2. Concrete Handlers: Classes implementing the Handler interface.
  3. Client: Initiates the request to the first handler in the chain.

docs.title: Key Components
docs.text:
1. LogHandler: An abstract class defining the structure for handling log messages.
  2. Concrete Handlers: ErrorLogHandler, FileLogHandler, and ConsoleLogHandler, each responsible for a specific log level.
  3. Client: Initiates the log request to the first handler in the chain.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. We create a chain of log handlers in order of decreasing severity: ERROR -> DEBUG -> INFO.
  2. When a log message comes in, it starts with the first handler (ErrorLogHandler).
  3. Each handler checks if it should handle the message based on the log level:
    • If the handler's level matches or is lower than the message's level, it processes the message and the chain stops.
    • If not, it passes the message to the next handler in the chain.
  4. This continues until an appropriate handler processes the message or the end of the chain is reached.

Handler Interface:

here handlers are linked together in chain , in each handler we will set next handler , in case current handler was not the intended one to process the request , it will pass the request next up in the chain of handlers, last handler will set the next handler as null

//logMessage() checks if the current handler is intended one ,if it is it will call the write() method to process otherwise it will call logMessage() method of next handler in chain

public abstract class LogHandler {
    protected LogHandler nextHandler;
    protected int level;

    public void setNextHandler(LogHandler nextHandler) {
        this.nextHandler = nextHandler;
    }

    public void logMessage(int level, String message) {
        if (this.level <= level) {
            write(message);
            return; // Stop here after handling the message
        }
        if (nextHandler != null) {
            nextHandler.logMessage(level, message);
        }
    }

    abstract protected void write(String message);
}

Concrete Handlers:

//Info log(console logs)
public class ConsoleLogHandler extends LogHandler {
    public ConsoleLogHandler(int level) {
        this.level = level;
    }

    @Override
    protected void write(String message) {
        System.out.println("Standard Console::Logger: " + message);
    }
}

//Debug logs (file level logs)
public class FileLogHandler extends LogHandler {
    public FileLogHandler(int level) {
        this.level = level;
    }

    @Override
    protected void write(String message) {
        System.out.println("File::Logger: " + message);
    }
}

//error logs
public class ErrorLogHandler extends LogHandler {
    public ErrorLogHandler(int level) {
        this.level = level;
    }

    @Override
    protected void write(String message) {
        System.out.println("Error Console::Logger: " + message);
    }
}

loggerchain object:

docs.title: 9.2 Chain of Responsibility design patten in sprin
1. Spring Security Filters
docs.text:
Spring Security is a prime example of the Chain of Responsibility pattern. When you configure security in your Spring Boot application, you're essentially setting up a chain of security filters.

// this is deprecated

@Configuration
@EnableWebSecurity
public class SecurityConfig extends WebSecurityConfigurerAdapter {

    @Override
    protected void configure(HttpSecurity http) throws Exception {
        http
            .addFilter(new JwtAuthenticationFilter())
            .addFilter(new JwtAuthorizationFilter())
            // ... other filters
    }
}

Each filter in the chain handles a specific security aspect (authentication, authorization, CSRF protection, etc.) and passes the request to the next filter if necessary.

docs.title: 2. Spring MVC Interceptors
docs.text:
HandlerInterceptors in Spring MVC also follow the Chain of Responsibility pattern. You can add multiple interceptors that process requests in a specific order.

@Configuration
public class WebConfig implements WebMvcConfigurer {

    @Override
    public void addInterceptors(InterceptorRegistry registry) {
        registry.addInterceptor(new LoggingInterceptor());
        registry.addInterceptor(new AuthenticationInterceptor());
        registry.addInterceptor(new AuditInterceptor());
    }
}

docs.title: 3. Exception Handling
docs.text:
Spring's @ControllerAdvice and exception handling mechanism can be seen as a form of Chain of Responsibility. Different exception handlers are checked in order until one that can handle the thrown exception is found.

@ControllerAdvice
public class GlobalExceptionHandler {

    @ExceptionHandler(ResourceNotFoundException.class)
    public ResponseEntity<ErrorResponse> handleResourceNotFoundException(ResourceNotFoundException ex) {
        // Handle the exception
    }

    @ExceptionHandler(ValidationException.class)
    public ResponseEntity<ErrorResponse> handleValidationException(ValidationException ex) {
        // Handle the exception
    }

    // More exception handlers...
}

docs.title: 4. Custom Middleware in Spring Boot Applications
docs.text:
You can implement your own Chain of Responsibility for processing requests or business logic. For example, a series of validators for a complex object:

public interface Validator<T> {
    void setNext(Validator<T> next);
    void validate(T request);
}

@Component
public class UserValidator implements Validator<User> {
    private Validator<User> next;

    @Override
    public void setNext(Validator<User> next) {
        this.next = next;
    }

    @Override
    public void validate(User user) {
        // Validate user
        if (next != null) {
            next.validate(user);
        }
    }
}

// Similar implementations for EmailValidator, PasswordValidator, etc.

@Service
public class UserService {
    private final Validator<User> validatorChain;

    public UserService(UserValidator userValidator, EmailValidator emailValidator, PasswordValidator passwordValidator) {
        this.validatorChain = userValidator;
        userValidator.setNext(emailValidator);
        emailValidator.setNext(passwordValidator);
    }

    public void registerUser(User user) {
        validatorChain.validate(user);
        // Proceed with registration if validation passes
    }
}

docs.title: Composition Over Inheritance:
docs.text: Composition over Inheritance is a design principle in object-oriented programming (OOP) that suggests using ==composition (combining simple objects to create more complex ones)== instead of inheritance (creating new classes by extending existing ones) for better flexibility and maintainability.

docs.title: 1. What is Inheritance?
docs.text:
Inheritance is when a child class (subclass) gets properties and methods from a parent class (superclass). Example:

class Animal {
    void eat() { System.out.println("Eating..."); }
}

class Dog extends Animal { }  // Dog inherits from Animal

Dog dog = new Dog();
dog.eat();  // Output: "Eating..."

Problem: Inheritance creates a tight coupling between classes. If Animal changes, Dog may break.

docs.title: 2. What is Composition?
docs.text:
Composition means building objects by combining other objects (like Lego blocks). Instead of inheriting behavior, a class contains another class. Example:

class Eater {
    void eat() { System.out.println("Eating..."); }
}

class Dog {
    private Eater eater = new Eater();  // Composition

    void eat() { eater.eat(); }  // Delegation
}
