Dog dog = new Dog();
dog.eat();  // Output: "Eating..."

Here, Dog has an Eater instead of being an Animal.

docs.title: 3. Why Composition is Better?
4. When to Use Inheritance?
docs.text:
Inheritance is still useful when:

  • There’s a clear "is-a" relationship (e.g., Dog is an Animal).
  • You need polymorphism (treating different subclasses the same way).

But prefer composition when:

  • You need flexibility (e.g., changing behavior at runtime).
  • You want reusable, modular code.

docs.title: 5. Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Composition = "Has-a" (A Car has an Engine).
  • Inheritance = "Is-a" (A Dog is an Animal).
  • Use composition to avoid deep inheritance hierarchies and tight coupling.
  • Favor interfaces + composition over big inheritance trees.

docs.title: Example in Real Life:
docs.text:
• Inheritance: A Tesla is a type of ElectricCar, which is a type of Car.
  • Composition: A Tesla has a Battery, Motor, and AutopilotSystem.

Composition makes it easier to swap the Battery or upgrade AutopilotSystem without rewriting the whole class.

docs.title: Final Thought
docs.text:
"Inheritance breaks encapsulation, composition preserves it." – Effective Java by Joshua Bloch

By using composition, you keep your code clean, flexible, and easy to maintain! 🚀

docs.title: SOLID Principles
docs.text: SOLID is an acronym for five design principles that work together to create more maintainable, understandable, and flexible code. These principles were introduced by Robert C. Martin (Uncle Bob).

docs.title: 1. Single Responsibility Principle (SRP)
docs.text:
Core idea: A class should have only one responsibility or job.

Example: Consider a UserManager class that handles both user authentication and sending email notifications. According to SRP, these are separate responsibilities that should be in different classes.

// Bad design (violates SRP)
public class UserManager {
    public void register(User user) {
        // Save user to database
        database.save(user);

        // Send welcome email
        String message = "Welcome " + user.getName();
        emailService.send(user.getEmail(), message);
    }
}

// Better design (follows SRP)
public class UserManager {
    private NotificationService notificationService;

    public UserManager(NotificationService notificationService) {
        this.notificationService = notificationService;
    }

    public void register(User user) {
        // Save user to database
        database.save(user);
        // Delegate notification to a specialized service
        notificationService.sendWelcomeMessage(user);
    }
}

public class NotificationService {
    public void sendWelcomeMessage(User user) {
        String message = "Welcome " + user.getName();
        emailService.send(user.getEmail(), message);
    }
}

docs.title: 2. Open/Closed Principle (OCP)
docs.text:
Core idea: Software entities should be open for extension but closed for modification.

You should be able to add new functionality without changing existing code.

Example: A payment processing system that needs to support different payment methods:

Benefits:

  • Adding new payment methods doesn't require changing existing code
  • Reduces risk of breaking working functionality
  • Makes the code more maintainable and extensible

docs.title: 3. Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP)
docs.text:
Core idea: Objects of a superclass should be replaceable with objects of its subclasses without affecting the correctness of the program.

In other words, ==a child class should be able to do everything its parent class can do.==

Good Design:

docs.title: 4. Interface Segregation Principle (ISP)
docs.text:
Core idea: Clients should not be forced to depend on interfaces they do not use.

==Many specific interfaces are better than one general-purpose interface. So break into smaller interfaces==

Example: Consider a multipurpose machine interface:

Benefits:

  • Classes only need to implement relevant methods
  • Reduces the impact of changes to unused methods
  • Creates more focused and cohesive interfaces

docs.title: 5. Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP)
docs.text:
Core idea: High-level modules should not depend on low-level modules.

Both should depend on abstractions.

Abstractions should not depend on details;

details should depend on abstractions.

Good Design

Benefits:

  • High-level modules are insulated from changes in low-level modules
  • Easier to test with mock implementations
  • More flexible system architecture
  • Supports dependency injection

docs.title: 3. DRY (Don t Repeat Yourself)
docs.text:
Core idea: Every piece of knowledge or logic should have a single, unambiguous representation within a system.

  • Easier maintenance (change logic in one place)
  • Reduces bugs (fixes apply everywhere)
  • Results in more concise code
  • Improves consistency

docs.title: 4. KISS (Keep It Simple, Stupid)
docs.text:
Core idea: Systems work best when they are kept simple rather than made complex. Simplicity should be a key goal in design, and unnecessary complexity should be avoided.

Benefits:

  • Easier to understand and maintain
  • Fewer bugs and edge cases
  • Lower cognitive load for developers
  • Often results in better performance

docs.title: 5. YAGNI (You Aren t Gonna Need It)
docs.text: Core idea: Don't add functionality until it's actually necessary. Avoid speculative features that aren't currently required.

docs.title: Separation of Concerns (SoC)
docs.text:
Core idea: Different aspects of a program should be handled by distinct and minimally overlapping modules.

Example: In a web application, separation might include:

  • Data access layer (repositories)
  • Business logic layer (services)
  • Presentation layer (controllers)
  • Cross-cutting concerns (logging, security)

docs.title: 1. Singleton Pattern
docs.text:
Core Idea:

  • Ensures one instance per application (e.g., database connection).
  • Spring Twist: Spring beans are singletons by default, but scoped to the IoC container (not JVM-wide).

Code Example:

public class Database {
    private static Database instance;

    private Database() {}  // Private constructor

    public static Database getInstance() {
        if (instance == null) {
            instance = new Database();  // Lazy initialization
        }
        return instance;
    }
}

How to Break?

  • Reflection Attack:

    Constructor<Database> constructor = Database.class.getDeclaredConstructor();
constructor.setAccessible(true);
Database hackedInstance = constructor.newInstance();  // New instance!

    Fix: Use enum or throw exception in private constructor.

Spring Use Case:

@Bean  // Singleton by default
public DataSource dataSource() {
    return new HikariDataSource();
}

docs.title: 2. Strategy Pattern
docs.text:
Core Idea:

  • Switch algorithms dynamically (e.g., payment methods).
  • Spring Twist: Leverage @Qualifier to inject different strategies.

Code Example:

interface PaymentStrategy {
    void pay(int amount);
}

class CreditCard implements PaymentStrategy {
    @Override
    public void pay(int amount) {
        System.out.println("Paid " + amount + " via Credit Card");
    }
}

class PaymentService {
    private PaymentStrategy strategy;

    public PaymentService(PaymentStrategy strategy) {
        this.strategy = strategy;  // Injected strategy
    }

    void processPayment(int amount) {
        strategy.pay(amount);
    }
}

Spring Use Case:

@Autowired
public PaymentService(@Qualifier("creditCard") PaymentStrategy strategy) {
    this.strategy = strategy;
}

docs.title: 3. Observer Pattern
docs.text:
Core Idea:

  • Pub-Sub model (e.g., event notifications).
  • Spring Twist: Built-in ApplicationEventPublisher.

Code Example:

class OrderEvent extends ApplicationEvent {
    public OrderEvent(String message) {
        super(message);
    }
}

@Service
class OrderService {
    @Autowired
    private ApplicationEventPublisher eventPublisher;

    void placeOrder() {
        eventPublisher.publishEvent(new OrderEvent("Order placed!"));
    }
}

@Component
class NotificationListener {
    @EventListener
    public void handleOrderEvent(OrderEvent event) {
        System.out.println("Received: " + event.getSource());
    }
}

docs.title: 4. Facade Pattern
docs.text:
Core Idea:

  • Simplify complex subsystems (e.g., microservices aggregation).

Code Example:

class ShippingFacade {
    private InventoryService inventory;
    private LogisticsService logistics;

    void shipProduct(String productId) {
        if (inventory.isAvailable(productId)) {
            logistics.scheduleDelivery(productId);
        }
    }
}

Spring Use Case:

@RestController
class OrderController {
    @Autowired
    private OrderFacade orderFacade;  // Hides order-processing complexity

    @PostMapping("/order")
    void createOrder() {
        orderFacade.processOrder();
    }
}

docs.title: 5. Factory Pattern
docs.text:
Core Idea:

  • Centralize object creation (e.g., different response formats).

Code Example:

class ResponseFactory {
    static Response create(String type) {
        return switch (type) {
            case "JSON" -> new JsonResponse();
            case "XML" -> new XmlResponse();
            default -> throw new IllegalArgumentException("Invalid type");
        };
    }
}

Spring Use Case:

@Bean
public RestTemplate restTemplate() {
    return new RestTemplateBuilder().build();  // Factory method
}

docs.title: Key Interview Insights
docs.text:
1. Singleton:
    • "Spring singletons are per-container, not per-JVM. Use @Scope("prototype") for non-singletons."
  2. Strategy:
    • "Avoid conditional logic (if-else) by delegating to strategies."
  3. Observer:
    • "Spring’s @EventListener is a lightweight observer implementation."
  4. Facade:
    • "Microservices often use API Gateways as facades."

docs.title: Step 1: Rough Idea and Core Requirements
docs.text:
we’re in a building, and people need to move between floors. They press buttons on their floor to call an elevator, step inside, pick their destination, and off they go.

So we can say key players would be Building with floors, elevators to ride in and buttons to call the elevator on the floor

Basic Flow : Someone presses button on a floor (up or down) an elevator shows up doors open , people hop in press floor number button they want to go to and elevator takes them there.

Let’s pick our first object: the Building. It’s the big boss holding everything together—floors and elevators live inside it.

docs.title: Step 2: Adding Floors
docs.text: Okay, a building’s nothing without floors. Let’s add Floor to the mix. Each floor has a number (like 0 for ground, 1, 2, etc.) and buttons for calling an elevator—up, down, or both, depending on where it is

docs.title: Updated Requirements:
docs.text:
• Floors have numbers and buttons.
  • Button presses signal a request (e.g., “I’m on floor 3, going up!”).

docs.title: Step 3: Bringing in the Elevators
docs.text: Now, lets add ElevatorCar, It’s got a current floor (where it’s at), a direction (up, down, or chilling as idle), and it needs to know where to stop next

docs.title: Updated Requirements:
docs.text:
• Elevators move between floors, have a location, and a direction.
  • They respond to requests (we’ll figure out how in a bit).

docs.title: New Objects:
docs.text:
• ElevatorCar: The elevator itself—current floor and direction.
  • Direction: Enum with UP, DOWN, IDLE.

docs.title: Step 4: Controlling the Chaos with ElevatorController
docs.text:
When a floor button’s pressed, something needs to decide which elevator goes where.

Let’s add ElevatorController to boss each ElevatorCar around. It’ll track requests (like “stop at floor 5”) and tell the car to move, stop, or chill.

docs.title: Updated Requirements:
docs.text:
• Each elevator has a brain to manage its movement.
  • It handles a list of floors to visit based on requests.

docs.title: New Object:
docs.text: • ElevatorController: Controls one ElevatorCar, managing its stops and movement.

docs.title: Relationships:
docs.text:
• ElevatorCar has one ElevatorController (composition again).
  • ElevatorController controls ElevatorCar.

docs.title: Step 5: Dispatching External Requests
docs.text: Now, when someone presses a floor button, who decides which elevator answers the call? Enter ExternalDispatcher! It’s like the building’s air traffic controller, picking the best ElevatorController for the job based on where elevators are and what they’re doing.

docs.title: Updated Requirements:
docs.text:
• Floor button presses go to a central system.
  • That system assigns the request to an elevator.

docs.title: New Object:
docs.text: • ExternalDispatcher: Takes floor requests and assigns them to an ElevatorController.

docs.title: Relationships:
docs.text:
• Floor talks to ExternalDispatcher (association).
  • ExternalDispatcher manages many ElevatorControllers.
  • Building has one ExternalDispatcher.

docs.title: Step 6: Inside the Elevator—InternalButtons
docs.text: Once people step into the elevator, they need to pick their floors. Let’s add InternalButtons so passengers can tell the elevator where to go. These buttons talk directly to the ElevatorController to add destination stops.

docs.title: Updated Requirements:
docs.text:
• Passengers inside the elevator request floors.
  • Those requests go to the elevator’s controller.

docs.title: New Object:
docs.text: • InternalButtons: The panel inside the elevator for picking floors.

docs.title: Relationships:
docs.text:
• ElevatorCar has InternalButtons.
  • InternalButtons sends requests to ElevatorController.

Added InternalButtons to ElevatorCar and a new set internalStops in ElevatorController for destinations.

docs.title: Step 7: Doors and Displays
docs.text: Let’s make this elevator feel real with ElevatorDoor and ElevatorDisplay. The door opens and closes , and the display shows the current floor and direction.

docs.title: Updated Requirements:
docs.text:
• Elevators have doors that open/close.
  • A display shows where the elevator is and where it’s headed.

docs.title: New Objects:
docs.text:
• ElevatorDoor: Manages door state.
  • ElevatorDisplay: Shows floor and direction.

docs.title: Relationships:
docs.text:
• ElevatorCar has ElevatorDoor and ElevatorDisplay (composition).

ElevatorDoor and ElevatorDisplay are snug inside ElevatorCar. We added methods to ElevatorCar that the controller can call

docs.title: How It All Works Together
docs.text:
Imagine this: You’re on floor 3, press the up button. Floor tells ExternalDispatcher, which picks an ElevatorController. That controller adds floor 3 to upStops.

The ElevatorCar moves up, stops, opens its ElevatorDoor, and updates the ElevatorDisplay.

You step in, hit floor 7 on the InternalButtons, and the controller adds 7 to internalStops.

docs.title: Rough Core Requirements
docs.text:
A board with numbered cells (typically 1-100) - Players taking turns rolling dice and moving - Snakes that send players backward - Ladders that move players forward - First player to reach the final cell wins

The key components would be: Game, Board, Players, Dice, Snakes, Ladders, and Cells.

Basic Flow: A player rolls a dice, moves their piece, checks if they landed on a snake or ladder, and adjusts position accordingly. Players take turns until someone reaches the final cell.

docs.title: Game
docs.text:
• Fields: Board, List of Players, Dice, currentPlayerIndex
  • Methods:
    • initializeGame(): Sets up the board with snakes, ladders, and players
    • movePlayer(): Updates player position based on dice roll and handles snake/ladder effects
    • isGameOver(): Checks if any player has reached the final position
    • nextTurn(): Advances to the next player in the list

docs.title: Board
docs.text:
• Fields: size, Map of position to Cell
  • Methods:
    • initialize(): Creates all cells for the board
    • addSnake(head, tail): Creates a snake from head position to tail position
    • addLadder(start, end): Creates a ladder from start position to end position
    • getCell(position): Returns the cell at a given position

docs.title: Cell
docs.text:
• Fields: position, nextPosition (same as position unless it's a snake or ladder)
  • Methods:
    • getPosition(): Returns the cell's position on the board
    • getNextPosition(): Returns where a player should end up after landing on this cell
    • isSnake()/isLadder(): Checks if this cell is a snake head or ladder start

docs.title: Player
docs.text:
• Fields: name, position
  • Methods:
    • move(steps): Updates the player's position by given number of steps
    • hasWon(boardSize): Checks if player has reached the final position

docs.title: Dice
docs.text:
• Fields: numFaces
  • Methods:
    • roll(): Returns a random number between 1 and numFaces

docs.title: Design Patterns Used
docs.text:
1. Factory Pattern (GameFactory):
    • Creates preconfigured game instances
    • Encapsulates the complex initialization logic for board setup
    • Makes it easy to create standard or custom game configurations
  2. Optional - Singleton Pattern (for Game):
    • Could ensure only one game instance exists
    • Useful if extending to networked gameplay

docs.title: 3.0 Vending Machine (general overview)
docs.text:
What does it DO?

    • Sells items (snacks, drinks).
    • Accepts money (coins, maybe notes/cards later).
    • Lets users choose an item.
    • Gives the item if paid correctly.
    • Gives back change if needed.
    • Tells the user what's happening (display messages).
    • Needs to know when it's out of stock.

  • What physical/logical PARTS does it need?

    • The Machine itself: The main controller.
    • Items: Things to sell (Cola, Chips, Candy). Each has a name, price, and maybe a code (like "A1", "B2").
    • Storage/Inventory: Shelves or slots holding the items. Needs to track how many of each item are left.
    • Money Box: Place to accept and store money. Needs to track the current amount inserted for a transaction. Maybe also total money collected.
    • Selection Panel: Buttons or keypad for the user to enter the item code.
    • Dispenser: The slot where the item drops out.
    • Change Return: The slot where extra money comes back.
    • Display Screen: To show price, messages ("Insert Coin", "Out of Stock").
    • Internal State: What is the machine currently doing? Is it waiting for someone? Waiting for money? Dispensing?

    • What ACTIONS can happen?

    • User selects an item.

    • User inserts money.
    • Machine dispenses an item.
    • Machine returns change.
    • User cancels the transaction.
    • Admin restocks items.
    • Admin collects money.

    • How do these connect?

    • The Machine contains the Inventory.

    • The Machine uses the Money Box (or logic for handling money).
    • The Machine reacts to Selection Panel input.
    • The Machine's behaviour depends heavily on its Internal State.
    • Selecting an item (if available) changes the State to "waiting for money".
    • Inserting enough money changes the State to "ready to dispense".
    • Dispensing changes the Inventory count and changes the State back to "idle" (after giving change).

Let's refine the parts into potential classes and their responsibilities.

  1. VendingMachine (The Main Class):

    • Responsibility: Orchestrates the whole process. Knows the current state, manages transitions between states. Holds references to other major components.
    • Potential Fields: currentState (an object representing the state), inventory (an object managing items), currentBalance (money inserted for this transaction), selectedItem (the item the user picked).
    • Potential Methods: selectItem(code), insertMoney(amount), dispenseItem(), cancelTransaction(), getChange().

    • Item:

    • Responsibility: Represents a product being sold.

    • Potential Fields: code (String, e.g., "A1"), name (String, e.g., "Cola"), price (double or int).

    • Inventory:

    • Responsibility: Manages the stock of items.

    • Potential Fields: A way to store items and their counts (e.g., a Map<Item, Integer> or Map<String, Pair<Item, Integer>> where the String is the item code).
    • Potential Methods: getItem(code), getQuantity(code), isAvailable(code), deductItem(code), addItem(item, quantity).

    • State (The State Pattern): This is a crucial design pattern for vending machines because their behaviour changes drastically based on what just happened.

    • Responsibility: Define an interface for actions. Each specific state (Idle, Accepting Money, Dispensing) will implement this interface differently.

    • Potential Methods (in the interface): selectItem(vendingMachine, code), insertMoney(vendingMachine, amount), dispenseItem(vendingMachine), cancelTransaction(vendingMachine).
    • Concrete State Classes:
      • IdleState: Allows item selection. Inserting money might be ignored or initiate selection.
      • HasSelectionState (or AcceptingMoneyState): An item has been selected. Allows money insertion. Allows cancellation. Might allow changing selection.
      • DispensingState: Item is being dispensed. No other actions allowed until done. Calculates change.
      • SoldOutState: The selected item is out of stock. Maybe allows selecting another item or cancelling.
      • NoStockState: The whole machine is empty (less common for LLD focus, but possible).

docs.title: 3.1 Vending Machine  Design
docs.text:
Core Classes:

  • VendingMachine: Controls the overall system as a Singleton
  • Item: Represents individual products with price and code
  • ItemSlot: Manages product quantity at specific locations
  • Inventory: Tracks all items and their availability
  • PaymentProcessor: Handles money operations
  • State: Manages different operational modes

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
A ticket booking system needs to:

  • Allow users to browse and select events (e.g., movies, concerts).
  • Enable seat selection and booking.
  • Process payments securely.
  • Manage bookings and cancellations.
  • Prevent double-booking of seats (handle concurrency).

docs.title: Key Classes
docs.text:
Here are the main classes we'll design:

  • Event: Represents an event (e.g., a movie or concert).
  • Venue: The physical location hosting events, containing multiple screens.
  • Screen: A specific hall or stage within the venue.
  • Seat: Individual seats that users can book.
  • User: The person making the booking.
  • Booking: A user's reservation for specific seats.
  • Payment: Manages payment transactions.

To manage these effectively, we’ll need:

  • EventFactory: A factory to create different types of events.
  • BookingManager: A singleton to centralize booking operations and handle concurrency.

docs.title: Class Relationships
docs.text:
The relationships between these classes are:

  • Venue has many Screens (1-to-many).
  • Screen has many Seats (1-to-many).
  • Event is associated with one Screen (1-to-1).
  • User can have many Bookings (1-to-many).
  • Booking is associated with one Event, many Seats, and one Payment (1-to-1, 1-to-many, 1-to-1).
  • EventFactory creates Events.
  • BookingManager manages Bookings.

docs.title: ==Event== (Abstract/Base Class)
docs.text:
• Represents a generic event, serving as the base for specific event types (e.g., movies, concerts).
  • Attributes:
    • eventId: String - Unique identifier for the event.
    • name: String - Event name (e.g., "Inception").
    • date: Date - Date and time of the event.
    • screen: Screen - The screen hosting the event.
  • Methods:
    • getAvailableSeats(): List<Seat> - Returns a list of unbooked seats for the event.

docs.title: ==MovieEvent== (Extends Event)
docs.text:
• Represents a movie event, inheriting from Event.
  • Attributes:
    • director: String - Director of the movie.

docs.title: ==ConcertEvent== (Extends Event)
docs.text:
• Purpose: Represents a concert event, inheriting from Event.
  • Attributes:
    • performers: List<String> - List of performers in the concert.

docs.title: ==EventFactory== (Factory Pattern)
docs.text:
• Purpose: Encapsulates the creation of Event objects (e.g., MovieEvent, ConcertEvent) to hide instantiation logic and make the system extensible.
  • Methods:
    • createMovieEvent(name: String, date: Date, screen: Screen): MovieEvent - Creates a MovieEvent.
    • createConcertEvent(name: String, date: Date, screen: Screen): ConcertEvent - Creates a ConcertEvent.

docs.title: ==Venue==
docs.text:
• Represents a physical location (e.g., a multiplex) hosting events.
  • Attributes:
    • venueId: Unique identifier (String)
    • name: Venue name (String)
    • address: Venue location (String)
    • screens: List of screens (List<Screen>)
  • Methods:
    • addScreen(screen): Adds a screen to the venue
    • getScreens(): Returns the list of screens

docs.title: ==Screen==
docs.text:
• Represents a specific hall or stage within a venue.
  • Attributes:
    • screenId: Unique identifier (String)
    • name: Screen name (e.g., "Screen 1") (String)
    • seats: List of seats (List<Seat>)
  • Methods:
    • addSeat(seat): Adds a seat to the screen
    • getSeats(): Returns the list of seats

docs.title: ==Seat==
docs.text:
• Represents an individual seat that can be booked.
  • Attributes:
    • seatId: Unique identifier (String)
    • row: Row number (int)
    • number: Seat number in the row (int)
    • isBooked: Booking status (boolean)
    • version: Version number for concurrency control (int)
  • Methods:
    • bookSeat(): Marks the seat as booked and increments the version.
    • cancelBooking(): Marks the seat as available and increments the version.

docs.title: ==User==
docs.text:
• Represents a user booking tickets.
  • Attributes:
    • userId: Unique identifier (String)
    • name: User's name (String)
    • email: User's email (String)
  • Methods:
    • makeBooking(booking): Creates a new booking
    • cancelBooking(booking): Cancels an existing booking

docs.title: ==Booking==
docs.text:
• Represents a user’s reservation for an event.
  • Attributes:
    • bookingId: Unique identifier (String)
    • user: The user who made the booking (User)
    • event: The booked event (Event)
    • seats: List of booked seats (List<Seat>)
    • payment: Associated payment (Payment)
    • status: Booking status (e.g., "confirmed", "cancelled") (String)
  • Methods:
    • confirmBooking(): Confirms the booking post-payment
    • cancelBooking(): Cancels the booking and releases seats

docs.title: ==Payment==
docs.text:
• Manages payment transactions.
  • Attributes:
    • paymentId: Unique identifier (String)
    • amount: Payment amount (double)
    • status: Payment status (e.g., "pending", "completed") (String)
  • Methods:
    • processPayment(): Processes the payment
    • refundPayment(): Refunds the payment if cancelled

BookingManager

  • Manages all booking-related operations across the system, ensuring thread safety and consistency.
  • Attributes:
    • instance: BookingManager - Static reference to the single instance (private).
  • Methods:
    • getInstance(): BookingManager (Static)
      • Returns the single instance of BookingManager, creating it if it doesn’t exist.
    • bookSeats(booking: Booking): boolean
      • Attempts to book the seats specified in the Booking object. Returns true if successful, false otherwise (e.g., if seats are unavailable).
    • cancelBooking(booking: Booking): void
      • Cancels the specified booking, releasing the associated seats.

docs.title: Concurrency Control
docs.text:
To prevent double-booking, we use optimistic locking on the Seat class via the version attribute:

  • When a user tries to book a seat, the system checks the seat’s current version against the database.
  • If they match, the booking proceeds, the seat is marked as booked, and the version increments.
  • If they don’t match (another user booked it), the booking fails, and the user is prompted to select different seats.

Optimistic Locking is a technique used to prevent such race conditions. It assumes that conflicts (like double-booking) are rare but still possible. Instead of locking resources upfront (as in Pessimistic Locking ), it allows operations to proceed normally but checks for conflicts at the last moment before committing changes.

docs.title: General Flow
docs.text:
Here’s how the system works in a typical ticket booking scenario (happy path):

  1. Browse Events:
    • The user browses available events (e.g., movies, concerts).
    • The system retrieves Event objects, created by EventFactory (e.g., EventFactory.createMovieEvent("Inception", date, screen)).
  2. Select Event:
    • The user selects an event, and the system calls event.getAvailableSeats() to display unbooked seats for the associated Screen.
  3. Select Seats:
    • The user chooses seats from the list. The system temporarily holds these seats to prevent other users from booking them (e.g., using a timeout mechanism).
  4. Create Booking:
    • A Booking object is created with the User, Event, selected Seats, and a Payment in "Pending" status.
    • The user initiates the booking via user.makeBooking(booking).
  5. Book Seats:
    • The BookingManager (accessed via BookingManager.getInstance()) handles the booking process.
    • BookingManager.bookSeats(booking) checks each seat’s version to ensure it’s not booked by another user (optimistic locking).
    • If all seats are available, each seat’s bookSeat() method is called, marking it as booked and incrementing its version.
  6. Process Payment:
    • The Payment object’s processPayment() method is invoked to process the payment (e.g., via a payment gateway).
    • If the payment succeeds, the Payment status updates to "Completed".
  7. Confirm Booking:
    • On successful payment, booking.confirmBooking() updates the Booking status to "Confirmed".
    • The user receives a confirmation (e.g., via email).
  8. Handle Concurrency:
    • If another user tries to book the same seat simultaneously, BookingManager detects a version mismatch during bookSeats(), and the booking fails.
    • The user is prompted to select different seats.
  9. Cancel Booking (Optional):
    • If the user cancels, user.cancelBooking(booking) calls BookingManager.cancelBooking(booking).
    • The Booking status is set to "Cancelled", payment.refundPayment() is called, and each seat’s cancelBooking() method releases it, incrementing the version.

docs.title: Factory Pattern
docs.text:
• Used to create Event objects (e.g., MovieEvent, ConcertEvent) without exposing instantiation logic.
  • Example: EventFactory with methods like createMovieEvent().

docs.title: Singleton Pattern
docs.text: • A BookingManager class (not detailed here) could be a singleton to centralize booking coordination.

docs.title: Observer Pattern
docs.text: • Optionally, notify users of booking updates (e.g., via email/SMS). We'll keep this as an extension for simplicity.

docs.title: 1. Service Discovery
docs.text:
In a microservices architecture, services are dynamic entities. They can be deployed, scaled, or even terminated based on demand and load. ==Service discovery is the mechanism that enables services to locate and communicate with each other without hardcoding network locations.(like ips )==

Why Service Discovery is Crucial:

  • Dynamic Environments: Microservices often run in cloud environments where instances come and go. Hardcoding IP addresses and ports is impractical and error-prone.
  • Scalability: Service discovery handles automatic registration and deregistration of instances as services scale up or down.
  • Resilience: If a service instance fails, service discovery helps clients quickly route traffic to healthy instances, minimizing downtime.

beyond eureka ,consul, zookeper, there is spring cloud kubernetes service discovery

Kubernetes Service Discovery:

Kubernetes provides a powerful, native service discovery mechanism that simplifies communication between pods and services within a cluster. It abstracts away the complexities of IP addresses and ports, allowing you to focus on the logical names of your services.

Key Concepts:

  1. Pods: The smallest deployable unit in Kubernetes, ==representing one or more containers that work together.== Pods are ephemeral, meaning they can be created or destroyed dynamically.
  2. Services: ==An abstraction that defines a logical set of pods and a policy by which to access them.== ==Services provide a stable network endpoint for accessing a group of pods, even as individual pods come and go.==
  3. Endpoints: The actual network addresses (IP addresses and ports) of the pods that make up a service.
  4. Service Selectors: Labels used to identify and group pods that belong to a particular service.

How Kubernetes Service Discovery Works:

  1. Service Creation: When you create a Service in Kubernetes, it is assigned a stable IP address and DNS name within the cluster's internal network.
  2. Pod Selection: The Service uses selectors to identify the pods that match its criteria (based on labels).
  3. Endpoint Maintenance: Kubernetes automatically updates the Service's endpoints with the IP addresses and ports of the selected pods.
  4. Service Access: Other pods within the cluster can access the Service using its stable IP address or DNS name. Kubernetes automatically load balances requests to the underlying pods.
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: my-service
spec:
  selector:
    app: my-app
  ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 80
      targetPort: 8080

In this example:

  • The Service my-service will be assigned a stable IP address and a DNS name like my-service.default.svc.cluster.local.
  • It will select pods with the label app: my-app.
  • It will expose those pods on port 80, forwarding traffic to port 8080 on the pods.

Key Benefits of Kubernetes Service Discovery:

  • Automatic Load Balancing: Kubernetes distributes traffic across pods evenly.
  • Self-Healing: If a pod fails, Kubernetes automatically removes it from the Service's endpoints and routes traffic to healthy pods.
  • DNS-Based Access: Services can be accessed using human-readable DNS names, making communication between services more intuitive.

Example:

@Autowired
private RestTemplate restTemplate;

public String getProductInfo() {
    String url = "http://product-service/products"; // Service name as URL
    return restTemplate.getForObject(url, String.class);
}

Without Kubernetes Service Discovery:

In a non-Kubernetes environment, or if you chose not to use Kubernetes' built-in service discovery, you'd typically face these challenges:

  1. Hardcoded Endpoints: You would need to know the exact IP address and port of the target service, which can be problematic in dynamic environments where instances change frequently.
  2. Manual Updates: If the target service's IP address or port changes, you would need to manually update the configuration in your client service, leading to potential errors and downtime.
  3. No Load Balancing: You would need to implement your own load-balancing mechanism if you have multiple instances of the target service.
@Autowired
private RestTemplate restTemplate;

public String getProductInfo() {
    String url = "http://192.168.1.10:8080/products"; // Hardcoded IP and port
    return restTemplate.getForObject(url, String.class);
}

In this example, the IP address and port of the product-service are hardcoded, making it difficult to scale or handle changes in the environment.

But if we have configured kubernetes service discovery , refering to first example Behind the scenes, Kubernetes resolves the product-service name to the actual IP address and port of a healthy pod, ensuring your request reaches the right destination. It also handles load balancing automatically if you have multiple instances of the product-service.

Additional Configurations:

While Kubernetes service discovery simplifies the process, you might still need some additional configurations in your Spring Boot application:

  • Enable Discovery Client: Annotate your application with @EnableDiscoveryClient to enable Spring Cloud's integration with Kubernetes service discovery.
  • Load Balanced RestTemplate: Annotate your RestTemplate with @LoadBalanced to enable client-side load balancing, distributing requests across multiple instances of the target service.

@SpringBootApplication
@EnableDiscoveryClient
public class MyApplication {
    @Bean
    @LoadBalanced
    public RestTemplate restTemplate() {
        return new RestTemplate();
    }
}

docs.title: 2. Circuit breaker pattern
docs.text:
The circuit breaker pattern helps microservices handle failures more gracefully.

It detects when a service fails and stops calling it for a while, giving it time to recover. This way, the whole application doesn't get bogged down by repeated failures, improving overall reliability.

Basic request flow :

  • Client sends a request to create an order to the Order service.
  • Order service processes the order and calls the Payment service to handle the payment.
  • Payment service processes the payment and returns the result.
  • Order service completes the order creation based on the payment result.

Problems in Microservices Communication

docs.title: 1. Immediate Failures
docs.text:
If the Payment service is down or unreachable, the Order service will receive an immediate failure response. This can lead to a poor user experience if not handled properly.

We can catch exceptions when the Payment microservice fails and handle them gracefully. For example:

try {
    paymentService.processPayment(order);
} catch (PaymentException e) {
    // Handle payment failure
}

docs.title: 2. Timeout Failures
docs.text: Sometimes, the Payment service might be slow to respond, causing a timeout in the Order service. This ties up resources in the Order service and can lead to degraded performance.

docs.title: 3. Cascading Failures
docs.text: If the Payment service is consistently failing or timing out, it can lead to a cascading failure where the Order service also becomes unresponsive due to exhausted resources.

docs.title: 4. Overhead of Remote Calls
docs.text:
Each call to the Payment service incurs network overhead. Making repeated calls to a failing service wastes resources and degrades overall system performance.

We can use an interceptor to wrap all requests with the circuit breaker pattern. This interceptor can catch and handle exceptions.

docs.title: Circuit Breaker Pattern
docs.text:
The Circuit Breaker pattern ==addresses these issues by wrapping remote service calls and monitoring their behavior==. It has three states:

  1. Closed: Normal operation, requests pass through to the remote service.
  2. Open: Requests fail fast without calling the remote service.
  3. Half-Open: A limited number of requests are allowed through to test if the remote service has recovered. the circuit breaker is allowing a limited number of "test" requests to pass through to the Payment Service to determine if the service has recovered.

docs.title: How it works:
docs.text:
1. The circuit starts in the Closed state.
  2. If failures exceed a threshold, the circuit Opens.(causing break in flow)
  3. In the Open state, requests fail fast without calling the remote service.
  4. After a timeout, the circuit moves to Half-Open state.
  5. In Half-Open state, a limited number of requests are allowed through.
  6. If these requests succeed, the circuit Closes; if they fail, it Opens again.

Implementing Circuit Breaker with Resilience4j 

import io.github.resilience4j.circuitbreaker.annotation.CircuitBreaker;

@Service
public class OrderService {

    @CircuitBreaker(name = "paymentService", fallbackMethod = "paymentFallback")
    public String placeOrder(Order order) {
        return paymentService.processPayment(order);
    }

    public String paymentFallback(Order order, Throwable t) {
        // Handle fallback logic
        return "Payment service is currently unavailable. Please try again later.";
    }
}

Resilience4j uses the Decorator pattern to wrap the target method with the circuit breaker functionality. This allows for easy configuration and customization of the circuit breaker behavior.

docs.title: Custom Configuration
docs.text:
You can customize the Circuit Breaker behavior in your application.yml: 

resilience4j.circuitbreaker:
  instances:
    paymentService:
      registerHealthIndicator: true
      slidingWindowSize: 10
      minimumNumberOfCalls: 5
      permittedNumberOfCallsInHalfOpenState: 3
      waitDurationInOpenState: 10000
      failureRateThreshold: 50

or we can also customize in java level also 

@CircuitBreakerConfiguration(name = "paymentService")
public class PaymentCircuitBreakerConfiguration {

    @Bean
    public CircuitBreakerConfig circuitBreakerConfig() {
        return CircuitBreakerConfig.custom()
                .failureRateThreshold(50)
                .waitDurationInOpenState(Duration.ofSeconds(30))
                .permittedNumberOfCallsInHalfOpenState(2)
                .build();
    }
}

This java based configuration sets the failure rate threshold to 50%, the open state duration to 30 seconds, and allows 2 "test" requests in the half-open state.

Request flow using circuit breaker:

  1. The client sends a request to the Order Service.
  2. The Order Service invokes the Payment Service through the Circuit Breaker.
  3. If the Payment Service is available, the request is processed normally.
  4. If the Payment Service is unavailable or exhibiting high latency, the Circuit Breaker trips and fails the request immediately, without waiting for a response.
  5. The fallback method is called to provide a default response, such as returning a default payment status or offering an alternative payment method.

docs.title: 6. Understanding TypeReference in Jackson
docs.text: When deserializing JSON into Java objects using Jackson, you often encounter a common challenge related to Java's type erasure. Java's type system doesn't retain information about generic types at runtime. This means that while you can have a class like List<Customer>, at runtime, the JVM only sees a List, with no information about the Customer part. This is known as type erasure.

docs.title: The Problem:
docs.text:
• When you try to deserialize a JSON array into a specific Java collection type (like a List<Customer>), Jackson needs to know the exact generic type to correctly instantiate and populate the collection.
  • Due to type erasure, List<Customer>.class isn't a valid expression, and simply using List.class doesn't provide Jackson with enough information about the Customer type.

docs.title: The Solution: TypeReference
docs.text:
• Jackson provides TypeReference as a way to work around the limitations of type erasure.
  • TypeReference is an abstract class that you extend with an empty anonymous class, providing Jackson with the necessary generic type information.

docs.title: How TypeReference Works:
docs.text:
• When you write new TypeReference<List<Customer>>(){}, you're creating an anonymous subclass of TypeReference. This subclass captures and retains the generic type information (List<Customer>) at runtime.
  • Jackson uses this type information to correctly deserialize the JSON into a list of Customer objects, fully aware of the Customer type contained within the list.

docs.title: Usage Example:
docs.text:
java

ObjectMapper objectMapper = new ObjectMapper(); List<Customer> customers = objectMapper.readValue(jsonInput, new TypeReference<List<Customer>>(){});

  • In this example, jsonInput could be a String, File, InputStream, etc., containing the JSON data.
  • The objectMapper.readValue method uses the TypeReference to understand that it should deserialize the JSON into a List of Customer objects.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
• TypeReference is a powerful tool provided by Jackson to preserve generic type information that would otherwise be lost due to type erasure.
  • It enables precise deserialization of generic types, such as collections of specific object types, by providing Jackson with the necessary type information at runtime.
  • This approach is essential when working with generic collections in Java and ensures that deserialization processes are type-safe and accurate.

docs.title: 1. The Data Pipeline Challenges Before Kafka
Problem 1: Message Queues (e.g., RabbitMQ, ActiveMQ)
docs.text:
How They Work:

  • Single queue holds messages
  • Competing consumers: Only one consumer gets each message
  • Messages deleted after consumption

Limitations:

  1. No Replayability
    • Example: If the Analytics service crashes, it loses messages processed while down.
  2. Single Consumer Pattern
    • Cannot broadcast the same message to multiple services.
  3. No Order Guarantees
    • Under load, parallel consumers may process messages out of order.

Real-World Impact:

  • E-commerce Scenario:
    • Order service → Queue → Payment service
    • Analytics service cannot access the same orders without duplication.

docs.title: Problem 2: Publish-Subscribe (e.g., Redis Pub/Sub, MQTT)
docs.text:
How They Work:

  • Publishers send messages to channels
  • Subscribers receive messages in real-time

Limitations:

  1. No Persistence
    • Messages vanish after delivery.
    • Late subscribers (e.g., a new monitoring service) miss historical data.
  2. No Backpressure Handling
    • Fast producers overwhelm slow consumers.
  3. No Scalable Storage
    • Not designed for high-volume, long-term retention.

Real-World Impact:

  • IoT Use Case:
    • Sensor data → Pub/Sub → Real-time dashboard (works)
    • But: Cannot replay data for batch analytics.

docs.title: B. Database Logs (e.g., MySQL Binlog)
docs.text:
How They Work:

  • Append-only logs of database changes
  • Used for replication/auditing

Limitations:

  1. Not Designed for High Throughput
    • Optimized for durability, not millions of events/sec.
  2. Tight Coupling to Database
    • Hard to use for general-purpose event streaming.
  3. No Built-in Partitioning
    • Scaling requires manual sharding.

docs.title: Problem 1: Decoupling Producers & Consumers
docs.text:
Kafka’s Solution:

  • Persistent Log: Messages stored even after consumption.
  • Multiple Consumer Groups:
    • Each group independently reads the full log.

Example:

Order Service → Kafka Topic → [Payment Group, Analytics Group, Notifications Group]
  • Payment processes in real-time
  • Analytics runs hourly batch jobs
  • Notifications handle failures asynchronously

docs.title: Problem 2: Scalable Real-Time Data Handling
docs.text:
Kafka’s Solution:

  • Partitioning: Splits topics across brokers for parallel processing.
  • Horizontal Scaling:
    • Add partitions to increase throughput.
    • Consumers scale with partitions (1:1 mapping per group).

Example:

  • user_clicks topic with 12 partitions → 12 consumers per group.
  • Throughput scales linearly with partitions.

docs.title: Problem 3: Fault-Tolerant Data Retention
docs.text:
Kafka’s Solution:

  • Replicated Partitions:
    • Leader + followers (ISR) per partition.
    • Survives broker failures.
  • Disk-Based Storage:
    • Configurable retention (time/size).

Example:

  • 3-day retention for transaction_logs → Recover from outages.

docs.title: 3. Key Innovations vs. Alternatives
docs.text: Feature Traditional Systems Kafka Persistence Ephemeral (queues) or DB-bound (logs) Decoupled, durable log Consumption Single consumer (queues) or ephemeral (Pub/Sub) Multiple consumer groups Ordering No guarantees (scaled queues) Per-partition ordering Scalability Vertical scaling (bigger servers) Horizontal partitioning

docs.title: Case 1: Uber’s Real-Time Tracking
docs.text:
• Problem:
    • Drivers (producers) and services (billing, ETA, surge pricing) need the same data.
  • Solution:
    • driver_updates topic → Multiple consumer groups.

docs.title: Case 2: Netflix Recommendation Engine
docs.text:
• Problem:
    • User clicks must feed real-time + batch pipelines.
  • Solution:
    • user_activity topic → Real-time (Flink) + Batch (Spark).

docs.title: 5. Why This Matters
docs.text:
• Microservices: Kafka replaces point-to-point APIs with event-driven flows.
  • Data Lakes: Acts as a buffer between producers and slow sinks (e.g., Hadoop).
  • Disaster Recovery: Replicated logs enable data recovery.

Key Takeaway: Kafka solves the "write once, process many times" problem at scale.

Next: Phase 1.2 (Core Architecture) will explain how these solutions are implemented in Kafka’s design.

docs.title: Roadmap
docs.text:
• Caching Basics: Fundamental concepts and types of caching.
  • Spring Boot Caching Abstraction: How to use Spring's caching annotations and configure caching.
  • Caffeine Cache: Configuring and using Caffeine as an in-memory cache.
  • Introduction to Redis: Core Redis concepts and data structures.
  • Redis with Spring Boot: Integrating and using Redis in a Spring Boot application.
  • Advanced Redis Concepts: More complex Redis features and architectures.
  • Performance Considerations and Best Practices: Guidelines for effective caching.
  • Small Project: A practical application of the concepts learned.

docs.title: 1. Basics of Feign (needs work)
docs.text:
Feign is a declarative HTTP client developed by Netflix and later adopted by the Spring community. It simplifies the process of writing HTTP clients in Java. Instead of manually writing code to send HTTP requests and parse responses, you define an interface with annotations, and Feign generates the actual client implementation for you.

Key Concepts:

  • Declarative: You describe what you want, not how to do it.
  • Interface-based: Your client is defined as a Java interface.
  • Annotation-driven: Use annotations to specify HTTP methods, headers, and parameters.

Spring Cloud Feign vs OpenFeign:

  1. Spring Cloud Feign:
    • Part of the Spring Cloud Netflix stack.
    • Integrated with other Spring Cloud components like Ribbon for load balancing.
    • Uses Spring's dependency injection and auto-configuration.
  2. ==OpenFeign:==
    • The original, standalone Feign library.
    • Can be used outside of Spring ecosystem.
    • More flexible, but requires more manual configuration

we can make use of following dependencies while using feign, out which necessary one is OpenFeign core we also added Gson (for JSON processing), and SLF4J (for logging)  

 <dependency>
            <groupId>io.github.openfeign</groupId>
            <artifactId>feign-core</artifactId>
            <version>${feign.version}</version>
        </dependency>
        <dependency>
            <groupId>io.github.openfeign</groupId>
            <artifactId>feign-gson</artifactId>
            <version>${feign.version}</version>
        </dependency>
        <dependency>
            <groupId>io.github.openfeign</groupId>
            <artifactId>feign-slf4j</artifactId>
            <version>${feign.version}</version>
        </dependency>

Setup Main class: We create a Feign.Builder bean that sets up JSON encoding/decoding and logging.

@SpringBootApplication
public class FeignDemoApplication {

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SpringApplication.run(FeignDemoApplication.class, args);
    }

    @Bean
    public Feign.Builder feignBuilder() {
        return Feign.builder()
                .encoder(new GsonEncoder())
                .decoder(new GsonDecoder())
                .logger(new Slf4jLogger())
                .logLevel(Logger.Level.FULL);
    }
}

docs.title: Testing in Spring Boot: A Complete Guide
docs.text: I'll explain Spring Boot testing in a clear, simple way to help you prepare for interviews. Let's cover JUnit 5, Mockito, and Spring Boot Test step by step.

docs.title: Spring Boot Test Basics
docs.text: Spring Boot provides special tools that make testing easier. The core components are:

docs.title: @SpringBootTest
docs.text:
This annotation creates a full application context for your tests. It loads all your Spring beans and configurations.

@SpringBootTest
class MyApplicationTests {
    // Test methods here
}

When you use @SpringBootTest, Spring Boot starts up almost everything as if your application was running normally. This is good for integration tests but can be slow.

docs.title: @WebMvcTest
docs.text:
This is for testing only your web layer (controllers) without starting the full application:

@WebMvcTest(UserController.class)
class UserControllerTest {
    @Autowired
    private MockMvc mockMvc;

    @MockBean
    private UserService userService;

    @Test
    void shouldReturnUser() throws Exception {
        // Test code here
    }
}

@WebMvcTest is faster than @SpringBootTest because it only loads the web layer.

docs.title: @DataJpaTest
docs.text:
This is for testing your JPA repositories:

@DataJpaTest
class UserRepositoryTest {
    @Autowired
    private UserRepository userRepository;

    @Test
    void shouldSaveUser() {
        // Test code here
    }
}

@DataJpaTest sets up an in-memory database for testing your repositories without affecting your real database.

docs.title: JUnit 5 Key Features
docs.text: JUnit 5 is the testing framework used with Spring Boot. Here are important parts to know:

docs.title: Test Lifecycle Annotations
docs.text:
@BeforeAll // Runs once before all tests
@AfterAll // Runs once after all tests
@BeforeEach // Runs before each test
@AfterEach // Runs after each test
@Test // Marks a method as a test
@Disabled // Skips a test

docs.title: Assertions
docs.text:
Assertions check if your code behaves as expected:

// Basic assertions
assertEquals(expected, actual);
assertTrue(condition);
assertFalse(condition);
assertNull(object);
assertNotNull(object);

// Advanced assertions
assertThrows(Exception.class, () -> { methodThatShouldThrowException(); });
assertTimeout(Duration.ofMillis(100), () -> { slowMethod(); });

// Multiple assertions at once
assertAll(
    () -> assertEquals(1, calculator.add(0, 1)),
    () -> assertEquals(2, calculator.add(1, 1))
);

docs.title: Parameterized Tests
docs.text:
These let you run the same test with different inputs:

@ParameterizedTest
@ValueSource(strings = {"apple", "banana", "orange"})
void testFruitLength(String fruit) {
    assertTrue(fruit.length() > 3);
}

docs.title: Mockito Essentials
docs.text: Mockito helps you create and control fake objects for testing.

docs.title: Creating Mocks
docs.text:
There are several ways to create mocks:

// Using @Mock annotation
@Mock
private UserService userService;

// Or creating manually
UserService userService = Mockito.mock(UserService.class);

Remember to initialize your mocks with either MockitoAnnotations.openMocks(this) or @ExtendWith(MockitoExtension.class) at the class level.

docs.title: Stubbing Methods
docs.text:
Stubbing means telling your mock what to return when certain methods are called:

// Return a specific value
when(userService.findById(1L)).thenReturn(new User("John"));

// Throw an exception
when(userService.findById(999L)).thenThrow(new NotFoundException());

// Return different values on consecutive calls
when(counter.getCount())
    .thenReturn(1)
    .thenReturn(2)
    .thenReturn(3);

docs.title: Argument Matchers
docs.text:
When you don't care about the exact argument values:

// Any long value
when(userService.findById(anyLong())).thenReturn(new User());

// Any non-null string
when(validator.isValid(anyString(), any())).thenReturn(true);

Common matchers include: any(), anyInt(), anyString(), argThat().

docs.title: Verifying Interactions
docs.text:
Check if certain methods were called with specific arguments:

// Verify a method was called exactly once
verify(userService).save(user);

// Verify with specific arguments
verify(userService).deleteById(5L);

// Verify a method was never called
verify(userService, never()).deleteAll();

// Verify a method was called a specific number of times
verify(userService, times(3)).findAll();

docs.title: MockBean in Spring Boot
docs.text:
@MockBean integrates Mockito with Spring Boot's application context:

@SpringBootTest
class UserServiceTest {
    @Autowired
    private UserService userService;

    @MockBean
    private UserRepository userRepository;

    @Test
    void test() {
        when(userRepository.findById(1L)).thenReturn(Optional.of(new User("John")));
        User user = userService.getUserById(1L);
        assertEquals("John", user.getName());
    }
}

docs.title: Capturing Arguments
docs.text:
When you need to inspect the arguments passed to a method:

@Test
void shouldSaveUserWithCorrectData() {
    ArgumentCaptor<User> userCaptor = ArgumentCaptor.forClass(User.class);

    userService.createUser("John", 30);

    verify(userRepository).save(userCaptor.capture());
    User capturedUser = userCaptor.getValue();

    assertEquals("John", capturedUser.getName());
    assertEquals(30, capturedUser.getAge());
}

docs.title: Answer Interface
docs.text:
For more complex stubbing behavior:

when(userRepository.save(any(User.class))).thenAnswer(invocation -> {
    User user = invocation.getArgument(0);
    user.setId(1L); // Simulate ID generation
    return user;
});

docs.title: Spying on Real Objects
docs.text:
A spy is a partial mock that uses the real object but allows overriding some methods:

List<String> realList = new ArrayList<>();
List<String> spyList = Mockito.spy(realList);

// Real method is called
spyList.add("one");

// Override a method
doReturn(100).when(spyList).size();

assertEquals(1, spyList.size()); // Returns 100, not 1

docs.title: Mock Static Methods
docs.text:
With Mockito 3.4.0 and above, you can mock static methods:

try (MockedStatic<Utility> mockedStatic = Mockito.mockStatic(Utility.class)) {
    mockedStatic.when(() -> Utility.getCurrentDate()).thenReturn(fixedDate);

    // Code that uses Utility.getCurrentDate()
    LocalDate result = myService.processWithDate();

    assertEquals(fixedDate, result);
}

docs.title: Integration Testing
docs.text: Integration tests check how different parts of your application work together.

docs.title: TestRestTemplate
docs.text:
For testing REST APIs:

@SpringBootTest(webEnvironment = WebEnvironment.RANDOM_PORT)
class UserControllerIntegrationTest {
    @Autowired
    private TestRestTemplate restTemplate;

    @Test
    void shouldReturnUserById() {
        ResponseEntity<User> response = restTemplate.getForEntity("/users/1", User.class);

        assertEquals(HttpStatus.OK, response.getStatusCode());
        assertEquals("John", response.getBody().getName());
    }
}

docs.title: @DirtiesContext
docs.text:
Use this when your test modifies the application context and you want it reset for other tests:

@SpringBootTest
@DirtiesContext(classMode = ClassMode.AFTER_EACH_TEST_METHOD)
class DatabaseModifyingTest {
    // Test methods that change database state
}

docs.title: Using a Real Database
docs.text:
For database tests with a real database:

@SpringBootTest
@TestPropertySource(properties = {
    "spring.datasource.url=jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/test_db",
    "spring.jpa.hibernate.ddl-auto=create-drop"
})
class RealDatabaseTest {
    // Tests using an actual MySQL database
}

docs.title: Test Helper Methods
docs.text:
Create reusable methods for common test operations:

private User createTestUser(String name, int age) {
    User user = new User();
    user.setName(name);
    user.setAge(age);
    return user;
}

private void assertUserEquals(User expected, User actual) {
    assertEquals(expected.getId(), actual.getId());
    assertEquals(expected.getName(), actual.getName());
    assertEquals(expected.getAge(), actual.getAge());
}

docs.title: TestConfiguration
docs.text:
Create test-specific beans:

@TestConfiguration
static class TestConfig {
    @Bean
    public PaymentGateway testPaymentGateway() {
        return new TestPaymentGateway();
    }
}

docs.title: Interview Questions and Answers
docs.text: Here are some common interview questions about testing in Spring Boot:

docs.title: Q1: What is the difference between @MockBean and @Mock?
docs.text:
Answer:

  • @Mock is a pure Mockito annotation that creates a mock object.
  • @MockBean is a Spring Boot annotation that creates a mock and adds it to the Spring application context, replacing any existing bean of the same type.
  • Use @Mock for unit tests when you don't need Spring's context.
  • Use @MockBean for integration tests when you need to replace a bean in Spring's context.

docs.title: Q2: How would you test a Spring Boot REST controller?
docs.text:
Answer: There are two main approaches:

  1. Using @WebMvcTest for focused web layer testing:

    @WebMvcTest(UserController.class)
class UserControllerTest {
    @Autowired
    private MockMvc mockMvc;

    @MockBean
    private UserService userService;

    @Test
    void shouldGetUser() throws Exception {
        when(userService.findById(1L)).thenReturn(new User("John"));

        mockMvc.perform(get("/users/1"))
            .andExpect(status().isOk())
            .andExpect(jsonPath("$.name").value("John"));
    }
}

  2. Using @SpringBootTest with TestRestTemplate for full integration testing:

    @SpringBootTest(webEnvironment = WebEnvironment.RANDOM_PORT)
class UserControllerIntegrationTest {
    @Autowired
    private TestRestTemplate restTemplate;

    @Test
    void shouldGetUser() {
        ResponseEntity<User> response = restTemplate.getForEntity("/users/1", User.class);
        assertEquals(HttpStatus.OK, response.getStatusCode());
        assertEquals("John", response.getBody().getName());
    }
}

docs.title: Q3: How do you test Spring Data JPA repositories?
docs.text:
Answer:

@DataJpaTest
class UserRepositoryTest {
    @Autowired
    private UserRepository userRepository;

    @Autowired
    private TestEntityManager entityManager;

    @Test
    void shouldFindByName() {
        // Given
        User user = new User("John", 30);
        entityManager.persist(user);
        entityManager.flush();

        // When
        User found = userRepository.findByName("John");

        // Then
        assertNotNull(found);
        assertEquals(30, found.getAge());
    }
}

The @DataJpaTest annotation:

  • Sets up an in-memory database
  • Configures Hibernate and Spring Data
  • Performs entity scanning
  • Enables SQL logging

docs.title: Q4: How do you handle database tests in Spring Boot?
docs.text:
Answer: Spring Boot offers several approaches:

  1. In-memory database: Use H2 or HSQL with @DataJpaTest
  2. Test containers: Use Docker containers with real databases
  3. Actual test database: Configure a separate database for testing
  4. Database rollback: Use @Transactional to roll back changes after tests

Example with test containers:

@SpringBootTest
@Testcontainers
class DatabaseTest {
    @Container
    static PostgreSQLContainer<?> postgres = new PostgreSQLContainer<>("postgres:13");

    @DynamicPropertySource
    static void registerProperties(DynamicPropertyRegistry registry) {
        registry.add("spring.datasource.url", postgres::getJdbcUrl);
        registry.add("spring.datasource.username", postgres::getUsername);
        registry.add("spring.datasource.password", postgres::getPassword);
    }

    // Test methods here
}

docs.title: Q5: Explain the testing pyramid in Spring Boot applications.
docs.text:
Answer: The testing pyramid has three layers:

  1. Unit Tests (bottom layer): Test individual components like services or utils. They use mocks and are fast.
  2. Integration Tests (middle layer): Test how components work together. They may use Spring context and in-memory databases.
  3. End-to-End Tests (top layer): Test the entire application flow. They use the real application environment.

The pyramid shape means you should have:

  • Many unit tests (fast, focused)
  • Some integration tests
  • Few end-to-end tests (slow, broad)

This approach gives you fast feedback and good test coverage.

docs.title: Q6: How do you test Spring Boot applications with external dependencies like databases and messaging systems?
docs.text:
Answer:

  1. For databases: Use in-memory databases (H2) or test containers
  2. For REST APIs: Use MockWebServer or WireMock
  3. For messaging: Use embedded brokers or test containers

Example with WireMock:

@SpringBootTest
class ExternalApiTest {
    @Autowired
    private UserService userService;

    WireMockServer wireMockServer = new WireMockServer(8089);

    @BeforeEach
    void setup() {
        wireMockServer.start();

        wireMockServer.stubFor(get(urlEqualTo("/api/external/users/1"))
            .willReturn(aResponse()
                .withStatus(200)
                .withHeader("Content-Type", "application/json")
                .withBody("{\"id\":1,\"name\":\"John\"}")));
    }

    @AfterEach
    void tearDown() {
        wireMockServer.stop();
    }

    @Test
    void shouldFetchExternalUser() {
        User user = userService.fetchExternalUser(1L);
        assertEquals("John", user.getName());
    }
}

docs.title: Q7: How can you set up test data for your Spring Boot tests?
docs.text:
Answer: There are several approaches:

  1. Use @Sql to run SQL scripts:

    @SpringBootTest
@Sql("/test-data.sql")
class UserServiceIntegrationTest {
    // Tests using data from test-data.sql
}

  2. Use TestEntityManager in @DataJpaTest:

    @DataJpaTest
class UserRepositoryTest {
    @Autowired
    private TestEntityManager entityManager;

    @BeforeEach
    void setup() {
        entityManager.persist(new User("John", 30));
        entityManager.persist(new User("Jane", 25));
        entityManager.flush();
    }
}

  3. Create helper methods or builder classes:

    private User createTestUser() {
    return User.builder()
        .name("Test User")
        .email("test@example.com")
        .role(Role.USER)
        .build();
}

docs.title: Q8: How would you test a service that uses @Scheduled methods?
docs.text:
Answer: For testing scheduled methods:

  1. Extract the scheduled logic into a separate method
  2. Make the scheduled method call that separate method
  3. Test the separate method directly
// Original service
@Service
public class ReportService {
    @Scheduled(cron = "0 0 12 * * ?")
    public void generateDailyReport() {
        // Logic here
    }
}

// Refactored service
@Service
public class ReportService {
    @Scheduled(cron = "0 0 12 * * ?")
    public void generateDailyReport() {
        doGenerateDailyReport();
    }

    // This method can now be tested directly
    public void doGenerateDailyReport() {
        // Logic here
    }
}

// Test
@SpringBootTest
class ReportServiceTest {
    @Autowired
    private ReportService reportService;

    @Test
    void shouldGenerateReport() {
        reportService.doGenerateDailyReport();
        // Assert expected results
    }
}

docs.title: Q9: What's the difference between @MockBean and @SpyBean?
docs.text:
Answer:

  • @MockBean creates a complete mock that returns default values (null, 0, etc.) unless you specifically stub methods.
  • @SpyBean creates a partial mock that calls real methods unless you override them.

Example:

@SpringBootTest
class EmailServiceTest {
    // Complete mock - all methods return null by default
    @MockBean
    private UserRepository userRepository;

    // Partial mock - real methods are called by default
    @SpyBean
    private EmailSender emailSender;

    @Test
    void test() {
        // Stub a method on the mock
        when(userRepository.findById(1L)).thenReturn(Optional.of(new User("John")));

        // Override a method on the spy
        doNothing().when(emailSender).send(any());

        // Other methods on emailSender will use the real implementation
    }
}

docs.title: Q10: How do you test Spring Security in a Spring Boot application?
docs.text:
Answer: There are several approaches:

  1. Using @WithMockUser to mock authentication:

    @SpringBootTest
class SecurityTest {
    @Autowired
    private MockMvc mockMvc;

    @Test
    @WithMockUser(username = "admin", roles = {"ADMIN"})
    void adminCanAccessSecuredEndpoint() throws Exception {
        mockMvc.perform(get("/admin/dashboard"))
            .andExpect(status().isOk());
    }

    @Test
    @WithMockUser(username = "user", roles = {"USER"})
    void userCannotAccessSecuredEndpoint() throws Exception {
        mockMvc.perform(get("/admin/dashboard"))
            .andExpect(status().isForbidden());
    }
}

  2. Using SecurityMockMvcRequestPostProcessors:

    @SpringBootTest
class SecurityTest {
    @Autowired
    private MockMvc mockMvc;

    @Test
    void loginWithValidCredentials() throws Exception {
        mockMvc.perform(post("/login")
            .with(csrf())
            .param("username", "admin")
            .param("password", "password"))
            .andExpect(status().is3xxRedirection())
            .andExpect(redirectedUrl("/dashboard"));
    }
}

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
For Spring Boot testing interviews, make sure you understand:

  1. Different test annotations (@SpringBootTest, @WebMvcTest, @DataJpaTest)
  2. JUnit 5 basics (lifecycle, assertions, parameterized tests)
  3. Mockito functionality (creating mocks, stubbing, verification, argument matchers)
  4. Integration testing approaches
  5. How to test different layers (controllers, services, repositories)

Practice explaining these concepts clearly and be ready to write code examples for each one.

docs.title: Deep Dive into InvocationHandler and invoke() in Java Dynamic Proxies
docs.text: Let’s break down how dynamic proxies work in Java, focusing on the InvocationHandler and its invoke() method. This is key to understanding Spring’s AOP (Aspect-Oriented Programming) and annotations like @Transactional.

docs.title: a) java.lang.reflect.Proxy
docs.text:
• A built-in Java class that creates proxy objects at runtime.
  • Requires:
  • ClassLoader (to load the proxy class).
  • Interfaces the proxy should implement.
  • InvocationHandler (to define proxy behavior).

docs.title: b) InvocationHandler
docs.text:
• An interface with a single method: 
    public interface InvocationHandler {
    Object invoke(Object proxy, Method method, Object[] args) throws Throwable;
}
  • You implement this to intercept method calls on the proxy.

docs.title: 2. How invoke() Works
docs.text: When a method is called on the proxy object: 1. The JVM redirects the call to the InvocationHandler.invoke(). 2. The invoke() method decides what to do (e.g., log, cache, block access). 3. You can forward the call to the real object (or not).

docs.title: Parameters of invoke()
docs.text: Parameter Description Object proxy The proxy object itself (rarely used). Method method The method being called (e.g., pay() in PaymentService). Object[] args Arguments passed to the method (e.g., amount in pay(100)).

docs.title: Return Value
docs.text: • Returns whatever the proxied method should return (or you can modify it).

docs.title: a) Interface
docs.text:
public interface PaymentService {
    void pay(int amount);
}

docs.title: b) Real Implementation
docs.text:
public class RealPaymentService implements PaymentService {
    @Override
    public void pay(int amount) {
        System.out.println("Processing payment: $" + amount);
    }
}

docs.title: c) InvocationHandler (The Brain of the Proxy)
docs.text:
import java.lang.reflect.*;

public class LoggingHandler implements InvocationHandler {
    private final Object target;  // Real object (e.g., RealPaymentService)

    public LoggingHandler(Object target) {
        this.target = target;
    }

    @Override
    public Object invoke(Object proxy, Method method, Object[] args) throws Throwable {
        System.out.println("[LOG] Calling method: " + method.getName());

        // Forward the call to the real object
        Object result = method.invoke(target, args);

        System.out.println("[LOG] Method call completed.");
        return result;
    }
}

docs.title: d) Creating the Proxy
docs.text:
public class Client {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        PaymentService realService = new RealPaymentService();

        // Create a proxy
        PaymentService proxy = (PaymentService) Proxy.newProxyInstance(
            PaymentService.class.getClassLoader(),  // Classloader
            new Class[] { PaymentService.class },   // Interfaces to implement
            new LoggingHandler(realService)         // Your custom logic
        );

        proxy.pay(100);  // Calls LoggingHandler.invoke()
    }
}

Output: 

[LOG] Calling method: pay
Processing payment: $100
[LOG] Method call completed.

docs.title: a) Dynamic vs. Static Proxy
docs.text: Feature Static Proxy Dynamic Proxy Creation Manual class (e.g., DatabaseProxy) Runtime-generated by Proxy.newProxyInstance() Flexibility Limited to one interface Works for any interface Use Case Simple lazy loading AOP, logging, security

docs.title: b) Real-World Uses
docs.text:
1. Spring AOP (e.g., @Transactional, @Cacheable).
  2. Spring creates dynamic proxies to add behavior (transactions/caching) around your methods.
  3. Mocking Frameworks (e.g., Mockito).
  4. Uses proxies to mock method calls in tests.
  5. RPC (Remote Procedure Call).
  6. Proxies forward calls to remote services (e.g., Feign Client).

docs.title: Q: Why use InvocationHandler instead of subclassing?
docs.text: • Answer: Proxies work with interfaces, not concrete classes. Subclassing would require knowing the class upfront.

docs.title: Q: Can you access the proxy object inside invoke()?
docs.text: • Answer: Yes (via the proxy parameter), but avoid calling proxy methods from within invoke()—it causes infinite recursion!

docs.title: Q: How does Spring use this?
docs.text:
• Answer: When you use @Transactional, Spring creates a proxy that:
  • Starts a transaction (invoke() begins).
  • Calls your method.
  • Commits/rolls back the transaction (invoke() ends).

docs.title: 6. Cheat Sheet
docs.text:
Term What It Does Proxy Java’s class to create dynamic proxies. InvocationHandler Defines what the proxy should do when a method is called. invoke() The method that intercepts calls to the proxy. method.invoke() Forwards the call to the real object (like super.method() in subclassing).

Now you see how InvocationHandler is the "brain" behind dynamic proxies! Want to see how Spring’s @Transactional uses this under the hood? 😊

docs.title: 1. Maven Introduction and POM (Project Object mode
docs.text:
Maven is not just a ==build tool== but a comprehensive ==project management too==l that simplifies the process of building, reporting, and documenting Java projects.

Key Features:

  • Dependency management
  • Build lifecycle management
  • Project structure standardization
  • Plugin-based architecture

Spring Boot Project Structure with Maven 

my-spring-boot-app/
├── src/
│   ├── main/
│   │   ├── java/                # Java source code
│   │   ├── resources/           # Application properties, static resources
│   └── test/                    # Test source code
├── pom.xml                      # Project Object Model file

docs.title: Understanding POM (Project Object Model)
docs.text:
• POM Structure: The heart of any Maven project, managing dependencies, plugins, and project configurations.

Basic POM Structure: 

<project xmlns="http://maven.apache.org/POM/4.0.0"
         xmlns:xsi="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema-instance"
         xsi:schemaLocation="http://maven.apache.org/POM/4.0.0 http://maven.apache.org/xsd/maven-4.0.0.xsd">
    <modelVersion>4.0.0</modelVersion>
    <groupId>com.example</groupId>
    <artifactId>my-app</artifactId>
    <version>1.0.0</version>
    <packaging>jar</packaging>
</project>

  • <modelVersion>: Always 4.0.0, indicating the POM model version.
  • <groupId>: Identifier for the group (usually corresponds to a package structure).
  • <artifactId>: Name of the project, should be unique within the group.
  • <version>: Defines the version of the project.

  • <packaging>: Defines the build output type (e.g., jar, war).

  • <project> : specifies xml schema of pom , to which it needs to adhere to , we cannot just replace <groupid> with <xyz> there are already predefined tags in that schema which it enforces

    ****Parent Definition: 

    <parent>
    <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
    <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-parent</artifactId>
    <version>3.1.6</version>
</parent>

    Inheriting configurations and dependencies from a common parent, such as Spring Boot’s starter parent, to reduce redundancy and maintain consistency across multiple projects. there is super pom which is every pom is child to , if we didn't specify the parent pom ,  the super pom is direct considered as parent implicitely

Repositories 

<repositories>
    <repository>
        <id>central</id>
        <url>https://repo.maven.apache.org/maven2</url>
    </repository>
</repositories>

Specifies remote repositories to search for dependencies and plugins. The <repositories> section is used to define additional repositories besides Maven Central.

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text:
<dependencies>
    <dependency>
        <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
        <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-web</artifactId>
        <version>3.1.6</version>
    </dependency>
</dependencies>

Dependency Management

<dependencyManagement>
    <dependencies>
        <dependency>
            <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
            <artifactId>spring-boot-dependencies</artifactId>
            <version>3.1.6</version>
            <scope>import</scope>
            <type>pom</type>
        </dependency>
    </dependencies>
</dependencyManagement>

Manages versions of dependencies used across multiple modules. Dependencies are declared here and referenced in child POMs without specifying versions.

docs.title: Plugin Management
docs.text:
<build>
    <pluginManagement>
        <plugins>
            <plugin>
                <groupId>org.apache.maven.plugins</groupId>
                <artifactId>maven-compiler-plugin</artifactId>
                <version>3.8.1</version>
                <configuration>
                    <source>17</source>
                    <target>17</target>
                </configuration>
            </plugin>
        </plugins>
    </pluginManagement>
</build>

 Similar to dependency management but for plugins. This section configures ==plugin defaults that child POMs can inherit. ==

  • The build section configures the build process.
  • plugins define additional tools or processes to be executed during the build.
  • This example configures the compiler plugin to use Java 17.

if we didn't have child poms . we would only specify <plugin> tag 

<build>
  <plugins>
    <plugin>
      <groupId>org.apache.maven.plugins</groupId>
      <artifactId>maven-compiler-plugin</artifactId>
      <version>3.8.1</version>
      <configuration>
        <source>1.8</source>
        <target>1.8</target>
      </configuration>
    </plugin>
  </plugins>
</build>

Build Section and Plugins

<build>
    <plugins>
        <plugin>
            <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
            <artifactId>spring-boot-maven-plugin</artifactId>
        </plugin>
    </plugins>
</build>

Distribution Management

<distributionManagement>
    <repository>
        <id>internal-repo</id>
        <url>http://repo.example.com/releases</url>
    </repository>
    <snapshotRepository>
        <id>internal-snapshots</id>
        <url>http://repo.example.com/snapshots</url>
    </snapshotRepository>
</distributionManagement>

Manages how and where the project is distributed.
