  • execution: This designator means the join point is the execution of a method.
  • *: The first asterisk is a wildcard representing any return type.
  • com.example.service: This is the package name.
  • *: The second asterisk is a wildcard representing any class in the specified package.
  • *: The third asterisk is a wildcard representing any method name.
  • (..): The double dots represent any number of parameters (zero or more).

So, this expression matches the execution of any method in any class in the com.example.service package, regardless of its return type or parameters.

joinPoint.proceed()

Imagine you have a regular method in your code, like this:

public void doSomething() {
    System.out.println("Doing something");
}

 Now, when you use @Around advice, it's like wrapping this method in another method. The joinPoint.proceed() call is essentially saying "now run the original method". Here's a visualization

@Around("execution(* doSomething())")
public Object aroundAdvice(ProceedingJoinPoint joinPoint) throws Throwable {
    System.out.println("Before doing something");

    Object result = joinPoint.proceed(); // This runs the original doSomething() method

    System.out.println("After doing something");
    return result;
}

So, when you call doSomething(), it actually runs:

  1. "Before doing something"
  2. The original "Doing something"
  3. "After doing something"

now this is for single method called doSomething(), we can set this to match every method in a package it will be like wrapping that method inside other method where when we decide what code needs to be executed before and after the actual method execution

ProceedingJoinPoint vs JoinPoint

Imagine you're a security guard (the aspect) at a party (your program).

With a regular JoinPoint (used in @Before, @After, etc.), you're like a guard who can see who's coming in and out, but you can't stop them. You can only watch and take notes.

With a ProceedingJoinPoint (used in @Around), you're like a guard who can actually stop people, check their IDs, maybe even turn them away. The proceed() method is like you saying "Okay, you can go in now" to a guest.

 In code terms:

  • With JoinPoint, you can see information about the method being called, but you can't control its execution.
  • With ProceedingJoinPoint, you can control whether the method runs, when it runs, and even change what it returns.

Example:

Following example explains how ProceedingJoint has control over your method execution

  • We have a BankAccount class with a withdraw method.
  • The SecurityAspect uses @Around advice to "wrap" the withdraw method.
  • In the secureWithdrawal method:
    • We check the withdrawal amount.
    • If it's over $1000, we don't allow the withdrawal (we don't call proceed()).
    • If it's $1000 or less, we allow the withdrawal by calling proceed().
  • The proceed() call is what actually runs the original withdraw method.
import org.aspectj.lang.ProceedingJoinPoint;
import org.aspectj.lang.annotation.Around;
import org.aspectj.lang.annotation.Aspect;
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;

@Aspect
@Component
public class SecurityAspect {

    @Around("execution(* com.example.BankAccount.withdraw(..))")
    public Object secureWithdrawal(ProceedingJoinPoint joinPoint) throws Throwable {
        Object[] args = joinPoint.getArgs();
        double amount = (Double) args[0];

        if (amount > 1000) {
            System.out.println("Withdrawal amount exceeds limit. Cancelling transaction.");
            return false;  // Don't proceed with the withdrawal
        }

        System.out.println("Withdrawal amount within limit. Proceeding with transaction.");
        return joinPoint.proceed();  // Proceed with the original withdrawal method
    }
}

public class BankAccount {
    public boolean withdraw(double amount) {
        System.out.println("Withdrawing $" + amount);
        // Actual withdrawal logic here
        return true;
    }
}

So, if someone tries to withdraw $500, the code will:

  1. Print "Withdrawal amount within limit. Proceeding with transaction."
  2. Then run the original withdraw method, printing "Withdrawing $500"

But if someone tries to withdraw $1500, it will:

  1. Print "Withdrawal amount exceeds limit. Cancelling transaction."
  2. Return false, without ever running the original withdraw method

docs.title: 3.3 @Aspect, @Pointcut, @Before, @After, etc annot
docs.text:
Spring AOP leverages annotations to define aspects, pointcuts, and advice. These annotations make it easier to implement AOP concepts in a clear and concise manner.

@Aspect:

This annotation is used to declare a class as an aspect. 

@Aspect
@Component
public class LoggingAspect {
    // Aspect methods go here
}

@Pointcut

This annotation is used to declare a pointcut expression. It allows you to define reusable pointcut expressions.

@Pointcut("execution(* com.example.service.*.*(..))")
public void serviceLayer() {}

docs.title: Advice Annotations
docs.text:
@Before:

@Before("serviceLayer()")
public void beforeAdvice(JoinPoint joinPoint) {
    System.out.println("Before method: " + joinPoint.getSignature().getName());
}

@After

@After("serviceLayer()")
public void afterAdvice(JoinPoint joinPoint) {
    System.out.println("After method: " + joinPoint.getSignature().getName());
}

@AfterThrowing

@AfterThrowing(pointcut = "serviceLayer()", throwing = "ex")
public void afterThrowingAdvice(JoinPoint joinPoint, Exception ex) {
    System.out.println("Method " + joinPoint.getSignature().getName() + " threw exception: " + ex.getMessage());
}

@AfterReturning

@AfterReturning(pointcut = "serviceLayer()", returning = "result")
public void afterReturningAdvice(JoinPoint joinPoint, Object result) {
    System.out.println("Method " + joinPoint.getSignature().getName() + " returned: " + result);
}

@Around

@Around("serviceLayer()")
public Object aroundAdvice(ProceedingJoinPoint joinPoint) throws Throwable {
    System.out.println("Before method execution: " + joinPoint.getSignature().getName());
    try {
        Object result = joinPoint.proceed();
        System.out.println("After method execution: " + joinPoint.getSignature().getName());
        return result;
    } catch (Exception e) {
        System.out.println("Exception thrown in: " + joinPoint.getSignature().getName());
        throw e;
    }
}

Working example with these annoatations

  • We define a ComprehensiveAspect that uses all the advice types we discussed.
  • We create an ExampleService with two methods: one that completes successfully and another that throws an exception.
  • In the main method, we run the application and call both service methods to demonstrate how the different types of advice are triggered
import org.aspectj.lang.JoinPoint;
import org.aspectj.lang.ProceedingJoinPoint;
import org.aspectj.lang.annotation.*;
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;

@Aspect
@Component
public class ComprehensiveAspect {

    @Pointcut("execution(* com.example.service.*.*(..))")
    public void serviceLayer() {}

    @Before("serviceLayer()")
    public void beforeAdvice(JoinPoint joinPoint) {
        System.out.println("Before method: " + joinPoint.getSignature().getName());
    }

    @After("serviceLayer()")
    public void afterAdvice(JoinPoint joinPoint) {
        System.out.println("After method: " + joinPoint.getSignature().getName());
    }

    @AfterReturning(pointcut = "serviceLayer()", returning = "result")
    public void afterReturningAdvice(JoinPoint joinPoint, Object result) {
        System.out.println("Method " + joinPoint.getSignature().getName() + " returned: " + result);
    }

    @AfterThrowing(pointcut = "serviceLayer()", throwing = "ex")
    public void afterThrowingAdvice(JoinPoint joinPoint, Exception ex) {
        System.out.println("Method " + joinPoint.getSignature().getName() + " threw exception: " + ex.getMessage());
    }

    @Around("serviceLayer()")
    public Object aroundAdvice(ProceedingJoinPoint joinPoint) throws Throwable {
        System.out.println("Before method execution: " + joinPoint.getSignature().getName());
        try {
            Object result = joinPoint.proceed();
            System.out.println("After method execution: " + joinPoint.getSignature().getName());
            return result;
        } catch (Exception e) {
            System.out.println("Exception thrown in: " + joinPoint.getSignature().getName());
            throw e;
        }
    }
}

@Service
public class ExampleService {
    public String doSomething() {
        System.out.println("Doing something...");
        return "Done";
    }

    public void doSomethingRisky() throws Exception {
        System.out.println("Doing something risky...");
        throw new Exception("Oops!");
    }
}

@SpringBootApplication
public class AopDemoApplication {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        ApplicationContext context = SpringApplication.run(AopDemoApplication.class, args);
        ExampleService service = context.getBean(ExampleService.class);

        service.doSomething();
        try {
            service.doSomethingRisky();
        } catch (Exception e) {
            // Expected exception
        }
    }
}

docs.title: 4. Creating Custom Annotations
docs.text:
Custom annotations allow you to mark methods or classes for specific AOP behavior without changing their code. Here's how to create and use a custom annotation:

We define a custom annotation @LogExecutionTime.

// Custom annotation
@Target(ElementType.METHOD)
@Retention(RetentionPolicy.RUNTIME)
public @interface LogExecutionTime {
}

We create an aspect that uses this annotation as a pointcut.

// Aspect to use the custom annotation
@Aspect
@Component
public class LoggingAspect {

    @Around("@annotation(LogExecutionTime)")
    public Object logExecutionTime(ProceedingJoinPoint joinPoint) throws Throwable {
        long start = System.currentTimeMillis();

        Object result = joinPoint.proceed();

        long executionTime = System.currentTimeMillis() - start;

        System.out.println(joinPoint.getSignature() + " executed in " + executionTime + "ms");
        return result;
    }
}

We can now use @LogExecutionTime on any method we want to log execution time for.

@Service
public class ExampleService {

    @LogExecutionTime
    public void slowMethod() throws InterruptedException {
        Thread.sleep(1000);
    }
}

Creating Custom Pointcuts

Custom pointcuts allow you to define reusable pointcut expressions. Here's an example:

In below example:

  1. We define three pointcuts: inServiceLayer(), anyPublicMethod(), and publicServiceMethod().
  2. publicServiceMethod() combines the other two pointcuts.
  3. We use publicServiceMethod() in our @Before advice.
import org.aspectj.lang.annotation.Aspect;
import org.aspectj.lang.annotation.Before;
import org.aspectj.lang.annotation.Pointcut;
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;

@Aspect
@Component
public class AuditingAspect {

    @Pointcut("within(com.example.service.*)")
    public void inServiceLayer() {}

    @Pointcut("execution(public * *(..))")
    public void anyPublicMethod() {}

    @Pointcut("inServiceLayer() && anyPublicMethod()")
    public void publicServiceMethod() {}

    @Before("publicServiceMethod()")
    public void auditMethodCall() {
        System.out.println("Public service method called");
    }
}

We can combine custom annotations and pointcuts for even more flexibility:

In this example:

We define a custom @Auditable annotation that can take a custom message.

@Target({ElementType.METHOD, ElementType.TYPE})
@Retention(RetentionPolicy.RUNTIME)
public @interface Auditable {
    String value() default "";
}

 We create a pointcut that matches methods with this annotation.

We combine this with our inServiceLayer() pointcut.

Our advice uses the custom message if provided, or a default message if not.

@Aspect
@Component
public class AuditingAspect {

    @Pointcut("within(com.example.service.*)")
    public void inServiceLayer() {}

    @Pointcut("@annotation(auditable)")
    public void auditableMethod(Auditable auditable) {}

    @Around("inServiceLayer() && auditableMethod(auditable)")
    public Object auditMethod(ProceedingJoinPoint joinPoint, Auditable auditable) throws Throwable {
        String auditMessage = auditable.value().isEmpty() 
            ? "Method audited: " + joinPoint.getSignature().toShortString()
            : auditable.value();

        System.out.println("Before: " + auditMessage);
        Object result = joinPoint.proceed();
        System.out.println("After: " + auditMessage);

        return result;
    }
}

 We can now use @Auditable on any method in our service layer, optionally providing a custom audit message.

@Service
public class UserService {
    @Auditable("User created")
    public void createUser(String username) {
        // User creation logic
    }

    @Auditable
    public void updateUser(String username) {
        // User update logic
    }
}

These techniques allow you to create more modular and reusable aspects, making your AOP code cleaner and more maintainable.

docs.title: JWT (1)
docs.text:
A JWT is a compact, self-contained way to securely transmit information between parties as a JSON object. It's often used for:

  • Authentication: Verify the identity of a user.
  • Authorization: Determine what a user is allowed to do (their permissions).
  • Information Exchange: Securely share information between systems.

Structure of a JWT

A JWT consists of three parts separated by dots:

  1. Header: Contains information about how the JWT is signed (e.g., the algorithm used).
  2. Payload: Contains the "claims" (statements) about an entity (usually, the user) and additional data. This is where permissions are typically included.
  3. Signature: A cryptographic hash of the header and payload, used to verify the JWT's authenticity and integrity.

{
  "header": {
    "alg": "HS256",
    "typ": "JWT"
  },
  "payload": {
    "sub": "1234567890",  // User ID
    "name": "John Doe",
    "permissions": ["read:messages", "write:messages", "delete:messages"]
  },
  "signature": "xxxxxxx"
}

How JWTs Work with OAuth

OAuth is an authorization framework (not authentication). It focuses on how applications or users grant access to their resources. Here's a simplified flow:

  1. User wants to use App A: App A needs access to data in App B (e.g., the user's profile on a social network).
  2. App A requests authorization: App A redirects the user to App B's authorization server.
  3. User grants permission: The user logs into App B and approves App A's request.
  4. App B issues a JWT: App B's authorization server sends a JWT back to App A. This JWT includes information about the permissions granted.
  5. App A uses the JWT: App A includes the JWT in its requests to App B's resource server.
  6. App B's resource server verifies the JWT: It checks the signature and the permissions to ensure the request is valid.

Is the JWT Converted Back to JSON?

Yes, the receiving system (e.g., App B's resource server) decodes the JWT to access the payload (including the permissions). However, it doesn't necessarily need to convert it back to a pretty-printed JSON format; the data is readily available as a JSON object for processing.

docs.title: Need for Spring Batch / Batch Processing
docs.text:
Batch processing involves executing high-volume, repetitive data tasks without user interaction. It’s crucial for scenarios like:

  • Processing large datasets (e.g., financial transactions, logs, or reports).

  • Automating resource-heavy tasks (e.g., payroll, billing, or data migration).

  • Running tasks during off-peak hours to avoid system overload.

Without batch processing, developers would manually manage:

  • Memory optimization for large data.

  • Error recovery and retries.

  • Transaction management.

  • Job scheduling and monitoring.

docs.title: 1. Bank: End-of-Day Transaction Settlement
docs.text:
• Task: Process millions of transactions nightly to update balances, detect fraud, and generate statements.

  • Challenges:

    • Scale: Millions of records must be processed quickly.

    • Consistency: Transactions must be atomic (no partial updates).

    • Failure Recovery: If the job fails at 3 AM, restarting from the last successful step is critical.

  • Spring Batch Solution:

    • Chunk Processing: Processes data in chunks (e.g., 100 records at a time) to reduce memory overhead.

    • Transactional Writers: Ensures each chunk is committed only if all records succeed.

    • Job Restartability: Resumes from the last failed step using persisted metadata.

docs.title: 2. E-commerce: Daily Sales Analytics
docs.text:
• Task: Aggregate daily sales data from multiple sources (DB, CSV, APIs) to generate revenue reports.

  • Challenges:

    • Data Variety: Reading/writing from disparate sources (DB, flat files, REST APIs).

    • Error Handling: Skipping invalid records (e.g., malformed CSV rows) without stopping the entire job.

    • Performance: Processing terabytes of data efficiently.

  • Spring Batch Solution:

    • ItemReader/ItemWriter: Prebuilt readers/writers for databases, files, and JSON/XML.

    • Skip Logic: Configurable rules to skip bad records and log them for review.

    • Parallel Processing: Multi-threaded steps or partitioning to split work across nodes.

docs.title: 3. Telecom: Monthly Customer Billing
docs.text:
• Task: Calculate usage charges for millions of customers and generate invoices.

  • Challenges:

    • Complex Business Logic: Tiered pricing, discounts, and taxes.

    • Auditing: Tracking which records were processed and which failed.

    • Scalability: Handling spikes in data volume (e.g., holiday season).

  • Spring Batch Solution:

    • Decoupling Steps: Break the job into logical steps (e.g., data extraction → pricing → invoice generation).

    • Listeners: Use ItemProcessListener to audit successes/failures.

    • Cloud-Native Scaling: Integrate with Kubernetes to scale horizontally.

docs.title: When to Use Spring Batch?
docs.text:
• Use: Large-scale data ETL, scheduled reporting, regulatory compliance jobs.

  • Avoid: Real-time processing (use Kafka Streams or Spring WebFlux instead).

docs.title: How Data Flows Through the Architecture (Restaurant Analogy)
docs.text: Imagine running a restaurant kitchen where meals (data) move through different stages before reaching customers. Here’s how Spring Batch components map to this workflow:

docs.title: 1️⃣ Job → The Entire Meal Preparation Process
docs.text: A Job represents the full process of preparing and serving meals. 🔹 Example: "Prepare Dinner for 100 Customers."

docs.title: 2️⃣ Step → Each Major Phase of the Meal Preparation
docs.text:
A Step is a distinct phase of the job. 🔹 Example Steps:

Step 1: Gather Ingredients

Step 2: Cook the Dish

Step 3: Plate & Serve

Each step follows the read → process → write pattern.

docs.title: 3️⃣ ItemReader → Fetching Ingredients (Reading Data)
docs.text:
The ItemReader extracts raw data from a source, just like fetching ingredients from storage. 🔹 Example:

  • Read vegetables from the pantry.
  • Read meat from the refrigerator.
  • Read spices from the spice rack.

📌 This is where the batch job retrieves data before processing it.

docs.title: 4️⃣ ItemProcessor → Cooking & Transforming Ingredients
docs.text:
The ItemProcessor applies transformations to the data—similar to how a chef prepares ingredients. 🔹 Example:

    • Chop vegetables. - Cook pasta. - Season the dish.

📌 This is where business logic or data transformation happens in Spring Batch.

docs.title: 5️⃣ ItemWriter → Serving the Dish (Writing Data)
docs.text:
The ItemWriter delivers the processed data to the destination—just like serving food to customers. 🔹 Example:

    • Plate the dish. - Deliver it to the table. - Serve it to customers.

📌 This is where data gets written to a database, file, or another system.

docs.title: 6️⃣ Chunk-Oriented Processing → Cooking in Batches for Efficiency
docs.text:
Instead of cooking one meal at a time, a restaurant prepares multiple portions in chunks to increase efficiency. 🔹 Example:

    • Instead of making one plate of pasta at a time, cook 10 servings together. - Instead of chopping one carrot at a time, chop a whole batch before moving to the next task.

📌 Spring Batch processes data in chunks (e.g., 10 records at a time) instead of processing one record at a time.

docs.title: 7️⃣ JobRepository → The Kitchen Logbook (Tracking Progress)
docs.text:
The JobRepository tracks and logs everything happening in the kitchen. 🔹 Example:

    • Which dishes have been prepared? - Which orders are still in progress? - Were any dishes delayed or canceled?

📌 In Spring Batch, this stores job execution details like status, timestamps, and failure logs.

docs.title: 8️⃣ JobLauncher → The Expediter (Starting the Meal Preparation)
docs.text:
The JobLauncher initiates the job—similar to a restaurant expediter (or manager) who signals when to start cooking. 🔹 Example:

    • The restaurant opens for dinner service, and the manager says: "Start preparing meals for 100 customers!"

📌 This is where Spring Batch jobs get triggered—either manually, via a scheduler, or from an external system.

docs.title: 2. Spring Batch   How data flows
docs.text:
1. Job Launching :

    • The JobLauncher starts a job by invoking the run() method with a Job and JobParameters.
    • Example: jobLauncher.run(processOrdersJob, new JobParametersBuilder().addString("input.file", "orders.csv").toJobParameters());

    • Job Execution :

    • The Job retrieves its configuration from the JobRepository and starts executing its steps sequentially or in parallel (depending on the configuration).

    • Step Execution :

    • Each Step executes in a loop, processing data in chunks:

      • Chunk-Oriented Processing :
        • The ItemReader reads a chunk of data (e.g., 100 items).
        • The ItemProcessor processes each item in the chunk (optional).
        • The ItemWriter writes the processed items to the destination.
      • The TransactionManager ensures that the entire chunk is executed within a transaction. If any item fails, the transaction is rolled back.

    • Metadata Tracking :

    • The JobRepository tracks the progress of the job and step executions. It stores information such as:

      • Job status (e.g., STARTED, COMPLETED, FAILED).
      • Step status (e.g., STARTED, COMPLETED, FAILED).
      • Number of items read, processed, and written.

    • Listeners :

    • Listeners are invoked at various points in the lifecycle of the job and steps:

      • Before/after job execution.
      • Before/after step execution.
      • Before/after reading, processing, or writing an item.

    • Scaling :

    • For large datasets, the Partitioner divides the data into smaller partitions.

    • The TaskExecutor processes these partitions in parallel, improving throughput.

docs.title: 3. Job
docs.text:
A Job is the top-level unit of work in Spring Batch. It represents an entire batch process and consists of one or more Steps .

  • Key Concepts :

    • JobInstance : A logical run of a job. For example, running a job with different input parameters creates different instances.
    • JobExecution : Represents a single attempt to execute a job. If a job fails and is restarted, it will have multiple executions but still belong to the same instance.
@Bean
public Job processOrdersJob(JobBuilderFactory jobBuilderFactory, Step processOrdersStep) {
    return jobBuilderFactory.get("processOrdersJob")
                            .start(processOrdersStep)
                            .build();
}

docs.title: What Are We Doing?
docs.text:
• Purpose : Define a Job named processOrdersJob.
  • Details :
    • A Job is the top-level unit of work in Spring Batch.
    • Here, we create a job using the JobBuilderFactory.
    • The job starts with a single step (processOrdersStep).
    • The .start() method specifies the first step in the job.
    • The .build() method finalizes the job configuration.

This defines the overall batch process. In this case, the job consists of one step (processOrdersStep).

  • Real-World Example :

    • A job could be "Process Orders," which includes steps like reading orders from a file, processing them, and writing them to a database.

docs.title: 4. Step
docs.text:
A Step is an independent phase within a job. Each step typically involves reading, processing, and writing data.

  • Key Concepts :

    • StepExecution : Represents the execution of a specific step within a job.
    • Chunk-Oriented Processing : Steps process data in chunks (batches) rather than one item at a time.

Configuration:

@Bean
public Step processOrdersStep(StepBuilderFactory stepBuilderFactory, ItemReader<Order> reader,
                              ItemProcessor<Order, Invoice> processor, ItemWriter<Invoice> writer) {
    return stepBuilderFactory.get("processOrdersStep")
                             .<Order, Invoice>chunk(10)
                             .reader(reader)
                             .processor(processor)
                             .writer(writer)
                             .build();
}

  • Purpose : Define a Step named processOrdersStep.

  • Details :

    • A Step represents an independent phase within a job.
    • Here, we configure the step to process data in chunks of size 10.
    • The <Order, Invoice> generic types specify:
      • Input type: Order (read from the ItemReader).
      • Output type: Invoice (written by the ItemWriter after processing).
    • The .reader(), .processor(), and .writer() methods wire up the components for reading, processing, and writing data.
    • The .build() method finalizes the step configuration.

    • Real-World Example :

    • A step could be "Read Orders from CSV," where you read 100 orders at a time, process them, and write them to a database.

docs.title: 5.ItemReader
docs.text:
The ItemReader is responsible for reading data from a source (e.g., database, file, API). It reads one item at a time and passes it to the processor.

@Bean
public FlatFileItemReader<Order> reader() {
    FlatFileItemReader<Order> reader = new FlatFileItemReader<>();
    reader.setResource(new ClassPathResource("orders.csv"));
    reader.setLineMapper(new DefaultLineMapper<>() {{
        setLineTokenizer(new DelimitedLineTokenizer() {{
            setNames("orderId", "customerId", "amount");
        }});
        setFieldSetMapper(new BeanWrapperFieldSetMapper<>() {{
            setTargetType(Order.class);
        }});
    }});
    return reader;
}

  • Purpose : Define an ItemReader to read order data from a CSV file.
  • Details :
    • We use FlatFileItemReader to read data from a flat file (orders.csv).
    • The .setResource() method specifies the file location (in this case, on the classpath). The file orders.csv is located in the classpath (e.g., src/main/resources/).
    • The LineMapper converts each line of the file into an Order object:
      • DelimitedLineTokenizer: Splits each line into fields based on delimiters (e.g., commas).
      • BeanWrapperFieldSetMapper: Uses Spring’s reflection-based mechanism to automatically map the extracted tokens to the Order class’s fields.
orderId,customerId,amount
1,101,100.00
2,102,200.00

  • Common Implementations :

    • FlatFileItemReader: Reads data from flat files (CSV, TXT).
    • JdbcCursorItemReader: Reads data from a database using JDBC.
    • JpaPagingItemReader: Reads data using JPA with paging.

 example:

  • Reading customer data from a CSV file for further processing.

docs.title: 6. ItemProcessor
docs.text:
The ItemProcessor is an optional component that processes or transforms the data read by the ItemReader before it is written by the ItemWriter.

  • Use Cases :

    • Data transformation (e.g., converting raw data into a structured format).
    • Filtering (e.g., skipping invalid records).

Configuration

@Bean
public ItemProcessor<Order, Invoice> processor() {
    return order -> {
        // Transform Order into Invoice
        Invoice invoice = new Invoice();
        invoice.setOrderId(order.getOrderId());
        invoice.setCustomerId(order.getCustomerId());
        invoice.setAmount(order.getAmount());
        return invoice;
    };
}
  • Purpose : Define an ItemProcessor to transform Order objects into Invoice objects.
  • Details :
    • The ItemProcessor takes an Order object as input and returns an Invoice object as output.
    • In this example, we map fields from the Order object to the Invoice object.
    • This is where you can also perform additional transformations, validations, or filtering.
  • This component processes each Order object and converts it into an Invoice object.

Example:

Converting raw order data into formatted invoice data.

docs.title: 7. ItemWriter
docs.text:
The ItemWriter writes the processed data to a destination (e.g., database, file, API). It writes items in chunks, which improves performance by reducing the number of I/O operations.

  • Common Implementations :

    • FlatFileItemWriter: Writes data to flat files.
    • JdbcBatchItemWriter: Writes data to a database using JDBC.
    • JpaItemWriter: Writes data using JPA.
@Bean
public FlatFileItemWriter<Invoice> writer() {
    FlatFileItemWriter<Invoice> writer = new FlatFileItemWriter<>();
    writer.setResource(new FileSystemResource("invoices.csv"));
    writer.setLineAggregator(new DelimitedLineAggregator<>() {{
        setDelimiter(",");
        setFieldExtractor(new BeanWrapperFieldExtractor<>() {{
            setNames(new String[]{"orderId", "customerId", "amount"});
        }});
    }});
    return writer;
}
    • Purpose : Define an ItemWriter to write Invoice objects to a CSV file. - Details : - We use FlatFileItemWriter to write data to a flat file (invoices.csv). - The .setResource() method specifies the output file location. - The LineAggregator converts Invoice objects into lines of text: - DelimitedLineAggregator: Joins fields into a comma-separated string. - BeanWrapperFieldExtractor: Extracts fields from the Invoice object.

This component writes processed Invoice objects to a CSV file.

orderId,customerId,amount
1,101,100.00
2,102,200.00

Overall

Here’s what happens when the job runs:

  1. Job Execution :

    • The processOrdersJob is launched.
    • It starts executing the processOrdersStep.

    • Step Execution :

    • The ItemReader reads 10 Order objects from the orders.csv file.

    • The ItemProcessor transforms each Order object into an Invoice object.
    • The ItemWriter writes the 10 Invoice objects to the invoices.csv file.
    • This process repeats until all orders are processed.

    • Chunk-Oriented Processing :

    • Each chunk of 10 items is processed within a single transaction.

    • If any item in the chunk fails, the entire chunk is rolled back.

docs.title: 1. What Transaction Management Does
docs.text: In Spring Batch, Transaction Management ensures that each chunk of data is processed reliably. If something goes wrong during processing, the transaction is rolled back, and the system can recover gracefully.

docs.title: Key Goals :
docs.text:
• Data Integrity : Ensure that incomplete or erroneous data is not written to the target system.
  • Fault Tolerance : Handle failures gracefully by retrying or skipping problematic records.
  • Performance : Minimize the overhead of managing transactions while processing large datasets.

docs.title: 2. How It Works in Spring Batch
docs.text:
Spring Batch integrates seamlessly with Spring’s declarative transaction management . Each chunk of data is executed within a transaction. Here’s how it works:

  1. Start Transaction :

    • When a chunk begins processing, a transaction is started.

    • Read, Process, Write :

    • The ItemReader reads data.

    • The ItemProcessor processes the data.
    • The ItemWriter writes the data.

    • Commit or Rollback :

    • If all operations succeed, the transaction is committed.

    • If any operation fails, the transaction is rolled back, and the chunk is retried or skipped based on the configuration.

docs.title: 3. Chunk-Oriented Transactions
docs.text: Spring Batch uses a chunk-oriented processing model , where data is processed in small batches (chunks). Each chunk is executed within a transaction.

docs.title: Example :
docs.text:
• Suppose you’re processing 1,000 records with a chunk size of 10.
  • Spring Batch will:
    • Read 10 records.
    • Process the 10 records.
    • Write the 10 records.
    • Commit the transaction.
  • This process repeats until all 1,000 records are processed.

docs.title: Why Use Chunk-Oriented Transactions?
docs.text:
• Efficiency : Processing data in chunks reduces the number of I/O operations and improves performance.
  • Reliability : If a chunk fails, only that chunk is rolled back, not the entire job.

docs.title: 4. Error Handling and Rollbacks
docs.text: Spring Batch provides robust mechanisms for handling errors during batch processing. Here are some common strategies:

docs.title: Retry :
docs.text:
• If an operation fails due to a transient error (e.g., network timeout), Spring Batch can retry the operation a specified number of times.

  • Example:

    .faultTolerant()
.retry(Exception.class)
.retryLimit(3)

docs.title: Skip :
docs.text:
• If an item cannot be processed (e.g., invalid data), Spring Batch can skip it and continue processing the rest of the chunk.

  • Example:

    .faultTolerant()
.skip(Exception.class)
.skipLimit(10)

docs.title: Rollback :
docs.text:
• If an unrecoverable error occurs, Spring Batch rolls back the transaction and marks the job as failed.

  • You can customize which exceptions trigger a rollback:

    .faultTolerant()
.noRollback(CustomException.class)

docs.title: 5. Configuration Example
docs.text:
Here’s how you configure transaction management and error handling in a Spring Batch step:

@Bean
public Step processOrdersStep(StepBuilderFactory stepBuilderFactory, ItemReader<Order> reader,
                              ItemProcessor<Order, Invoice> processor, ItemWriter<Invoice> writer) {
    return stepBuilderFactory.get("processOrdersStep")
                             .<Order, Invoice>chunk(10)
                             .reader(reader)
                             .processor(processor)
                             .writer(writer)
                             .faultTolerant() // Enable fault tolerance
                             .retry(Exception.class) // Retry on exceptions
                             .retryLimit(3) // Retry up to 3 times
                             .skip(Exception.class) // Skip on exceptions
                             .skipLimit(10) // Allow up to 10 skips
                             .noRollback(CustomException.class) // Don't rollback for CustomException
                             .build();
}

docs.title: 1. What Are Listeners?
docs.text: Listeners in Spring Batch allow you to execute custom logic at various points during the execution of a job or step. They are particularly useful for logging, monitoring, and handling events like job start, job completion, or item processing errors.

docs.title: Key Benefits :
docs.text:
• Logging : Log the start and end of jobs or steps.
  • Monitoring : Track progress and performance metrics.
  • Error Handling : Perform custom actions when errors occur.
  • Custom Actions : Execute additional logic (e.g., sending notifications) at specific points in the lifecycle.

docs.title: 2. Types of Listeners
docs.text: Spring Batch provides several types of listeners, each targeting a different level of granularity:

docs.title: 2.1 JobExecutionListener
docs.text:
• Purpose : Hooks into the lifecycle of a job.
  • Methods :
    • beforeJob(JobExecution jobExecution): Called before the job starts.
    • afterJob(JobExecution jobExecution): Called after the job completes.

docs.title: 2.2 StepExecutionListener
docs.text:
• Purpose : Hooks into the lifecycle of a step.
  • Methods :
    • beforeStep(StepExecution stepExecution): Called before the step starts.
    • afterStep(StepExecution stepExecution): Called after the step completes.

docs.title: 2.3 ItemReadListener
docs.text:
• Purpose : Hooks into the lifecycle of reading items.
  • Methods :
    • beforeRead(): Called before an item is read.
    • afterRead(T item): Called after an item is successfully read.
    • onReadError(Exception ex): Called if an error occurs while reading.

docs.title: 2.4 ItemProcessListener
docs.text:
• Purpose : Hooks into the lifecycle of processing items.
  • Methods :
    • beforeProcess(T item): Called before an item is processed.
    • afterProcess(T item, S result): Called after an item is successfully processed.
    • onProcessError(T item, Exception e): Called if an error occurs while processing.

docs.title: 2.5 ItemWriteListener
docs.text:
• Purpose : Hooks into the lifecycle of writing items.
  • Methods :
    • beforeWrite(List<? extends S> items): Called before items are written.
    • afterWrite(List<? extends S> items): Called after items are successfully written.
    • onWriteError(Exception exception, List<? extends S> items): Called if an error occurs while writing.

docs.title: 9.2 Listeners , use , configurations
docs.text:
You can implement listeners by either:

  1. Implementing Listener Interfaces directly.

  2. Using Annotations (@BeforeJob, @AfterJob, etc.).

    public class MyJobExecutionListener implements JobExecutionListener {

    @Override
    public void beforeJob(JobExecution jobExecution) {
        System.out.println("Job is starting: " + jobExecution.getJobInstance().getJobName());
    }

    @Override
    public void afterJob(JobExecution jobExecution) {
        if (jobExecution.getStatus() == BatchStatus.COMPLETED) {
            System.out.println("Job completed successfully!");
        } else if (jobExecution.getStatus() == BatchStatus.FAILED) {
            System.out.println("Job failed!");
        }
    }
}

docs.title: Wiring the Listener
docs.text:
@Bean
public Job processOrdersJob(JobBuilderFactory jobBuilderFactory, Step processOrdersStep) {
    return jobBuilderFactory.get("processOrdersJob")
                            .start(processOrdersStep)
                            .listener(new MyJobExecutionListener()) // Add listener
                            .build();
}

docs.title: Scenario : Logging Job and Step Execution
docs.text:
Suppose you want to log the start and end times of a job and its steps.

public class LoggingJobExecutionListener implements JobExecutionListener {

    @Override
    public void beforeJob(JobExecution jobExecution) {
        System.out.println("Job started at: " + jobExecution.getStartTime());
    }

    @Override
    public void afterJob(JobExecution jobExecution) {
        System.out.println("Job ended at: " + jobExecution.getEndTime());
    }
}

public class LoggingStepExecutionListener implements StepExecutionListener {

    @Override
    public void beforeStep(StepExecution stepExecution) {
        System.out.println("Step started at: " + stepExecution.getStartTime());
    }

    @Override
    public void afterStep(StepExecution stepExecution) {
        System.out.println("Step ended at: " + stepExecution.getEndTime());
    }
}

docs.title: Wiring the Listeners
docs.text:
@Bean
public Job processOrdersJob(JobBuilderFactory jobBuilderFactory, Step processOrdersStep) {
    return jobBuilderFactory.get("processOrdersJob")
                            .start(processOrdersStep)
                            .listener(new LoggingJobExecutionListener()) // Add Job Listener
                            .build();
}

@Bean
public Step processOrdersStep(StepBuilderFactory stepBuilderFactory, ItemReader<Order> reader,
                              ItemProcessor<Order, Invoice> processor, ItemWriter<Invoice> writer) {
    return stepBuilderFactory.get("processOrdersStep")
                             .<Order, Invoice>chunk(10)
                             .reader(reader)
                             .processor(processor)
                             .writer(writer)
                             .listener(new LoggingStepExecutionListener()) // Add Step Listener
                             .build();
}

docs.title: Interview questions
docs.text: 1. What is Spring Batch and why is it used?

docs.title: ✅ Goal:
docs.text: We want to make sure that when someone sends a POST request to create a user, the data is valid — for example, name should not be blank and email should be valid.

docs.title: 🧱 Step 1: Add Dependency (if not already there)
docs.text:
Make sure your pom.xml includes validation starter:

<dependency>  
    <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>  
    <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-validation</artifactId>  
</dependency>

This gives you access to Bean Validation annotations like @NotBlank, @Email, etc.

docs.title: 📦 Step 2: Create a DTO (Data Transfer Object)
docs.text:
Create a class to receive the request body:

public class UserDTO {

    @NotBlank(message = "Name is required")  
    private String name;

    @Email(message = "Email should be valid")  
    private String email;

    // Getters and setters

    public String getName() {  
        return name;  
    }

    public void setName(String name) {  
        this.name = name;  
    }

    public String getEmail() {  
        return email;  
    }

    public void setEmail(String email) {  
        this.email = email;  
    }  
}

docs.title: 🌐 Step 3: Use @Valid in Controller
docs.text:
Now, use @Valid in your controller method to validate the input:

@RestController  
@RequestMapping("/api/users")  
public class UserController {

    @PostMapping  
    public ResponseEntity<?> createUser(@Valid @RequestBody UserDTO userDTO, BindingResult result) {

        if (result.hasErrors()) {  
            return ResponseEntity.badRequest().body("Validation failed: " + result.getAllErrors());  
        }

        return ResponseEntity.ok("User is valid!");  
    }  
}

docs.title: 🔍 What’s happening here?
docs.text:
• @Valid: Tells Spring to validate the userDTO object based on the rules defined (like @NotBlank, @Email)
  • BindingResult: Holds any validation errors that occur
  • If there are errors, we return a 400 Bad Request with error messages
  • If no errors, we proceed normally

docs.title: 🧪 Step 4: Try It Out
docs.text: Send a POST request using Postman or curl:

docs.title: ❌ Invalid Example (name is blank, email invalid):
docs.text:
{  
  "name": "",  
  "email": "not-an-email"  
}

Response:

Validation failed: [Field error in object 'userDTO' on field 'name': ...  
Field error in object 'userDTO' on field 'email': ...]

docs.title: ✅ Valid Example:
docs.text:
{  
  "name": "Alice",  
  "email": "alice@example.com"  
}

Response:

User is valid!

docs.title: 🎯 Summary
docs.text:
Part Purpose @NotBlank, @Email Define validation rules @Valid Triggers validation BindingResult Captures validation errors

Let me know if you want to add custom error responses or handle validation globally using @ControllerAdvice!

docs.title: REST
docs.text:
REST Specifications:

REpresentational State Transfer

the key abstraction of information in REST is a resource.

Any information that can be named can be a resource: a document or image, a temporal service, a collection of other resources, a non-virtual object (e.g. a person), and so on.

REST uses a resource identifier to identify the particular resource involved in an interaction between components.

The state of the resource at any particular timestamp is known as resource representation.

A representation consists of data, metadata describing the data and hypermedia links which can help the clients in transition to the next desired state.

Hypertext (or hypermedia) means the simultaneous presentation of information and controls such that the information becomes the affordance through which the user (or automaton) obtains choices and selects actions. Hypertext can be anything xml,json,etc.

With HATEOAS, a client interacts with a network application whose application servers provide information dynamically through hypermedia.

the client does not need to know if a resource is employee or device. It should act on the basis of media-type associated with the resource.

i.e. resource representations shall be self-descriptive.

resource methods are to be used to perform the desired transition.

Guiding Principle:

1.Client–server :req is always originated from client

2.Stateless– Each request from client to server must contain all of the information necessary to understand the request,Session state is therefore kept entirely on the client.

3.Cacheable :

**4.Uniform interface: **

docs.title: Spring
docs.text:
• Inversion of control(IOC) does just what its name says: it inverts the traditional hierarchy of control for fulfilling object relationships. ==Instead of relying on application code to define how objects relate to each other, relationships are defined by the framework.== As a methodology, IOC introduces consistency and predictability to object relations, but it does require you, as the developer, to give up some fine-grained control.
  • Dependency injection(DI) is a mechanism where the framework "injects" dependencies into your app. It's the practical implementation of IOC. Dependency injection hinges onpolymorphism, in the sense that it allows the fulfilment of areference typeto change based on configurations in the framework. The framework injects variable references rather than having them manually fulfilled in application code.

what is spring ? IOC DI implementations.

docs.title: Java persistance api
docs.text:
persistence refers to the characteristic of state of a system that ==outlives (persists more than) the process that created it.==

This is achieved in practice by storing the state as data in*computer data storage.*

System is described a stateful if it is designed to remember preceding events or user interactions;[1]the remembered information is called thestateof the system.

docs.title: What is @ControllerAdvice?
docs.text: @ControllerAdvice is a specialization of the @Component annotation which allows you to handle exceptions globally across all controllers. I==t helps in separating exception handling logic from the main business logic of your application.==

docs.title: Benefits of Using @ControllerAdvice
docs.text:
1. Consistent Exception Handling Across Controllers: Ensures uniform handling of exceptions across multiple controllers.
  2. Reduces Boilerplate Code: Centralizes exception handling logic, reducing code duplication.
  3. Separation of Concerns: Keeps exception handling logic separate from business logic.
  4. Handling Common Exceptions: Manages common exceptions in a unified way.

Exception handling in action: Step 1: Create a Custom Exception Class

public class CustomException extends RuntimeException {
    public CustomException(String message) {
        super(message);
    }
}

docs.title: Step 2: Define a Global Exception Handler with @ControllerAdvice
docs.text:
import org.springframework.http.HttpStatus;
import org.springframework.http.ResponseEntity;
import org.springframework.web.bind.annotation.ControllerAdvice;
import org.springframework.web.bind.annotation.ExceptionHandler;

@ControllerAdvice
public class GlobalExceptionHandler {

    @ExceptionHandler(CustomException.class)
    public ResponseEntity<String> handleCustomException(CustomException ex) {
        return ResponseEntity.status(HttpStatus.BAD_REQUEST).body(ex.getMessage());
    }

    @ExceptionHandler(Exception.class)
    public ResponseEntity<String> handleGenericException(Exception ex) {
        return ResponseEntity.status(HttpStatus.INTERNAL_SERVER_ERROR).body("An unexpected error occurred");
    }
}
  • @ControllerAdvice: Marks the class as a global exception handler.
  • @ExceptionHandler: Used to define methods that handle specific exceptions.
  • handleCustomException: Handles CustomException and returns a 400 Bad Request response with the exception message.
  • handleGenericException: Handles any other exceptions and returns a 500 Internal Server Error response with a generic message.

docs.title: Creating a Custom Error Response
docs.text:
To standardize error responses, create a custom error response class.

public class ErrorResponse {
    private String message;
    private int status;

    public ErrorResponse(String message, int status) {
        this.message = message;
        this.status = status;
    }

    // getters and setters
}

Using Custom Error Response in @ControllerAdvice

@ControllerAdvice
public class GlobalExceptionHandler {

    @ExceptionHandler(CustomException.class)
    public ResponseEntity<ErrorResponse> handleCustomException(CustomException ex) {
        ErrorResponse errorResponse = new ErrorResponse(ex.getMessage(), HttpStatus.BAD_REQUEST.value());
        return new ResponseEntity<>(errorResponse, HttpStatus.BAD_REQUEST);
    }

    @ExceptionHandler(Exception.class)
    public ResponseEntity<ErrorResponse> handleGenericException(Exception ex) {
        ErrorResponse errorResponse = new ErrorResponse("An unexpected error occurred", HttpStatus.INTERNAL_SERVER_ERROR.value());
        return new ResponseEntity<>(errorResponse, HttpStatus.INTERNAL_SERVER_ERROR);
    }
}

  • ErrorResponse: Custom error response object to provide a consistent error structure.
  • handleCustomException and handleGenericException: Methods return ErrorResponse objects wrapped in ResponseEntity.

docs.title: Use Cases for @ControllerAdvice
docs.text:
1. Consistent Exception Handling Across Controllers:

    • Example: An API with multiple endpoints where any request can result in a CustomException.

    • Reducing Boilerplate Code:

    • Example: Handling ValidationException in a centralized way for form validation errors across different endpoints.

    • Separation of Concerns:

    • Example: Controllers focus on handling requests and business logic, while @ControllerAdvice manages all the error responses.

    • Handling Common Exceptions:

    • Example: Catching IllegalArgumentException and responding with a 400 Bad Request for any controller method.

docs.title: 1. Client Request
docs.text: The process begins when a client (e.g., web browser, mobile app) sends an HTTP request to the Spring Boot application.

docs.title: 2. Servlet Container
docs.text: The request first reaches the Servlet Container, such as Apache Tomcat. A Servlet Container is a web server that provides an environment for Java servlets to run.

docs.title: Key Term: Servlet
docs.text: A servlet is a Java class that handles web requests. It receives requests from clients, processes them, and sends back responses.

docs.title: 3. Filters
docs.text: Before reaching the DispatcherServlet, the request passes through a chain of Filters.

docs.title: Key Term: Filter
docs.text: Filters are components that intercept requests before they reach the servlet (or responses before they return to the client). They can modify the request/response or perform actions like logging, authentication, or compression.

docs.title: 4. DispatcherServlet
docs.text: The DispatcherServlet is a front controller in Spring MVC that handles all incoming HTTP requests.

docs.title: Key Term: DispatcherServlet
docs.text: It's a special servlet that centralizes request handling, dispatching requests to appropriate handlers (usually controllers) based on the request URL.

docs.title: 5. Interceptors
docs.text: After the DispatcherServlet, but before reaching the controller, the request goes through Interceptors.

docs.title: Key Term: Interceptor
docs.text: Interceptors can perform operations before the request reaches the controller, after the controller has processed it, or after the complete request has been processed.

docs.title: 6. Controller
docs.text: The Controller handles the request, performs necessary operations, and prepares the response.

docs.title: Key Term: Controller
docs.text: In Spring MVC, a controller is a component that handles web requests, processes them (often interacting with services and repositories), and returns a response.

docs.title: 7. Response Path
docs.text: The response follows the reverse path: Controller → Interceptors → DispatcherServlet → Filters → Servlet Container → Client.

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
1. Filters:
    • Authentication and authorization
    • Request/response logging
    • CORS handling
    • Request/response transformation
  2. Interceptors:
    • Handling cross-cutting concerns (e.g., logging, auditing)
    • Modifying the ModelAndView
    • Validating user session
  3. Controllers:
    • Handling specific business logic
    • Processing form submissions
    • Returning API responses
    • Rendering views

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
In a Spring Boot application, an HTTP request flows through several layers:

  1. It first reaches the Servlet Container (e.g., Tomcat).
  2. Then it passes through a chain of Filters for pre-processing.
  3. The DispatcherServlet receives the request and determines which handler (Controller) should process it.
  4. Before reaching the Controller, the request may be intercepted by Interceptors.
  5. The Controller processes the request and prepares a response.
  6. The response then flows back through Interceptors, DispatcherServlet, and Filters before being sent back to the client.

This layered approach allows for separation of concerns, making the application more modular and easier to maintain. Filters and Interceptors provide hooks for cross-cutting concerns, while Controllers focus on specific business logic.

docs.title: 2.HATEOS
docs.text:
HATEOAS stands for Hypermedia as the Engine of Application State. 

Tt's a principle of REST API design that essentially tells the client what it can do next.

docs.title: Simple Explanation
docs.text:
Imagine you're using a GPS navigation system:

  1. You tell it where you want to go.
  2. It gives you the current step (e.g., "Turn right on Main St.").
  3. After you complete that step, it gives you the next one.
  4. This continues until you reach your destination.

HATEOAS works similarly in APIs:

  1. The client makes a request to the API.
  2. The API responds with data AND information about what the client can do next.
  3. The client uses this information to make further requests.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Let's say we have a simple book management API. Here's how a HATEOAS-compliant response might look when requesting information about a book:

{
  "id": 1,
  "title": "The Great Gatsby",
  "author": "F. Scott Fitzgerald",
  "links": [
    {
      "rel": "self",
      "href": "/api/books/1"
    },
    {
      "rel": "update",
      "href": "/api/books/1",
      "method": "PUT"
    },
    {
      "rel": "delete",
      "href": "/api/books/1",
      "method": "DELETE"
    },
    {
      "rel": "reviews",
      "href": "/api/books/1/reviews"
    }
  ]
}

In this response:

  1. We get the book data (id, title, author).
  2. We also get a list of "links" that tell the client what it can do next:
    • "self": Get this book's details again
    • "update": Update this book's information
    • "delete": Delete this book
    • "reviews": Get reviews for this book

The client doesn't need to know in advance what URLs to use or what actions are possible. The API tells the client everything it needs to know about what to do next.

docs.title: Benefits of HATEOAS
docs.text:
1. Discoverability: Clients can dynamically discover what actions are possible.
  2. Loose coupling: Clients don't need to hard-code API endpoints.
  3. Evolution: The API can change over time without breaking clients.
  4. Self-documenting: The API responses themselves serve as documentation.

docs.title: How @Transactional Works Under the Hood (Spring Boot)
1. What is @Transactional?
docs.text:
• It’s a Spring annotation that wraps a method in a database transaction.
  • If the method succeeds → commits the transaction.
  • If an exception occurs → rolls back the transaction.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
@Service
public class BankService {

    @Autowired
    private AccountRepository accountRepository;

    @Transactional  
    public void transferMoney(Long fromId, Long toId, double amount) {
        // Deduct from sender
        Account fromAccount = accountRepository.findById(fromId).orElseThrow();
        fromAccount.setBalance(fromAccount.getBalance() - amount);
        accountRepository.save(fromAccount);

        // Add to receiver
        Account toAccount = accountRepository.findById(toId).orElseThrow();
        toAccount.setBalance(toAccount.getBalance() + amount);
        accountRepository.save(toAccount);

        // If anything fails, BOTH changes are rolled back!
    }
}

docs.title: A. Proxy-Based Magic (Default Behavior)
docs.text: When you annotate a method with @Transactional, Spring creates a proxy around your object.

docs.title: Step-by-Step Process
docs.text:
1. Proxy Creation

    • Spring wraps your BankService in a proxy (like a middleman).
    • This proxy handles transaction logic.

    • Before Method Execution

    • Opens a database transaction (via DataSource).

    • Sets transaction isolation level (e.g., READ_COMMITTED).

    • Method Execution

    • Calls the real method (transferMoney()).

    • After Method Execution

    • If no exception → commits the transaction.

    • If exception → rolls back the transaction.

docs.title: B. How Does Spring Decide to Roll Back?
docs.text:
• By default, only unchecked exceptions (RuntimeException) trigger rollback.

  • Checked exceptions (like IOException) do not rollback unless specified:

    @Transactional(rollbackFor = {SQLException.class, IOException.class})

docs.title: 3. What Happens When You Call @Transactional from Another Method?
Case 1: Calling from the Same Class (Problem!)
docs.text:
@Service
public class BankService {

    public void processPayment() {
        transferMoney(1L, 2L, 100.0);  // ❌ Transaction WON'T work!
    }

    @Transactional  
    public void transferMoney(Long fromId, Long toId, double amount) {
        // ... logic
    }
}

docs.title: Why?
docs.text:
• Proxy limitation: Spring’s proxy only intercepts external method calls (from another Spring bean).
  • When you call transferMoney() internally, it bypasses the proxy → no transaction!

docs.title: Option 1: Self-Injection (Hacky but works)
docs.text:
@Service
public class BankService {

    @Autowired  
    private BankService self;  // Inject a proxy of itself!

    public void processPayment() {
        self.transferMoney(1L, 2L, 100.0);  // ✅ Now transactional!
    }

    @Transactional  
    public void transferMoney(Long fromId, Long toId, double amount) {
        // ... logic
    }
}

docs.title: Option 2: TransactionTemplate (Recommended)
docs.text:
@Service
public class BankService {

    @Autowired  
    private TransactionTemplate transactionTemplate;

    public void processPayment() {
        transactionTemplate.execute(status -> {
            transferMoney(1L, 2L, 100.0);  // ✅ Manual transaction
            return null;
        });
    }

    public void transferMoney(Long fromId, Long toId, double amount) {
        // ... logic
    }
}

docs.title: 4. Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. @Transactional uses proxies → only works on external method calls.
  2. Internal calls bypass the proxy → no transaction!
  3. Solutions:
    • Self-injection (not ideal, but quick).
    • TransactionTemplate (better for programmatic control).
  4. Always test transactions (use @DataJpaTest or logs to verify).

docs.title: Interview Question Example
docs.text:
Q: "If I call an @Transactional method from another method in the same class, does the transaction work?" A:

"No, because Spring’s transaction management relies on proxies. When you call the method internally, it bypasses the proxy, so no transaction is created. To fix this, you can either self-inject the bean or use TransactionTemplate for programmatic transactions."

docs.title: 1. Propagation (How Transactions Nest)
docs.text:
Key for Interviews: Focus on REQUIRED and REQUIRES_NEW.

  • REQUIRED (Default)

    • Joins existing transaction; creates new if none.
    • When to use: 90% of cases (e.g., service calling repository).
    @Transactional  // Default is REQUIRED
public void methodA() {
    methodB();  // Shares methodA's transaction.
}

  • REQUIRES_NEW

    • Suspends current transaction, starts a fresh one.
    • When to use: Audit logs, async tasks (must not roll back with main tx).
    @Transactional(propagation = Propagation.REQUIRES_NEW)
public void auditLog() { /* New tx, independent of caller */ }

Interview Q: "What happens if REQUIRED calls REQUIRES_NEW?"

"REQUIRES_NEW pauses the caller’s tx, runs in a new tx, and resumes caller’s tx after. Rollbacks are independent."

docs.title: 2. Isolation (How Txs See Each Other)
docs.text:
Key for Interviews: Know READ_COMMITTED vs. SERIALIZABLE.

  • READ_COMMITTED (Default)

    • No dirty reads (uncommitted changes invisible).
    • Pitfall: Non-repeatable reads (re-querying may show different data).

    • SERIALIZABLE

    • No dirty/non-repeatable/phantom reads.

    • Trade-off: Slow (locks rows/tables).
    • Use case: Stock trading, banking.

Interview Q: "When would you use SERIALIZABLE?"

"Only for financial systems needing absolute consistency. Avoid due to performance cost."

docs.title: 3. Rollback Rules
docs.text:
• Default: Only RuntimeException triggers rollback.

  • Customize:

    @Transactional(rollbackFor = {SQLException.class})

Trick Q: "If a checked exception is thrown but not in rollbackFor, does it roll back?"

"No, unless it’s also a RuntimeException."

docs.title: 4. Internal Method Call Trap
docs.text:
Problem:

@Transactional  
public void methodA() {
    methodB();  // ❌ No transaction! (Proxy bypassed)
}

@Transactional  
public void methodB() { /* ... */ }

Fix:

  1. Self-injection:

    @Autowired private MyService self;  
self.methodB();  // ✅ Works (but hacky).

  2. TransactionTemplate:

    @Autowired TransactionTemplate txTemplate;  
txTemplate.execute(() -> { methodB(); return null; });

Interview Q: "Why doesn’t @Transactional work for internal calls?"

"Spring proxies only intercept external calls. Internal calls bypass the proxy."

docs.title: Summary Table
docs.text: Topic Key Points Propagation REQUIRED (default), REQUIRES_NEW (independent tx). Isolation READ_COMMITTED (default), SERIALIZABLE (banking). Rollback Unchecked exceptions only; customize with rollbackFor. Internal Calls Use self-injection or TransactionTemplate.

docs.title: Interview Hack
docs.text:
If asked to design a transactional service:

@Service
public class PaymentService {
    @Transactional  // REQUIRED by default
    public void processPayment() {
        deductAmount();
        updateLedger();  // Both in same tx.
    }

    @Transactional(propagation = Propagation.REQUIRES_NEW)
    public void logAudit() { /* Always new tx */ }
}

Mention:

  • "I’d use REQUIRES_NEW for audit logs to ensure they persist even if payment fails."

docs.title: @ConditionalOnProperty
docs.text:
The @ConditionalOnProperty annotation provides a powerful mechanism for conditional bean registration based on the presence and values of properties in your application's environment (like those in application.properties or application.yml). It allows you to create flexible configurations that adapt to different environments or runtime conditions.

How It Works

  1. Target: You apply this annotation to a configuration class, a bean method within a configuration class, or directly to a component class.

  2. Property Check: Spring Boot evaluates the specified property (or properties) at runtime. It checks:

    • If the property exists in the environment.
    • If the property has a specific value (optional).

    • Conditional Registration:

    • If the property check passes (i.e., the property exists and has the desired value, if specified), the annotated bean is registered and becomes part of your application context.

    • If the property check fails, the bean is not registered, effectively disabling that component or configuration.

Example: Feature Flag

Imagine you want to enable or disable a "premium features" module in your application based on a configuration property:

@Configuration
@ConditionalOnProperty(name = "app.features.premium", havingValue = "enabled")
public class PremiumFeaturesConfig {
    // ... beans for premium features
}

 usage: 

@Service
public class MyService {

    @Autowired(required = false)  // Allow injection to fail if bean is absent
    private PremiumFeature premiumFeature;

    // ... other service methods

    public void doSomething() {
        if (premiumFeature != null) {
            // Premium feature is enabled, use it
            premiumFeature.somePremiumMethod(); 
        } else {
            // Premium feature is not enabled, provide alternative behavior
        }
    }
}

In your application.properties:

app.features.premium=enabled  // or "disabled"

  • If app.features.premium is "enabled," the PremiumFeaturesConfig is processed, and its beans are created.
  • If app.features.premium is "disabled" or missing, the configuration class is ignored.

  • @Autowired(required = false): This ensures that if the PremiumFeature bean doesn't exist (because the property is not "enabled"), the service still starts up without throwing an exception.
  • The if (premiumFeature != null) check is crucial to determine if the premium feature is accessible.

@ConditionalOnBean: You can alternatively annotate a specific method in your service with @ConditionalOnBean, making the method's execution dependent on the presence of a particular premium feature bean.

@Service
public class MyService {

    @Autowired
    private ApplicationContext context; // Get the application context

    public void doSomething() {
        // ... regular logic
    }

    @ConditionalOnBean(PremiumFeature.class)
    public void doSomethingPremium() {
        // This method is only called if PremiumFeature bean exists
        PremiumFeature premiumFeature = context.getBean(PremiumFeature.class);
        premiumFeature.somePremiumMethod();
    }
}

Common Use Cases

  • Feature Flags/Toggles: Enable/disable features at runtime.
  • Environment-Specific Configurations: Load different beans based on the environment (e.g., development, testing, production).
  • External Service Integration: Conditionally configure beans for interacting with external systems if the necessary credentials are provided.

Annotation Attributes

  • name (or value): The name of the property to check.
  • havingValue (optional): The expected value of the property for the bean to be registered.
  • matchIfMissing (optional): Controls whether the condition matches if the property is missing (default is false).
  • prefix (optional): A prefix to apply to the property name.
@Component
@ConditionalOnProperty(prefix = "app.db", name = "type", havingValue = "mysql")
public class MySQLDataSource {
    // ... configuration for MySQL data source
}

@Component
@ConditionalOnProperty(prefix = "app.db", name = "type", havingValue = "postgresql")
public class PostgreSQLDataSource {
    // ... configuration for PostgreSQL data source
}

docs.title: @EnableConfigurationProperties
docs.text:
The primary function of @EnableConfigurationProperties is to bind external configuration values (typically from properties files like application.properties or application.yml) to a Java class. This class acts as a holder for those configuration properties, making them readily accessible within your Spring Boot application.

Key Advantages

  1. Type-Safety: You get strongly typed access to your configuration. This means you can use the configuration values directly as members of your Java class, with all the benefits of compile-time type checking and IDE support.

  2. Organization: It helps you structure your configuration in a clean and maintainable way. Instead of scattering configuration values throughout your code, you centralize them within a dedicated class.

  3. Validation: Spring Boot automatically validates your configuration properties based on the annotations you apply to your class members (e.g., @NotNull, @Min, @Max).

Working Example: MyAppClient Configuration

Let's imagine you're building a Spring Boot application that interacts with an external service called "MyApp." You need to store and use configuration values like the base URL of the MyApp API, an API key, and maybe a timeout value.

  1. Properties Class (MyAppClientProperties)

    @ConfigurationProperties(prefix = "myapp.client")
public class MyAppClientProperties {

    private String baseUrl;
    private String apiKey;
    private int timeout = 5000; // Default timeout in milliseconds

    // Getters and Setters (omitted for brevity)
}
    • @ConfigurationProperties(prefix = "myapp.client"): This instructs Spring Boot to look for configuration values starting with the prefix "myapp.client" in your properties files.
    • The class members (baseUrl, apiKey, timeout) map directly to properties like myapp.client.baseUrl, myapp.client.apiKey, and myapp.client.timeout.
    • Properties File (application.properties or application.yml)
    myapp.client.baseUrl=https://api.myapp.com
myapp.client.apiKey=your_secret_api_key

  2. *Enabling Configuration Properties *

    @Configuration
@EnableConfigurationProperties(MyAppClientProperties.class)
public class MyAppClientConfig {
    // ... your configuration beans
}

    @EnableConfigurationProperties: This activates the binding process, linking the values from your properties file to your MyAppClientProperties class.

docs.title: 1. JDBC (Java database connection)
docs.text:
Plain JDBC Example and Problems

Plain JDBC is the basic way to connect Java to databases, like connecting to MySQL to fetch book titles. Here's a simple example:

public class DatabaseConnection {

    private static final String JDBC_DRIVER = "org.h2.Driver"; // Example for H2 database
    private static final String DB_URL = "jdbc:h2:~/test"; // Example for H2 database
    private static final String USER = "sa"; // Example user
    private static final String PASS = ""; // Example password

    public Connection getConnection() {
        try {
            Class.forName(JDBC_DRIVER);
            return DriverManager.getConnection(DB_URL, USER, PASS);
        } catch (ClassNotFoundException | SQLException e) {
            e.printStackTrace(); // Handle the exception properly in a real application
            return null;
        }
    }
}


public class DatabaseOperations {

    private DatabaseConnection dbConnection;

    public DatabaseOperations(DatabaseConnection dbConnection) {
        this.dbConnection = dbConnection;
    }

    public void createTable() {
        try (Connection connection = dbConnection.getConnection();
             Statement statement = connection.createStatement()) {

            String sql = "CREATE TABLE Users (id INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY, name VARCHAR(255), age INT)";
            statement.executeUpdate(sql);
            System.out.println("Table created successfully.");

        } catch (SQLException e) {
            e.printStackTrace(); // Handle the exception properly in a real application
        }
    }

    public void insertUser(String name, int age) {
        try (Connection connection = dbConnection.getConnection();
             PreparedStatement preparedStatement = connection.prepareStatement("INSERT INTO Users (name, age) VALUES (?, ?)")) {

            preparedStatement.setString(1, name);
            preparedStatement.setInt(2, age);
            preparedStatement.executeUpdate();
            System.out.println("User inserted successfully.");

        } catch (SQLException e) {
            e.printStackTrace(); // Handle the exception properly in a real application
        }
    }

    public void readUsers() {
        try (Connection connection = dbConnection.getConnection();
             PreparedStatement preparedStatement = connection.prepareStatement("SELECT * FROM Users");
             ResultSet resultSet = preparedStatement.executeQuery()) {

            while (resultSet.next()) {
                int id = resultSet.getInt("id");
                String name = resultSet.getString("name");
                int age = resultSet.getInt("age");
                System.out.println("User [ID: " + id + ", Name: " + name + ", Age: " + age + "]");
            }

        } catch (SQLException e) {
            e.printStackTrace(); // Handle the exception properly in a real application
        }
    }
}

However, this approach has issues:

  • Boilerplate Overload: Notice how much code is dedicated to connection management, statement creation, and resource cleanup versus actual business logic.
  • Manual Resource Management: We have to manually close connections, statements, and result sets in finally blocks, which is error-prone.
  • Exception Handling Complexity: SQLException is a checked exception that must be caught or declared, leading to cluttered code.
  • Manual Mapping: We have to manually map database rows to Java objects by working with the ResultSet.
  • SQL String Literals: SQL queries are written as strings, which are not checked at compile time.
  • No Object-Relational Mapping: There's a disconnect between our object model and the relational database structure.

docs.title: Enter Spring JDBC: The First Improvement
docs.text:
Spring JDBC aims to simplify the process by handling the boilerplate code for you. Think of it as having pre-fabricated walls for your house - still not a complete solution, but much better than doing everything manually.

import org.springframework.beans.factory.annotation.Autowired;
import org.springframework.jdbc.core.JdbcTemplate;
import org.springframework.stereotype.Repository;

import java.util.List;

@Repository
public class UserRepository {

    private final JdbcTemplate jdbcTemplate;

    @Autowired
    public UserRepository(JdbcTemplate jdbcTemplate) {
        this.jdbcTemplate = jdbcTemplate;
    }

    public void createTable() {
        jdbcTemplate.execute("CREATE TABLE Users (userId INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY, userName VARCHAR(255), age INT)");
    }

    public void insertUser(String userName, int age) {
        String insertQuery = "INSERT INTO Users (userName, age) VALUES (?, ?)";
        jdbcTemplate.update(insertQuery, userName, age);
    }

    public List<User> getUsers() {
        String selectQuery = "SELECT * FROM Users";
        return jdbcTemplate.query(selectQuery, (rs, rowNum) -> {
            User user = new User();
            user.setUserId(rs.getInt("userId"));
            user.setUserName(rs.getString("userName"));
            user.setAge(rs.getInt("age"));
            return user;
        });
    }
}

public class User {
    private int userId;
    private String userName;
    private int age;
}


import org.springframework.beans.factory.annotation.Autowired;
import org.springframework.stereotype.Service;

import java.util.List;

@Service
public class UserService {

    private final UserRepository userRepository;

    @Autowired
    public UserService(UserRepository userRepository) {
        this.userRepository = userRepository;
    }

    public void createTable() {
        userRepository.createTable();
    }

    public void insertUser(String userName, int age) {
        userRepository.insertUser(userName, age);
    }

    public List<User> getUsers() {
        List<User> users = userRepository.getUsers();
        users.forEach(user -> System.out.println(user)); // Print users
        return users;
    }
}


spring.datasource.url=jdbc:h2:mem:userDB
spring.datasource.driver-class-name=org.h2.Driver
spring.datasource.username=sa
spring.datasource.password=
spring.h2.console.enabled=true

docs.title: Spring JDBC: What's Improved?
docs.text:
1. Simplified Resource Management: No more manual connection opening/closing or try-catch-finally blocks.
  2. Exception Translation: Spring translates low-level SQLExceptions into more meaningful DataAccessExceptions.
  3. Reduced Boilerplate: The JdbcTemplate handles most of the repetitive code for us.
  4. RowMapper Concept: We can define mappers that convert ResultSet rows to domain objects in a reusable way.
  5. Simplified Operations: Methods like update(), query(), and queryForObject() handle common database operations.

docs.title: But Wait, We Still Have Problems!
docs.text:
Despite these improvements, Spring JDBC still has limitations:

  1. SQL Everywhere: We're still writing raw SQL in our code.
  2. Manual Object Mapping: Although improved with RowMapper, we still explicitly map columns to properties.
  3. No Object-Relational Features: No support for inheritance, complex relationships, lazy loading, etc.
  4. No Caching: Every query hits the database directly.
  5. Object-Relational Impedance Mismatch: Java objects and relational tables are fundamentally different structures.

This is where Object-Relational Mapping (ORM) comes in! Let's explore how it solves these issues in our next section.

docs.title: The ORM Approach: Bridging Objects and Relations
docs.text: ORM tools let you think and work with objects while automatically handling the database aspects.

docs.title: 2.1 ORM JPA Hibernate
docs.text:
JPA is the intermediary that ==bridges your Java objects with the database tables.==

It's a specification that defines the concepts and APIs,

while implementations like Hibernate provide the actual functionality.

docs.title: Object-Relational Mapping (ORM)
docs.text: ORM is a programming technique that converts data between incompatible type systems - specifically between object-oriented programming languages and relational databases.

docs.title: ORM provides a mapping layer that handles:
docs.text:
1. Object-Table Mapping: Maps classes to tables and fields to columns
  2. Identity Management: Tracks object identity and manages persistence state
  3. Relationship Handling: Manages one-to-one, one-to-many, many-to-many relationships
  4. Inheritance Strategies: Maps object inheritance to database structures
  5. Lazy Loading: Loads data on demand to optimize performance
  6. Caching: Reduces database access by caching entities
  7. Query Abstraction: Provides object-oriented query languages

docs.title: JPA Specification
docs.text:
JPA (Java Persistence API) is a specification that standardizes ORM for Java applications. It defines:

  1. Entity Model: How to define persistent entities
  2. EntityManagerFactory & EntityManager: Interfaces for managing entities
  3. Persistence Context: The runtime environment for entity instances
  4. Entity Lifecycle: The states and transitions of entity instances
  5. Query Language (JPQL): An object-oriented query language
  6. Criteria API: A programmatic query building API
  7. Transaction Management: Integration with JTA or resource-local transactions

docs.title: Hibernate as JPA Implementation
docs.text:
Hibernate implements the JPA specification and adds additional features.

  1. Native API: Session, SessionFactory alongside EntityManager
    • Hibernate provides its own APIs (Session and SessionFactory) in addition to the JPA EntityManager.
    • The Session interface in Hibernate is an extension of the JPA EntityManager, providing more granular control over database operations.
