For example, SessionImpl (Hibernate's class) directly implements the EntityManager interface, allowing developers to use Hibernate-specific methods alongside standard JPA methods.

  1. Enhanced Features: Additional mapping types, query features, caching options

    • Hibernate extends JPA by offering advanced mapping capabilities, query enhancements, and caching mechanisms.
    • While JPA provides basic entity mapping and caching, Hibernate introduces additional mapping types (e.g., @Any, @Formula) and query features like HQL (Hibernate Query Language). It also supports multi-level caching (Level 1, Level 2, and Query Cache), which gives developers finer control over performance optimization.

    • Hibernate-specific Extensions: Filters, interceptors, custom types

    • Hibernate includes extensions like filters, interceptors, and custom types that are not part of the JPA specification.

    • Filters allow dynamic filtering of data at runtime (e.g., applying a "soft delete" filter). Interceptors enable pre/post-event handling for persistence operations (e.g., logging changes). Custom types let developers define how Java objects are mapped to database columns, providing flexibility beyond JPA’s standard mappings.

    • Dialect System: Database-specific SQL generation

    • Hibernate uses a dialect system to generate SQL queries optimized for specific databases.

    • The dialect system abstracts database-specific SQL syntax and features. For example, Hibernate can generate MySQL-specific SQL when using the MySQLDialect or Oracle-specific SQL with OracleDialect. This ensures portability across different databases while leveraging their native capabilities.

  1. Performance Optimizations: Batch processing, connection management
    • Hibernate offers advanced performance optimizations such as batch processing and efficient connection management.
    • Batch processing allows multiple SQL statements to be executed in a single database round-trip, reducing latency. Connection management features like connection pooling and lazy loading optimize resource usage. These optimizations are crucial for handling large datasets and high-concurrency scenarios efficiently.

Hibernate acts as both a JPA provider (implementing the JPA specification) and an enhanced ORM framework with additional features. It bridges the gap between JPA's standardization and the need for advanced functionality, making it a powerful tool for object-relational mapping in Java applications.

docs.title: 1. Persistence Unit
docs.text:
Here we provide jpa provider name like hibernate

A Persistence Unit defines a set of entity classes that can be managed together under a shared configuration. It serves as the foundation for JPA (Java Persistence API) configuration and specifies how entities are persisted, retrieved, and managed.

<persistence-unit name="my-persistence-unit" transaction-type="RESOURCE_LOCAL">
    <!-- Provider: Specifies the JPA implementation -->
    <provider>org.hibernate.jpa.HibernatePersistenceProvider</provider>

    <!-- Entity Classes: List of entities managed by this unit -->
    <class>com.example.Employee</class>
    <class>com.example.Department</class>
    <!-- Other entities -->

    <!-- Properties: Configuration settings -->
    <properties>
        <!-- Connection properties -->
        <property name="javax.persistence.jdbc.url" value="jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/mydb"/>
        <property name="javax.persistence.jdbc.user" value="root"/>
        <property name="javax.persistence.jdbc.password" value="password"/>
        <property name="javax.persistence.jdbc.driver" value="com.mysql.cj.jdbc.Driver"/>

        <!-- Dialect: Database-specific SQL generation -->
        <property name="hibernate.dialect" value="org.hibernate.dialect.MySQLDialect"/>

        <!-- Cache Configuration -->
        <property name="hibernate.cache.use_second_level_cache" value="true"/>
    </properties>
</persistence-unit>

docs.title: Persistence Unit in Spring Boot JPA
docs.text:
In Spring Boot JPA , the need for a persistence.xml file is eliminated. Instead, Spring Boot simplifies configuration by using:

  1. application.properties or application.yml

    • Centralized configuration for database connections, JPA settings, and other properties.

    • Auto-Configuration

    • Spring Boot automatically configures the persistence unit based on the entities discovered in the application and the properties provided.

    • Custom Configuration Classes (Optional)

    • For advanced use cases, you can define custom configuration classes to override default behavior.

docs.title: 2. EntityManagerFactory
docs.text:
The EntityManagerFactory is a thread-safe, typically singleton factory responsible for creating EntityManager instances. It represents the runtime instantiation of a persistence unit and serves as the backbone of JPA operations.

  • In Spring Boot, the EntityManagerFactory is automatically created based on the application.properties file and entity scanning.

  • One Per Persistence Unit:

    • Each persistence unit defined in persistence.xml or configured in Spring Boot corresponds to exactly one EntityManagerFactory .

    • Thread-Safe and Expensive to Create:

    • The EntityManagerFactory is thread-safe and designed to be shared across the application.

    • Caching Metadata and Resources:

    • The EntityManagerFactory caches:

      • Entity metadata : Information about entity mappings, relationships, and annotations.
      • Prepared statements : Reusable SQL statements to improve performance.
      • Dialect information : Database-specific SQL generation rules.

    • Creates EntityManager Instances:

    • The EntityManagerFactory is responsible for creating lightweight, non-thread-safe EntityManager instances:

      EntityManager em = emf.createEntityManager();

docs.title: 3. EntityManager
docs.text:
The EntityManager is the ==primary interface for performing persistence operations on entities==.

It acts as the first point of interaction between the application code and the persistence context, enabling CRUD operations and managing the lifecycle of entities.

EntityManager em = emf.createEntityManager(); // Create an EntityManager instance
try {
    em.getTransaction().begin(); // Begin a transaction

    // Create and persist a new entity
    Employee emp = new Employee("John", "IT", 75000.0);
    em.persist(emp); // Make the entity managed

    em.getTransaction().commit(); // Commit the transaction
} finally {
    em.close(); // Close the EntityManager to release resources
}

docs.title: Key Characteristics of EntityManager
docs.text:
1. Not Thread-Safe (One Per Thread/Transaction):

    • The EntityManager is not thread-safe and should be used within a single thread or transaction.
    • Each thread or transaction typically requires its own EntityManager instance.

    • Lightweight and Short-Lived:

    • It is created for a specific operation or transaction and closed when no longer needed.

    • Manages Persistence Context:

    • The EntityManager manages a persistence context , which is a collection of entity instances being tracked by the EntityManager.

    • The EntityManager is responsible for managing the lifecycle and state transitions of entities

  2. Provides CRUD Operations:

    • The EntityManager provides methods for performing persistence operations:
      • persist(entity): Makes a transient entity persistent (inserts it into the database).
      • find(Class<T> entityClass, Object primaryKey): Retrieves an entity by its primary key.
      • remove(entity): Removes a managed entity from the database.
      • merge(entity): Merges the state of a detached entity into the persistence context.

    • Creates and Executes Queries:

    • The EntityManager allows you to create and execute queries using JPQL

      List<Employee> employees = em.createQuery("SELECT e FROM Employee e WHERE e.department = :dept", Employee.class)
                             .setParameter("dept", "IT")
                             .getResultList();

docs.title: 4. Persistence Context
docs.text: The Persistence Context is the runtime environment where entity instances are managed by the EntityManager . It acts as a bridge between the application and the database, ensuring efficient and consistent interaction.

docs.title: Role of the Persistence Context
docs.text:
1. Acts as a First-Level Cache:

    • The persistence context serves as a first-level cache , storing managed entities to reduce database queries.
    • When an entity is retrieved or persisted, it is stored in the persistence context. Subsequent requests for the same entity within the same context return the cached instance instead of querying the database again.

    • Tracks Changes to Entities (Dirty Checking):

    • The persistence context automatically tracks changes made to managed entities.

    • Within a single persistence context, there is only one instance of an entity for a given database row.
    Employee emp1 = em.find(Employee.class, 1L); // Fetches entity from database
Employee emp2 = em.find(Employee.class, 1L); // Returns the same instance (from the persistence context)
System.out.println(emp1 == emp2); // true

  2. Manages Entity State Transitions:

    • The persistence context manages the lifecycle and state transitions of entities:
        • New (Transient) : A newly created entity not yet associated with the persistence context. - Managed : An entity that is being tracked by the persistence context. - Detached : An entity no longer associated with the persistence context. - Removed : An entity scheduled for deletion from the database.

    • Can Be Transaction-Scoped or Extended:

    • Transaction-Scoped : The persistence context exists only for the duration of a single transaction.

    • Extended : The persistence context spans multiple transactions, typically used in stateful applications

EntityManager operations like insert, update , delate are transaction bounded, meaning it first check if transaction is open , if it isnt it throws exception While read operations arent transaction bound

docs.title: Transaction Manager:
docs.text: The Transaction Manager plays a critical role in coordinating transactions across resources (e.g., databases) and synchronizing them with the persistence context.

docs.title: 1. Transaction Manager and EntityManager:
docs.text:
transactions are managed by the container using JTA (Java Transaction API) . The EntityManager works in conjunction with the Container-Managed Transaction Manager .

  • The container-managed transaction manager handles transaction boundaries (begin, commit, rollback) automatically.

  • The EntityManager is injected into the application using @PersistenceContext

    @PersistenceContext
private EntityManager em;

    • When a method annotated with @Transactional (or equivalent) is invoked, the container starts a transaction and associates the persistence context with it.
    • The EntityManager interacts with the persistence context to manage entities and synchronize changes with the database.
    • At the end of the transaction, the container commits the transaction and flushes changes to the database.

    • The container manages the EntityManagerFactory and injects EntityManager instances into the application using @PersistenceUnit or @PersistenceContext.

        • The EntityManagerFactory is typically configured via persistence.xml or Spring Boot's application.properties.

docs.title: 5. Entity
docs.text:
Java classes annotated to be persisted to the database:

Represents a database record as a Java object.

docs.title: 6. Transaction Manager
docs.text:
Coordinates transactions across the persistence layer:

  • Ensures ACID properties
  • Coordinates with database transactions
  • Can be resource-local or JTA (Java Transaction API)
  • Defines boundaries of persistence context when transaction-scoped

docs.title: 7. Dialect
docs.text:
Adapts SQL generation to specific database platforms:

Role:

  • Encapsulates database-specific SQL syntax
  • Handles pagination, locking, sequences
  • Determines supported features
  • Maps Java types to database-specific type

docs.title: 8. JPQL (Java Persistence Query Language)
docs.text:
An object-oriented query language similar to SQL but operates on entities:

// JPQL query
TypedQuery<Employee> query = em.createQuery(
    "SELECT e FROM Employee e WHERE e.department = :dept", Employee.class);
query.setParameter("dept", "IT");
List<Employee> employees = query.getResultList();
  • Provides database-agnostic query capabilities
  • Operates on entity model rather than tables
  • Supports joins, aggregations, projections
  • Can be statically defined or dynamically built

docs.title: 1. New/Transient
docs.text:
• Entity instance created with new operator
  • No persistent identity (no database record)
  • Not associated with persistence context
  • Changes not tracked
  • Will be garbage collected if not referenced
  • No database operations triggered by changes
// New/Transient entity
Employee employee = new Employee("Jane", "HR", 85000.0);
// No database impact yet

docs.title: 2. Managed/Persistent
docs.text:
• Has persistent identity (primary key)
  • Associated with an active persistence context
  • Changes automatically detected and synchronized (dirty checking)
  • Lazy loading of associations possible
  • Will be saved to database on flush/commit
// Making entity managed
em.persist(employee);  // Now it's managed

// Or retrieving managed entity
Employee managed = em.find(Employee.class, 1L);
managed.setSalary(90000.0);  // Change tracked automatically

docs.title: 3. Detached
docs.text:
• Has persistent identity (was previously persisted)
  • No longer associated with persistence context
  • Changes not tracked or synchronized
  • Lazy associations may not be accessible
  • Can be reattached via merge()
// Detaching entity
em.detach(managed);  // Now it's detached
managed.setDepartment("Finance");  // Change not tracked

// Or when closing EntityManager
em.close();  // All entities become detached

docs.title: 4. Removed
docs.text:
• Scheduled for deletion from database
  • Still managed until flush/commit
  • Will be deleted upon transaction commit
  • Can be reactivated via persist()
// Marking entity for removal
em.remove(managed);  // Scheduled for deletion
// Will be deleted on commit

docs.title: Lifecycle Callback Methods
docs.text: Entities can define methods that are called when lifecycle events occur:

docs.title: The Persistence Operations Flow
docs.text:
how persistence operations flow through the architecture:

Persist Operation

2. Find Operation:

3. Update Operation:

4. Delete Operation:

docs.title: What is First-Level Caching?
docs.text:
First-level caching (also called the persistence context cache) is JPA's automatic caching mechanism that operates within a single EntityManager instance. It's not something you turn on or off—it's always working for you.

  • Automatic Identity Management: When you load an entity, JPA remembers it. If you ask for the same entity again (by ID), JPA returns the instance from memory rather than hitting the database.

  • Dirty Checking: JPA tracks changes to managed entities, so it knows exactly what fields to update when you commit. 

    @Transactional
public void giveRaise(Long employeeId, double percentage) {
    Employee emp = entityManager.find(Employee.class, employeeId);

    // Modify the entity
    double currentSalary = emp.getSalary();
    double newSalary = currentSalary * (1 + percentage/100);
    emp.setSalary(newSalary);

    // No need to call update/save/merge!
    // When transaction commits, changes are detected and SQL UPDATE is generated
}

  • Repeatable Reads: You get consistent views of your data within a transaction, even if the database changes underneath.

    • The first-level cache ensures you get consistent views of your data within a transaction 

      @Transactional
public void consistencyExample(Long employeeId) {
    Employee emp1 = entityManager.find(Employee.class, employeeId);

    // Even if another transaction updates this employee in the database now,
    // we'll still see our original version within this transaction

    Employee emp2 = entityManager.find(Employee.class, employeeId);
    // emp1 and emp2 are identical, regardless of external changes

    // To see updates from the database:
    entityManager.refresh(emp1);
}

docs.title: Scope: Per EntityManager (Usually Per Request)
docs.text: The first-level cache is tied to a specific EntityManager instance. In web applications, that typically means one cache per HTTP request:

docs.title: Why We Need Second-Level Caching
docs.text:
First-level caching is fantastic, but it has fundamental limitations based on its design. Think of it as having a notebook that you throw away after each conversation. What if you want to remember things across multiple conversations?

Here's why second-level caching becomes necessary:

docs.title: Limitations of First-Level Cache
docs.text:
1. Request-Scoped: First-level cache is bound to a specific EntityManager instance. In web applications, this typically means it only lasts for the duration of a single HTTP request.
  2. No Cross-Request Sharing: When a user makes a second request, we fetch the same data all over again, even if nothing has changed.
  3. No Application-Wide Sharing: Different users requesting the same data (like product information in an e-commerce site) each get their own copy from the database.
  4. Resource Duplication: The same entity might be loaded hundreds of times across different user sessions, consuming unnecessary memory and database resources.

Imagine running an e-commerce site where thousands of users view the same products. With only first-level caching, we'd hit the database for each user's request to see that bestselling laptop - even though the data rarely changes!

Second-level cache solves these problems by providing a shared cache that spans:

  • Multiple EntityManager instances
  • Multiple user sessions
  • Multiple HTTP requests
  • The entire application's lifetime (or until cache entries expire)

It sits between your application and the database, reducing load and improving response times for data that's frequently read but rarely changed.

docs.title: Enabling Second-Level Cache in Spring Boot
docs.text:
Setting up second-level caching in Spring Boot involves several steps. 

  <!-- JCache API -->
    <dependency>
        <groupId>javax.cache</groupId>
        <artifactId>cache-api</artifactId>
    </dependency>


 <!-- Hibernate JCache integration -->
    <dependency>
        <groupId>org.hibernate</groupId>
        <artifactId>hibernate-jcache</artifactId>
    </dependency>


 <!-- Caffeine cache provider -->
    <dependency>
        <groupId>com.github.ben-manes.caffeine</groupId>
        <artifactId>caffeine</artifactId>
    </dependency>


  <!-- Caffeine JCache implementation -->
    <dependency>
        <groupId>com.github.ben-manes.caffeine</groupId>
        <artifactId>jcache</artifactId>
    </dependency>

docs.title: Why Each Dependency?
docs.text:
1. cache-api (JCache/JSR-107): This is the standard Java caching API that provides a common interface for different cache implementations. It's like a universal adapter that allows Hibernate to work with any compliant cache provider.
  2. hibernate-jcache: This is Hibernate's integration with the JCache standard. It allows Hibernate to use any JCache-compliant provider for second-level caching.
  3. caffeine: This is a high-performance, near-optimal caching library. It's known for its speed and efficiency in memory management.
  4. jcache (Caffeine's JCache implementation): This allows Caffeine to implement the JCache API, making it compatible with Hibernate's JCache integration.

docs.title: Configure Hibernate Properties
docs.text:
Next, we need to configure Hibernate to use second-level caching in our application.properties or application.yml file:

# Enable second level cache
spring.jpa.properties.hibernate.cache.use_second_level_cache=true

# Specify the cache region factory
spring.jpa.properties.hibernate.cache.region.factory_class=org.hibernate.cache.jcache.JCacheRegionFactory

# Optionally enable query cache (for caching HQL/JPQL query results)
spring.jpa.properties.hibernate.cache.use_query_cache=true


# Configure Caffeine as our provider
spring.cache.type=caffeine
spring.cache.caffeine.spec=maximumSize=1000,expireAfterWrite=300s

docs.title: Step 3: Configure Cache Regions with Caffeine
docs.text:
We can create a configuration class to define specific cache settings for different entities:

@Configuration
public class CacheConfig {

    @Bean
    public CacheManager cacheManager() {
        // Create Caffeine cache manager
        CaffeineCacheManager cacheManager = new CaffeineCacheManager();

        // Set default cache configuration
        cacheManager.setCaffeine(Caffeine.newBuilder()
                .expireAfterWrite(30, TimeUnit.MINUTES)
                .maximumSize(1000));

        // Add specific cache names we want to create
        cacheManager.setCacheNames(Arrays.asList(
                "com.example.entity.Product",
                "com.example.entity.Category",
                "query.findAllActiveProducts"
        ));

        return cacheManager;
    }

docs.title: Step 4: Mark Entities as Cacheable
docs.text:
Now, we need to tell Hibernate which entities we want to cache:

@Entity
@Table(name = "products")
@Cacheable
@org.hibernate.annotations.Cache(usage = CacheConcurrencyStrategy.READ_WRITE)
public class Product {
    @Id
    @GeneratedValue(strategy = GenerationType.IDENTITY)
    private Long id;

    private String name;
    private String description;
    private BigDecimal price;
    private boolean active;

    // Collections need their own cache settings
    @OneToMany(mappedBy = "product")
    @org.hibernate.annotations.Cache(usage = CacheConcurrencyStrategy.READ_WRITE)
    private Set<Review> reviews = new HashSet<>();

    // Getters and setters...
}

@Entity
@Table(name = "categories")
@Cacheable
@org.hibernate.annotations.Cache(usage = CacheConcurrencyStrategy.READ_ONLY)
public class Category {
    @Id
    @GeneratedValue(strategy = GenerationType.IDENTITY)
    private Long id;

    private String name;

    // Getters and setters...
}

docs.title: Step 5: Cache Queries When Needed
docs.text:
For frequently executed queries whose results change infrequently:

@Repository
public class ProductRepository extends JpaRepository<Product, Long> {

    @QueryHints({
        @QueryHint(name = "org.hibernate.cacheable", value = "true"),
        @QueryHint(name = "org.hibernate.cacheRegion", value = "query.findAllActiveProducts")
    })
    @Query("SELECT p FROM Product p WHERE p.active = true")
    List<Product> findAllActiveProducts();
}

docs.title: Cache Regions and Region Factory
docs.text: Cache regions in Hibernate are logical partitions for storing different types of cached data. Think of them as separate drawers in a filing cabinet, each dedicated to specific types of documents.

docs.title: What Are Cache Regions?
docs.text:
1. Entity Regions: Each entity class gets its own region, typically named after the fully qualified class name (e.g., com.example.entity.Product).
  2. Collection Regions: For cached collections, named after the entity class and collection field (e.g., com.example.entity.Product.reviews).
  3. Query Regions: For cached query results, either using default name or a custom one.
  4. Custom Regions: You can define special-purpose regions with custom names.

docs.title: The Region Factory
docs.text:
The Region Factory is the component responsible for creating and managing these cache regions. It's the bridge between Hibernate and the underlying cache provider.

In our case, we're using JCacheRegionFactory, which connects Hibernate to any JCache-compatible provider

docs.title: Configuring Caffeine Cache Policies
docs.text: Caffeine provides several configuration options that let us fine-tune caching behavior. Here are the key parameters:

docs.title: Size-Based Eviction
docs.text:
Controls how many entries the cache can hold:

// Maximum entries in the cache
.maximumSize(1000)

docs.title: Time-Based Eviction
docs.text:
Controls how long entries stay in the cache:

// Expire after last write
.expireAfterWrite(30, TimeUnit.MINUTES)

// Expire after last access
.expireAfterAccess(1, TimeUnit.HOURS)

docs.title: Understanding Cache Concurrency Basics
docs.text:
Think of the second-level cache as a shared resource that multiple sessions can access simultaneously. Without proper coordination, this could lead to data inconsistency issues such as:

  1. One transaction updates an entity while another reads a stale version
  2. Two transactions update the same entity at the same time
  3. A transaction reads partially updated data

To address these challenges, Hibernate offers several concurrency strategies that provide different trade-offs between performance and data consistency.

docs.title: 1. Read-Only Strategy
docs.text:
The simplest and highest-performing strategy for data that never changes.

How it works:

  • The cache assumes the data never changes
  • Multiple sessions can read the same cached data concurrently
  • Any attempt to update a read-only entity will throw an exception
  • Extremely high performance as there's no locking or synchronization

Best used for:

  • Reference data (countries, currencies)
  • Configuration settings that rarely change
  • Historical records that are immutable

docs.title: 2. Read-Write Strategy
docs.text:
Provides strong consistency for frequently updated data while maintaining good read performance.

How it works:

  • Uses a "soft locking" mechanism
  • When an entity is being updated, it's marked with a lock in the cache
  • Other sessions bypass the cache and go directly to the database when they see a lock
  • After the update transaction completes, the cache is updated with the new version
  • Provides strong consistency but with more overhead than read-only

Best used for:

  • Frequently accessed data that changes regularly
  • When strong consistency is required
  • Balance between read performance and write consistency

docs.title: 3. Nonstrict Read-Write Strategy
docs.text: A simple approach for data that is occasionally updated and where occasional stale reads are acceptable.

docs.title: 4. Transactional Strategy
docs.text: The strongest consistency model, using the underlying cache provider's transactional capabilities.

docs.title: Implementation Details
docs.text:
Here's how we can configure these strategies in your Hibernate application:

@Entity
@Cacheable
@org.hibernate.annotations.Cache(
    usage = CacheConcurrencyStrategy.READ_WRITE,  // Choose your strategy
    region = "product"
)
public class Product {
    // Entity definition
}


# Enable second-level cache
hibernate.cache.use_second_level_cache=true

# Specify cache provider
hibernate.cache.region.factory_class=org.hibernate.cache.jcache.JCacheRegionFactory

# For EHCache specifically
hibernate.javax.cache.provider=org.ehcache.jsr107.EhcacheCachingProvider

# Configure regions with specific TTL
hibernate.javax.cache.uri=ehcache.xml

docs.title: Choosing the Right Strategy
docs.text:
The best strategy depends on your specific use case:

  1. Read-Only: Use when data never changes or is updated outside the application
  2. Nonstrict Read-Write: Use for infrequently updated data where occasional stale reads are acceptable
  3. Read-Write: Use for frequently updated data requiring strong consistency
    1. Transactional: Use for critical data requiring transaction isolation

docs.title: Basic Concept
docs.text:
A one-to-one unidirectional relationship is where one entity references another, but not vice versa. Think of it like a person having a passport - the person knows about their passport, but the passport doesn't know about its owner (in the database relationship sense).

example using Employee and EmployeeDetail entities:

@Entity
@Table(name = "employee")
public class Employee {
    @Id
    @GeneratedValue(strategy = GenerationType.IDENTITY)
    private Long id;

    private String name;

    @OneToOne(cascade = CascadeType.ALL)
    @JoinColumn(name = "detail_id")
    private EmployeeDetail employeeDetail;

    // getters and setters
}

observe we only mentioned the reference table name here and by default it will treat primary key of that table as foreign key in the current table

@Entity
@Table(name = "employee_detail")
public class EmployeeDetail {
    @Id
    @GeneratedValue(strategy = GenerationType.IDENTITY)
    private Long id;

    private String address;
    private String phoneNumber;

    // getters and setters
}

In this example, the Employee has a reference to EmployeeDetail, but EmployeeDetail doesn't know about the Employee. This is a unidirectional relationship.

In this example, the Employee has a reference to EmployeeDetail, but EmployeeDetail doesn't know about the Employee. This is a unidirectional relationship.

The SQL that Hibernate generates for the employee table will look something like:

CREATE TABLE employee (
    id BIGINT NOT NULL AUTO_INCREMENT,
    name VARCHAR(255),
    detail_id BIGINT,
    PRIMARY KEY (id),
    FOREIGN KEY (detail_id) REFERENCES employee_detail(id)
);

 If we want to use our own custom name for the join column, we can specify it in the @JoinColumn annotation:

@OneToOne(cascade = CascadeType.ALL)
@JoinColumn(name = "employee_detail_fk", referencedColumnName = "id")
private EmployeeDetail employeeDetail;

This would create a foreign key column named employee_detail_fk instead of the default detail_id.

docs.title: One-to-One with Composite Key
docs.text:
When dealing with composite keys, we need to use a different approach. Let's create an example with a composite key:

A composite key is defined using @Embeddable and embedded into the entity using @EmbeddedId

@Embeddable
public class EmployeeDetailId implements Serializable {
    private String departmentCode;
    private String employeeCode;

    // constructors, equals, hashCode
}


@Entity
@Table(name = "employee_detail")
public class EmployeeDetail {
    @EmbeddedId
    private EmployeeDetailId id;

    private String address;
    private String phoneNumber;

    // getters and setters
}

 In below case, we use @JoinColumns (plural) to specify multiple join columns, one for each component of the composite key.

i.e. The @OneToOne relationship is established using @JoinColumns to map multiple columns in the owning entity (Employee) to the composite key of the target entity (EmployeeDetail).

@Entity
@Table(name = "employee")
public class Employee {
    @Id
    @GeneratedValue(strategy = GenerationType.IDENTITY)
    private Long id;

    private String name;

    @OneToOne(cascade = CascadeType.ALL)
    @JoinColumns({
        @JoinColumn(name = "department_code", referencedColumnName = "departmentCode"),
        @JoinColumn(name = "employee_code", referencedColumnName = "employeeCode")
    })
    private EmployeeDetail employeeDetail;

    // getters and setters
}

docs.title: Cascade Types in Detail
docs.text: Cascade operations determine what happens to related entities when you perform an operation on the owning entity. Let's explore each type:

docs.title: CascadeType.PERSIST
docs.text:
This cascades the persist (save) operation from the owning entity to the related entity.

@OneToOne(cascade = CascadeType.PERSIST)
private EmployeeDetail employeeDetail;

With this setting, if you save an Employee, the associated EmployeeDetail will also be saved:

EmployeeDetail detail = new EmployeeDetail();
detail.setAddress("123 Main St");
detail.setPhoneNumber("555-1234");

Employee employee = new Employee();
employee.setName("John Doe");
employee.setEmployeeDetail(detail);

// Only need to save the employee, the detail will be saved too
employeeRepository.save(employee);

docs.title: CascadeType.MERGE
docs.text:
This cascades the merge operation, which is useful when you want to update both the owning and related entities.

@OneToOne(cascade = CascadeType.MERGE)
private EmployeeDetail employeeDetail;


Employee employee = employeeRepository.findById(1L).get();
employee.setName("Jane Doe");
employee.getEmployeeDetail().setAddress("456 Oak St");

// Updates both employee and employeeDetail
employeeRepository.save(employee);

docs.title: CascadeType.REMOVE
docs.text:
This cascades the remove operation, deleting the related entity when the owning entity is deleted.

@OneToOne(cascade = CascadeType.REMOVE)
private EmployeeDetail employeeDetail;


Employee employee = employeeRepository.findById(1L).get();
employeeRepository.delete(employee); // This will also delete the associated EmployeeDetail

docs.title: CascadeType.REFRESH
docs.text:
This cascades the refresh operation, which reloads the state of both the owning and related entities from the database.

meaning it will skip the first level cache check and will directly fetch from db

docs.title: CascadeType.ALL
docs.text: This cascades all operations (PERSIST, MERGE, REMOVE, REFRESH, DETACH).

docs.title: Fetch Types: Eager vs. Lazy Loading
docs.text: While cascade types control what happens with write operations, fetch types control how entities are loaded from the database.(concerns loading of child entities if parent owning entity is loaded)

docs.title: Eager Loading
docs.text:
Eager loading loads the related entity immediately when the owning entity is loaded.

@OneToOne(fetch = FetchType.EAGER)
private EmployeeDetail employeeDetail;

This is the default for @OneToOne and @ManyToOne relationships.

Example:

Employee employee = employeeRepository.findById(1L).get();
// employeeDetail is already loaded
String address = employee.getEmployeeDetail().getAddress();

docs.title: Lazy Loading
docs.text:
Lazy loading defers loading the related entity until it's actually accessed.

@OneToOne(fetch = FetchType.LAZY)
private EmployeeDetail employeeDetail;

This is the default for @OneToMany and @ManyToMany relationships.

Employee employee = employeeRepository.findById(1L).get();
// employeeDetail is not loaded yet

// This will trigger a database query to load the employeeDetail
String address = employee.getEmployeeDetail().getAddress();

docs.title: The LazyInitializationException Problem
docs.text: When using lazy loading, you might encounter a LazyInitializationException when trying to access a lazily loaded entity outside of a transaction:

docs.title: 6.2 OneToMany ManyToOne
docs.text:
OneToMany Unidirectional 15:45 -

Orphan Removal 24:36 -

OneToMany Bidirectional 38:09 -

ManyToOne Unidirectional and Bidirectional 42:19 -

ManyToMany Unidirectional 53:41 - ManyToMany Bidirectional

docs.title: 7. JPQL, Derived Query, N+1 Problem, Joins, Pagina
What is a Servlet?
docs.text:
A servlet is a Java class that handles HTTP requests and generates responses. Think of it as the foundational building block for Java-based web applications.

At its core, a servlet:

  1. Receives HTTP requests from clients (browsers, mobile apps, etc.)
  2. Processes those requests (often interacting with databases or other services)
  3. Returns HTTP responses (HTML, JSON, XML, etc.)

Here's what a basic servlet looks like:

public class HelloServlet extends HttpServlet {
    @Override
    protected void doGet(HttpServletRequest request, HttpServletResponse response) 
            throws ServletException, IOException {
        response.setContentType("text/html");
        PrintWriter out = response.getWriter();
        out.println("<html><body>");
        out.println("<h1>Hello, World!</h1>");
        out.println("</body></html>");
    }
}

Let's understand how the servlet environment works:

  1. Servlet Container: Also known as a web container (like Tomcat, Jetty, or WebSphere), it provides the runtime environment for servlets.
  2. Deployment Descriptor (web.xml): This configuration file maps URLs to specific servlets:
<servlet>
    <servlet-name>HelloServlet</servlet-name>
    <servlet-class>com.example.HelloServlet</servlet-class>
</servlet>
<servlet-mapping>
    <servlet-name>HelloServlet</servlet-name>
    <url-pattern>/hello</url-pattern>
</servlet-mapping>

Servlet Lifecycle:

  • init(): Called once when the servlet is loaded
  • service(): Called for each request (which delegates to methods like doGet or doPost)
  • destroy(): Called when the servlet is unloaded

docs.title: The Problem with Raw Servlets
docs.text:
When building complex applications with raw servlets, several challenges emerge:

  • Writing HTML directly in Java code is cumbersome
  • Mapping different URLs to different servlets becomes complex
  • Sharing data between servlets is difficult
  • Handling forms and file uploads requires a lot of boilerplate code
  • No built-in dependency injection or component management

This is where frameworks like Spring emerged to solve these problems.

Here is basic request and response flow with servelt

docs.title: 2. Spring MVC
docs.text:
Spring MVC (Model-View-Controller) is built on top of the servlet API but provides a more structured approach:

  • DispatcherServlet: This is Spring's front controller pattern implementation. It's a special servlet that handles all incoming requests and dispatches them to appropriate handlers.
  • Handler Mapping: Determines which controller should handle a request based on URL patterns.
  • Controllers: Instead of extending HttpServlet, you create classes with annotations like @Controller.
  • View Resolvers: Convert logical view names to actual view implementations (like JSP pages or Thymeleaf templates).

Here's a Spring MVC controller example:

@Controller
public class HelloController {
    @GetMapping("/hello")
    public String hello(Model model) {
        model.addAttribute("message", "Hello, World!");
        return "greeting"; // This refers to a view template named "greeting"
    }
}

docs.title: Spring's DispatcherServlet: The Central Component
docs.text:
==The DispatcherServlet is the heart of Spring MVC and is itself a servlet. It replaces the need to create multiple servlets for different endpoints.==

Here's how it's configured in a traditional Spring application:

Web.xml

<!-- web.xml -->
<servlet>
    <servlet-name>dispatcher</servlet-name>
    <servlet-class>org.springframework.web.servlet.DispatcherServlet</servlet-class>
    <init-param>
        <param-name>contextConfigLocation</param-name>
        <param-value>/WEB-INF/spring-mvc-config.xml</param-value>
    </init-param>
    <load-on-startup>1</load-on-startup>
</servlet>

<servlet-mapping>
    <servlet-name>dispatcher</servlet-name>
    <url-pattern>/</url-pattern>
</servlet-mapping>

docs.title: Request Processing in the DispatcherServlet
docs.text:
When a request arrives, the DispatcherServlet follows this process:

  1. Consults the HandlerMapping to identify the appropriate controller
  2. Invokes the controller method to process the request
  3. The controller returns a logical view name and model data
  4. The ViewResolver translates the logical view name to an actual View implementation
  5. The View renders the model data into the response

docs.title: 3.0 Spring boot spring web
docs.text:
Spring Boot greatly simplifies this setup. With Spring Boot:

  1. The DispatcherServlet is auto-configured
  2. No need for explicit web.xml or other servlet configurations
  3. Embedded servlet containers (Tomcat, Jetty, Undertow) eliminate the need for separate deployment

In Spring Boot, a minimal web application looks like:  

@SpringBootApplication
public class Application {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SpringApplication.run(Application.class, args);
    }
}

@RestController
class HelloController {
    @GetMapping("/hello")
    public String hello() {
        return "Hello, World!";
    }
}

docs.title: The Complete Picture: Spring's Request Handling Stack:
docs.text:
'

The complete Spring web stack includes:

  1. Filters: Intercept requests before they reach the DispatcherServlet (useful for authentication, logging, etc.)
  2. DispatcherServlet: The central servlet that coordinates the request handling
  3. Handler Mappings: Match requests to handlers (controllers)
  4. Handler Adapters: Execute the handlers and adapt their return values
  5. Controllers: Process requests and return responses
  6. View Resolvers and Views: Convert controller responses to HTTP responses
  7. Exception Handlers: Process exceptions that occur during request handling

docs.title: Spring Security Integration
docs.text:
Spring Security integrates with this architecture through filters:

@Configuration
@EnableWebSecurity
public class SecurityConfig extends WebSecurityConfigurerAdapter {
    @Override
    protected void configure(HttpSecurity http) throws Exception {
        http
            .authorizeRequests()
                .antMatchers("/public/**").permitAll()
                .anyRequest().authenticated()
            .and()
            .formLogin();
    }
}

These security filters are added to the filter chain that processes requests before they reach the DispatcherServlet.

docs.title: Spring Boot Auto-Configuration
docs.text:
Spring Boot's magic comes from auto-configuration. When you add spring-boot-starter-web to your project:

  1. It detects that you want a web application
  2. Automatically configures an embedded servlet container (Tomcat by default)
  3. Sets up the DispatcherServlet and required components
  4. Configures a default error page

docs.title: From Raw Servlets to Modern Spring Boot
docs.text:
Let's trace the evolution:

  1. Raw Servlets: Manual handling of HTTP requests and responses 

    public class MyServlet extends HttpServlet {
    protected void doGet(HttpServletRequest request, HttpServletResponse response) {
        // Manual processing
    }
}

2. Spring MVC with XML Configuration: Structured approach but still verbose

<servlet>
    <servlet-name>dispatcher</servlet-name>
    <servlet-class>org.springframework.web.servlet.DispatcherServlet</servlet-class>
</servlet>

3. Spring MVC with Java Configuration: More concise but still requires setup

@Configuration
@EnableWebMvc
public class WebConfig implements WebMvcConfigurer {
    // Configuration methods
}

   4. Spring Boot: Minimal configuration with sensible defaults

considering we have added spring web starter dependencies

@SpringBootApplication
public class Application {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SpringApplication.run(Application.class, args);
    }
}

  • Servlets are Java classes that handle HTTP requests and responses.
  • Servlet Containers (like Tomcat) provide the runtime environment for servlets.
  • Spring MVC builds on servlets, with the DispatcherServlet as its front controller.
  • Spring Boot simplifies Spring MVC configuration by auto-configuring the servlet environment.

docs.title: 4. Filters and Interceptors
docs.text:
Filters and Interceptors are used to perform pre-processing and post-processing tasks for HTTP requests. However, they operate at different levels and serve distinct purposes.

Basic flow and placement

docs.title: What are Filters?
docs.text:
• Filters are part of the Servlet API , which is a lower-level API than Spring MVC.
  • They are executed before the request reaches the DispatcherServlet (the core component of Spring MVC).
  • Filters can be applied to any request that hits the servlet container, regardless of whether it is handled by Spring or not.

docs.title: Key Characteristics of Filters
docs.text:
1. Scope : Filters apply to all incoming requests at the servlet container level.
  2. Execution Timing : Filters are executed before the DispatcherServlet processes the request and after the response is generated.
  3. Use Cases :
    • Logging and auditing.
    • Authentication and authorization.
    • Modifying the request/response headers or body.
    • Compression or encryption of data.
  4. Configuration : Filters are typically configured in web.xml or via Java-based configuration using FilterRegistrationBean.

docs.title: How Filters Work
docs.text:
• A filter intercepts the request and response objects.
  • It can modify these objects, perform checks, or even block the request entirely.
  • After processing, the filter passes the request and response to the next filter in the chain or to the DispatcherServlet.

docs.title: What are Interceptors?
docs.text:
• Interceptors are part of the Spring MVC framework and operate at a higher level than filters.
  • They are executed after the DispatcherServlet  has mapped the request to a specific handler (controller method).

docs.title: Key Characteristics of Interceptors
docs.text:
1. Scope : Interceptors apply only to requests handled by Spring MVC (i.e., requests mapped to controllers).
  2. Execution Timing : Interceptors are executed during the request processing lifecycle managed by Spring MVC.
  3. Use Cases :
    • Adding or modifying model attributes.
    • Performing pre- and post-processing logic for controller methods.
    • Validating request parameters or headers.
    • Logging and performance monitoring.
  4. Configuration : Interceptors are registered with the WebMvcConfigurer interface or through XML configuration.

docs.title: How Interceptors Work
docs.text:
• An interceptor can execute logic before the controller method is invoked (preHandle), after the controller method but before the view is rendered (postHandle), and after the view is rendered (afterCompletion).
  • Interceptors have access to the HandlerMethod (the controller method) and the ModelAndView object, making them more

docs.title: Differences Between Filters and Interceptors
docs.text: Feature Filters Interceptors Framework Part of Servlet API Part of Spring MVC Scope Applies to all requests Applies to Spring MVC-handled requests Execution Timing Before DispatcherServlet After DispatcherServlet Access to Context Limited toHttpServletRequestandHttpServletResponse Access toHandlerMethod,ModelAndView, and other Spring-specific objects Use Cases General-purpose tasks like security, logging, compression Controller-specific tasks like modifying models, validating inputs Configuration Configured viaweb.xmlorFilterRegistrationBean Configured via <span style="color: #eb5757;">WebMvcConfigurer</span>

docs.title: Flow of Request Processing in Spring Web
docs.text:
1. Request Received by Servlet Container :

    • The request first hits the servlet container (e.g., Tomcat).
    • Filters are executed in the order they are defined.

    • DispatcherServlet :

    • After passing through all filters, the request reaches the DispatcherServlet.

    • The DispatcherServlet maps the request to a specific controller method.

    • Interceptor Execution :

    • Once the request is mapped to a controller method, interceptors are executed in the following phases:

      • preHandle: Before the controller method is invoked.
      • postHandle: After the controller method is invoked but before the view is rendered.
      • afterCompletion: After the view is rendered.

    • Response Sent Back :

    • The response flows back through the interceptors and filters in reverse order.

      • we can apply post processing when the response is flowing back

docs.title: Deep Dive: Implementing Filters in Spring Boot
docs.text: Filters in Spring Boot are part of the Servlet API and provide a powerful mechanism to intercept and manipulate HTTP requests and responses before they reach your controllers or after they leave them. Let's explore this in detail.

docs.title: 1. Understanding the Filter Lifecycle
docs.text:
A filter goes through three main lifecycle methods:

  • init(): Called once when the filter is initialized
  • doFilter(): Called for every request
  • destroy(): Called when the filter is being taken out of service

docs.title: Step 1: Create Filter Class
docs.text:
import javax.servlet.*;
import javax.servlet.http.HttpServletRequest;
import java.io.IOException;

@Component
public class LoggingFilter implements Filter {

    @Override
    public void init(FilterConfig filterConfig) throws ServletException {
        // Initialization code - runs once when application starts
        System.out.println("LoggingFilter initialized");
    }

    @Override
    public void doFilter(ServletRequest request, ServletResponse response, FilterChain chain)
            throws IOException, ServletException {

        // Cast to HttpServletRequest to get HTTP-specific methods
        HttpServletRequest httpRequest = (HttpServletRequest) request;

        // 1. Pre-processing - before the request reaches the controller
        long startTime = System.currentTimeMillis();
        System.out.println("Request started for: " + httpRequest.getRequestURI());
        System.out.println("Request method: " + httpRequest.getMethod());

        // Continue the filter chain
        chain.doFilter(request, response);

        // 2. Post-processing - after the response is generated
        long duration = System.currentTimeMillis() - startTime;
        System.out.println("Request completed in " + duration + "ms");
    }

    @Override
    public void destroy() {
        // Cleanup code - runs when application is shutting down
        System.out.println("LoggingFilter destroyed");
    }
}

docs.title: Step 2: Understanding the doFilter Method
docs.text:
The doFilter method is where the main logic resides:

  1. The code before chain.doFilter() executes before the request reaches your controllers
  2. chain.doFilter() passes the request to the next filter in the chain or to the servlet
  3. The code after chain.doFilter() executes after the response is generated

docs.title: Adding Request/Response Wrappers
docs.text:
To modify request/response content, you need wrapper classes:

import javax.servlet.*;
import javax.servlet.http.HttpServletRequest;
import javax.servlet.http.HttpServletRequestWrapper;
import java.io.*;

public class CachingRequestWrapper extends HttpServletRequestWrapper {
    private byte[] body;

    public CachingRequestWrapper(HttpServletRequest request) throws IOException {
        super(request);
        this.body = request.getInputStream().readAllBytes();
    }

    @Override
    public ServletInputStream getInputStream() {
        return new ServletInputStream() {
            private final ByteArrayInputStream bais = new ByteArrayInputStream(body);

            @Override
            public boolean isFinished() {
                return bais.available() == 0;
            }

            @Override
            public boolean isReady() {
                return true;
            }

            @Override
            public void setReadListener(ReadListener listener) {
                throw new UnsupportedOperationException();
            }

            @Override
            public int read() throws IOException {
                return bais.read();
            }
        };
    }
}

docs.title: Using the Wrapper in Filter
docs.text:
@Override
public void doFilter(ServletRequest request, ServletResponse response, FilterChain chain)
        throws IOException, ServletException {

    HttpServletRequest httpRequest = (HttpServletRequest) request;

    // Wrap the request to enable multiple reads of the input stream
    CachingRequestWrapper wrappedRequest = new CachingRequestWrapper(httpRequest);

    // Log request body
    String requestBody = new String(wrappedRequest.getBody());
    System.out.println("Request body: " + requestBody);

    // Continue with the wrapped request
    chain.doFilter(wrappedRequest, response);
}

docs.title: Method 1: Using @Component (Automatic Registration)
docs.text:
@Component
@Order(1) // Lower numbers have higher priority
public class FirstFilter implements Filter {
    // implementation
}

@Component
@Order(2)
public class SecondFilter implements Filter {
    // implementation
}

docs.title: Method 2: Manual Registration via Configuration
docs.text:
@Configuration
public class FilterConfig {

    @Bean
    public FilterRegistrationBean<LoggingFilter> loggingFilter() {
        FilterRegistrationBean<LoggingFilter> registrationBean = new FilterRegistrationBean<>();

        registrationBean.setFilter(new LoggingFilter());
        registrationBean.addUrlPatterns("/api/*");
        registrationBean.setOrder(1);

        return registrationBean;
    }
}

docs.title: Authentication Filter
docs.text:
@Component
public class AuthFilter implements Filter {

    @Override
    public void doFilter(ServletRequest request, ServletResponse response, FilterChain chain)
            throws IOException, ServletException {

        HttpServletRequest httpRequest = (HttpServletRequest) request;
        String authHeader = httpRequest.getHeader("Authorization");

        if (!isValidToken(authHeader)) {
            HttpServletResponse httpResponse = (HttpServletResponse) response;
            httpResponse.sendError(HttpServletResponse.SC_UNAUTHORIZED, "Invalid token");
            return;
        }

        chain.doFilter(request, response);
    }

    private boolean isValidToken(String token) {
        // Implement your token validation logic
        return token != null && token.startsWith("Bearer ");
    }
}

docs.title: CORS Filter
docs.text:
@Component
public class CorsFilter implements Filter {

    @Override
    public void doFilter(ServletRequest request, ServletResponse response, FilterChain chain)
            throws IOException, ServletException {

        HttpServletResponse httpResponse = (HttpServletResponse) response;
        httpResponse.setHeader("Access-Control-Allow-Origin", "*");
        httpResponse.setHeader("Access-Control-Allow-Methods", "GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, OPTIONS");
        httpResponse.setHeader("Access-Control-Allow-Headers", "Content-Type, Authorization");
        httpResponse.setHeader("Access-Control-Max-Age", "3600");

        chain.doFilter(request, response);
    }
}

docs.title: 6. Testing Your Filter
docs.text:
Create a simple test controller:

@RestController
@RequestMapping("/test")
public class TestController {

    @GetMapping
    public String testEndpoint() {
        return "Hello from test endpoint";
    }
}

Then make requests and check your console/logs to verify the filter is working.

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Keep filters lightweight - They execute for every request
  2. Be careful with request/response wrapping - It consumes memory
  3. Use proper ordering for filters that depend on each other
  4. Consider using interceptors for Spring-specific functionality
  5. Handle exceptions properly in filters to avoid breaking the chain

Filters are powerful tools for cross-cutting concerns in your Spring Boot application. They operate at a lower level than Spring MVC interceptors, giving you more control over the HTTP request/response cycle.

docs.title: Before Spring: Tight Coupling
docs.text: Back in the old days (pre-Spring), your code looked like this:

docs.title: Inversion of Control (IoC)
docs.text:
"Don't call us, we'll call you."

That’s the essence of IoC. It means the control of object creation and management is inverted —

instead of your objects managing their own dependencies, some external framework or container does it for you .

Before IoC:

  • Your classes were responsible for creating and managing their own dependencies.
  • Your classes don’t care how things get created — only that they’re available when needed.

docs.title: Dependency Injection (DI) – How IoC Works in Practice
docs.text:
DI is the mechanism through which IoC is implemented.

There are 3 main types of DI in Spring:

  1. Constructor Injection
  2. Setter Injection
  3. Field Injection (via @Autowired)

docs.title: Constructor Injection – Preferred Way (Immutable & Testable)
docs.text:
Why use constructor injection?

  • Ensures required dependencies are present at construction time.
  • Makes your objects immutable.
  • Easier to test and reason about.

docs.title: What is the Spring Context?
docs.text:
• Definition: The Spring context is the memory space in the application where object instances (beans) are stored for Spring to manage.
  • Purpose:
    • Allows Spring to control and manage the lifecycle of objects.
    • Enables Spring to provide features like dependency injection, transaction management, and testing.
    • Acts as a bridge between application code and Spring's functionalities.
  • Analogy: Think of the Spring context as a container where you register the objects you want Spring to manage.

docs.title: Adding Beans to the Spring Context
docs.text: To add beans to the context, you need to register them explicitly. This ensures Spring is aware of their existence.

docs.title: Ways to Add Beans:
docs.text:
1. Using the @Bean Annotation

    • A method annotated with @Bean is executed during Spring's context initialization.

    • The returned object from the method is added as a bean to the context.

      @Configuration
public class ProjectConfig {

    @Bean
    Parrot parrot() {
        var p = new Parrot();
        p.setName("Koko");
        return p;
    }
}

      Steps:

      1. Annotate the class with @Configuration to designate it as a configuration class.
      2. Define a method that returns the object you want as a bean.

      3. Annotate the method with @Bean.

      4. The method name becomes the bean name unless explicitly defined.

      5. The object returned is added to the Spring context.
        • Example - Accessing the Bean:
      public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        var context = new AnnotationConfigApplicationContext(ProjectConfig.class);
        Parrot p = context.getBean(Parrot.class);
        System.out.println(p.getName());  // Output: Koko
    }
}

  2. Adding Multiple Beans:

    • You can add multiple beans of the same type to the context by defining multiple @Bean methods.

    • Each method should have a unique name.

      @Configuration
public class ProjectConfig {

    @Bean
    Parrot parrot1() {
        var p = new Parrot();
        p.setName("Koko");
        return p;
    }

    @Bean
    Parrot parrot2() {
        var p = new Parrot();
        p.setName("Miki");
        return p;
    }
}

      Use getBean("beanName", BeanClass.class) to retrieve a specific instance.

  3. Adding Beans of Different Types:

    • The Spring context can store objects of different types like String, Integer, etc.

    • These are treated the same as custom objects. 

      @Configuration
public class ProjectConfig {

    @Bean
    String message() {
        return "Hello, Spring!";
    }

    @Bean
    Integer number() {
        return 42;
    }
}

docs.title: Comparing Methods for Adding Beans
docs.text:
Spring provides three main ways to add beans to the context:

  1. @Bean Annotation:

    • Flexible: Works for both user-defined and library classes.
    • Suitable for adding multiple beans of the same type.

    • Stereotype Annotations (e.g., @Component):

    • Easier for user-defined classes.

    • Requires less code but less flexible than @Bean.

    • Programmatic Registration:

    • Used for dynamic or conditional bean registration.

    • Requires more code but offers the most customization.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. The Spring context is essential for Spring’s ability to manage object instances.
  2. The @Bean annotation is a powerful and flexible way to add objects to the Spring context.
  3. Multiple beans of the same type can coexist in the context, each identified by a unique name.
  4. Spring context can manage a wide variety of object types, from custom objects to primitives.

docs.title: Using Stereotype Annotations to Add Beans
docs.text:
• Definition: Stereotype annotations are used to mark classes that Spring should instantiate and manage as beans.
  • Common Annotations:
    1. @Component
    2. @Service - Indicates a service component in the business layer.
    3. @Repository - Marks a data access component.
    4. @Controller - Used for Spring MVC controllers.

docs.title: Steps to Add Beans with Stereotype Annotations
docs.text:
1. Annotate the Class:

    • Apply the appropriate stereotype annotation (e.g., @Component) to the class you want Spring to manage.

      @Component
public class Parrot {
    private String name;

    public String getName() {
        return name;
    }

    public void setName(String name) {
        this.name = name;
    }
}

  2. Enable Component Scanning:

    • Use the @ComponentScan annotation in a configuration class to specify the package where Spring should look for annotated classes.

      @Configuration
@ComponentScan(basePackages = "com.example")
public class ProjectConfig {
}

  3. Access the Bean:

    • Retrieve the bean from the Spring context.

      var context = new AnnotationConfigApplicationContext(ProjectConfig.class);
Parrot p = context.getBean(Parrot.class);

docs.title: Advantages:
docs.text:
• Reduces boilerplate code by avoiding the need to define methods for each bean.
  • Provides specialized annotations for better organization (e.g., @Service for business logic).

docs.title: Limitations:
docs.text:
• Only works for classes you control (i.e., you cannot add external library classes as beans).
  • Offers less control over the creation process compared to the @Bean annotation.

docs.title: Adding Beans Programmatically
docs.text: Introduced in Spring 5, programmatic registration of beans provides flexibility in dynamic scenarios.

docs.title: Steps for Programmatic Registration:
docs.text:
1. Create an Instance:

    • Instantiate the object you want to add to the context.

      Parrot parrot = new Parrot();
parrot.setName("Kiki");

  2. Define a Supplier:

    • Create a Supplier for the object.

      Supplier<Parrot> parrotSupplier = () -> parrot;

  3. Use registerBean:

    • Call the registerBean method on the Spring context to add the bean.

      context.registerBean("parrotBean", Parrot.class, parrotSupplier);

  4. Access the Bean:

    • Retrieve the bean from the context as usual

      Parrot retrievedParrot = context.getBean(Parrot.class);

docs.title: When to Use:
docs.text: • Scenarios requiring dynamic bean registration (e.g., conditional beans based on runtime parameters).

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Stereotype Annotations (e.g., @Component) simplify bean registration by automatically adding annotated classes to the Spring context.
  2. Programmatic Bean Registration allows dynamic addition of beans, providing flexibility for runtime conditions.
  3. Each method for bean addition (annotations or programmatic) has specific use cases and trade-offs in terms of control, simplicity, and applicability.

docs.title: Useful Annotations for Bean Lifecycle
docs.text:
1. @PostConstruct:

    • Executes initialization logic after the bean is created.

    • Example

      @Component
public class Parrot {
    @PostConstruct
    public void init() {
        this.name = "Kiki";
    }
}

  2. @PreDestroy:

    • Executes cleanup logic before the bean is destroyed.
    • Caution: Avoid placing critical operations here, as Spring may fail to call this method under some circumstances.

docs.title: 3. Relationships Among Beans
docs.text:
This section explains how to establish relationships among beans defined in a Spring configuration file. It focuses on two primary approaches:

  1. Direct Method Call Between @Bean Methods (Wiring).
  2. Using @Bean Method Parameters (Auto-Wiring).

The relationships enable one object to delegate tasks to another, reflecting common practices in object-oriented design.

1 Wiring Beans Using Direct Method Calls

Definition: ==Wiring involves explicitly calling one @Bean annotated method from another.== Spring ensures the method returns the same bean instance already in the context, avoiding redundant creation.

@Configuration
public class ProjectConfig {

  @Bean
  public Parrot parrot() {  
    Parrot p = new Parrot();
    p.setName("Koko");
    return p;
  }

  @Bean
  public Person person() {
    Person p = new Person();
    p.setName("Ella");
    p.setParrot(parrot());  // Direct method call
    return p;
  }
}

  • parrot() defines a Parrot bean named "Koko."
  • person() defines a Person bean named "Ella" and assigns the Parrot bean to the Person using the direct method call parrot().

docs.title: Execution Flow:
docs.text:
1. Spring creates the parrot bean and adds it to the context.
  2. Spring creates the person bean. When person() calls parrot(), Spring fetches the existing parrot bean from the context instead of creating a new instance.

docs.title: 2 Auto-Wiring Beans Using Method Parameters
docs.text:
Definition: In auto-wiring, Spring injects a bean into a method parameter by matching the parameter's type with a bean in the context. This method avoids direct method calls and provides better flexibility.

@Configuration
public class ProjectConfig {

  @Bean
  public Parrot parrot() {
    Parrot p = new Parrot();
    p.setName("Koko");
    return p;
  }

  @Bean
  public Person person(Parrot parrot) {  // Parameter injection
    Person p = new Person();
    p.setName("Ella");
    p.setParrot(parrot);
    return p;
  }
}
  1. person() takes a Parrot parameter.
  2. Spring detects the parameter type and injects the parrot bean from the context automatically.

docs.title: Execution Flow:
docs.text:
1. Spring creates the parrot bean and stores it in the context.
  2. When calling person(), Spring injects the parrot bean into the method's parameter.

Advantages of Auto-Wiring:

  • Decouples the configuration logic from method calls.
  • Works seamlessly with beans defined using annotations like @Component or @Service.

docs.title: Key Takeaways from Section 3.1
docs.text:
1. Beans can establish relationships in Spring using wiring or auto-wiring approaches.
  2. Wiring involves direct method calls, ensuring Spring fetches the existing bean.
  3. Auto-Wiring uses method parameters, making it more flexible for different bean types.
  4. Both approaches adhere to Spring's IoC principles, letting Spring manage the lifecycle and dependencies of beans.

docs.title: Using the @Autowired Annotation to Inject Beans
docs.text:
The @Autowired annotation simplifies the establishment of relationships between beans. By annotating a field, constructor, or setter, Spring automatically resolves and injects the required dependency from the context.

There are three main ways to use @Autowired:

  1. Field Injection.
  2. Constructor Injection.
  3. Setter Injection.

docs.title: Field Injection
docs.text:
Definition: Using @Autowired on a field directly injects a bean into the class.

@Component
public class Parrot {
    private String name = "Koko";

    @Override
    public String toString() {
        return "Parrot : " + name;
    }
}
____________________________________________________________________
@Component
public class Person {
    private String name = "Ella";

    @Autowired
    private Parrot parrot;  // Field Injection

    @Override
    public String toString() {
        return "Person's name: " + name + ", Person's parrot: " + parrot;
    }
}

____________________________________________________________________
@Configuration
@ComponentScan(basePackages = "com.example")
public class ProjectConfig {}
____________________________________________________________________

public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        var context = new AnnotationConfigApplicationContext(ProjectConfig.class);
        Person p = context.getBean(Person.class);
        System.out.println(p);
    }
}
____________________________________________________________________

console:

Person's name: Ella, Person's parrot: Parrot : Koko

  • Fields cannot be final, limiting immutability.
  • Dependency injection via fields is harder to test and maintain.

docs.title: 2 Constructor Injection
docs.text:
Definition: Injecting dependencies via a constructor is the most recommended and commonly used approach. This allows the fields to be final and ensures immutability.

@Component
public class Person {
    private final Parrot parrot;

    @Autowired  // Constructor Injection
    public Person(Parrot parrot) {
        this.parrot = parrot;
    }

    @Override
    public String toString() {
        return "Person's parrot: " + parrot;
    }
}

 When Spring creates the Person bean, it automatically injects the Parrot bean into the constructor.

Advantages:

  • Supports immutability.
  • Works seamlessly in unit testing by allowing explicit parameter injection.

docs.title: 3 Setter Injection
docs.text:
Definition: Dependencies are injected using a setter method. This is less common in modern development but can be used for optional dependencies.

@Component
public class Person {
    private Parrot parrot;

    @Autowired  // Setter Injection
    public void setParrot(Parrot parrot) {
        this.parrot = parrot;
    }

    @Override
    public String toString() {
        return "Person's parrot: " + parrot;
    }
}

Limitations:

  • Does not support immutability.
  • Rarely used in production due to verbosity and less clarity.

docs.title: 5. Circular Dependencies
docs.text:
A circular dependency happens when:

  1. Bean A requires Bean B to be created.
  2. Bean B requires Bean A to be created.

Spring’s dependency injection mechanism cannot resolve this because neither bean can be instantiated without the other.

Solutions for Circular Dependencies

  1. Refactor Dependencies

    • The most effective way to solve circular dependencies is to redesign the classes to remove the mutual dependency.

    • Example: Instead of Parrot depending on Person and vice versa, introduce a service or intermediary layer.

      @Component
public class ParrotService {
    private final Parrot parrot;
    private final Person person;

    @Autowired
    public ParrotService(Parrot parrot, Person person) {
        this.parrot = parrot;
        this.person = person;
    }
}

  2. Use @Lazy Annotation

    • Mark one of the dependencies as @Lazy to delay its initialization until it is actually needed.

      @Component
public class Parrot {
    private final Person person;

    @Autowired
    public Parrot(@Lazy Person person) {
        this.person = person;
    }
}

      Effect: Spring will initialize Person lazily, breaking the circular dependency.

  3. Use @PostConstruct

    • Initialize one of the dependencies after both beans are created using @PostConstruct.

docs.title: 6. Choosing from Multiple Beans in the Spring Cont
docs.text: When the Spring context contains multiple beans of the same type, the framework faces ambiguity when deciding which bean to inject.

docs.title: Scenario 1: Matching by Parameter Name
docs.text:
• Behavior: Spring selects a bean whose name matches the parameter name in a constructor or method.

    @Configuration
public class ProjectConfig {

    @Bean
    public Parrot parrot1() {
        Parrot p = new Parrot();
        p.setName("Koko");
        return p;
    }

    @Bean
    public Parrot parrot2() {
        Parrot p = new Parrot();
        p.setName("Miki");
        return p;
    }

    @Bean
    public Person person(Parrot parrot2) {  // Matches the bean named 'parrot2'
        Person p = new Person();
        p.setName("Ella");
        p.setParrot(parrot2);
        return p;
    }
}

docs.title: Using @Primary
docs.text:
• Behavior: When multiple beans exist, Spring selects the bean marked as primary.

    @Bean
@Primary
public Parrot parrot2() {
    Parrot p = new Parrot();
    p.setName("Miki");
    return p;
}

    Effect:

    • Even without specifying a parameter name, Spring defaults to the @Primary bean.

docs.title: Using @Qualifier
docs.text:
• Definition: The @Qualifier annotation explicitly specifies which bean to inject, avoiding ambiguity.

    @Configuration
public class ProjectConfig {

    @Bean
    public Parrot parrot1() {
        Parrot p = new Parrot();
        p.setName("Koko");
        return p;
    }

    @Bean
    public Parrot parrot2() {
        Parrot p = new Parrot();
        p.setName("Miki");
        return p;
    }

    @Bean
    public Person person(@Qualifier("parrot2") Parrot parrot) {
        Person p = new Person();
        p.setName("Ella");
        p.setParrot(parrot);
        return p;
    }
}

docs.title: Scenario 4: No Matching Strategy
docs.text:
• If none of the above strategies is applied:
    • Result: Spring throws a NoUniqueBeanDefinitionException.
    • Solution: Use @Primary or @Qualifier to resolve the conflict.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. When multiple beans of the same type exist, Spring needs a strategy to resolve ambiguity.
  2. Use parameter names, @Primary, or @Qualifier annotations to guide Spring in selecting the correct bean.
  3. The @Qualifier annotation is explicit and robust, reducing the risk of issues during refactoring.
  4. Failing to specify a bean in ambiguous situations results in a NoUniqueBeanDefinitionException.

docs.title: 1. Stereotypes annotations
docs.text:
Spring provides four main stereotype annotations:

  1. @Component
  2. @Controller
  3. @Service
  4. @Repository

All these annotations (@Component, @Service, @Repository, @Controller) are meta-annotated with @Component, meaning that Spring treats them all as @Component behind the scenes.

They serve the same primary purpose: marking a class as a Spring bean, which means that Spring will manage its lifecycle and dependencies.

While these annotations may seem interchangeable at first glance, they serve distinct purposes in a Spring application's architecture. Let's explore why they're not interchangeable and how Spring manages them differently.

Annotation Primary Use Layer Additional Functionality @Component Generic components Any Base for all other stereotype annotations @Controller Web request handling Presentation Enables handler mapping for @RequestMapping @Service Business logic Service No additional functionality (semantic) @Repository Data access and persistence Data Access Exception translation to DataAccessException

  • @Component
    • This is the most generic stereotype annotation.
    • It's used to mark any Spring-managed component that doesn't fit into more specific categories.
    • All other stereotype annotations are specializations of @Component.
  • @Controller
    • Used in the presentation layer for handling web requests.
    • Automatically detected by Spring MVC's component scanning.
    • Enables additional web-related functionality:
      • Handler mapping for @RequestMapping annotations.
      • Model attribute handling.
      • Automatic view resolution.
  • @Service
    • Indicates that the class contains business logic.
    • Doesn't provide any additional functionality over @Component.
    • Serves as a semantic marker for service layer components.
  • @Repository
    • Used for data access objects (DAOs) or repository classes.
    • Enables automatic persistence exception translation.
    • Translates technology-specific exceptions (e.g., SQLException) into Spring's DataAccessException hierarchy.

Now, let's address why these annotations are not interchangeable and how Spring manages them differently, despite their identical definition at the class level:

  1. Semantic Meaning:
    • While all these annotations mark a class as a Spring-managed component, they carry different semantic meanings.
    • This semantic difference helps developers and tools  to understand the intended purpose of each component in the application architecture.
  2. AOP (Aspect-Oriented Programming) Pointcuts:
    • Spring and other frameworks often use these annotations as pointcuts for applying aspects.
    • For example, transaction management might be applied differently to @Service and @Repository beans.
  3. Exception Handling:
    • @Repository triggers automatic exception translation.
    • Spring wraps platform-specific exceptions and rethrows them as one of Spring's unified unchecked DataAccessException subclasses.
  4. Request Mapping:
    • @Controller is specifically recognized by Spring MVC for mapping web requests.
    • It enables the use of @RequestMapping and other web-specific annotations.
  5. Component Scanning:
    • While all these annotations are picked up by component scanning, Spring may apply different post-processing steps based on the specific annotation.
  6. Framework Integration:
    • Other frameworks or tools may provide special handling for specific annotations.
    • For instance, Spring Data repositories are typically annotated with @Repository.

We can see in the diagram that component scan detects all strereotype annotations

but BeanPostProcessor applies specific processing based on the annotation type

docs.title: 1.1 Background handling by spring
docs.text:
Spring's common handling for stereotype annotations involves several key processes and components. Let's break this down:

  1. Component Scanning
  2. Bean Definition Creation
  3. Bean Post-Processing
  4. Dependency Injection
  5. Lifecycle Management

  1. Component Scanning:
    • Spring scans the classpath for classes annotated with stereotype annotations.
    • This is typically configured using @ComponentScan or automatically with @SpringBootApplication.
  2. Bean Definition Creation:
    • For each detected component, Spring creates a bean definition.
    • This definition includes metadata about the bean, such as its class, scope, and dependencies.
  3. Bean Post-Processing:
    • After bean definitions are created, various ==BeanPostProcessors== run to modify or enhance the beans.
    • This is where much of the "magic" of Spring happens, like applying AOP proxies.
  4. Dependency Injection:
    • Spring resolves and injects dependencies for each bean.
    • This can be done via constructor injection, setter injection, or field injection.
  5. Lifecycle Management:
    • Spring manages the complete lifecycle of each bean, from instantiation to destruction.
    • This includes calling any defined initialization or destruction methods.

Key Components involved:

Component Description Role in Annotation Handling ClassPathBeanDefinitionScanner Scans the classpath for candidate components Detects classes with stereotype annotations BeanDefinitionRegistry Holds bean definitions Stores metadata about detected components BeanFactory Central interface for accessing beans Manages bean instances BeanPostProcessor Interface for modifying bean instances Applies additional processing to beans AutowiredAnnotationBeanPostProcessor Specific BeanPostProcessor Handles dependency injection ApplicationContext High-level interface for accessing beans Coordinates the overall process

  • Detection:
    • During component scanning, Spring uses ClassPathBeanDefinitionScanner to find classes annotated with @Component or its specializations.
    • It looks at the class-level annotations to determine if a class should be considered a Spring-managed component.
  • Bean Definition Creation:
    • For each detected component, Spring creates a BeanDefinition.
    • The BeanDefinition includes metadata such as the bean's class, scope, lazy-init flag, and dependencies.
  • Post-Processing:
    • Various BeanPostProcessors run on each bean.
    • A crucial one is AutowiredAnnotationBeanPostProcessor, which handles dependency injection for @Autowired, @Value, and @Inject annotations.
  • Proxy Creation:
    • If necessary, Spring creates proxy objects around the original bean instances.
